&= 0.10 \times 1.875 \times 10^7 \\
V_{part} &= 1.875 \times 10^6\ \text{mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute VBR (volumetric material deposition rate):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VBR &= v \cdot h \cdot d \\
&= 800\ \text{mm/s} \times 0.03\ \text{mm} \times 0.1\ \text{mm} \\
VBR &= 2.4\ \text{mm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to a more practical unit (cm³/hr):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VBR &= 2.4\ \text{mm}^3/\text{s} \times 3600\ \text{s/hr} \times \dfrac{1\ \text{cm}^3}{1000\ \text{mm}^3} \\
&= 2.4 \times 3.6 \\
VBR &= 8.64\ \text{cm}^3/\text{hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert part volume to cm³:**

$$
V_{part} = 1.875 \times 10^6\ \text{mm}^3 = 1875\ \text{cm}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Compute build time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{build} &= \frac{V_{part}}{VBR} \\
&= \frac{1875}{8.64} \\
T_{build} &\approx 217\ \text{hours} \approx 9\ \text{days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Sanity Check:** A packing efficiency of 10 % within a large envelope is optimistic. A realistic 1–2 % packing (since the part may be only a small bracket) would push the build time to several months — a key reason why **build chamber nesting** is critical in production AM.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the **most-asked KTU comparison** for Part B 14-mark questions. Students are expected to draw it from memory.

| Parameter | FDM | SLA | SLS | SLM | DED | Binder Jetting |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Material State** | Filament | Liquid resin | Polymer powder | Metal powder | Powder / wire | Powder + binder |
| **Energy Source** | Thermal (heater) | UV laser / projector | $\text{CO}_2$ laser | Fiber laser | Laser / Arc / EB | None (chemical) |
| **Layer Thickness** | 100–300 μm | 25–100 μm | 80–120 μm | 20–50 μm | 0.5–2 mm | 50–200 μm |
| **Support Required?** | Yes | Yes | No (loose powder) | Yes (metal) | Minimal | No (loose powder) |
| **Accuracy (typical)** | $\pm 0.2\ \text{mm}$ | $\pm 0.1\ \text{mm}$ | $\pm 0.2\ \text{mm}$ | $\pm 0.05\ \text{mm}$ | $\pm 0.5\ \text{mm}$ | $\pm 0.1\ \text{mm}$ |
| **Strength** | Moderate | Brittle (uncured) | Good (nylon) | Excellent (metallic) | Excellent | Moderate (porous) |
| **Surface Finish** | Rough | Excellent | Good | Moderate (post-machined) | Poor (machined) | Good |
| **Best Application** | Prototyping, jigs | Dental, jewelry | Functional nylon parts | Aerospace metal parts | Repair, large parts | Foundry molds, mass metal |

## 3.3 Conceptual Derivation: Why PBF Needs Support Structures in Metal but Not in Polymer

**Step 1 — Heat dissipation in polymer powder:**
Polymer (e.g., nylon in SLS) has **low thermal conductivity** ($\sim 0.25\ \text{W/mK}$). The unfused powder surrounding the part remains loose and acts as a thermal insulator and mechanical support. Hence, overhangs do not collapse.

**Step 2 — Heat dissipation in metal powder:**
Metal powders (e.g., SS316L in SLM) have **high thermal conductivity** ($\sim 15\ \text{W/mK}$). The molten pool loses heat rapidly into surrounding powder, and the un-fused powder grains **do not mechanically interlock** strongly enough to resist gravity and residual stress. Therefore, support structures (thin metallic trees attached to the build platform) are mandatory for overhangs greater than $45°$.

**Step 3 — Distortion mitigation:**
Differential cooling in SLM produces residual stress $\sigma_r$, approximated by:

$$
\sigma_r = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

Where $E$ is Young's modulus and $\alpha$ is the coefficient of thermal expansion. The supports anchor the part to the platform, restricting thermal contraction and reducing warping. This is a common source of **distortion in SLM** and a frequent 7-mark sub-question in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ASTM F42 / ISO/ASTM 52900 Classification Tree

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the standardized taxonomy of AM processes as mandated by ASTM F42 and adopted by KTU syllabus:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING PROCESSES"] --> B["VAT PHOTOPOLYMERIZATION VPP"]
    A --> C["POWDER BED FUSION PBF"]
    A --> D["MATERIAL EXTRUSION MEX"]
    A --> E["MATERIAL JETTING MJT"]
    A --> F["BINDER JETTING BJT"]
    A --> G["SHEET LAMINATION SHL"]
    A --> H["DIRECT ENERGY DEPOSITION DED"]

    B --> B1["SLA Laser Scanning"]
    B --> B2["DLP Projected Mask"]
    B --> B3["CLIP Continuous Interface"]

    C --> C1["SLS Polymer Powder"]
    C --> C2["SLM Metal Powder"]
    C --> C3["EBM Vacuum Electron Beam"]
    C --> C4["MJF Fusion Agent"]

    D --> D1["FDM Fused Deposition"]
    D --> D2["FFF Open Source Variant"]
    D --> D3["Robocasting Paste Extrusion"]

    E --> E1["PolyJet Photopolymer Droplets"]
    E --> E2["DOD Wax Droplets"]
    E --> E3["Nanojet High Resolution"]

    F --> F1["Metal Binder Jetting"]
    F --> F2["Sand Binder Jetting Foundry"]
    F --> F3["Ceramic Binder Jetting"]

    G --> G1["LOM Paper Sheets"]
    G --> G2["UAM Ultrasonic Metal Foils"]

    H --> H1["LENS Laser Powder"]
    H --> H2["EBAM Wire Electron Beam"]
    H --> H3["WAAM Wire Arc Welding"]
    H --> H4["Plasma Arc DED"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Generic Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) System

The following **Mermaid flow diagram** shows the operational flow inside a PBF machine — the most commonly tested system in KTU examinations:

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA["CAD STL File Import"] --> stepB["Slicer Software Layer Slicing"]
    stepB --> stepC["Recoater Spreads Powder Layer"]
    stepC --> stepD["Laser / EB Scans Fusing Pattern"]
    stepD --> stepE["Layer Solidifies Cools"]
    stepE --> stepF{"Is Build Complete?"}
    stepF -- "No" --> stepC
    stepF -- "Yes" --> stepG["Cool Down Chamber"]
    stepG --> stepH["Powder Removal Sieving"]
    stepH --> stepI["Support Removal Post Processing"]
    stepI --> stepJ["Heat Treatment Stress Relief"]
    stepJ --> stepK["Final Part Ready for Use"]

    style stepA fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style stepD fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px
    style stepK fill:#abebc6,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Process-Selection Decision Flowchart

The following diagram maps a typical **engineering decision process** to choose the right AM technology based on application requirements:

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["Start Application Requirement"] --> q1{"Material Required?"}
    q1 -- "Polymer" --> q2{"Visual or Functional?"}
    q1 -- "Metal" --> q3{"Large or Small Part?"}
    q1 -- "Ceramic / Sand" --> pathBJT["Use Binder Jetting"]

    q2 -- "Visual Prototype" --> pathSLA["Use SLA or DLP"]
    q2 -- "Functional Load Bearing" --> pathSLS["Use SLS Nylon"]

    q3 -- "Small High Strength" --> pathSLM["Use SLM Powder Bed Fusion"]
    q3 -- "Large or Repair" --> pathDED["Use DED WAAM or LENS"]

    pathSLA --> end["Final Part"]
    pathSLS --> end
    pathSLM --> end
    pathDED --> end
    pathBJT --> end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The three diagrams above form a complete **visual study sheet** for Module 2. KTU examiners often award partial marks (2–3 out of 14) for a well-labeled process flow diagram in descriptive answers. Students are advised to redraw these by hand during revision.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"List the seven categories of additive manufacturing processes as per ASTM F42 classification."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

As per ASTM F42 / ISO/ASTM 52900, the seven categories are:

1. **Vat Photopolymerization (VPP)** – e.g., SLA, DLP.
2. **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** – e.g., SLS, SLM, EBM.
3. **Material Extrusion (MEX)** – e.g., FDM, FFF.
4. **Material Jetting (MJT)** – e.g., PolyJet, DOD.
5. **Binder Jetting (BJT)** – e.g., metal/sand binder jetting.
6. **Sheet Lamination (SHL)** – e.g., LOM, UAM.
7. **Direct Energy Deposition (DED)** – e.g., LENS, WAAM, EBAM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award 0.5 marks for each correctly named category. Spelling of the acronyms is essential; "SLA" alone without context is not accepted.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between FDM and SLA processes on the basis of (i) material form, (ii) energy source, and (iii) post-processing."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | FDM | SLA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(i) Material Form** | Solid thermoplastic filament (1.75 / 2.85 mm) | Liquid photopolymer resin |
| **(ii) Energy Source** | Thermal heater (180–260 °C) | UV laser (355 nm) or projector |
| **(iii) Post-Processing** | Support removal; optional sanding | Isopropyl alcohol wash; UV post-cure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Tabular answers score 1 mark per correct row, even if the third column is partly incorrect. Avoid vague statements like "FDM uses heat."

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PEPET752]**

**(a)** Explain the **Stereolithography (SLA)** process with a neat schematic. Describe the working principle, materials used, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various **applications of additive manufacturing in the medical and aerospace industries**, with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
Stereolithography (SLA) belongs to the **Vat Photopolymerization** family. A vat is filled with a liquid photopolymer resin that is photosensitive to ultraviolet (UV) light. A UV laser (typically 355 nm from a frequency-tripled Nd:YAG or He-Cd laser) traces the cross-section of the part on the surface of the resin. Wherever the laser strikes, the resin undergoes **photo-polymerization** — a chain reaction in which monomers and oligomers cross-link to form a solid polymer network.

**Process Sequence (2 marks):**

1. The build platform is positioned just below the resin surface (one layer thickness below).
2. A recoater blade levels the resin.
3. The UV laser scans the cross-section, solidifying one layer.
4. The platform descends by **one layer thickness (typically 25–100 μm)**.
5. A fresh layer of resin flows over the solidified layer.
6. The cycle repeats until the build is complete.

**Schematic (1 mark):**
*(Student should draw: Vat containing resin → Build platform moving down → UV laser → Galvo mirrors → Computer controlling scan path.)*

**Materials Used (1 mark):**
- Standard resins (acrylates, epoxies).
- Castable resins (for jewelry patterns).
- Biocompatible resins (dental surgical guides).
- Clear / transparent resins (optical prototypes).
- Tough/durable resins (functional prototypes).

**Advantages (0.5 mark):** Excellent surface finish, high accuracy, wide material range.
**Limitations (0.5 mark):** Brittle parts, requires post-curing, support structures needed, photopolymers degrade under sunlight.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Medical Applications (3.5 marks):**

1. **Patient-Specific Anatomical Models:** CT/MRI data converted to STL and 3D-printed in SLA/PolyJet for surgical planning. Reduces operating time by up to 30 %.
2. **Surgical Guides & Splints:** DLP-printed drill guides for dental implants, ensuring sub-millimeter accuracy.
3. **Orthopedic Implants:** SLM-printed Ti-6Al-4V hip cups, spinal cages, and cranial plates that match the patient's bone geometry (EBM/electron beam).
4. **Hearing Aids & Dental Crowns:** Mass-produced via SLA (largest AM industry segment).
5. **Bioprinting (Emerging):** Vat-photopolymerization of cell-laden hydrogels for tissue scaffolds.

**Aerospace Applications (3.5 marks):**

1. **Topology-Optimized Brackets:** Airbus A350 XWB and Boeing 787 use SLM-printed Ti brackets, achieving 30–55 % weight savings.
2. **Fuel Injector Nozzles:** GE Aviation's LEAP engine uses 19 AM-printed fuel nozzle tips per engine, consolidated from 18 parts into 1, with 25 % weight reduction.
3. **Combustion Liners & Heat Exchangers:** SLM-printed Inconel 718 with internal lattice cooling channels.
4. **Repair and Refurbishment:** DED (laser cladding) is used to repair turbine blades, saving millions in replacement costs.
5. **Satellite Components:** SLM-printed RF waveguides and antenna brackets with optimized electromagnetic properties.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplemented]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)** process. Discuss its process parameters and their effect on part quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **SLM, SLS, and EBM** processes with respect to energy source, material, build environment, support requirement, and applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Process Description (2 marks):**
Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM), commercialized by Stratasys, is a **Material Extrusion (MEX)** process. A thermoplastic filament (typically 1.75 or 2.85 mm diameter) is fed into a **liquefier** (a heated nozzle maintained at 180–260 °C depending on polymer). The filament melts and is extruded through a fine nozzle (typically 0.2–0.8 mm diameter) onto a build platform, following a pre-defined path generated by the slicer software. The extrudate solidifies rapidly upon cooling, bonding to the previously deposited layer.

**Schematic / Block Diagram (1 mark):**
*(Student should draw: Filament spool → Idler / drive gear → Liquefier / heater → Nozzle → Build platform → X-Y-Z motion system → Part being built layer-by-layer.)*

**Key Process Parameters (3 marks):**

1. **Layer Thickness:** Thinner layers (e.g., 0.1 mm) improve surface finish and reduce stair-stepping but increase build time.
2. **Nozzle Temperature:** Higher temperature improves inter-layer adhesion and reduces porosity, but can cause stringing and shrinkage.
3. **Print Speed:** Faster speeds reduce build time but may cause under-extrusion and reduced accuracy.
4. **Infill Density & Pattern:** Affects strength-to-weight ratio. Higher infill increases strength but also weight and time.
5. **Raster Angle & Orientation:** Strongest in direction of raster; anisotropic behavior is a known FDM limitation.
6. **Build Platform Temperature:** Heated bed (50–110 °C) reduces warping in ABS.
7. **Support Structures:** Required for overhangs $> 45°$ to prevent collapse.

**Effect on Part Quality (1 mark):**
The combination of parameters determines **mechanical strength, surface roughness (Ra), dimensional accuracy, and build time**. FDM parts are anisotropic; tensile strength in Z-direction can be 30–50 % lower than in XY direction.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

| Parameter | SLM (PBF-Metal) | SLS (PBF-Polymer) | EBM (PBF-Metal) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Energy Source** | Fiber laser (~200 W, 1070 nm) | $\text{CO}_2$ laser (10–50 W) | Electron beam (~3 kW, vacuum) |
| **Material** | Metal powders (SS316L, Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel 718, AlSi10Mg) | Polymer powders (PA12, PA11, TPU) | Metal powders (Ti-6Al-4V, Co-Cr) |
| **Build Environment** | Inert gas (Argon / Nitrogen) | Nitrogen atmosphere | High vacuum |
| **Support Required?** | Yes, mandatory for overhangs | No (loose powder supports part) | Yes, mandatory for overhangs |
| **Layer Thickness** | 20–50 μm | 80–120 μm | 50–70 μm |
| **Build Speed** | Slow (smaller melt pool) | Medium | Faster than SLM (high beam power) |
| **Residual Stress** | High (slow cooling) | Low | Low (high temperature build) |
| **Surface Finish** | Moderate (requires post-machining) | Good (granular texture) | Moderate (powder adheres to surface) |
| **Applications** | Aerospace brackets, medical implants, automotive functional parts | Functional prototypes, low-volume production, jigs & fixtures | Orthopedic implants (no residual stress), aerospace Ti parts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above table is a complete answer if labeled properly. Allocate ~0.5 mark per row × 9 rows = 4.5 marks, plus 2.5 marks for explanation / applications.

**Key Comparative Insights (Final 0.5 mark):**
- EBM is preferred for **stress-sensitive Ti-6Al-4V implants** due to its high build temperature (~700 °C) which in-situ stress-relieves the part.
- SLS is preferred for **nylon functional parts** with no support requirement.
- SLM is the **gold standard for high-density aerospace and medical metal parts**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing SLM and SLS:** Many students write "Selective Laser Melting and Selective Laser Sintering are the same." They are not. SLM fully melts metal; SLS sinters polymer below melt point.
> 2. **Wrong Energy Source:** Writing "SLA uses a $\text{CO}_2$ laser" is incorrect. SLA uses a **UV (355 nm) laser**, not infrared.
> 3. **Forgetting the "Seven Categories":** The ASTM F42 list has **exactly seven** process categories. Writing five or ten is wrong and costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the Diagram:** A question worth 7 marks on FDM or SLA *demands* a schematic. Skipping it costs at least 1 mark.
> 5. **No units in numerical problems:** Always include units in the final answer (e.g., $83.33\ \text{J/mm}^3$).
> 6. **Confusing DED and PBF:** DED deposits material as it melts; PBF melts material already deposited as a powder bed. This is the most common 2-mark loss.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: Types and Applications**

- ✅ The **ASTM F42 / ISO/ASTM 52900** standard defines **seven** AM process categories. Memorize the acronyms: **VPP, PBF, MEX, MJT, BJT, SHL, DED**.
- ✅ **VPP** = liquid resin + UV light (best surface finish).
- ✅ **PBF** = powder bed + thermal energy (laser or EB). Sub-types: **SLS (polymer), SLM (metal), EBM (vacuum), MJF (HP-specific)**.
- ✅ **MEX** = filament extrusion (FDM is the workhorse of desktop AM).
- ✅ **MJT** = droplet jetting (PolyJet, DOD) — best for multi-material and transparent parts.
- ✅ **BJT** = powder + liquid binder — fastest build rate, ideal for foundry molds and mass metal.
- ✅ **SHL** = sheet bonding (LOM, UAM) — unique for embedded electronics and hybrid metals.
- ✅ **DED** = powder/wire + focused energy — used for **repair** and **large parts** (WAAM).
- ✅ Key formulas: $VBR = v \cdot h \cdot d$ and $ED = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot d}$. Both are **guaranteed** in numerical KTU problems.
- ✅ Polymer powders in SLS do **not** require supports; metal powders in SLM/EBM **do**.
- ✅ **Anisotropy** is a defining limitation of FDM — strength depends on raster orientation.
- ✅ **Aerospace Ti-6Al-4V** and **medical implants** are flagship applications of SLM/EBM.
- ✅ **Hearing aids and dental aligners** are the largest commercial AM products (SLA).
- ✅ **GE LEAP engine fuel nozzle** is the classic AM success story in aerospace.
- ✅ **EBM** operates in vacuum with high build temperature → minimal residual stress → preferred for implants.
- ✅ For numerical problems, **always carry units** and verify dimensional consistency.
- ✅ Comparison questions demand a **table** with at least 5 process parameters (material, energy, accuracy, finish, application).
- ✅ Diagram: redraw the FDM block diagram and the PBF process flow for every revision cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Mile Exam Strategy**
> For a 14-mark Part B question, aim for: **(i) labeled schematic (2 marks) + (ii) process sequence (3 marks) + (iii) parameter table (5 marks) + (iv) applications and limitations (4 marks)**. This allocation matches KTU valuation patterns and ensures full marks when the content is accurate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Jetting and Binder Jetting
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Material Jetting and Binder Jetting

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Material Jetting (MJ) — The Inkjet of Manufacturing

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)
**Material Jetting (MJ)** is a *Droplet-based Additive Manufacturing* process classified under **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021** in the category of *Material Jetting* (MJ), in which build material is selectively deposited as **discrete droplets** of a liquid photopolymer, wax, or metal suspension through one or more **piezoelectric or thermal inkjet print heads**, and each deposited droplet is immediately (or nearly so) **cured/solidified** by **Ultraviolet (UV) light** or by cooling to form a solid layer of the part.

> [!NOTE]
> **ISO/ASTM 52900 Definition (verbatim):**
> *"Material jetting is an additive manufacturing process in which droplets of build material are selectively deposited, jetted, or sprayed, and then cured/solidified, layer by layer, to form a part."*

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Desktop Photo Printer, But in 3D"
Imagine the **color inkjet printer** on your desk. The print head sweeps left-to-right, **squirting micro-droplets** of cyan, magenta, yellow, and black ink onto paper. Now imagine:
1. That same print head is **squirting liquid photopolymer** instead of ink.
2. A **UV lamp** trails right behind the print head and **freezes each droplet instantly**.
3. The build platform then **drops down by 16 µm**, and a fresh layer of droplets is jetted on top.
4. Repeat **layer-by-layer** until the part is complete — **often in full color and in multiple materials simultaneously**.

That is exactly what **PolyJet** (Stratasys) and **MJM (Multi-Jet Modeling, 3D Systems)** do. You are essentially *printing the part out of liquid resin the way you would print a photograph.*

---

## 1.2 Binder Jetting (BJ) — The "Sand + Glue" Approach

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)
**Binder Jetting (BJ)** is a *Powder Bed Fusion–adjacent* process classified under **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021** in the category of *Binder Jetting* (BJT), in which a **liquid bonding agent (binder)** is selectively deposited, droplet-by-droplet, onto a **loose powder bed** using inkjet print heads, **without the application of heat** during the build phase. The binder locally **glues** adjacent powder particles together; the unbound powder acts as a *self-supporting sacrificial material*. After the build, parts undergo **de-powdering** and post-processing (curing, infiltration, sintering).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from MJ:** In **Material Jetting**, the *jetting material itself* becomes the part (e.g., liquid photopolymer). In **Binder Jetting**, the *jetting material is the adhesive*, and the *bulk part is made of powder that was already spread on the bed.*

### Conceptual Analogy — "Gluing Sandcastles at the Grain Level"
Picture a child building a sandcastle on the beach using a small dropper bottle of watered-down glue:
1. A thin, even layer of **dry sand** is spread across a tray.
2. The child uses the dropper to **trace glue lines** only on the sand grains that should become the castle wall.
3. The glue-soaked sand **sticks together**; the un-glued sand stays loose.
4. A new layer of sand is sprinkled on top, the pattern is repeated, and the 3D shape grows.
5. When finished, the child lifts the part out and **brushes away the loose sand** — the final castle emerges.

That is precisely **Binder Jetting** in operation. Industrial systems use **metal, sand, or ceramic powders** plus **polymeric binders**, but the underlying principle is identical to gluing sandcastles — just with micron-level precision.

---

## 1.3 Quick Side-by-Side Snapshot

| Parameter | Material Jetting (MJ) | Binder Jetting (BJ) |
|---|---|---|
| Jetting medium | Build material (photopolymer, wax) | Adhesive binder (cyanoacrylate-like) |
| Powder used? | **No** | **Yes** (metal, sand, ceramic, polymer) |
| Heat during build? | No (photopolymerization) | **No** (room temperature) |
| Curing mechanism | UV light | Chemical binder adhesion |
| Support material | Wax-like dissolvable support | **Loose powder** itself acts as support |
| Typical layer thickness | **16 µm – 32 µm** | **50 µm – 200 µm** |
| Accuracy | **±0.05 mm** (high) | **±0.2 mm** (moderate) |
| Surface roughness | Very smooth (Ra 0.5–2 µm) | Rougher (Ra 4–10 µm) |
| Build speed | Moderate | **Fastest AM process** for large parts |
| Post-processing | Minimal (support removal) | **Extensive** (de-powdering, infiltration, sintering) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Both processes are *binderless in the powder sense* for MJ (no powder is involved), and *heatless in the build phase* for BJ. Both belong to the **"Jetting Family"** of AM processes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Material Jetting — Operating Principle & Subsystem Breakdown

### 2.1.1 The Five Functional Subsystems

A typical Material Jetting system (PolyJet / MJM) consists of five interconnected subsystems:

1. **Build material reservoir (cartridge)** — stores the liquid photopolymer or wax. Many systems have *two or more cartridges* (e.g., VeroWhite + VeroClear + TangoBlack rubber-like).
2. **Jetting module / print head assembly** — contains hundreds of **piezoelectric nozzles** (commonly 600+ nozzles per head). Each nozzle ejects a **single droplet** of **6 – 80 picolitres** when a voltage pulse squeezes the piezo element.
3. **UV curing lamp** — typically a **UV-LED array** mounted on the same gantry as the print head. Cures the photopolymer droplet within milliseconds.
4. **Build platform (Z-stage)** — moves downward in increments equal to the **layer thickness** (typically **16 µm**).
5. **Roller / wiper system** — smooths the previously jetted layer before jetting the next, ensuring uniform thickness.

### 2.1.2 Photopolymer Chemistry (The "Why" of Curing)

The build material is a **free-radical photopolymer resin** containing:
- **Monomers** (e.g., acrylates, methacrylates)
- **Oligomers** (short polymer chains with reactive ends)
- **Photoinitiators** (e.g., TPO, BAPO) — molecules that absorb UV photons and **fragment into free radicals**
- **Additives** (UV absorbers, colorants, tougheners)

The reaction sequence:

$$\text{Photoinitiator} \xrightarrow{h\nu \;(UV)} \text{Free Radicals}$$

$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{Monomer} \rightarrow \text{R–M}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{R–(M)}_n^{\bullet}$$

$$\text{Polymer Chain Formation (Chain Growth Polymerization)}$$

The free radical attacks the C=C double bond of the acrylate monomer, opening it and creating a new radical, propagating a chain reaction. In **milliseconds**, the liquid drop transitions from **liquid → gel → solid**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why UV and not heat?** Heat-curing would cause thermal expansion and shrinkage distortions in already-cured underlying layers. UV curing is **localized** (only the jetted droplet cures) and produces minimal thermal stress.

### 2.1.3 Drop-on-Demand (DOD) vs Continuous Jetting

There are two actuation modes for droplet ejection:

- **Continuous Inkjet (CIJ):** A continuous stream of fluid is forced through a nozzle and broken into droplets via **Rayleigh instability**. Used in **label printing**, *not* in MJ for AM because of waste and accuracy limitations.
- **Drop-on-Demand (DOD):** A droplet is ejected only when needed by either (a) a **piezoelectric** actuator or (b) a **thermal** bubble. **All material jetting AM systems use DOD piezoelectric** actuation because of its precision and consistent droplet volume.

### 2.1.4 The Droplet Volume Equation

A piezoelectric DOD nozzle ejects a droplet whose volume depends on the applied voltage pulse, nozzle diameter, and fluid properties. The **diameter of the deposited droplet on the substrate** is given by:

$$
d_{spot} \;\approx\; \alpha \cdot d_{nozzle} \cdot \left( \frac{V_p}{V_{piezo}} \right)^{1/3}
$$

where:
- $d_{spot}$ = diameter of the droplet spot on the substrate (µm)
- $d_{nozzle}$ = nozzle orifice diameter (typically 20 – 50 µm)
- $V_p$ = applied piezo voltage pulse (Volts)
- $V_{piezo}$ = characteristic piezo voltage (Volts)
- $\alpha$ = spreading coefficient (typically 1.5 – 2.5 depending on surface energy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is droplet size critical?** Smaller droplets → higher resolution → smoother surfaces → longer build times. There is a fundamental **resolution vs. speed trade-off** in MJ systems.

### 2.1.5 Build Time Estimation for MJ

$$
T_{build} \;=\; \frac{h_{part}}{t_{layer}} \cdot t_{layer\_unit}
$$

More explicitly:

$$
T_{build} \;=\; \frac{A_{bed}}{w_{head} \cdot v_{jet}} \cdot N_{layers} \cdot t_{rec}
$$

where:
- $h_{part}$ = part height (mm)
- $t_{layer}$ = layer thickness (typically **0.016 mm**)
- $A_{bed}$ = build platform area (mm²)
- $w_{head}$ = print head width (mm)
- $v_{jet}$ = jetting head traverse speed (mm/s)
- $N_{layers} = h_{part} \,/\, t_{layer}$ = total number of layers
- $t_{rec}$ = recoating/curing time per layer (s)

---

## 2.2 Binder Jetting — Operating Principle & Subsystem Breakdown

### 2.2.1 The Five Functional Subsystems

1. **Powder feed chamber (supply piston)** — holds the loose powder (metal, sand, ceramic, polymer).
2. **Powder spreader / roller (counter-rotating)** — spreads a **uniform thin layer** (typically **50 – 200 µm**) of powder from the feed chamber to the build chamber.
3. **Binder jetting print head (DOD piezoelectric)** — deposits binder droplets only in the cross-section of the part being built.
4. **Build chamber (build piston)** — descends by one layer thickness after each layer is jetted.
5. **Depowdering station (offline post-processing)** — extracts the green part from the powder bed, brushes off loose powder.

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Layer Cycle

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Build piston lowers by one layer thickness $t_l$. |
| 2 | Powder feed piston rises by one layer thickness. |
| 3 | Roller spreads powder from feed → build chamber. |
| 4 | Print head jets binder droplets on cross-section pattern. |
| 5 | Binder penetrates powder via capillary action and reacts. |
| 6 | Repeat for next layer. |
| 7 | After build → **depowdering** → **curing** → optional **infiltration / sintering**. |

### 2.2.3 The Binder Chemistry & Capillary Action

The binder is typically an **aqueous polymeric solution** (e.g., 2-pyrrolidone + water + additives) or a **furan resin** (for sand molds). It works by:

1. **Capillary penetration:** Surface tension pulls binder into the pores between powder grains. The depth of penetration follows the **Washburn equation**:

$$
L^2 \;=\; \frac{r \, \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{2 \, \eta} \cdot t
$$

where:
- $L$ = depth of binder penetration (m)
- $r$ = effective capillary radius (m) (≈ powder particle radius)
- $\gamma_{LV}$ = liquid–vapor surface tension (N/m)
- $\theta$ = contact angle (°)
- $\eta$ = binder viscosity (Pa·s)
- $t$ = time (s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Washburn equation tells us:** Fine powders → small $r$ → slow binder penetration. Coarse powders → fast penetration but **rougher surfaces** and **lower green strength**.

2. **Chemical bonding / polymerization:** Once in contact, the binder reacts (often via crosslinking with the powder surface or internal polymerization) to form a **green part** with adequate handling strength.

### 2.2.4 Saturation — A Critical Concept

**Saturation $S$** is the ratio of the volume of binder deposited to the volume of voids in the powder:

$$
S \;=\; \frac{V_{binder}}{V_{voids}} \;=\; \frac{V_{binder}}{V_{bulk} \cdot \phi_{voids}}
$$

where $\phi_{voids}$ is the powder packing porosity (typically **0.35 – 0.55** for a randomly packed bed).

- $S < 1$ → **under-saturation** → weak, porous parts
- $S = 1$ → **full saturation** → optimal green strength
- $S > 1$ → **over-saturation** → bleeding, curling, dimensional inaccuracy

### 2.2.5 Volumetric Shrinkage During Sintering (Metal BJ)

Most metal binder jetting requires a **sintering** post-process to densify the part:

$$
\Delta V \;=\; V_0 \cdot (1 - \rho_{green} / \rho_{solid})
$$

$$
\Delta L \;=\; L_0 \cdot \left[1 - \left(\frac{\rho_{green}}{\rho_{solid}}\right)^{1/3}\right]
$$

where:
- $\rho_{green}$ = green part relative density (typically 50 – 65%)
- $\rho_{solid}$ = fully dense solid material (theoretical, e.g., 7.87 g/cm³ for steel)
- $\Delta L / L_0$ = linear shrinkage fraction (typically **15 – 20%** for metal BJ)

> [!WARNING]
> **Shrinkage compensation is critical!** The CAD model must be **uniformly scaled up** (by $1/\left(1-\Delta L/L_0\right)$) to achieve target final dimensions. KTU examiners love testing this scaling step.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Compilation)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_{layers} = h_{part} \,/\, t_{layer}$ | Number of layers for a given part height |
| 2 | $d_{spot} \approx \alpha \, d_{nozzle} (V_p / V_{piezo})^{1/3}$ | Droplet spot diameter on substrate (MJ) |
| 3 | $T_{build} = \dfrac{A_{bed}}{w_{head} \, v_{jet}} \cdot N_{layers} \cdot t_{rec}$ | Total build time (MJ) |
| 4 | $L^2 = \dfrac{r \, \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{2 \, \eta} \cdot t$ | Washburn penetration depth (BJ) |
| 5 | $S = V_{binder} \,/\, (V_{bulk} \cdot \phi_{voids})$ | Binder saturation ratio (BJ) |
| 6 | $\Delta L / L_0 = 1 - (\rho_{green}/\rho_{solid})^{1/3}$ | Linear shrinkage post-sintering (BJ metal) |
| 7 | $V_{droplet} = (4/3) \pi r_{drop}^3$ | Single droplet volume |
| 8 | $\rho_{bulk} = \rho_{solid} \cdot (1 - \phi_{voids})$ | Packed bulk powder density |
| 9 | $V_{binder\,req} = A_{layer} \cdot t_{layer} \cdot \phi_{voids} \cdot S$ | Required binder volume per layer (BJ) |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_{binder} = \rho_{binder} \cdot V_{droplet} \cdot f_{drop}$ | Mass flow rate of binder (kg/s) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Material Jetting is widely used in **jewellery prototyping** (wax patterns for investment casting), **dental** (crowns, aligners), and **medical models**. Binder Jetting is dominant in **large sand casting moulds** (foundries), **low-volume metal parts** (ExOne, Desktop Metal), and **architectural models with full colour**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Build Time for a Material Jetting System

**Problem Statement:**
A PolyJet system has the following specifications:
- Build platform area: $A_{bed} = 250 \text{ mm} \times 250 \text{ mm} = 62{,}500 \text{ mm}^2$
- Part height: $h_{part} = 50 \text{ mm}$
- Layer thickness: $t_{layer} = 0.016 \text{ mm} \; (16 \,\mu m)$
- Print head traverse speed: $v_{jet} = 600 \text{ mm/s}$
- Print head width: $w_{head} = 100 \text{ mm}$ (effective jetting strip width)
- Recoating + UV cure time per layer: $t_{rec} = 3 \text{ s}$

**Find:** Total build time $T_{build}$.

### Step 1 — Compute the number of layers

$$
N_{layers} \;=\; \frac{h_{part}}{t_{layer}}
$$

$$
N_{layers} \;=\; \frac{50 \text{ mm}}{0.016 \text{ mm/layer}}
$$

$$
N_{layers} \;=\; 3125 \text{ layers}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the time to jet one full platform (one pass per layer)

The print head must traverse the platform width. With effective head width $w_{head}$:

$$
N_{passes} \;=\; \frac{W_{bed}}{w_{head}} \;=\; \frac{250 \text{ mm}}{100 \text{ mm}} \;=\; 2.5 \;\text{(round up to 3 passes)}
$$

$$
t_{jet,\,per\,layer} \;=\; N_{passes} \cdot \frac{L_{bed}}{v_{jet}}
$$

where $L_{bed} = 250$ mm is the traverse length.

$$
t_{jet,\,per\,layer} \;=\; 3 \cdot \frac{250 \text{ mm}}{600 \text{ mm/s}}
$$

$$
t_{jet,\,per\,layer} \;=\; 3 \cdot 0.4167 \text{ s} \;=\; 1.25 \text{ s/layer}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the time per layer (jetting + recoating/curing)

$$
t_{layer,\,total} \;=\; t_{jet,\,per\,layer} \;+\; t_{rec}
$$

$$
t_{layer,\,total} \;=\; 1.25 \text{ s} \;+\; 3.0 \text{ s} \;=\; 4.25 \text{ s/layer}
$$

### Step 4 — Total build time

$$
T_{build} \;=\; N_{layers} \cdot t_{layer,\,total}
$$

$$
T_{build} \;=\; 3125 \times 4.25 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{build} \;=\; 13{,}281.25 \text{ s} \;\approx\; 3.69 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** A 50 mm tall part at 16 µm layers should take a few hours — our answer (~3.7 h) is realistic for PolyJet.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Shrinkage-Compensated Scaling Factor (Metal Binder Jetting)

**Problem Statement:**
A stainless-steel (316L) part is to be produced via metal Binder Jetting. After binder jetting, the green part is sintered to full density.
- Green part density: $\rho_{green} = 60\% \text{ of theoretical} = 0.60 \times 7.87 = 4.722 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- Theoretical density of 316L: $\rho_{solid} = 7.87 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- Target final length of the part: $L_{final} = 100 \text{ mm}$
- Target final diameter: $D_{final} = 25 \text{ mm}$

**Find:** The dimensions of the CAD model (pre-compensation) that must be designed.

### Step 1 — Compute the linear shrinkage fraction

$$
\frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \;=\; 1 - \left(\frac{\rho_{green}}{\rho_{solid}}\right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \;=\; 1 - (0.60)^{1/3}
$$

$$
(0.60)^{1/3} \;=\; 0.8434
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \;=\; 1 - 0.8434 \;=\; 0.1566
$$

Thus the linear shrinkage is **15.66 %**.

### Step 2 — Compute the scaling factor

Since the CAD length $L_{CAD}$ shrinks by 15.66 % to give $L_{final}$:

$$
L_{final} \;=\; L_{CAD} \cdot (1 - 0.1566) \;=\; L_{CAD} \cdot 0.8434
$$

Rearranging:

$$
L_{CAD} \;=\; \frac{L_{final}}{0.8434}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply to length

$$
L_{CAD} \;=\; \frac{100 \text{ mm}}{0.8434} \;=\; 118.57 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply to diameter

$$
D_{CAD} \;=\; \frac{25 \text{ mm}}{0.8434} \;=\; 29.64 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5 — Verify (reverse direction)

After sintering:

$$
L_{final,\,check} \;=\; 118.57 \times 0.8434 \;=\; 100.00 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Insight:** Isotropic (uniform) scaling assumes **uniform green density** throughout the part. In practice, BJ parts have **non-uniform density** near surfaces → **anisotropic shrinkage** → distortion. Hence, real production uses FEA compensation in software like **Magics / Netfabb**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Required Binder Volume Per Layer (BJ)

**Problem Statement:**
A binder jetting system prints a layer with:
- Layer area (cross-section of part): $A_{layer} = 1500 \text{ mm}^2$
- Layer thickness: $t_{layer} = 0.08 \text{ mm}$
- Powder packing porosity: $\phi_{voids} = 0.45$
- Target saturation: $S = 0.9$

**Find:** Volume of binder required for this layer.

### Step 1 — Compute volume of powder bed per layer

$$
V_{bulk} \;=\; A_{layer} \cdot t_{layer}
$$

$$
V_{bulk} \;=\; 1500 \text{ mm}^2 \times 0.08 \text{ mm} \;=\; 120 \text{ mm}^3
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the void volume available for binder

$$
V_{voids} \;=\; V_{bulk} \cdot \phi_{voids}
$$

$$
V_{voids} \;=\; 120 \times 0.45 \;=\; 54 \text{ mm}^3
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the binder volume required

$$
V_{binder} \;=\; S \cdot V_{voids}
$$

$$
V_{binder} \;=\; 0.9 \times 54 \;=\; 48.6 \text{ mm}^3
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to mass (assuming binder density $\rho_{binder} = 1.05 \text{ g/cm}^3$)

$$
m_{binder} \;=\; \rho_{binder} \cdot V_{binder}
$$

$$
m_{binder} \;=\; 1.05 \times 48.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g} \;=\; 0.051 \text{ g} \;=\; 51 \text{ mg}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Even though only **48.6 mm³** of binder is needed per layer, the print head deposits **discrete droplets**, so the number of droplets is $N_{drops} = V_{binder} / V_{droplet}$. For a 30 pL droplet: $N_{drops} = 48.6 \times 10^3 / 30 = 1620$ droplets per layer for this 1500 mm² cross-section.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection Logic

| Requirement | Choose MJ if... | Choose BJ if... |
|---|---|---|
| Need ultra-smooth surface | ✔ (Ra < 2 µm) | ✘ (Ra 4 – 10 µm) |
| Need multi-material part | ✔ (digital materials) | ✘ (single material only) |
| Need full-colour part | ✔ | ✔ (with colour binder) |
| Part is large (> 500 mm) | ✘ (limited bed) | ✔ (beds up to 2200 × 1200 mm) |
| Need metal part | ✘ (rare, indirect via casting) | ✔ (direct metal BJ) |
| Tolerances < 0.1 mm | ✔ | ✘ |
| Cost per part low | ✘ (resin expensive) | ✔ (powder cheap) |
| Fastest build | ✘ | ✔ (no heat, only inkjet) |
| High green/green strength needed | N/A | ✔ (sinnerable) |
| Functional rubber-like part | ✔ (TangoPlus) | ✘ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP PEPET752]**
**Differentiate between Material Jetting and Binder Jetting based on (a) the jetted medium, (b) the role of powder, and (c) post-processing requirements. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Material Jetting | Binder Jetting |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Jetted medium | The **build material itself** — liquid photopolymer, wax | The **adhesive binder** — polymeric solution that glues powder |
| (b) Role of powder | **No powder used** — only liquid resin | Powder is the **primary build material**; powder bed acts as support |
| (c) Post-processing | Minimal — only **support material removal** (water jet for soluble supports) | Extensive — **depowdering + curing + infiltration or sintering** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuator tip:** A clean three-row table earns full marks. A bullet-list style with all three points also acceptable. Never confuse "jetted material" with "binder" — this is the most common error.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP PEPET752]**
**What is the role of the photoinitiator in a material jetting photopolymer resin? Explain in 3 lines. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A **photoinitiator** (e.g., TPO, BAPO, Irgacure 819) is a molecule that **absorbs UV photons** (typically at **365–405 nm**) and **fragments into free radicals**. These free radicals initiate **chain-growth polymerization** of acrylate monomers, converting the liquid droplet into a solid polymer within milliseconds. This enables **localized, rapid curing** of each jetted droplet without heating the entire build chamber.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model QP PEPET752]**

**Part (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of the **Material Jetting (PolyJet)** process. List **four advantages** and **three limitations** of MJ. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b)** A PolyJet system jets a droplet of diameter **32 µm** at a piezo voltage of **80 V**. The characteristic piezo voltage is 60 V. If the spreading coefficient is **α = 2.0**, calculate the spot diameter on the substrate. If the layer thickness is **16 µm** and the part height is **40 mm**, compute the **total number of layers**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.3 of these notes)**

**Working Principle:**
The PolyJet process operates as follows:
1. A **liquid photopolymer resin** (UV-curable) is fed from cartridges to a **piezoelectric DOD print head** containing hundreds of nozzles.
2. The CAD model is sliced into thin horizontal layers (typically **16 µm** thick).
3. For each layer, the print head traverses the build platform, **ejecting picolitre droplets** of resin at locations defined by the slice.
4. A **UV lamp** (mounted alongside the head) **cures each droplet within milliseconds** via free-radical photopolymerization.
5. The **build platform descends by one layer thickness**, and the cycle repeats.
6. For overhangs and complex geometries, a **wax-like support material** is jetted from a separate nozzle and later dissolved in a water jet.
7. After build, the part is removed, supports are dissolved, and the part is **post-cured under UV** for full strength.

**Advantages (any four):**
1. **Highest accuracy** (±0.05 mm) and **excellent surface finish** (Ra ~ 1 µm) among AM processes.
2. **Multi-material capability** — multiple cartridges can be mixed to create **digital materials** with graded properties (e.g., rigid + rubber blend).
3. **Full-colour parts** via CMYK-style inkjet mixing.
4. **No thermal stress** — UV curing is localized and fast.
5. Low material waste (unused resin can be recycled).

**Limitations (any three):**
1. **Limited material range** — primarily photopolymers (not engineering thermoplastics or metals).
2. **Photopolymers are UV-sensitive** — degrade under sunlight over time.
3. **Slow for large volumes** — droplet-by-droplet deposition.
4. **Support material** is always required for overhangs > ~30°.
5. **Cost** — resin and machines are expensive.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:** $d_{nozzle} = 32 \, \mu m$, $V_p = 80 \, V$, $V_{piezo} = 60 \, V$, $\alpha = 2.0$, $t_{layer} = 16 \, \mu m$, $h_{part} = 40 \, mm$.

**Step 1 — Compute the spot diameter:**

$$
d_{spot} \;\approx\; \alpha \cdot d_{nozzle} \cdot \left(\frac{V_p}{V_{piezo}}\right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
\frac{V_p}{V_{piezo}} \;=\; \frac{80}{60} \;=\; 1.333
$$

$$
(1.333)^{1/3} \;\approx\; 1.1006
$$

$$
d_{spot} \;\approx\; 2.0 \times 32 \times 1.1006
$$

$$
d_{spot} \;\approx\; 70.44 \; \mu m
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of layers:**

$$
N_{layers} \;=\; \frac{h_{part}}{t_{layer}}
$$

$$
N_{layers} \;=\; \frac{40 \, \text{mm}}{0.016 \, \text{mm/layer}}
$$

$$
N_{layers} \;=\; 2500 \; \text{layers}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Spot diameter ≈ **70.44 µm**
- Total number of layers = **2500 layers**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP PEPET752]**

**Part (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of the **Binder Jetting** process. Explain the **Washburn equation** and its significance in determining binder penetration depth. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b)** A binder jetting system uses a stainless-steel powder (316L) with a powder bed porosity of **0.50**. The layer thickness is **100 µm**, and the part cross-section at one layer is **20 mm × 20 mm = 400 mm²**. Calculate:
(i) The **volume of binder** required per layer if saturation is **0.95**.
(ii) The **CAD-side scaling factor** if the green part density is 55% of theoretical after printing, and the part will be sintered to full density. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.4)**

**Working Principle:**
1. The **CAD model** is sliced into layers (typically **80–200 µm**).
2. A **powder feed piston** rises by one layer thickness, and a **counter-rotating roller** spreads a uniform layer of powder across the **build chamber**.
3. A **piezoelectric DOD print head** deposits **binder droplets** selectively on the cross-section of the part.
4. The binder penetrates the powder via **capillary action (Washburn equation)** and chemically bonds adjacent particles to form a **green part layer**.
5. The build piston descends by one layer thickness, and the cycle repeats.
6. After the build, the part is **excavated**, **depowdered**, and **post-processed** (curing and/or sintering).

**Washburn Equation & Significance:**

$$
L^2 \;=\; \frac{r \, \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{2 \, \eta} \cdot t
$$

**Where:**
- $L$ = depth of binder penetration
- $r$ = effective capillary radius (≈ powder particle radius)
- $\gamma_{LV}$ = binder–vapor surface tension
- $\theta$ = contact angle between binder and powder
- $\eta$ = binder viscosity
- $t$ = time

**Significance:**
1. It tells us that **finer powders** (smaller $r$) lead to **slower binder penetration**, requiring **longer curing times** or lower-viscosity binders.
2. It shows that **higher viscosity binders** penetrate slower.
3. It allows the operator to **optimize droplet spacing** so binder from adjacent droplets merges uniformly before curing.
4. If $L$ is too small relative to layer thickness, the part will be **weakly bonded across layers** → delamination.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:**
- Porosity $\phi_{voids} = 0.50$
- Layer thickness $t_{layer} = 100 \, \mu m = 0.1 \, mm$
- Layer area $A_{layer} = 20 \times 20 = 400 \, mm^2$
- Saturation $S = 0.95$
- Green density ratio = 55% (i.e., $\rho_{green}/\rho_{solid} = 0.55$)

**Step (i) — Volume of binder per layer:**

$$
V_{bulk} \;=\; A_{layer} \cdot t_{layer} \;=\; 400 \times 0.1 \;=\; 40 \; mm^3
$$

$$
V_{voids} \;=\; V_{bulk} \cdot \phi_{voids} \;=\; 40 \times 0.50 \;=\; 20 \; mm^3
$$

$$
V_{binder} \;=\; S \cdot V_{voids} \;=\; 0.95 \times 20
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{binder} \;=\; 19.0 \; mm^3 \; \text{per layer}}
$$

**Step (ii) — Scaling factor:**

$$
\text{Scaling factor} \;=\; \frac{1}{(1 - \Delta L/L_0)} \;=\; \frac{1}{(\rho_{green}/\rho_{solid})^{1/3}}
$$

$$
\text{Scaling factor} \;=\; \frac{1}{(0.55)^{1/3}}
$$

$$
(0.55)^{1/3} \;\approx\; 0.8193
$$

$$
\text{Scaling factor} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.8193}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Scaling factor} \;\approx\; 1.2206}
$$

This means the CAD model must be **uniformly enlarged by 22.06%** before printing so that, after sintering shrinkage, the final part matches the intended dimensions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing MJ and BJ:** Writing "in MJ, powder is jetted" — *wrong*. Powder is **spread** in BJ, not jetted.
> 2. **Missing units:** Always carry units in numerical problems. A final answer "100" without "mm" loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the cube root:** The shrinkage formula uses $(ρ_{green}/ρ_{solid})^{1/3}$, **not** $1/3$ directly. Cubing/mixing is a frequent error.
> 4. **Neglecting the spreading coefficient $\alpha$:** Some students compute spot diameter without $\alpha$, losing 1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing up saturation:** Using $S = 0.95$ as a *volume ratio* instead of *void-volume ratio* will give an answer off by a factor of $\phi_{voids}$.
> 6. **Writing "binder cures the powder":** The binder does **not** cure in the UV sense — it **dries** and/or **chemically bonds** the powder by adhesion/setting.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Summary — Memorize Before Exam:**

- [ ] **Material Jetting (MJ)** = jets the **build material** (photopolymer); cured by **UV light**; **no powder**.
- [ ] **Binder Jetting (BJ)** = jets only the **adhesive binder**; **powder is the build material**; **room-temperature build**.
- [ ] MJ uses **Drop-on-Demand (DOD) piezoelectric** inkjet heads; droplets are **6–80 pL**; layer thickness = **16 µm**.
- [ ] BJ layer thickness = **80–200 µm**; uses **aqueous polymeric binder** (e.g., 2-pyrrolidone).
- [ ] **Photoinitiator** absorbs UV → generates free radicals → initiates polymerization of acrylates.
- [ ] **Saturation $S$** = volume binder / volume voids; optimal $S \approx 0.9–1.0$.
- [ ] **Washburn equation** governs binder penetration in BJ: $L^2 = \frac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{2\eta} \cdot t$.
- [ ] **Shrinkage compensation** in metal BJ: CAD scaled by factor $1 / (\rho_{green}/\rho_{solid})^{1/3}$; typical linear shrinkage **15–20%**.
- [ ] **MJ advantages:** multi-material, full-colour, ultra-smooth, ±0.05 mm accuracy.
- [ ] **BJ advantages:** large parts (2200×1200 mm bed), no supports needed (powder self-supports), fast, low cost.
- [ ] **BJ limitations:** porous green parts need sintering/infiltration; lower accuracy (±0.2 mm).
- [ ] **MJ limitations:** UV-sensitive photopolymers; limited material palette; supports required.
- [ ] Key formulas (master these):
  - $N_{layers} = h_{part}/t_{layer}$
  - $d_{spot} = \alpha \cdot d_{nozzle} \cdot (V_p/V_{piezo})^{1/3}$
  - $L^2 = \dfrac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{2\eta} t$
  - $\Delta L/L_0 = 1 - (\rho_{green}/\rho_{solid})^{1/3}$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Capabilities and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Process Capabilities and Applications** in Additive Manufacturing (AM) refers to the systematic evaluation and characterization of the operational envelope, performance metrics, material compatibility, geometric complexity tolerance, and industrial use-cases of each of the seven standardized AM process categories defined by **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021**. This includes dimensional accuracy, surface roughness, build volume, layer thickness range, support requirements, and post-processing needs that determine the suitability of an AM process for a given engineering application.

The seven standardized AM process categories are:
1. **Vat Photopolymerization (VPP)** – Stereolithography (SLA), Digital Light Processing (DLP)
2. **Material Extrusion (MEX)** – Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM/FFF)
3. **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** – Selective Laser Sintering (SLS), Selective Laser Melting (SLM), Electron Beam Melting (EBM)
4. **Material Jetting (MJT)** – PolyJet, Multi-Jet Modeling (MJM)
5. **Binder Jetting (BJT)** – Powder bed + liquid binder
6. **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** – Laser Metal Deposition (LMD), Wire-Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM)
7. **Sheet Lamination (SHL)** – Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM), Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing (UAM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 2, students must focus on the comparative analysis of process capabilities (accuracy, build size, material range) and map specific industries (aerospace, biomedical, automotive, tooling) to appropriate AM technologies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "3D Printing Toolkit"**
>
> Think of the seven AM process categories as **seven different tools in a carpenter's toolbox**. Just as a carpenter chooses a hammer for nails, a saw for wood, and a chisel for fine detail, an engineer must choose the right AM process for the right job:
>
> - **FDM** is like a **hot glue gun** — affordable and easy, but rough around the edges (visible layer lines).
> - **SLA/DLP** is like a **photographic darkroom** — uses light to harden liquid resin with high detail.
> - **SLS/SLM** is like a **laser welding iron** — fuses powder particles using intense heat.
> - **DED** is like a **3D welding torch** — deposits material exactly where needed, often for repairs.
> - **Binder Jetting** is like a **2D inkjet printer on steroids** — sprays glue layer-by-layer.
> - **Material Jetting** is like an **inkjet printer for photopolymers** — highest surface finish.
> - **Sheet Lamination** is like **gluing paper sheets together** then cutting the shape.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

The standard benchmark metrics for process capability evaluation in AM are:
- **Dimensional Accuracy** (typically ±0.1 mm for industrial systems, ±0.025 mm for high-end systems)
- **Surface Roughness (Ra)** in **µm**
- **Build Volume** in **mm³** or **cm³**
- **Layer Thickness** range: **10 µm to 500 µm**
- **Minimum Feature Size** in **mm**
- **Material Density** (relative to wrought, expressed in %)
- **Stiffness/Anisotropy Index** (ratio of Z-axis to XY-axis mechanical properties)
- **Standard Reference Temperature**: **20°C** (testing condition per ISO/ASTM)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Capability Comparison Framework

Each AM process is evaluated against five capability dimensions:

### A. Material Capability
The range of processable materials including polymers, metals, ceramics, composites, and biomaterials.

### B. Geometric Capability
- Minimum wall thickness
- Minimum hole diameter
- Overhang angle (typically requires support if > **45°** from vertical)
- Enclosed/internal features accessibility

### C. Accuracy Capability
- **Volumetric Accuracy** = (Measured Dimension − Nominal Dimension) / Nominal Dimension × 100%
- Expressed as a percentage tolerance

### D. Surface Capability
- **Ra (Arithmetic Mean Roughness)** measured in **µm**
- Affected by layer thickness, stair-stepping effect, and post-processing

### E. Throughput Capability
- **Build Rate** = Build Volume / Build Time (cm³/hour)
- **Deposition Rate** for DED processes = Nozzle feed rate × Cross-sectional area

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Formula Reference Table**

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Typical Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $Ra = \frac{1}{L} \int_{0}^{L} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$ | Arithmetic mean surface roughness | µm |
| $V_{build} = L \times W \times H$ | Build envelope volume | mm³ or cm³ |
| $R_{dep} = v_{feed} \times A_{cross}$ | Deposition rate (DED) | mm³/s |
| $\Delta h = h_{layer}$ | Layer thickness resolution | µm |
| $A_{acc} = \frac{\vert D_{m} - D_{n} \vert}{D_{n}} \times 100$ | Dimensional accuracy (% deviation) | % |
| $t_{build} = \frac{V_{build}}{R_{build}}$ | Total build time | hours |
| $E_{v} = \frac{P_{laser}}{v_{scan} \times h_{layer} \times t_{layer}}$ | Volumetric Energy Density (VED) | J/mm³ |
| $\rho_{rel} = \frac{\rho_{part}}{\rho_{wrought}} \times 100$ | Relative density (part vs wrought) | % |
| $AI = \frac{\sigma_{Z}}{\sigma_{XY}}$ | Anisotropy Index (Z vs XY strength) | dimensionless |
| $\text{Stair-step error} = h \cdot \tan(\theta)$ | Staircase effect error on angled surface | mm |

Where:
- $D_{m}$ = Measured dimension, $D_{n}$ = Nominal dimension
- $P_{laser}$ = Laser power (W)
- $v_{scan}$ = Scan speed (mm/s)
- $h_{layer}$ = Layer thickness (mm)
- $t_{layer}$ = Hatch spacing (mm)
- $\theta$ = Surface angle from vertical

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Process capability mapping is the foundation of **Design for Additive Manufacturing (DfAM)**. Engineers use capability charts to:
1. **Select processes** for functional prototypes, end-use parts, or tooling
2. **Predict part performance** (anisotropy, fatigue life) before fabrication
3. **Optimize cost** by matching tolerance requirements to process capability
4. **Certify parts** for regulated industries (AS9100 for aerospace, ISO 13485 for medical)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Energy Density (VED) — Critical for PBF & DED

The **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** is the most important process parameter in powder-based AM processes. It quantifies the energy delivered per unit volume of material and directly determines part density, porosity, and mechanical strength.

### Step 1: Define the Melt Pool Volume
The melt pool is approximated as a rectangular volume defined by the layer thickness $h$, hatch spacing $t$, and the effective interaction length $L$.

$$
V_{melt} = h \times t \times L
$$

### Step 2: Define the Effective Interaction Length
The interaction length is the ratio of the scan speed $v$ (in mm/s) to the effective interaction time (approximately inverse of the laser exposure frequency for a single pulse train). For a continuous wave laser in single-track scanning:

$$
L = \frac{d_{spot}}{v_{scan}}
$$

Where $d_{spot}$ is the laser spot diameter (in mm).

### Step 3: Energy Delivered per Track
The energy delivered by the laser over the interaction length is:

$$
E_{track} = P_{laser} \times t_{interaction} = P_{laser} \times \frac{d_{spot}}{v_{scan}}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute into VED Definition
By definition, $VED = E_{track} / V_{melt}$. Substituting the expressions:

$$
VED = \frac{P_{laser} \times \frac{d_{spot}}{v_{scan}}}{h \times t \times \frac{d_{spot}}{v_{scan}}}
$$

### Step 5: Simplification — $d_{spot}$ and $v_{scan}$ Cancel

$$
\boxed{VED = \frac{P_{laser}}{v_{scan} \times h \times t}}
$$

> **Textual Explanation:** The spot diameter and scan velocity cancel out because they affect both the numerator (energy delivered) and denominator (melt volume) proportionally. The final VED depends only on laser power, scan speed, layer thickness, and hatch spacing.

### Step 6: Numerical Example
**Problem:** Calculate VED for SLM of Ti-6Al-4V with $P_{laser} = 200$ W, $v_{scan} = 800$ mm/s, $h = 30$ µm $= 0.030$ mm, $t = 0.1$ mm.

$$
VED = \frac{200}{800 \times 0.030 \times 0.1}
$$

$$
VED = \frac{200}{2.4} = 83.33 \;\; \text{J/mm}^3
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** For Ti-6Al-4V, the optimal VED window is typically **60–120 J/mm³**. Below 60 J/mm³ results in lack-of-fusion porosity; above 120 J/mm³ causes keyholing porosity. Our value of 83.33 J/mm³ lies in the safe processing window.

## 3.2 Derivation: Stair-Stepping Surface Error

The stair-stepping effect is a fundamental geometric limitation of all layer-based AM processes. The maximum deviation from the intended angled surface is:

### Step 1: Geometric Setup
Consider an inclined surface making angle $\theta$ with the vertical (build) axis. The vertical rise between two successive layer points equals the layer thickness $h$.

### Step 2: Horizontal Run Calculation
The horizontal projection of the layer thickness along the inclined surface is:

$$
\Delta x = \frac{h}{\tan(\theta)}
$$

### Step 3: Normal Deviation from CAD Surface
The perpendicular distance from the ideal CAD surface to the apex of the stairstep is:

$$
E_{stair} = h \cdot \cos(\theta) \times \sin(\theta) \cdot \frac{1}{\cos(\theta)} = h \cdot \sin(\theta)
$$

Wait, applying the standard stair-step error formula derived from the **inscribed circle** of the step triangle:

$$
\boxed{E_{stair} = h \cdot \tan(\theta)}
$$

> **Textual Explanation:** This is the maximum normal-direction error between the actual stair-stepped surface and the ideal smooth inclined surface. The error is zero at $\theta = 0°$ (vertical walls) and approaches $h$ asymptotically as $\theta \to 90°$ (horizontal surfaces).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (7 AM Processes)

| **Process** | **Typical Accuracy** | **Ra (µm)** | **Min. Layer (µm)** | **Materials** | **Build Volume Range** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SLA** | ±0.1 mm | 0.5 – 5 | 25 | Photopolymers | Small–Medium |
| **DLP** | ±0.05 mm | 0.5 – 3 | 25 – 50 | Photopolymers | Small–Medium |
| **FDM** | ±0.2 mm | 5 – 20 | 100 | Thermoplastics | Small–Large |
| **SLS** | ±0.2 mm | 6 – 15 | 80 | Polymers (PA12, PA11) | Medium–Large |
| **SLM** | ±0.1 mm | 5 – 20 | 20 | Metals (Ti, Al, SS) | Small–Medium |
| **EBM** | ±0.2 mm | 10 – 25 | 50 | Metals (Ti, Co-Cr) | Small–Medium |
| **DED** | ±0.5 mm | 15 – 50 | 250 | Metals (wide range) | Large–Very Large |
| **MJT** | ±0.02 mm | 0.5 – 2 | 14 | Photopolymers, wax | Small–Medium |
| **BJT** | ±0.2 mm | 5 – 10 | 80 | Metals, sand, ceramics | Medium–Large |
| **LOM** | ±0.2 mm | 10 – 30 | 100 | Paper, polymer, metal foil | Medium–Large |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**List any three AM processes and write their typical achievable dimensional accuracies.**

**Model Answer:**
The three AM processes with their typical dimensional accuracies are:
1. **Stereolithography (SLA):** ±0.1 mm (0.004 inch)
2. **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM):** ±0.2 mm (0.008 inch) for desktop, ±0.05 mm for industrial
3. **Selective Laser Melting (SLM):** ±0.1 mm to ±0.05 mm depending on build size and material

These accuracies are dependent on process parameters such as layer thickness, machine calibration, material properties, and post-processing treatments.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
**What is the difference between Material Jetting and Binder Jetting in terms of process mechanism and application?**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Material Jetting (MJT)** | **Binder Jetting (BJT)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Jetted Material** | Photopolymer resin droplets (build material) | Liquid binder droplets (adhesive) |
| **Process** | Inkjet-like printhead deposits curable resin, instantly UV-cured | Binder selectively joins loose powder particles |
| **Post-Processing** | UV post-cure required | Depowdering + infiltration/sintering required |
| **Typical Application** | High-fidelity visual prototypes, medical models, wax patterns for investment casting | Large metal parts, sand casting molds, full-color prototypes |

The fundamental distinction is that **MJT jets the actual build material**, while **BJT jets only a binder** to adhere powder.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2 Model QP]**
**(a)** Explain the process capabilities of any three AM processes in terms of dimensional accuracy, surface finish, and build volume. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Discuss in detail the aerospace and biomedical applications of additive manufacturing, citing specific processes and materials. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The process capabilities of three major AM processes are:

**1. Stereolithography (SLA) – Vat Photopolymerization**
- **Dimensional Accuracy:** ±0.1 mm (0.4% of nominal dimension typically). Achievable on small to medium parts.
- **Surface Finish:** Excellent, Ra = 0.5 to 5 µm. Smoothest among all AM processes due to UV resin curing and low layer thickness (25–100 µm).
- **Build Volume:** Industrial systems up to 1500 × 750 × 550 mm; desktop systems typically 130 × 130 × 130 mm.
- **Limitations:** Limited to photopolymers; requires support structures; UV post-cure required.

**2. Selective Laser Melting (SLM) – Powder Bed Fusion**
- **Dimensional Accuracy:** ±0.1 mm for small features, decreasing to ±0.2 mm for larger parts (>100 mm). Achievable for metals.
- **Surface Finish:** Ra = 5–20 µm as-built; can be improved to 1–5 µm via bead blasting or polishing.
- **Build Volume:** Typical industrial systems 250 × 250 × 300 mm; large systems up to 800 × 400 × 500 mm.
- **Limitations:** High residual stress; requires support; expensive powder; build size constraints due to laser power density.

**3. Directed Energy Deposition (DED) – DED**
- **Dimensional Accuracy:** ±0.5 mm to ±1 mm — lowest among metal AM processes. Multi-axis systems can achieve higher accuracy.
- **Surface Finish:** Coarse, Ra = 15–50 µm. Machining almost always required.
- **Build Volume:** Very large, 1000 × 1000 × 1000 mm or larger. WAAM systems can build multi-meter components.
- **Limitations:** Lower resolution, requires subsequent machining; excellent for repair and large near-net-shape parts.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Aerospace Applications:**

1. **Topology-Optimized Brackets and Structural Components:** Airbus A350 XWB and Boeing 787 use SLM-printed **Ti-6Al-4V brackets** achieving 30–55% weight reduction. The lattice and bionic structures are impossible with conventional manufacturing.

2. **Turbine Engine Components:** GE Aviation uses **DED (laser cladding)** to repair and manufacture **Inconel 718 turbine blades**. The LEAP fuel nozzle tip is an SLM-printed part consolidating 18 traditional parts into 1, with 25% weight saving.

3. **Rocket Engine Injectors:** SpaceX and Rocket Lab use **DED and SLM** for **Inconel and copper-alloy thrust chamber injectors** with internal cooling channels that cannot be machined.

4. **Bionic Air Ducts:** SLM-printed **complex internal ducting** with conformal flow paths in aircraft environmental control systems.

**Biomedical Applications:**

1. **Patient-Specific Cranial Implants:** **SLM in Ti-6Al-4V** is used to fabricate custom cranial plates from CT scan data, achieving perfect anatomical fit.

2. **Dental Crowns and Bridges:** **DLP/SLA using biocompatible photopolymer resins** produces dental patterns; final crowns are cast in zirconia or Co-Cr. **Binder Jetting in Co-Cr** directly produces metal dental copings.

3. **Orthopedic Implants:** **EBM in Ti-6Al-4V** produces acetabular cups, spinal cages, and porous bone-interface scaffolds with controlled porosity (porosity = 60–80%, pore size 400–700 µm) facilitating osseointegration.

4. **Surgical Guides and Anatomical Models:** **SLA or MJT (Multi-Jet)** produces patient-specific surgical cutting guides and pre-operative planning models from MRI/CT data, improving surgical accuracy by 30–40%.

5. **Hearing Aids:** **SLM in Ti** and **DLP resin** produce custom in-canal hearing aid shells — one of the highest-volume custom AM applications globally (>10 million units annually).

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Model QP]**
**(a)** With a neat process selection flowchart, explain how an engineer would select an appropriate AM process for a given functional metal part. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Compare Vat Photopolymerization and Material Extrusion processes in terms of principle, materials, accuracy, applications, and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**AM Process Selection Methodology for Functional Metal Parts:**

The selection follows a **hierarchical decision tree**:

**Step 1 — Define Functional Requirements**
- Mechanical load (tensile, fatigue, impact)
- Operating environment (temperature, corrosion)
- Dimensional tolerance and surface finish required
- Part size (small <250 mm, medium 250–500 mm, large >500 mm)

**Step 2 — Material Selection**
Match functional requirements to material families: Titanium alloys (aerospace, biomedical), Aluminum alloys (lightweight), Stainless steels (corrosion resistance), Nickel superalloys (high temperature), Tool steels (tooling).

**Step 3 — Size-Based Process Selection**
- **Small to Medium (<500 mm):** SLM or EBM
- **Large to Very Large (>500 mm):** DED (LMD, WAAM, EBF³)

**Step 4 — Resolution vs Throughput Trade-off**
- **High resolution + small feature:** SLM preferred
- **High build rate + coarser tolerance:** DED preferred
- **No support needed for overhangs:** Binder Jetting alternative

**Step 5 — Cost & Post-Processing**
- **Low volume, high value:** SLM
- **High volume, larger part:** Binder Jetting + sintering
- **Repair/additive on existing part:** DED

**Step 6 — Certification & Inspection**
- CT scanning for internal defects
- HIP (Hot Isostatic Pressing) for critical aerospace parts
- Mechanical testing per ASTM F3122 (Ti), F3187 (Al), etc.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Comparison: VPP (Vat Photopolymerization) vs MEX (Material Extrusion)**

| **Parameter** | **VPP (SLA/DLP)** | **MEX (FDM/FFF)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Working Principle** | Selective curing of liquid photopolymer resin by UV light (laser in SLA, projector in DLP) | Selective extrusion of thermoplastic filament through heated nozzle |
| **Material State** | Liquid photopolymer (epoxy or acrylate resin) | Solid thermoplastic filament (ABS, PLA, PETG, Nylon, PEEK) |
| **Layer Thickness** | 25 – 100 µm | 100 – 300 µm (sometimes 50 µm) |
| **Dimensional Accuracy** | ±0.05 to ±0.1 mm | ±0.1 to ±0.3 mm |
| **Surface Finish (Ra)** | 0.5 – 5 µm | 5 – 20 µm |
| **Mechanical Properties** | Brittle, low elongation, UV-degradable | Moderate, anisotropic, depends on polymer |
| **Build Speed** | Slow (point-by-point in SLA) to fast (full layer in DLP) | Moderate, geometry dependent |
| **Support Structures** | Required for overhangs >45° and islands | Always required for overhangs and bridges |
| **Typical Applications** | Investment casting patterns, dental models, jewelry, optical prototypes, hearing aids | Concept models, functional prototypes, jigs/fixtures, low-cost end-use parts |
| **Post-Processing** | UV post-cure, support removal, sanding | Support removal, sanding, painting, vapor smoothing |
| **Material Cost** | High (resin $50–300/L) | Low (filament $20–100/kg) |
| **Equipment Cost** | $3,000 – $500,000+ | $200 – $300,000+ |
| **Limitations** | Limited material range, photopolymer degradation, resin handling hazards | Visible layer lines, anisotropy, limited high-temperature polymers in low-end systems |
| **Office Safety** | Skin/resin exposure, uncured resin toxicity | Generally safe, minor fume extraction needed |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write generic "AM is used in aerospace" without naming **specific companies, parts, materials, and processes** (e.g., "GE LEAP fuel nozzle — SLM — Inconel 718 / Mar-M 509"). Loss: **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **Material Jetting (MJT)** with **Binder Jetting (BJT)**. Remember: MJT jets the *build material*; BJT jets only the *adhesive*. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** In VED calculations, forgetting to **convert µm to mm** for layer thickness. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Stating surface roughness Ra = 0 without mentioning post-processing. As-built roughness is always higher than machined roughness. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Writing applications without linking to a **specific process**. Always pair "application → process → material" in your answers.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- ✅ **Seven AM Process Categories** (ISO/ASTM 52900): VPP, MEX, PBF, MJT, BJT, DED, SHL — must memorize all seven in order.
- ✅ **Highest Resolution Process:** Material Jetting (PolyJet) — 14 µm layer thickness, Ra ≈ 0.5 µm.
- ✅ **Largest Build Volume Process:** DED / WAAM — can produce multi-meter metal parts.
- ✅ **VED Formula:** $VED = P / (v \times h \times t)$ — units J/mm³; typical safe window for Ti-6Al-4V is 60–120 J/mm³.
- ✅ **Stair-Step Error:** $E = h \cdot \tan(\theta)$ — increases with layer thickness and inclined surface angle.
- ✅ **MJT vs BJT:** MJT jets photopolymer (build material); BJT jets adhesive onto powder.
- ✅ **Aerospace Materials:** Ti-6Al-4V (SLM), Inconel 718 (SLM/DED), AlSi10Mg (SLM), Co-Cr (EBM).
- ✅ **Biomedical Materials:** Ti-6Al-4V (orthopedic), Co-Cr (dental), PEEK (cranial), Biocompatible resins (surgical guides).
- ✅ **Support Requirement Threshold:** Overhangs > 45° from vertical generally need support in FDM, SLA, SLM.
- ✅ **Anisotropy Index:** $AI = \sigma_Z / \sigma_{XY}$ — values < 0.8 indicate strong Z-direction weakness, typical for FDM.
- ✅ **Post-Processing Routes:** Support removal → Bead blasting → HIP → CNC machining → Surface treatment (anodizing, painting).
- ✅ **Standard Layer Thickness Range:** 10 µm (high-end MJT/SLA) to 500 µm (LOM/DED).
- ✅ **Industrial vs Desktop:** Industrial AM = ±0.05 mm; Desktop AM = ±0.2 mm typical.
- ✅ **AS9100 / ISO 13485:** Aerospace and medical quality standards, respectively, governing AM part certification.
- ✅ **Cost Drivers:** Material (~30%), Machine amortization (~30%), Labor/Post-processing (~25%), Energy (~5%), Overhead (~10%).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extrusion Based AM Processes: Principles of Melt Extrusion and Deposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Extrusion Based AM Processes: Principles of Melt Extrusion and Deposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Extrusion (Melt Extrusion)** is an Additive Manufacturing (AM) process classified under **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021** as a *Material Extrusion* process family, in which a thermoplastic or wax-based filament, pellet, or granulate is selectively melted (or softened to a semi-liquid state) inside a heated liquefier and continuously extruded through a computer-controlled nozzle to deposit beads (roads) of material layer-by-layer onto a build platform, where each newly deposited bead fuses with the previously solidified layer to form a solid 3D part.

The most common commercial implementation is **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)**, originally patented by **Stratasys (S. Scott Crump, 1989)**, and the open-source equivalent called **Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a hot glue gun controlled by a robotic arm.** A glue gun melts solid glue sticks and pushes the molten glue out of its nozzle. If we mounted this glue gun on a CNC-style XY gantry that draws precise 2D patterns based on a digital file, and after finishing one pattern, the gun lifts up by a tiny amount (one layer thickness) and draws the next pattern on top of the previous one — that is **exactly Melt Extrusion-based AM in a nutshell**. Each "drawing" is one cross-sectional slice of the final 3D object.

### 1.3 Key Process Metrics and Standard Nomenclature

| Term | Definition | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Bead (Road / Raster)** | A single continuous line of extruded material | Width: **0.4 mm – 1.0 mm** |
| **Layer Thickness (z-resolution)** | Vertical height of each deposited bead | **0.05 mm – 0.30 mm** |
| **Nozzle Diameter (d)** | Internal diameter of the extrusion orifice | **0.25 mm – 1.20 mm** |
| **Liquefier Temperature (Tₗ)** | Temperature at which the filament melts | **180 °C – 260 °C** for PLA |
| **Build Envelope** | Maximum printable volume | **200 × 200 × 200 mm³** (typical desktop) |
| **Volumetric Throughput (Q)** | Volume of material extruded per second | **~2 – 12 mm³/s** |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** The acronym **"E.D.F.M."** — **E**xtrude the filament, **D**eposit on the platform, **F**use with prior layer, **M**ove to next position.
