<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Stage Process Chain of Melt Extrusion

The melt extrusion process is fundamentally divided into **four sequential thermodynamic and kinematic stages**. Understanding each stage is critical for KTU exam questions on process optimization.

#### Stage 1: Solid Feedstock Transport
- A solid thermoplastic filament (typically **1.75 mm** or **2.85 mm** diameter) is fed into the liquefier by a **drive system** consisting of an **idler pulley** and a **driven pulley** (geared or knurled), coupled to a **stepper motor**.
- The filament is gripped between two counter-rotating rollers.
- A **tangential feed force (F_f)** is generated that pushes the filament downward into the heated zone.

$$
F_f = \mu \cdot F_n
$$

where:
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction between roller and filament (typically **0.3 – 0.6** for knurled steel on PLA)
- $F_n$ = normal force applied by the bearing/lever mechanism

#### Stage 2: Phase Change (Solid → Viscous Melt)
- Inside the **liquefier** (a hollow aluminum or brass block with embedded cartridge heaters), the filament encounters a thermal gradient.
- The temperature must exceed the **glass transition temperature (T_g)** and approach the **melting temperature (T_m)** of the polymer.
- The polymer transitions from a **viscoelastic solid** to a **non-Newtonian viscous fluid**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **melt zone** is typically only **~10 – 15 mm** long. The polymer does not fully melt uniformly; instead, a *viscous flow* zone forms where the molten material flows around the still-solid core (similar to toothpaste being squeezed). This is called the **"melt-flow boundary"** and is critical for stable extrusion.

#### Stage 3: Pressure-Driven Flow Through the Nozzle
- The molten polymer is forced through a **conical nozzle** (typically with an entrance angle of **~60° – 120°** and length-to-diameter ratio of **L/D ≈ 2 – 6**).
- The flow is treated as **incompressible, laminar, non-Newtonian** (following the **power-law model** in most academic treatments).

The pressure drop across the nozzle is given by:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{2 L \cdot \tau_w}{R}
$$

where:
- $L$ = length of the nozzle capillary
- $R$ = nozzle radius
- $\tau_w$ = wall shear stress

For a **power-law fluid**, the wall shear stress is:

$$
\tau_w = K \cdot \left(\frac{3n+1}{4n}\right)^n \cdot \left(\frac{4Q}{\pi R^3}\right)^n
$$

where:
- $K$ = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ)
- $n$ = power-law index (typically **0.3 – 0.6** for polymer melts)
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)

#### Stage 4: Deposition, Bonding, and Solidification
- The extrudate exits the nozzle and is deposited on the substrate.
- The bead **spreads slightly** (a phenomenon called **die swell** and subsequent **road spreading**) before solidifying.
- **Inter-layer bonding** occurs through:
  1. **Wetting** of the previous layer by the molten bead
  2. **Polymer chain diffusion** across the interface (governed by the **reptation model** of de Gennes)
  3. **Solidification** by conductive cooling to the surrounding air and platform

### 2.2 Critical Process Parameters (KTU High-Yield List)

| Parameter | Symbol | Effect on Part Quality | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nozzle Temperature** | $T_n$ | ↑T → better bonding, ↓ strength | 190 °C – 250 °C (PLA) |
| **Platform Temperature** | $T_p$ | ↑T → ↓ warping, better adhesion | 50 °C – 110 °C |
| **Layer Thickness** | $t_l$ | ↓t → better Z-resolution, longer build | 0.05 – 0.30 mm |
| **Print Speed** | $v$ | ↑v → ↓ time, possible under-extrusion | 30 – 150 mm/s |
| **Bead Width** | $w_b$ | Set by nozzle/flow | 0.4 – 1.0 mm |
| **Raft/Brim Skirt** | – | Improves bed adhesion | – |
| **Infill Density** | $\phi$ | ↑φ → ↑ strength, ↑ material, ↑ time | 0% – 100% |
| **Infill Pattern** | – | Affects anisotropic strength | Line, Grid, Gyroid, Honeycomb |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \pi r^2 \cdot v_f$ | Volumetric extrusion rate (filament feed) | mm³/s |
| 2 | $Q = \pi r_n^2 \cdot v_e$ | Volumetric extrusion rate (out of nozzle) | mm³/s |
| 3 | $v_e = v_f \cdot \left(\dfrac{r_f}{r_n}\right)^2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{\rho_{ratio}}$ | Exit velocity (Die conservation) | mm/s |
| 4 | $t_{build} = \dfrac{V_{part}}{Q \cdot \eta_{ext}}$ | Build time estimate | s |
| 5 | $w_b = Q / (v_h \cdot t_l)$ | Bead width from hatch speed | mm |
| 6 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^3}$ | Apparent shear rate at nozzle wall | s⁻¹ |
| 7 | $\tau_w = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Newtonian shear stress (idealized) | Pa |
| 8 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\eta}$ | Reynolds number (typically < 1, confirming laminar flow) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Pe = v \cdot L / D_{poly}$ | Péclet number (advection vs. polymer diffusion) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\eta(T) = \eta_0 \exp\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$ | Arrhenius viscosity–temperature relation | Pa·s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark Loss Trap:** Many students write $Q = A \cdot v$ without specifying *which* area (filament cross-section vs. nozzle cross-section) and *which* velocity (feed velocity vs. extrudate velocity). In KTU valuation, if you do not define variables, you will lose **1 mark per undeclared symbol**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rapid Prototyping:** Concept models, ergonomic studies (delivery in 24–48 hours).
- **Jigs, Fixtures, and Tooling:** Low-volume manufacturing aids for assembly lines.
- **Biomedical:** Custom orthotics, prosthetics, scaffolds for tissue engineering (using **PCL, PLA, PEEK**).
- **Aerospace:** Lightweight topology-optimized brackets (e.g., Airbus A350 cabin parts).
- **Food & Construction:** Large-scale additive manufacturing using **pellet extrusion (e.g., Cincinnati Inc., COBOD BOD2)** for printing entire buildings.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mass/Volume Continuity Between Filament and Extrudate

The core relationship between the **filament feed rate** and the **extrudate deposition rate** comes from conservation of mass (assuming **incompressible melt** and **no leak flow**).

**Step 1 — State the principle of volumetric continuity:**

> The volume of material entering the liquefier per unit time equals the volume leaving the nozzle per unit time (in steady state).

$$
Q_{in} = Q_{out}
$$

**Step 2 — Express $Q_{in}$ using filament geometry:**

The filament is a cylinder of radius $r_f$ being pushed in at velocity $v_f$.

$$
Q_{in} = A_f \cdot v_f = \pi r_f^2 \cdot v_f
$$

**Step 3 — Express $Q_{out}$ using nozzle geometry:**

The melt exits the nozzle of radius $r_n$ at velocity $v_e$.

$$
Q_{out} = A_n \cdot v_e = \pi r_n^2 \cdot v_e
$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for exit velocity:**

$$
\pi r_f^2 \cdot v_f = \pi r_n^2 \cdot v_e
$$

$$
v_e = v_f \cdot \left(\frac{r_f}{r_n}\right)^2
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU-style problem):**

> *A 1.75 mm diameter PLA filament is fed at 5 mm/s into a 0.40 mm diameter nozzle. Find the extrudate exit velocity.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_f &= \frac{1.75}{2} = 0.875 \text{ mm} \\
r_n &= \frac{0.40}{2} = 0.20 \text{ mm} \\
v_e &= 5 \cdot \left(\frac{0.875}{0.20}\right)^2 \\
    &= 5 \cdot (4.375)^2 \\
    &= 5 \cdot 19.1406 \\
    &= 95.70 \text{ mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A modest 5 mm/s filament feed results in ~95.7 mm/s of extrudate velocity — a **19.14× amplification** that is the geometric basis of FDM pressure-driven flow.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Bead Width from Hatch Speed and Volumetric Flow

When the nozzle moves horizontally at **hatch speed $v_h$** and extrudes at flow rate $Q$, the deposited bead forms a cross-section approximated as a **rectangle of width $w_b$ and height $t_l$** (layer thickness).

**Step 1 — Volumetric deposition rate along the path:**

$$
Q_{deposited} = w_b \cdot t_l \cdot v_h
$$

**Step 2 — Equate to the volumetric extrusion rate:**

$$
Q = w_b \cdot t_l \cdot v_h
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for bead width:**

$$
w_b = \frac{Q}{t_l \cdot v_h}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:**

> *Nozzle flow rate $Q = 4 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$, layer thickness $t_l = 0.20 \text{ mm}$, hatch speed $v_h = 50 \text{ mm/s}$. Find the bead width.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_b &= \frac{4}{0.20 \times 50} \\
    &= \frac{4}{10} \\
    &= 0.40 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Insight:** A common KTU question is the **inverse problem** — "If the target bead width is 0.45 mm and $v_h = 60$ mm/s with $t_l = 0.15$ mm, what flow rate must the extruder deliver?" Solution: $Q = 0.45 \times 0.15 \times 60 = 4.05$ mm³/s.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Estimate of Build Time

**Step 1 — Define the total volume of material to be deposited:**

$$
V_{total} = V_{shell} + V_{infill} = V_{outer} + (1 - \phi) \cdot V_{inner}
$$

where $\phi$ is the **infill density** (e.g., 0.20 for 20% infill).

**Step 2 — Account for extrusion efficiency:**

Real systems have non-zero leak-back, oozing, and prime towers, reducing useful deposition. Define $\eta_{ext}$ ≈ **0.95**.

**Step 3 — Total time:**

$$
t_{build} = \frac{V_{total}}{Q \cdot \eta_{ext}} + t_{travel} + t_{heatup}
$$

where $t_{travel}$ is the time spent in non-extruding moves and $t_{heatup}$ is the pre-print warmup.

**Step 4 — Analytical Matrix of Build Time Drivers:**

| Factor | Effect on Build Time | Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|
| ↑ Layer thickness $t_l$ | ↓ Time (fewer layers) | Strong (1/t_l) |
| ↑ Print speed $v_h$ | ↓ Time (linearly) | Strong (1/v) |
| ↑ Infill $\phi$ | ↑ Time (more material) | Linear in $(1-\phi)$ |
| ↑ Nozzle diameter $r_n$ | ↓ Time (larger bead) | Quadratic (r²) |
| ↑ Part height | ↑ Time (more layers) | Linear |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Bonding Quality via the Reptation Time

The **inter-layer bond strength** depends on how long the polymer chains remain above $T_g$ to allow interdiffusion. The **reptation time** $t_r$ (de Gennes) is the characteristic time for a chain to "escape" its tube.

$$
t_r = t_0 \cdot N^{3} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)
$$

where:
- $t_0$ = microscopic jump time (~10⁻¹² s)
- $N$ = number of monomers per chain (degree of polymerization)
- $E_a$ = activation energy of reptation
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant
- $T$ = absolute temperature of the interface

> **Critical Insight:** Since $t_r$ depends **exponentially** on $1/T$, a small increase in nozzle temperature dramatically increases chain mobility and thus **bond strength**. This is why KTU questions often emphasize that **Z-direction strength is 30 – 60% lower than XY strength** in FDM — the inter-layer bonds have less time at elevated temperature compared to intra-layer bonds.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Melt Extrusion System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    spoolA[Filament Spool\n1.75 or 2.85 mm] --> idlerA[Idler Pulley\nBearing-loaded]
    idlerA --> drvnA[Driven Pulley\nGeared Stepper Motor]
    drvnA --> liquefierA[Liquefier Block\nHeater 40W x2]
    liquefierA --> thermistorA[Thermistor\nClosed-loop T control]
    liquefierA --> nozzleA[Brass Nozzle\nd = 0.4 mm]
    nozzleA --> beadA[Deposited Bead\nw b = 0.4 mm, t l = 0.2 mm]
    beadA --> platformA[Heated Bed\nT p = 60 to 110 C]
    platformA --> partA[3D Part\nLayer by Layer]
    nozzleA --> gantryA[X Y Gantry\nBelt driven]
    gantryA --> zaxisA[Z Axis Lead Screw\n0.05 mm microstep]
    drvnA --> mcuA[Control Board\nStepper drivers]
    mcuA --> gcodeA[G-code Interpreter\nSlic3r / Cura]
    thermistorA --> pidA[PID Controller\nT setpoint]
    pidA --> liquefierA
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Material flows top-to-bottom (spool → idler → driven pulley → liquefier → nozzle → bead → platform → final part). The control loop (right side) regulates temperature via the PID controller and the thermistor feedback. Motion is commanded by G-code executed on the MCU.

### 4.2 Process Flow of a Single Bead Deposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Step 1: Slicing\nSTL to G-code] --> step2[Step 2: Filament\nengagement]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Heat\nto T m]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Pressure\nbuild-up]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Extrusion\nthrough nozzle]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Die\nswell and spread]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Wetting\nof prior layer]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Polymer\nchain interdiffusion]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9: Cooling\nand solidification]
    step9 --> step10[Step 10: Z-axis\nincrement]
    step10 --> step1
```

### 4.3 Schematic of the Liquefier Cross-Section (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inletTop[Filament Inlet\nr f = 0.875 mm] --> meltZoneA[Melt Zone\nL m = 10 to 15 mm\nT = T m + 10 C]
    meltZoneA --> convergingA[Converging Section\n60 to 120 deg cone]
    convergingA --> capillaryA[Capillary\nL = 1 to 2 mm, d = 0.4 mm]
    capillaryA --> exitA[Nozzle Exit\nv e = approx 100 mm per s]
    heaterA[Cartridge Heaters\n2 x 40 W] -.-> meltZoneA
    thermA[Thermistor] -.-> meltZoneA
    insulatorA[PTFE Insulation\nT max 260 C] --> meltZoneA
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    defectA[Defect Observed] --> q1{Symptoms?}

    q1 -->|Stringing| f2A[Oozing / retraction off\nT n too high / retract set wrong]
    q1 -->|Warping| f3A[Bed adhesion fail\nT p too low / no brim]

    q1 -->|Blobs / Zits| f5A[Pressure spike at start\nTune coast and wipe]

    f2A --> fix2A[Enable retract 1 to 2 mm\nLower T n]
    f3A --> fix3A[Set T p 60 to 70 C for PLA\nAdd brim width 5 mm]

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define Material Extrusion-based AM. List any two process limitations of FDM.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Material Extrusion-based AM is a process in which a thermoplastic filament is melted in a heated liquefier and selectively extruded through a nozzle to form a 3D part layer-by-layer, as per **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021**.
>
> **Limitations (any two):**
> 1. **Anisotropic mechanical properties** — Z-direction (inter-layer) strength is typically **30–60% lower** than XY-direction strength due to weak inter-layer bonding.
> 2. **Visible stair-stepping** on curved or slanted surfaces (low surface finish), requiring post-processing.
> 3. **Limited material range** compared to powder-bed or photopolymer processes.
> 4. **Long build times** for large, dense parts.
> 5. **Support structure requirement** for overhangs beyond **~45°**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of the liquefier in an FDM system? Why is its length kept short (~10–15 mm)?*

**Model Answer:**

> **Role:** The liquefier melts the incoming solid thermoplastic filament and converts it into a continuous, pressurized melt stream suitable for extrusion through the nozzle. It houses the **cartridge heaters**, **thermistor**, and the **nozzle seat**.
>
> **Why short length (~10–15 mm):**
> 1. **To avoid premature cooling** of the melt in the capillary, which would cause solidification and clogging.
> 2. **To maintain a steady temperature gradient** — heat input is concentrated near the nozzle exit.
> 3. **To reduce the dwell time** of the polymer at high temperature, thereby minimizing **thermal degradation** (chain scission, oxidation).
> 4. **To reduce back-pressure losses** and ensure responsive extrusion control.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** In the 2024 Scheme ESE, Module-internal choice means both Question A and Question B cover the same module. The student attempts **only one**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of the **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)** process. List the main **process parameters** that affect the dimensional accuracy and surface finish of the part. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 1.75 mm diameter PLA filament is fed into a 0.4 mm nozzle at a feed rate of 4 mm/s. The melt behaves as a **power-law fluid** with $K = 5000$ Pa·sⁿ and $n = 0.4$. The nozzle capillary length is 1.5 mm. Calculate:
- (i) The volumetric flow rate $Q$. **(2 Marks)**
- (ii) The wall shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$. **(2 Marks)**
- (iii) The wall shear stress $\tau_w$ using the Rabinowitsch correction. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle and Process Parameters:**

The FDM process consists of the following sub-systems:
1. **Filament feed system:** Stepper motor + drive roller + idler.
2. **Liquefier:** Heated block (180–260 °C) that melts the filament.
3. **Nozzle:** Conical tip of diameter 0.25–1.20 mm for controlled extrusion.
4. **Build platform:** Heated bed (50–110 °C) to prevent warping.
5. **Motion system:** XY gantry + Z lead screw.
6. **Controller:** Interprets G-code and commands motors + heaters.

**Working:** The 3D CAD model is first sliced into horizontal layers (typical $t_l = 0.1$–0.3 mm). For each layer, the controller commands the XY gantry to trace the cross-sectional contour while simultaneously extruding a bead of molten polymer. Once a layer is complete, the Z-axis raises the platform (or lowers the nozzle) by $t_l$, and the next layer is deposited on top. Bonding occurs via polymer chain interdiffusion across the interface.

**Process Parameters affecting accuracy/finish:**

| Parameter | Effect |
|---|---|
| Layer thickness | Thinner = smoother, more accurate, but slower |
| Nozzle temperature | Affects bead bonding & dimensional shrinkage |
| Print speed | Too fast = under-extrusion, ringing artifacts |
| Bead width & overlap | Affects surface ridges and porosity |
| Platform temperature | Warping control |
| Cooling fan speed | Surface gloss and dimensional control |
| Infill & shell parameters | Inner strength and outer accuracy |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Volumetric flow rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \pi r_f^2 \cdot v_f \\
  &= \pi (0.875)^2 \times 4 \\
  &= \pi \times 0.7656 \times 4 \\
  &= 9.62 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s} \\
  &= 9.62 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Wall shear rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{\gamma} &= \frac{4Q}{\pi R^3} \\
R &= 0.20 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \\
\dot{\gamma} &= \frac{4 \times 9.62 \times 10^{-9}}{\pi (2 \times 10^{-4})^3} \\
            &= \frac{38.48 \times 10^{-9}}{\pi \times 8 \times 10^{-12}} \\
            &= \frac{38.48 \times 10^{-9}}{25.13 \times 10^{-12}} \\
            &= 1531 \text{ s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Wall shear stress (with Rabinowitsch correction):**

For a power-law fluid, the corrected shear stress is:

$$
\tau_w = K \cdot \left(\frac{3n+1}{4n}\right)^n \cdot \left(\frac{4Q}{\pi R^3}\right)^n
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{3n+1}{4n} &= \frac{3(0.4)+1}{4(0.4)} = \frac{2.2}{1.6} = 1.375 \\
(1.375)^{0.4} &\approx 1.143 \\
\dot{\gamma}^n &= (1531)^{0.4} \approx 18.02 \\
\tau_w &= 5000 \times 1.143 \times 18.02 \\
       &\approx 1.03 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} \\
       &\approx 103 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pressure drop across nozzle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= \frac{2 L \cdot \tau_w}{R} \\
        &= \frac{2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.03 \times 10^{5}}{2 \times 10^{-4}} \\
        &= 1.545 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \approx 1.55 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A 1.5 MPa pressure gradient is sufficient to drive a viscous PLA melt through a 0.4 mm nozzle at ~9.62 mm³/s. This back-pressure is generated by the drive roller's tangential force on the filament.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **four stages** of melt extrusion in FDM. Discuss the **bonding mechanism** between consecutive layers and the role of **reptation** in determining inter-layer strength. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3D printed part has a target **bead width of 0.45 mm** with a **layer thickness of 0.15 mm** and a **hatch speed of 60 mm/s**. Calculate:
- (i) The required volumetric flow rate $Q$. **(2 Marks)**
- (ii) The corresponding filament feed velocity for a 1.75 mm filament through a 0.4 mm nozzle. **(2 Marks)**
- (iii) The build time if the part has an effective infill volume of **12,000 mm³**, with an extrusion efficiency of 95%, and a heat-up + travel overhead of **3 minutes**. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Stages and Bonding Mechanism:**

**Stage 1 — Filament Transport:** A stepper motor rotates a geared drive roller, gripping the filament against an idler. The tangential feed force $F_f = \mu F_n$ pushes the filament into the liquefier at a controlled velocity $v_f$ (typically 1–10 mm/s).

**Stage 2 — Phase Change:** Inside the liquefier, the polymer is heated above $T_g$ (glass transition) and toward $T_m$ (melting point). A thin molten film forms at the liquefier wall while the polymer core may still be solid — this is the **"melt-flow boundary"** condition. The transition from a viscoelastic solid to a viscous fluid is governed by the **Arrhenius equation** $\eta(T) = \eta_0 \exp(E_a / RT)$.

**Stage 3 — Pressure-Driven Extrusion:** The molten polymer is forced through the nozzle under pressure. The flow is **incompressible, laminar, non-Newtonian** ($Re < 1$, $Pe \gg 1$). The volumetric flow rate is conserved: $Q = \pi r_f^2 v_f = \pi r_n^2 v_e$.

**Stage 4 — Deposition and Bonding:**
- The extrudate exits the nozzle and undergoes slight **die swell** and **road spreading** due to surface tension and compressive stress.
- It **wets** the previously deposited layer within milliseconds.
- Polymer chains from the molten bead **diffuse across the interface** into the partially solidified previous layer (reptation).
- Conductive cooling to the air and platform solidifies the bead, locking the diffused chains in place.

**Reptation and Inter-Layer Strength:**
According to **de Gennes' reptation theory (1971)**, a polymer chain escapes its topological constraints by "snake-like" motion through a confining tube. The **reptation time** $t_r$ depends exponentially on $1/T$:

$$
t_r \propto N^3 \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)
$$

A higher interface temperature $T$ drastically reduces $t_r$, allowing more chains to diffuse and form **entanglements** across the boundary. The **inter-layer weld strength** is therefore a strong function of the **time spent above $T_g$** during deposition. This explains why:
- Z-direction strength is **much lower** than XY strength.
- Higher nozzle temperatures improve Z-strength up to a saturation point (beyond which degradation occurs).
- Faster print speeds **reduce** Z-strength (less time for interdiffusion).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Volumetric flow rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= w_b \cdot t_l \cdot v_h \\
  &= 0.45 \times 0.15 \times 60 \\
  &= 4.05 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Filament feed velocity:**

Using mass continuity $Q = \pi r_f^2 v_f$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_f &= \frac{Q}{\pi r_f^2} \\
    &= \frac{4.05}{\pi (0.875)^2} \\
    &= \frac{4.05}{2.405} \\
    &\approx 1.684 \text{ mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Build time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{deposition} &= \frac{V}{Q \cdot \eta_{ext}} = \frac{12000}{4.05 \times 0.95} = \frac{12000}{3.8475} \approx 3119.5 \text{ s} \\
              &\approx 51.99 \text{ minutes} \\
t_{total} &= 51.99 + 3.00 = 54.99 \text{ minutes} \approx 55 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The printer must extrude at **4.05 mm³/s** (with the stepper pushing the 1.75 mm filament at **1.68 mm/s**), and the part will take approximately **55 minutes** to print.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones for Melt Extrusion Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing filament velocity with extrudate velocity:** The two differ by a factor of $(r_f/r_n)^2$, which is **~19×** for typical 1.75 mm filament and 0.4 mm nozzle. Forgetting this is a **2–3 mark** penalty.
> 2. **Using Newtonian viscosity for a polymer melt:** Polymers are non-Newtonian; KTU expects the **power-law (Ostwald–de Waele)** model with the **Rabinowitsch correction** for accurate wall shear stress.
> 3. **Not stating Reynolds number:** Always verify and mention $Re < 1$ to justify the laminar flow assumption (worth 1 mark).
> 4. **Omitting units and symbol definitions:** Each undeclared symbol = **½ mark deduction** as per KTU strict valuation.
> 5. **Ignoring the effect of `v_h` on bead width:** If hatch speed is doubled at constant flow, bead width halves — students often miss this proportionality.
> 6. **Forgetting the die swell / road spreading term in deposition analysis:** Real beads are **5–15% wider** than the nozzle diameter due to surface tension release.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (1-liner):** Melt extrusion AM selectively deposits a molten thermoplastic bead layer-by-layer through a computer-controlled nozzle.
- **Patent origin:** **S. Scott Crump, Stratasys, 1989.**
- **Process chain:** Solid feed → Phase change → Pressure-driven flow → Deposition & bonding.
- **Key geometric equation:** $v_e = v_f (r_f / r_n)^2$ — extrudate speed is amplified by the **square of the radius ratio**.
- **Bead width formula:** $w_b = Q / (t_l \cdot v_h)$.
- **Build time:** $t = V / (Q \cdot \eta_{ext}) + t_{overhead}$.
- **Polymer flow model:** Power-law fluid with **Rabinowitsch correction**; Reynolds number **< 1** confirms laminar flow.
- **Bonding mechanism:** Wetting → **Reptation-based chain interdiffusion** → Solidification.
- **Anisotropy:** Z-strength ≪ XY-strength (typically 30–60% lower).
- **Critical parameters:** $T_n$ (nozzle), $T_p$ (platform), $t_l$ (layer), $v_h$ (hatch), $w_b$ (bead width), $\phi$ (infill).
- **Process limitations:** Visible stair-stepping, anisotropic strength, slow build for large parts, support structures for overhangs > 45°.
- **Material examples:** PLA, ABS, PETG, TPU, Nylon, PC, PEEK (high-temp), wax (investment casting patterns).
- **Common defects:** Stringing, warping, under-extrusion, delamination, blobs/zits, ringing/ghosting.
- **Examiner's tip:** Always state assumptions (incompressible, laminar, steady, isothermal) before any derivation — this is a **2-mark** short-circuit in any 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques - Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Techniques - Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition (ISO/ASTM 52900 Standard)

**Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF)** is a Material Extrusion (MEX) based Additive Manufacturing process, formally classified under the **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021** standard, in which a continuous thermoplastic filament is selectively fed into a heated liquefier, melted beyond its glass transition temperature ($T_g$) and well above its melting point ($T_m$), and then extruded through a nozzle orifice to be deposited layer-by-layer along a digitally defined toolpath onto a build platform, where it rapidly solidifies upon cooling to form a solid three-dimensional object.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology Note:** FFF is functionally identical to **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)**, which is a trademarked term originally coined by **Stratasys Inc.** in 1989. KTU examiners strictly use the generic term **FFF** in question papers. **Scott Crump** is credited as the inventor of this technology.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hot Glue Gun" Model

Imagine you are holding a precision **hot glue gun** that never drips when you don't press the trigger. Instead of drawing random lines, a computer tells the gun *exactly* where to move in 3D space and *exactly* how much melted plastic to squirt out.

| Step | Hot Glue Gun (Analogy) | FFF Printer (Reality) |
|------|------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Insert glue stick | Feed thermoplastic filament |
| 2 | Trigger pulled | Stepper motor pushes filament |
| 3 | Glue melts inside | Heater block melts polymer at **200–260 °C** |
| 4 | Glue sticks to surface | Bead bonds to previous layer |
| 5 | Glue hardens | Polymer cools below glass transition |
| 6 | Repeat until shape complete | Hundreds/thousands of layers stacked |

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental philosophy of FFF is **"additive layer-by-layer deposition of molten thermoplastic"** — exactly what a student must write as the opening sentence in any 14-mark KTU answer.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics for FFF

- **Nozzle Diameter ($d_n$):** Typically **0.2 mm – 1.0 mm** (most common: **0.4 mm**)
- **Layer Height ($h_l$):** Typically **0.05 mm – 0.4 mm** (most common: **0.1 mm – 0.2 mm**)
- **Build Volume:** Ranging from **100 mm³** (desktop) to **1000 mm³** (industrial)
- **Print Speed:** **30 – 150 mm/s** (linear travel speed)
- **Filament Diameter ($d_f$):** Standardized at **1.75 mm** or **2.85 mm**

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exams:** Remember **"FFF = Filament + Feed + Fuse"** — the filament is fed and fused by the nozzle to form parts.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 System Architecture — The Five Sub-Systems of an FFF Machine

An FFF machine is not just a "printer" — it is a coordinated **mechatronic system** consisting of five tightly integrated sub-systems:

1. **Material Delivery Sub-System** — Filament spool, guide tube, extruder (direct or Bowden)
2. **Liquefier / Hot-End Sub-System** — Heating element, thermistor, nozzle, heat break
3. **Motion Control Sub-System** — Stepper motors, lead screws, belts, linear rails (X, Y, Z axes)
4. **Build Platform Sub-System** — Heated bed (typically **50–110 °C**), PEI/glass surface
5. **Control Electronics** — Microcontroller (e.g., Arduino/Marlin), firmware, G-code interpreter

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Working Principle

- **Step 1 — Slicing:** The 3D CAD model (.STL) is digitally sliced into horizontal layers of thickness **$h_l$** using slicing software (Cura, PrusaSlicer, Simplify3D).
- **Step 2 — Filament Feeding:** A stepper motor-driven extruder gear pushes the **1.75 mm / 2.85 mm** thermoplastic filament into the hot-end.
- **Step 3 — Melting:** Inside the metal heat break and nozzle, the filament is heated above its **melting temperature ($T_m$)**, typically **180–260 °C**, converting it into a viscous melt.
- **Step 4 — Extrusion & Deposition:** The molten polymer is forced out through the nozzle orifice and deposited along the G-code-defined toolpath.
- **Step 5 — Bonding:** The freshly extruded bead fuses with the previously deposited layer through **thermal welding** (molecular interdiffusion across the interface).
- **Step 6 — Solidification:** Rapid cooling brings the polymer below its **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)**, locking the geometry in place.
- **Step 7 — Repetition:** The Z-axis steps up by exactly **$h_l$**, and the process repeats for thousands of layers.

### 2.3 Critical Process Parameters (The "Big Five")

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect on Part |
|-----------|--------|---------------|----------------|
| Layer Height | $h_l$ | **0.05–0.4 mm** | Lower = finer resolution, longer build |
| Nozzle Temperature | $T_n$ | **180–260 °C** | Higher = better layer adhesion |
| Bed Temperature | $T_b$ | **50–110 °C** | Higher = reduces warping |
| Print Speed | $v$ | **30–150 mm/s** | Higher = faster, but lower quality |
| Extrusion Multiplier | $E$ | **0.9–1.1** | Controls bead width / over-extrusion |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM MANDATE:** These formulas are repeatedly tested in KTU University exams (Dec 2023, June 2024 patterns). Memorize the units and standard filament diameters (**$d_f = 1.75$ mm** or **2.85 mm**).

$$
V_{\text{filament}} \;=\; \frac{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}}{4} \cdot L_{\text{filament}}
$$

$$
m_{\text{part}} \;=\; V_{\text{filament}} \cdot \rho_{\text{polymer}}
$$

$$
L_{\text{extruded}} \;=\; \frac{h_l \cdot w_e}{\frac{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}}{4}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{layers}} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{H_{\text{part}}}{h_l} \right\rceil
$$

$$
t_{\text{build}} \;\approx\; \frac{V_{\text{part}}}{Q_{\text{vol}}} \;+\; t_{\text{setup}}
$$

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | Notes |
|--------|----------|---------------|-------|
| $V_{\text{filament}}$ | Volume of filament consumed | mm³ | Cross-section × length |
| $d_f$ | Filament diameter | mm | **1.75** or **2.85** (standard) |
| $L_{\text{filament}}$ | Filament length fed | mm | Measured by extruder steps |
| $m_{\text{part}}$ | Mass of printed part | g | For cost estimation |
| $\rho_{\text{polymer}}$ | Polymer density | g/cm³ | PLA = **1.24**, ABS = **1.04**, PETG = **1.27** |
| $h_l$ | Layer height | mm | Set in slicer |
| $w_e$ | Extrusion width | mm | Usually $\approx 1.2 \times d_n$ |
| $N_{\text{layers}}$ | Total layers | dimensionless | Integer, rounded up |
| $H_{\text{part}}$ | Part height along Z | mm | From CAD model |
| $t_{\text{build}}$ | Total build time | min / h | Slicer output |
| $Q_{\text{vol}}$ | Volumetric flow rate | mm³/s | $Q = \pi r_n^2 \cdot v$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

FFF is the **workhorse of polymer AM** — it dominates desktop 3D printing for **rapid prototyping, jigs & fixtures, functional end-use parts, and architectural models**. In aerospace, automotive, and biomedical sectors, FFF prints sacrificial patterns for investment casting and patient-specific anatomical models for pre-surgical planning.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Filament Length Required for a Given Bead

**Problem Context:** A KTU numerical problem typically asks: *"Calculate the length of 1.75 mm filament required to print a single extruded bead of length $L_b$ = 100 mm with layer height $h_l$ = 0.2 mm and extrusion width $w_e$ = 0.48 mm."*

**Step 1 — Identify the volume of the extruded bead.**

The deposited bead has a roughly rectangular cross-section (in practice, a flattened ellipse). Its volume is:

$$
V_{\text{bead}} \;=\; h_l \cdot w_e \cdot L_b
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
V_{\text{bead}} \;=\; 0.2 \times 0.48 \times 100
$$

$$
V_{\text{bead}} \;=\; 9.6 \; \text{mm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Volume conservation between filament and bead.**

Since the filament is melted (volume conserved for an incompressible thermoplastic), the filament volume equals the bead volume:

$$
V_{\text{filament}} \;=\; \frac{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}}{4} \cdot L_f \;=\; V_{\text{bead}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for filament length $L_f$.**

$$
L_f \;=\; \frac{4 \cdot V_{\text{bead}}}{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}}
$$

Substituting ($d_f$ = 1.75 mm):

$$
L_f \;=\; \frac{4 \times 9.6}{\pi \times (1.75)^2}
$$

$$
L_f \;=\; \frac{38.4}{\pi \times 3.0625}
$$

$$
L_f \;=\; \frac{38.4}{9.6211}
$$

$$
L_f \;\approx\; 3.991 \;\text{mm} \;\approx\; 4.0 \;\text{mm}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check Rule:** Filament length is *much smaller* than bead length because the filament cross-section (≈ **2.41 mm²**) is much larger than the bead cross-section (≈ **0.096 mm²**). The ratio is ≈ **25×**, matching our result (100 mm / 4 mm ≈ 25). ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Layers in a Part

**Problem Context:** *A part of height $H$ = 75 mm is to be printed at a layer height $h_l$ = 0.25 mm. How many layers are required?*

**Step 1 — Apply the integer ceiling formula.**

$$
N_{\text{layers}} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{H_{\text{part}}}{h_l} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
N_{\text{layers}} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{75}{0.25} \right\rceil
$$

$$
N_{\text{layers}} \;=\; \left\lceil 300 \right\rceil
$$

$$
N_{\text{layers}} \;=\; 300 \;\text{layers}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If $H$ were not exactly divisible by $h_l$ (e.g., $H = 75.1$ mm), the ceiling operator must be applied. KTU examiners *frequently* test this with non-divisible numbers.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mass & Cost of a Printed Part

**Problem Context:** *Find the mass and material cost of a part with volume $V_{\text{part}} = 25{,}000$ mm³, printed in PLA (density $\rho = 1.24$ g/cm³) at ₹1500/kg.*

**Step 1 — Convert volume to cm³.**

$$
V_{\text{part}} \;=\; 25{,}000 \;\text{mm}^3 \;\times\; \frac{1\;\text{cm}^3}{1000\;\text{mm}^3}
$$

$$
V_{\text{part}} \;=\; 25 \;\text{cm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mass.**

$$
m_{\text{part}} \;=\; V_{\text{part}} \cdot \rho
$$

$$
m_{\text{part}} \;=\; 25 \;\times\; 1.24
$$

$$
m_{\text{part}} \;=\; 31.0 \;\text{g} \;=\; 0.031 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the material cost.**

$$
C_{\text{material}} \;=\; m_{\text{part}} \times \text{Rate}
$$

$$
C_{\text{material}} \;=\; 0.031 \;\times\; 1500
$$

$$
C_{\text{material}} \;=\; ₹46.5
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Expectation:** Always include the **waste factor** in real exams. Add **5–15%** for skirt, brim, purge, and failed first layers.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Choose FFF

| Engineering Requirement | FFF Suitable? | Reasoning |
|--------------------------|----------------|-----------|
| Rapid prototype (same day) | ✅ **Yes** | Low cost, fast setup |
| High-temperature part (>150 °C) | ❌ **No** | Use PEEK/SLS |
| Sub-50 µm surface finish | ❌ **No** | Use SLA/DLP |
| Multi-material printing | ⚠️ **Limited** | Only dual-nozzle FFF |
| Functional metal end-use part | ❌ **No** | Use SLM/DMLS |
| Low-cost jigs and fixtures | ✅ **Yes** | Perfect use case |
| Patient-specific anatomical model | ✅ **Yes** | PLA/ABS biocompatible options |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 FFF Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid

    A1["START"] --> A2["CAD Model STL"]
    A2 --> A3["Slicer Software"]

    A6 --> A7["Heat to T above Tm"]
    A7 --> A8["Extrude Through Nozzle"]
    A8 --> A9{"Layer Complete?"}
    A9 -->|No| A10["Z Axis Step Up"]
    A10 --> A8
    A9 -->|Yes| A11["Cooling and Solidification"]
    A11 --> A12["End of Build"]
    A12 --> A13["Part Removal"]
    A13 --> A14["Support Removal"]
    A14 --> A15["FINISH"]

```

```

### 4.4 Coordinate System and Layer Stacking Visualization

    D5 --> D6["Build Plate Y Plane"]

    style D2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000000,color:#000000

```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
**(3 Marks)** *With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF) process. Mention any **two** thermoplastic materials used.*

**Model Answer:**

Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF) is a material extrusion based additive manufacturing process in which a thermoplastic filament (typically **1.75 mm** diameter) is fed into a heated liquefier, melted above its melting temperature ($T_m$), and extruded through a nozzle orifice to be deposited layer-by-layer on a build platform as per the G-code path. Each layer bonds to the previously solidified layer through thermal welding. Once one layer is complete, the build platform (or nozzle) moves up by one **layer height ($h_l$)** and the process repeats.

**Two thermoplastic materials used in FFF:**
1. **Polylactic Acid (PLA)** — biodegradable, low warping, prints at **190–220 °C**.
2. **Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene (ABS)** — tough, heat-resistant, prints at **220–260 °C**.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**(3 Marks)** *List any **six** important process parameters of FFF that affect the quality of the printed part.*

**Model Answer (Six Parameters):**

1. **Layer Height ($h_l$)** — Determines vertical resolution.
2. **Nozzle Temperature ($T_n$)** — Affects layer adhesion and surface finish.
3. **Bed Temperature ($T_b$)** — Reduces warping and improves first-layer adhesion.
4. **Print Speed ($v$)** — Influences part strength and dimensional accuracy.
5. **Extrusion Width ($w_e$)** — Affects bead-to-bead bonding.
6. **Infill Density (%)** — Controls internal porosity and mechanical strength.

---

### 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **OPTION A: QUESTION A (14 MARKS)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Expected Pattern]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** *Explain the complete Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF) process with a labelled block diagram. Describe the role of the heated build platform and the slicer software.*

**(b) (7 Marks)** *A 3D printed part has a volume of 80 cm³ and is fabricated using PLA filament of 1.75 mm diameter. If the print uses 15% support/waste material and PLA density is 1.24 g/cm³, calculate: (i) the total mass of PLA required, (ii) the length of filament consumed. The cost of PLA is ₹1800/kg.*

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Process Explanation (7 Marks):**

The FFF process follows these stages:

**Stage 1 — Digital Preparation:** A 3D CAD model (typically .STL format) is imported into slicing software (e.g., Cura, PrusaSlicer). The slicer converts the mesh into horizontal cross-sections of thickness equal to the chosen **layer height**, and generates **G-code** containing X, Y, Z coordinates and extrusion commands.

**Stage 2 — Material Feeding:** A spool of thermoplastic filament (PLA, ABS, PETG, TPU) of diameter **1.75 mm or 2.85 mm** is mounted on the printer. A stepper motor drives a feed gear that pushes the filament through a Bowden tube (or directly) into the hot-end.

**Stage 3 — Melting:** Inside the **hot-end**, a heater block (with thermistor feedback) raises the temperature of the filament above its melting point ($T_m$). For PLA, $T_m \approx 150\,°\text{C}$ but extrusion occurs at **190–220 °C**.

**Stage 4 — Extrusion & Deposition:** The molten polymer is forced through a brass nozzle (typically **0.4 mm** diameter) and deposited onto the build platform along the G-code toolpath.

**Stage 5 — Solidification & Bonding:** The polymer rapidly cools below its glass transition temperature ($T_g$) and fuses with the previous layer.

**Stage 6 — Layer Stacking:** The Z-axis stepper raises the platform by **$h_l$** and Stage 4 repeats.

**Role of the Heated Build Platform:** Maintained at **50–110 °C**, the heated bed reduces **thermal gradient-induced warping**, improves **first-layer adhesion**, and minimizes **delamination** between layers and the bed.

**Role of Slicer Software:** Converts 3D geometry into machine-executable G-code, sets layer height, infill, supports, print speed, and temperature profiles — essentially acting as the *translator* between digital model and physical motion.

*(Block Diagram: Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes — 3 marks reserved for diagram.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $V_{\text{part}} = 80$ cm³
- $d_f = 1.75$ mm
- Waste factor = 15% → Total volume = $V_{\text{part}} \times 1.15$
- $\rho_{\text{PLA}} = 1.24$ g/cm³
- Rate = ₹1800/kg

**Step 1 — Compute total volume including waste.**

$$
V_{\text{total}} \;=\; 80 \times 1.15
$$

$$
V_{\text{total}} \;=\; 92 \;\text{cm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total mass of PLA.**

$$
m_{\text{total}} \;=\; V_{\text{total}} \times \rho
$$

$$
m_{\text{total}} \;=\; 92 \times 1.24
$$

$$
m_{\text{total}} \;=\; 114.08 \;\text{g} \;=\; 0.11408 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the cost.**

$$
C \;=\; 0.11408 \times 1800 \;=\; ₹205.34
$$

**Step 4 — Compute filament length consumed.**

First, convert volume to mm³:

$$
V_{\text{total}} \;=\; 92 \;\text{cm}^3 \;\times\; 1000 \;=\; 92{,}000 \;\text{mm}^3
$$

Apply filament volume formula:

$$
L_f \;=\; \frac{4 \cdot V_{\text{total}}}{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}}
$$

$$
L_f \;=\; \frac{4 \times 92{,}000}{\pi \times (1.75)^2}
$$

$$
L_f \;=\; \frac{368{,}000}{\pi \times 3.0625}
$$

$$
L_f \;=\; \frac{368{,}000}{9.6211}
$$

$$
L_f \;\approx\; 38{,}248 \;\text{mm} \;\approx\; 38.25 \;\text{m}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Mass required = **114.08 g**
- Filament length = **≈ 38.25 m**
- Material cost = **≈ ₹205.34**

---

#### **OPTION B: QUESTION B (14 MARKS — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Expected Pattern]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** *Compare the **Direct Drive** and **Bowden** extruder configurations used in FFF printers. Discuss their advantages, disadvantages, and typical applications.*

**(b) (7 Marks)** *Define **layer height**, **infill density**, and **support structure** in FFF. A part of dimensions 80 mm × 60 mm × 40 mm is printed with layer height 0.2 mm and 20% infill. The outer shell is solid (1.2 mm wall thickness). Calculate the number of layers and the approximate print time if each layer takes 45 seconds (excluding first-layer setup of 5 minutes).*

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Direct Drive vs Bowden Extruder (7 Marks):**

| Feature | Direct Drive Extruder | Bowden Extruder |
|---------|------------------------|-----------------|
| **Location** | Stepper mounted *directly above* the hot-end | Stepper mounted on the frame, away from hot-end |
| **Filament Path** | Short, rigid | Long, flexible PTFE tube |
| **Retraction** | Minimal (short path) | High (long path compensation needed) |
| **Weight on Carriage** | Heavy (motor + extruder) | Light (only hot-end) |
| **Print Speed** | Slower due to mass | Faster, higher acceleration possible |
| **Flexibility** | Can print **flexible filaments (TPU)** easily | Struggles with TPU (jamming risk) |
| **Maintenance** | Easier to clear jams | Harder to diagnose |
| **Applications** | Engineering-grade, flexible, precision | High-speed hobby printing, PLA/PETG |

**Conclusion:** Direct Drive offers better control and material versatility; Bowden offers speed advantage at lower cost.

---

**(b) Definitions and Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Definitions:**

- **Layer Height ($h_l$):** The vertical thickness of each deposited layer. Common values: **0.1 mm (fine), 0.2 mm (standard), 0.3 mm (draft)**.
- **Infill Density (%):** The percentage of internal volume filled with material (the rest being hollow or patterned). 20% infill = light-weight, 100% infill = solid.
- **Support Structure:** Auxiliary geometry printed to hold overhanging features (typically > 45° overhangs). Removed post-printing.

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Number of layers.**

$$
N_{\text{layers}} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{H}{h_l} \right\rceil
$$

$$
N_{\text{layers}} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{40}{0.2} \right\rceil \;=\; 200 \;\text{layers}
$$

**Step 2 — Print time.**

$$
t_{\text{layers}} \;=\; 200 \times 45 \;\text{s} \;=\; 9{,}000 \;\text{s}
$$

$$
t_{\text{layers}} \;=\; \frac{9{,}000}{60} \;=\; 150 \;\text{min} \;=\; 2 \;\text{h} \; 30 \;\text{min}
$$

**Step 3 — Add setup time.**

$$
t_{\text{total}} \;=\; 150 \;\text{min} \;+\; 5 \;\text{min} \;=\; 155 \;\text{min}
$$

**Final Answer:** **200 layers** and **155 minutes (≈ 2 h 35 min)** total print time.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Writing "mass = 114" without "g" or "kg" loses 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Using wrong filament diameter** — Default value is **1.75 mm**, NOT 2.85 mm, unless explicitly stated.
> 3. **Skipping the waste/support factor** — KTU frequently tests this (10–15% typical).
> 4. **Confusing FFF with FDM** — Always write "FFF (also known as FDM)" for full marks.
> 5. **Missing the diagram** — In 14-mark answers, a labelled block diagram is **mandatory** (3 marks reserved).
> 6. **Forgetting density unit conversion** — cm³ × g/cm³ = g, but mm³ × g/mm³ ≠ g. Convert consistently!

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — FFF**

- ✅ FFF = **Material Extrusion** (ISO/ASTM 52900 classification).
- ✅ Inventor: **Scott Crump (1989)**; Trademark: **FDM (Stratasys)**.
- ✅ Standard filament diameters: **1.75 mm** and **2.85 mm**.
- ✅ Standard nozzle diameter: **0.4 mm**.
- ✅ Common layer heights: **0.1 mm / 0.2 mm / 0.3 mm**.
- ✅ Common materials: **PLA, ABS, PETG, TPU, Nylon**.
- ✅ Build sequence: **CAD → STL → Slicer → G-code → Print**.
- ✅ Key formulas:
   - $V_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} \cdot L_f$
   - $m = V \cdot \rho$
   - $N_{\text{layers}} = \lceil H / h_l \rceil$
- ✅ Heated bed: **50–110 °C** → reduces warping.
- ✅ Nozzle temp: **180–260 °C** → above polymer $T_m$.
- ✅ Two extruder types: **Direct Drive** (precision) and **Bowden** (speed).
- ✅ Post-processing required: **support removal, sanding, annealing** (optional).
- ✅ Limitation: **Anisotropy** — Z-direction strength is weaker than X/Y.
- ✅ Advantages: **Low cost, simple, wide material range, office-safe**.
- ✅ Disadvantages: **Visible layer lines, support needed, slow for large parts**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) and Direct Ink Writing (DIW)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) and Direct Ink Writing (DIW)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)** is a **material extrusion** based Additive Manufacturing (AM) process, classified under the **ASTM/ISO 52900:2021** standard as the "Material Extrusion" category. In FDM, a continuous filament of **thermoplastic polymer** is fed into a heated **liquefier (hot-end)**, where it is melted to a semi-liquid state and then selectively extruded through a **nozzle** orifice. The nozzle deposits the molten material in a pre-defined path onto a **build platform** (or onto the previously deposited layer) in an **X-Y plane**, where it rapidly **solidifies**. The build platform (or the nozzle) is then displaced along the **Z-axis** by an amount equal to the **layer thickness**, and the next layer is deposited, fusing with the previous one to build a solid 3D part in a layer-by-layer fashion.

- **Inventor:** S. Scott Crump (1989), Commercialized by **Stratasys Inc.**
- **Synonyms/Trade Names:** Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF) — the open-source equivalent
- **Standard Filament Diameter:** **1.75 mm** and **2.85 mm** (commonly used)
- **Typical Nozzle Diameter:** **0.2 mm – 1.0 mm**

**Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine using a **hot glue gun** that, instead of dripping randomly, precisely traces a 2D outline of an object (say, a flower) on a flat table. Once the outline cools and hardens, you lower the gun by the thickness of one petal and trace the next layer on top, slightly offset. Repeat this hundreds of times, and you will "draw" a full 3D flower with the glue gun. **FDM does exactly this**, but with engineering-grade plastic instead of glue, controlled by a computer.

---

### 1.2 Direct Ink Writing (DIW)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct Ink Writing (DIW)**, also referred to as **Robocasting** or **Micro-Robotic Deposition**, is an extrusion-based AM process in which a **viscous, shear-thinning liquid or paste (ink)** is extruded through a **fine nozzle (typically 100 µm – 1 mm)** under controlled pressure (pneumatic, piston, or screw-driven) and deposited along a digitally defined path to fabricate 3D structures. Unlike FDM, DIW is **not restricted to thermoplastics** — it can process **ceramics, metals, cements, polymers, composites, hydrogels, and bio-inks**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
> Think of **decorating a cake with an icing syringe** — you squeeze the syringe, and the icing comes out as a continuous bead that you guide to create patterns. Now imagine if you could "freeze" each bead instantly and stack layer upon layer of icing patterns to form a 3D shape, working with icing of very different consistencies (chocolate ganache, royal icing, fondant). **DIW is the industrial, computer-controlled, micron-precision version of this cake-decorating concept**, but the "icing" can be technical ceramics, living cells, or conductive silver paste.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Both FDM and DIW are categorized under **Material Extrusion** processes. The key distinction lies in the **rheological state** of the feedstock — FDM uses a **thermally melted solid filament**, whereas DIW uses a **flow-controlled viscous fluid/paste at room (or slightly elevated) temperature**.

---

### 1.3 Comparative Snapshot

| Aspect | FDM | DIW |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Feedstock State** | Solid filament → molten | Viscous ink/paste at start |
| **Driving Energy** | Thermal (heater) | Pressure (pneumatic/mechanical) |
| **Material Class** | Thermoplastics only | Ceramics, metals, polymers, bio-inks |
| **Layer Thickness** | **0.05 mm – 0.4 mm** | **0.1 mm – 1.0 mm** |
| **Typical Resolution** | **100 – 300 µm** | **10 µm – 500 µm** |
| **Solvent Requirement** | None | Often required (for curing/drying) |
| **Post-Processing** | Minimal | Often mandatory (sintering, drying, curing) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 FDM — Working Principle & Subsystems

The FDM process relies on the precise coordination of **three mechanical subsystems**:

1. **Material Delivery Subsystem** — Filament spool, pinch-roller feeder (stepper motor-driven)
2. **Liquefier / Hot-End Subsystem** — Heater block, thermistor, nozzle (typically brass with Teflon insert)
3. **Motion / Positioning Subsystem** — X-Y gantry (or moving bed) for planar paths, Z-axis for layer stepping

**Step-by-Step Process Logic:**

1. **Slicing:** The 3D CAD model is virtually sliced into horizontal cross-sections of thickness `t_layer` by a **slicer software** (Cura, Simplify3D, PrusaSlicer).
2. **Path Generation:** Each slice is converted into **toolpaths** (outer perimeter, inner infill, support structures).
3. **Filament Feeding:** The pinch roller drives the filament into the heated liquefier at a controlled **feed rate `v_f` (mm/s)**.
4. **Phase Change:** The filament undergoes a **glass transition / melting** (e.g., PLA at **~180 °C**, ABS at **~230 °C**) inside the liquefier.
5. **Extrusion:** Molten polymer is forced through the nozzle (diameter `d_n`), generating an extruded bead of width `w_b`.
6. **Deposition & Solidification:** The bead is deposited onto the build platform (or previous layer). It **solidifies rapidly** (cooling rate can exceed **100 °C/s** for thin sections).
7. **Z-Step:** The build platform is lowered (or nozzle raised) by `t_layer`, and the process repeats.
8. **Bonding:** Adjacent beads within a layer **fuse by surface diffusion (neck formation)**, and successive layers bond via **thermal welding / autohesion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why layer adhesion matters:** The **inter-layer bond strength** in FDM parts is typically only **40–80 %** of the bulk material strength due to the **anisotropic "weld-line" effect**. This is a high-yield KTU exam point.

---

### 2.2 FDM — Critical Process Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect on Part |
|-----------|--------|---------------|----------------|
| **Nozzle Temperature** | $T_n$ | **180 °C – 300 °C** | Higher → better bonding, lower viscosity, warping |
| **Bed Temperature** | $T_b$ | **25 °C – 110 °C** | Reduces warping, improves first-layer adhesion |
| **Layer Thickness** | $t_L$ | **0.05 – 0.4 mm** | Thinner → smoother surface, longer build time |
| **Print Speed** | $v_p$ | **30 – 150 mm/s** | Faster → under-extrusion, weaker bonds |
| **Infill Density** | $\rho_i$ | **5 % – 100 %** | Higher → stronger, heavier part |
| **Raster Angle** | $\theta$ | **0° – 90°** | Affects mechanical anisotropy |
| **Nozzle Diameter** | $d_n$ | **0.2 – 1.0 mm** | Determines extrusion width |
| **Build Orientation** | — | Flat / Vertical / Angle | Affects strength + support requirement |

---

### 2.3 DIW — Working Principle & Rheology

The core physics of DIW is **not heat** but **rheology** — the flow and deformation of complex fluids.

**Three rheological regimes in DIW:**

1. **At Rest:** The ink must be **viscoelastic with a yield stress $\tau_y$** — it behaves like a solid and **does not flow** under its own weight (preventing slumping/collapse of deposited filaments).
2. **During Extrusion:** The applied pressure must exceed $\tau_y$, causing the ink to **shear-thin** (viscosity drops with shear rate) → it flows smoothly through the nozzle.
3. **After Deposition:** Once the pressure is removed, the ink **rapidly recovers its viscosity / structure** (thixotropy) → it holds its shape and supports subsequent layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The success of DIW hinges on this **yield-stress-fluid behavior**, mathematically captured by the **Herschel-Bulkley model** (the most general model) and the simplified **Bingham Plastic model**.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examination High-Yield Equations — FDM & DIW**

#### A. FDM — Volumetric Extrusion & Flow Equations

| # | Equation | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi d_n^{2}}{4} \cdot v_e$ | Volumetric flow rate; $v_e$ = extrusion velocity |
| 2 | $Q = A_c \cdot v_f$ | Feed-driven flow; $A_c$ = filament cross-section, $v_f$ = filament feed speed |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\pi d_n^{2}}{4} v_e = \dfrac{\pi d_f^{2}}{4} v_f$ | **Mass conservation** (incompressible melt) → $d_n^{2} v_e = d_f^{2} v_f$ |
| 4 | $w_b \approx 1.5 \cdot d_n$ | Bead width ≈ **1.5 × nozzle diameter** (empirical) |
| 5 | $R_a \approx 0.25 \cdot d_n$ | Surface roughness $R_a$ ≈ **1/4 of nozzle diameter** |
| 6 | $t_{\text{build}} = \dfrac{N_L \cdot t_L}{v_p} \cdot k_{\text{path}}$ | Build time; $N_L$ = number of layers, $k_{\text{path}}$ = path factor |

#### B. Hagen-Poiseuille Flow (Pressure-driven Newtonian extrusion)

| # | Equation | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 7 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi R^{4}}$ | Pressure drop across a **cylindrical nozzle** of length $L$ and radius $R$ |
| 8 | $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi R^{3}}$ | Wall shear rate in cylindrical tube flow |
| 9 | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 L}$ | Wall shear stress |

#### C. Non-Newtonian Rheology Models for DIW Inks

| # | Equation | Model | Description |
|---|----------|-------|-------------|
| 10 | $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ | **Bingham Plastic** | Linear after yielding |
| 11 | $\tau = \tau_y + K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | **Herschel-Bulkley** | General case; $K$ = consistency index, $n$ = flow index |
| 12 | $\eta(\dot{\gamma}) = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | **Apparent viscosity** | Useful for shear-thinning inks |
| 13 | $\dot{\gamma}_c = \left(\dfrac{\tau_y}{K}\right)^{1/n}$ | **Critical shear rate** | Below this, ink does not flow |

#### D. Geometrical / Resolution Equations

| # | Equation | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 14 | $\text{Resolution}_{xy} \approx d_n$ | Planar resolution bounded by nozzle diameter |
| 15 | $\text{Resolution}_{z} \approx t_L$ | Vertical resolution bounded by layer thickness |
| 16 | $\text{Vol. fraction solid} = 1 - \phi_{\text{pore}}$ | Porosity control (critical for DIW ceramics pre-sintering) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the filament diameter $d_f$ (e.g., 1.75 mm) with the nozzle diameter $d_n$ (e.g., 0.4 mm). They are **independent** parameters; the conservation equation (3) relates the **feed speed** and the **extrusion speed** through them. Use $\vert d \vert$ notation correctly — use `\vert` in latex.

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **FDM** is the **workhorse of desktop 3D printing** — used in jigs/fixtures, end-use polymer parts, low-volume manufacturing, prosthetics, and architectural models.
- **DIW** is the **frontier of multi-material AM** — enables **solid-state batteries**, **biomedical scaffolds**, **micro-electronics** (printed antennas, sensors), **bioprinting** (tissues and organs), and **technical ceramics** that cannot be melted.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass Conservation Between Filament Feed and Nozzle Extrusion (FDM)

**Goal:** Show that the volumetric flow rate of the molten polymer at the nozzle equals the volumetric feed rate of the solid filament.

**Step 1 — Define Volumetric Flow Rate of Solid Filament:**

The filament has a circular cross-section of diameter $d_f$. As it advances at feed speed $v_f$:

$$Q_f = A_f \cdot v_f$$

where $A_f = \dfrac{\pi d_f^{2}}{4}$ is the cross-sectional area of the filament.

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$Q_f = \frac{\pi d_f^{2}}{4} \cdot v_f$$

This is the **volume of solid filament entering the liquefier per unit time** (mm³/s).

**Step 3 — Define Volumetric Flow Rate at Nozzle Exit:**

The molten polymer exits a circular nozzle of diameter $d_n$ at an average velocity $v_e$:

$$Q_n = A_n \cdot v_e = \frac{\pi d_n^{2}}{4} \cdot v_e$$

**Step 4 — Apply Conservation of Mass (Incompressible Polymer Melt):**

Since polymer melts are practically **incompressible** (density change < 1 %), the mass entering per second must equal the mass leaving per second:

$$\rho_f \cdot Q_f = \rho_n \cdot Q_n$$

Assuming $\rho_f \approx \rho_n$ for an incompressible melt:

$$Q_f = Q_n$$

**Step 5 — Equate and Simplify:**

$$\frac{\pi d_f^{2}}{4} \cdot v_f = \frac{\pi d_n^{2}}{4} \cdot v_e$$

Cancel $\pi / 4$ on both sides:

$$d_f^{2} \cdot v_f = d_n^{2} \cdot v_e$$

**Step 6 — Solve for Nozzle Exit Velocity:**

$$v_e = \left(\frac{d_f}{d_n}\right)^{2} \cdot v_f$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Since $d_f \gg d_n$ (e.g., 1.75 mm vs 0.4 mm), the ratio $(d_f / d_n)^{2} \approx (1.75/0.4)^{2} \approx 19.1$. Thus the polymer exits the nozzle at roughly **19× the filament feed speed**. This is called the **draw-down ratio** and explains why the extruder must push the filament with significant force.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Hagen-Poiseuille Pressure Drop for DIW Ink Extrusion

**Goal:** Derive the pressure required to extrude a Newtonian ink through a cylindrical nozzle.

**Step 1 — Set Up the Cylindrical Nozzle Geometry:**

Consider a cylindrical tube of radius $R$, length $L$, with fully developed laminar flow driven by an axial pressure gradient $\Delta P / L$.

**Step 2 — Force Balance on a Fluid Cylinder of Radius $r$:**

The pressure force pushing the cylinder forward is balanced by the viscous shear force on its lateral surface:

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^{2} = \tau(r) \cdot 2 \pi r L$$

Solve for the local shear stress:

$$\tau(r) = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity (Newtonian Fluid):**

For a Newtonian fluid, $\tau(r) = -\mu \dfrac{dv}{dr}$ (the negative sign because velocity decreases with $r$):

$$-\mu \frac{dv}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L}$$

**Step 4 — Separate Variables and Integrate:**

$$dv = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot r \, dr$$

Integrate from an inner radius $r$ to the wall $R$, with boundary condition $v(R) = 0$ (no-slip):

$$\int_{v(r)}^{0} dv' = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{r}^{R} r' \, dr'$$

$$-v(r) = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \left[\frac{R^{2} - r^{2}}{2}\right]$$

$$v(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Volumetric Flow Rate:**

Integrate the velocity profile over the cross-section:

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} v(r) \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{0}^{R} \left(R^{2} r - r^{3}\right) dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[\frac{R^{2} r^{2}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{R}$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[\frac{R^{4}}{2} - \frac{R^{4}}{4}\right]$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^{4}}{8 \mu L}}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the Pressure Drop:**

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi R^{4}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Since $\Delta P \propto R^{-4}$, halving the nozzle radius increases the required pressure by **16×**. This is why DIW of sub-100 µm features requires **ultra-high-pressure syringe pumps** (often >1 MPa) and **carefully formulated shear-thinning inks** (Bingham/Herschel-Bulkley fluids), not simple Newtonian fluids.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Critical Shear Rate for a Herschel-Bulkley Ink (DIW)

**Goal:** Determine the minimum shear rate at which a Herschel-Bulkley ink begins to flow (i.e., exits the yield-stress regime).

**Step 1 — Start with the Herschel-Bulkley Equation:**

$$\tau = \tau_y + K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

where:
- $\tau_y$ = **yield stress** (Pa)
- $K$ = **consistency index** (Pa·sⁿ)
- $n$ = **flow behavior index** (dimensionless)

**Step 2 — Recognize the Yielding Condition:**

For flow to occur, the applied shear stress must exceed $\tau_y$. The **minimum** stress at which flow initiates is $\tau = \tau_y$. Substitute:

$$\tau_y = \tau_y + K \dot{\gamma}_c^{n}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the Critical Shear Rate $\dot{\gamma}_c$:**

Subtract $\tau_y$ from both sides:

$$0 = K \dot{\gamma}_c^{n}$$

This step appears to give $\dot{\gamma}_c = 0$, which is physically correct **for the onset** but does not capture practical extrusion. Instead, we define the **critical shear rate** as the point where the **viscous contribution** equals a specified threshold — equivalently, where $\dot{\gamma}$ becomes "non-negligible":

$$K \dot{\gamma}_c^{n} = \alpha \tau_y$$

where $\alpha$ is a tolerance factor (often $\alpha = 0.01$ for 1 % of yield stress). Taking the $n$-th root:

$$\dot{\gamma}_c = \left(\frac{\alpha \tau_y}{K}\right)^{1/n}$$

Or, for the conventional engineering definition ($\alpha = 1$):

$$\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_c = \left(\frac{\tau_y}{K}\right)^{1/n}}$$

**Step 4 — Practical Interpretation:**

If $\tau_y = 50$ Pa, $K = 10$ Pa·sⁿ, $n = 0.4$ (shear-thinning):

$$\dot{\gamma}_c = \left(\frac{50}{10}\right)^{1/0.4} = 5^{2.5} \approx 55.9 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

This means the ink needs a wall shear rate $> 56$ s⁻¹ to begin sustained flow — directly guiding the **minimum extrusion pressure** via equation (8).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | FDM | DIW |
|---------|-----|-----|
| **Driving Physics** | Thermal phase change | Viscous flow / rheology |
| **Governing Equation** | Hagen-Poiseuille (melt) + Conservation of mass | Herschel-Bulkley / Bingham |
| **Nozzle Diameter Sensitivity** | $\Delta P \propto R^{-4}$ (for melt) | Same (for ink); also $\propto \mu_{\text{eff}}$ |
| **Material Limitation** | Thermoplastics only | Wide (ceramics, bio, metals, polymers) |
| **Build Speed** | Moderate to fast | Slow (esp. for high-viscosity inks) |
| **Anisotropy** | Strong (weld lines) | Moderate to strong (depends on ink) |
| **Post-Processing** | Often none | **Often essential** (sintering, curing) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 FDM — Process Flow Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[CAD Model Input STL File] --> A2[Slicer Software Cura PrusaSlicer]
    A2 --> A3[G-code Generation Layer by Layer Toolpaths]
    A3 --> A4[Filament Spool ABS PLA PETG]
    A4 --> A5[Pinch Roller Feeder Stepper Motor Driven]
    A5 --> A6[Heated Liquefier Hot-End 180 to 300 C]
    A6 --> A7[Nozzle Diameter 0.2 to 1.0 mm]
    A7 --> A8[Deposition on Build Platform X-Y Motion]
    A8 --> A9[Layer Cooling and Solidification]
    A9 --> A10{Z Layer Complete?}
    A10 -- No --> A11[Z Step Build Platform Down by Layer Thickness]
    A11 --> A7
    A10 -- Yes --> A12[Finished 3D Part]
    A12 --> A13[Optional Post Processing Support Removal]

    style A1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style A6 fill:#ffe1e1
    style A7 fill:#ffe1e1
    style A12 fill:#d4f4dd
    style A13 fill:#d4f4dd
```

---

### 4.2 FDM — Functional Architecture of the Extrusion Head

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Filament Inlet 1.75 mm or 2.85 mm] --> B2[Drive Gear]
    B2 --> B3[Idler Bearing]
    B3 --> B4[Heater Block with Cartridge Heater]
    B4 --> B5[Thermistor Temperature Feedback]
    B5 --> B6[PID Controller]
    B6 --> B4
    B4 --> B7[Nozzle Tip Brass]
    B7 --> B8[Part Cooling Fan]
    B7 --> B9[Deposited Bead on Build Platform]

    style B4 fill:#ffd4d4
    style B6 fill:#fff4d4
    style B9 fill:#d4f4dd
```

---

### 4.3 DIW — Process Flow Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[CAD Model STL] --> C2[Slicer Toolpath with Extrusion Multiplier]
    C2 --> C3[Ink Reservoir Syringe or Cartridge]
    C3 --> C4[Pressure Source Pneumatic Piston or Screw]
    C4 --> C5[Micro Nozzle 100 um to 1 mm]
    C5 --> C6[Deposition on Substrate or Previous Layer]
    C6 --> C7{Ink Solidification Mechanism}
    C7 -- Thermal --> C8[Heated Substrate Drying]
    C7 -- Chemical --> C9[UV Cure or Crosslinking]
    C7 -- Solvent --> C10[Solvent Evaporation]
    C8 --> C11{Z Layer Done?}
    C9 --> C11
    C10 --> C11
    C11 -- No --> C12[Z Increment Step Up]
    C12 --> C6
    C11 -- Yes --> C13[Green Part Wet Unfired]
    C13 --> C14[Post Processing]
    C14 --> C15[Sintering for Ceramics]
    C14 --> C16[Debinding for Metals]
    C14 --> C17[Crosslinking for Polymers]
    C15 --> C18[Final Dense Part]
    C16 --> C18
    C17 --> C18

    style C4 fill:#fff4d4
    style C7 fill:#ffe1e1
    style C13 fill:#ffd4d4
    style C18 fill:#d4f4dd
```

---

### 4.4 DIW vs FDM — Functional Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph D1[FDM Subsystem]
        D11[Solid Filament Feedstock]
        D12[Thermal Energy Input]
        D13[Liquefier and Heater]
        D14[Pressure from Drive Roller]
        D15[Molten Bead Deposition]
    end

    subgraph D2[DIW Subsystem]
        D21[Liquid Paste or Ink Reservoir]
        D22[Rheological Control Only]
        D23[Pressure Source Pump Pneumatic Mechanical]
        D24[Yield Stress Behavior]
        D25[Viscous Bead Deposition]
    end

    D11 --> D13
    D12 --> D13
    D14 --> D13
    D13 --> D15

    D21 --> D23
    D22 --> D24
    D23 --> D24
    D24 --> D25

    style D13 fill:#ffd4d4
    style D23 fill:#d4e6f4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** These Mermaid block diagrams map the **functional architecture** of each process, showing how energy, material, and control inputs converge to produce the deposited bead. The functional mapping is preferred over pictorial schematics because it exposes the **causal dependencies** between subsystems, which is what KTU examiners test.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain the working principle of Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) with a neat sketch. List any two process parameters affecting the strength of FDM parts.**

**Model Answer:**

FDM is a material extrusion-based additive manufacturing process in which a thermoplastic filament is fed into a heated liquefier, melted, and extruded through a nozzle onto a build platform along a computer-defined path. The material solidifies upon deposition; the platform is then lowered by one layer thickness and the process is repeated to build a 3D part.

**Sketch (to be drawn in exam):**

```
   Filament (→) → [Heater Block / Liquefier] → Nozzle (↓)
                                                    |
                                                    v
                              [Previously deposited layer(s)]
                                                    |
                                              Build Platform
                                               (moves in Z)
```

**Two process parameters affecting strength:**

1. **Raster Angle ($\theta$):** The orientation of the deposited beads relative to the loading direction. A $0°$ raster (parallel to load) gives higher tensile strength than $90°$ (perpendicular).
2. **Infill Density ($\rho_i$):** Higher infill (closer to 100 %) increases the cross-sectional load-bearing area, raising strength. Typical range: 20 % – 100 %.
3. *(Any one more: Layer Thickness, Nozzle Temperature, Print Speed, Build Orientation)*

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is Direct Ink Writing (DIW)? Why are yield-stress fluids preferred over Newtonian fluids as DIW inks?**

**Model Answer:**

**Direct Ink Writing (DIW)** is an extrusion-based additive manufacturing process where a viscous, shear-thinning liquid or paste (ink) is extruded through a fine nozzle under controlled pressure and deposited in a layer-by-layer manner to build 3D structures. Unlike FDM, DIW does not require melting and can process ceramics, metals, polymers, composites, and bio-inks.

**Why yield-stress fluids (e.g., Bingham / Herschel-Bulkley) are preferred:**

| Reason | Explanation |
|--------|-------------|
| **Shape Retention** | Yield stress $\tau_y$ prevents the ink from flowing under gravity after deposition, so unsupported filaments do not slump/collapse. |
| **Smooth Extrusion** | Above $\tau_y$, the ink shear-thins (viscosity drops with shear rate), enabling smooth flow through narrow nozzles. |
| **Self-Supporting Structures** | Enables printing of **overhangs and tall slender features** without support material. |
| **Layer Stacking** | Each layer can support the weight of subsequent layers due to high zero-shear viscosity. |

A Newtonian fluid has **no yield stress** — once deposited, it would continue to spread, ruining dimensional accuracy. Hence, yield-stress behavior is essential for DIW.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

#### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) process** in detail. Discuss the **role of each subsystem** (material delivery, liquefier, motion system) and describe **how the layer bonding mechanism** affects the mechanical strength of the printed part. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A commercial FDM printer uses a filament of diameter **1.75 mm**, fed at **2.5 mm/s**, through a nozzle of diameter **0.4 mm**. The polymer melt is assumed incompressible. Calculate: **(i)** the volumetric flow rate of the filament, **(ii)** the extrusion velocity at the nozzle, and **(iii)** the bead width deposited if the bead height equals the layer thickness of **0.2 mm**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The FDM process (as detailed in Section 2.1) involves three subsystems:

1. **Material Delivery Subsystem:** The pinch-roller mechanism (driven by a stepper motor with a toothed drive gear pressing against an idler bearing) grips the filament and pushes it into the liquefier at a precise feed rate. An "anti-ooze" retract mechanism moves the filament backward at the end of each path to prevent stringing.

2. **Liquefier (Hot-End) Subsystem:** Contains a **heater cartridge** (resistive heating element, ~40 W), a **thermistor** for closed-loop temperature control, and a **brass nozzle** with a Teflon-lined throat (for low-friction guidance) in the case of PTFE-tube hot-ends, or all-metal for high-temperature polymers like PEEK. The filament melts in a **melt zone** of length ~10 mm and is pressurized by the incoming solid filament acting as a piston.

3. **Motion Subsystem:** Typically a **Cartesian** X-Y-Z gantry (CoreXY or bed-slinger). The X-Y axes move the nozzle (or bed) to trace the toolpath; the Z-axis steps the build platform down by `t_layer` after each layer.

**Layer Bonding Mechanism & Strength:**

- **Intra-layer bonding:** Adjacent beads in the same layer fuse by **surface diffusion and polymer chain entanglement** at the interface, forming a "neck." Bond strength is enhanced by higher nozzle temperature (better chain mobility) and lower print speed (more contact time).
- **Inter-layer bonding:** Successive layers bond via **autohesion** — the molten polymer of the new layer partially re-melts the surface of the previous layer. The bond is weaker than the bulk because the **thermal history differs** (the previous layer has cooled significantly before the next arrives).
- **Result:** FDM parts are **anisotropic** — tensile strength along layer interfaces (Z-direction) is typically **40 – 80 %** of the in-plane strength. Build orientation (flat vs upright) must be chosen based on the dominant load direction.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $d_f = 1.75$ mm
- $v_f = 2.5$ mm/s
- $d_n = 0.4$ mm
- $t_L = 0.2$ mm (bead height)

**(i) Volumetric flow rate of filament:**

$$Q_f = \frac{\pi d_f^{2}}{4} \cdot v_f = \frac{\pi \times (1.75)^{2}}{4} \times 2.5$$

$$Q_f = \frac{\pi \times 3.0625}{4} \times 2.5 = \frac{9.6211}{4} \times 2.5$$

$$Q_f = 2.4053 \times 2.5 = 6.013 \text{ mm}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**(ii) Nozzle exit (extrusion) velocity:**

By conservation of mass (incompressible melt):

$$v_e = \left(\frac{d_f}{d_n}\right)^{2} \cdot v_f = \left(\frac{1.75}{0.4}\right)^{2} \times 2.5$$

$$v_e = (4.375)^{2} \times 2.5 = 19.1406 \times 2.5 = 47.85 \text{ mm}/\text{s}$$

**(iii) Bead width:**

The volumetric flow rate also equals the volume of the deposited bead per unit time:

$$Q = w_b \cdot t_L \cdot v_p$$

If we assume the bead is deposited at the same speed as the print head speed $v_p \approx v_f \cdot$ (geometric conversion factor — taking the nozzle exit velocity to nozzle path ratio; assuming direct deposition where the print head speed equals $v_e$ for the bead):

Using mass conservation in the simplest form: the bead cross-section is $A_b = w_b \cdot t_L$.

Since $Q = A_b \cdot v_p$, and with $v_p \approx v_e = 47.85$ mm/s:

$$w_b = \frac{Q}{t_L \cdot v_p} = \frac{6.013}{0.2 \times 47.85}$$

$$w_b = \frac{6.013}{9.570} = 0.628 \text{ mm}$$

*(Alternative empirical result: $w_b \approx 1.5 \cdot d_n = 1.5 \times 0.4 = 0.6$ mm — consistent!)*

---

#### **Question B** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]

**(a)** With a block diagram, explain the **Direct Ink Writing (DIW) process**. Discuss the role of **rheology** (yield stress, shear-thinning) in determining printability, and list **three distinct material classes** processed by DIW with one example each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ceramic slurry ink used in DIW has a **Herschel-Bulkley** rheology with yield stress $\tau_y = 80$ Pa, consistency index $K = 25$ Pa·sⁿ, and flow index $n = 0.35$. It is extruded through a cylindrical nozzle of radius $R = 0.25$ mm and length $L = 10$ mm. If the ink is to be deposited at a volumetric flow rate $Q = 0.5$ mm³/s, calculate: **(i)** the **critical shear rate** $\dot{\gamma}_c$ for yielding, and **(ii)** the **pressure drop** $\Delta P$ required across the nozzle (assume the ink behaves as a Newtonian fluid with effective viscosity equal to its apparent viscosity at the wall shear rate $\dot{\gamma}_w$). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

*(Refer to Section 2.3 and the DIW flowchart in Section 4.3 for the block diagram description.)*

**Process Description:**
DIW uses a computer-controlled positioning system (X-Y-Z stages) to move a nozzle through which a viscous ink is extruded under pressure. The ink is housed in a syringe or cartridge and pressurized by a pneumatic, piston, or screw-driven system. After deposition, the ink solidifies via thermal drying, UV crosslinking, solvent evaporation, or a combination — forming a "green body" that may require subsequent sintering, debinding, or curing.

**Role of Rheology:**

- **Yield Stress ($\tau_y$):** Below $\tau_y$, the ink behaves as a solid — it does **not flow under its own weight**, so deposited filaments retain their shape (no slumping). Above $\tau_y$, the ink flows. The applied extrusion pressure must exceed the yield stress to initiate flow.
