- **Shear-Thinning Behavior:** Apparent viscosity decreases with increasing shear rate (typical $n < 1$ in Herschel-Bulkley). This allows the ink to **flow easily through the narrow nozzle** at high shear rates (during extrusion) yet **regain high viscosity at rest** (after deposition), enabling shape retention and layer stacking.

**Printability criteria** are often summarized in dimensionless numbers like $\Gamma = \tau_y / (\rho g h)$ (gravitational yielding number) — must exceed a critical value (typically > 1) for self-supporting filaments.

**Three material classes with examples:**

| # | Material Class | Example |
|---|----------------|---------|
| 1 | **Technical Ceramics** | Alumina ($Al_2O_3$), Zirconia ($ZrO_2$), Hydroxyapatite |
| 2 | **Metals (as inks/pastes)** | Silver nanoparticle ink, Copper paste, Tungsten heavy alloy |
| 3 | **Biomaterials / Hydrogels** | Gelatin methacryloyl (GelMA), Alginate, Collagen, Living cell bio-inks |

*(Other acceptable: Polymer composites, cements, food pastes, pharmaceuticals.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $\tau_y = 80$ Pa
- $K = 25$ Pa·sⁿ
- $n = 0.35$
- $R = 0.25$ mm $= 0.25 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $L = 10$ mm $= 10 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $Q = 0.5$ mm³/s $= 0.5 \times 10^{-9}$ m³/s

**(i) Critical Shear Rate:**

$$\dot{\gamma}_c = \left(\frac{\tau_y}{K}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\frac{80}{25}\right)^{1/0.35}$$

$$\dot{\gamma}_c = (3.2)^{2.857}$$

Compute: $\ln(3.2) = 1.1632$, so $1.1632 \times 2.857 = 3.323$

$$\dot{\gamma}_c = e^{3.323} \approx 27.74 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Pressure Drop:**

**Step 1 — Compute the wall shear rate:**

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{4 Q}{\pi R^{3}} = \frac{4 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-9}}{\pi \times (0.25 \times 10^{-3})^{3}}$$

Denominator: $\pi \times (0.25)^{3} \times 10^{-9} = \pi \times 0.015625 \times 10^{-9} = 4.909 \times 10^{-11}$ m³

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{2 \times 10^{-9}}{4.909 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 40.74 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the apparent viscosity** (at wall shear rate, treating Herschel-Bulkley as the governing relation):

The Herschel-Bulkley equation gives the **wall shear stress** at $\dot{\gamma}_w$:

$$\tau_w = \tau_y + K \dot{\gamma}_w^{n} = 80 + 25 \times (40.74)^{0.35}$$

Compute $(40.74)^{0.35}$: $\ln(40.74) = 3.707$, $0.35 \times 3.707 = 1.297$, so $(40.74)^{0.35} = e^{1.297} \approx 3.659$.

$$\tau_w = 80 + 25 \times 3.659 = 80 + 91.47 = 171.47 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute apparent viscosity:**

$$\eta_{\text{app}} = \frac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_w} = \frac{171.47}{40.74} \approx 4.21 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute pressure drop** (Hagen-Poiseuille with effective viscosity):

$$\Delta P = \frac{8 \eta_{\text{app}} L Q}{\pi R^{4}}$$

$R^{4} = (0.25 \times 10^{-3})^{4} = 3.906 \times 10^{-15}$ m⁴

$$\Delta P = \frac{8 \times 4.21 \times (10 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.5 \times 10^{-9})}{\pi \times 3.906 \times 10^{-15}}$$

Numerator: $8 \times 4.21 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-9} = 1.684 \times 10^{-10}$

Denominator: $\pi \times 3.906 \times 10^{-15} = 1.227 \times 10^{-14}$

$$\Delta P = \frac{1.684 \times 10^{-10}}{1.227 \times 10^{-14}} \approx 13{,}725 \text{ Pa} \approx 13.7 \text{ kPa}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Most students lose 1–2 marks by mixing mm and m in the Hagen-Poiseuille calculation. Convert **all dimensions to SI (m, s, Pa)** before plugging in.
> 2. **Forgetting the Yield Stress:** In Herschel-Bulkley problems, students often compute only the viscous term $K\dot{\gamma}^{n}$ and forget $\tau_y$. The yield stress is what makes DIW inks **printable** — don't omit it.
> 3. **Confusing Flow Index $n$:** For **shear-thinning inks**, $n < 1$. For shear-thickening, $n > 1$. Most DIW inks are shear-thinning.
> 4. **FDM Bead Width:** Use the conservation-of-volume equation, not the empirical $1.5 d_n$ rule, when the question asks for a calculated value.
> 5. **Anisotropy:** Always mention that FDM parts are **anisotropic** (Z-direction is the weakest) and recommend build orientation strategies.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — FDM & DIW**

### FDM — Key Takeaways:
- FDM = **Material Extrusion + Thermal Phase Change** (Thermoplastic filament → melt → deposition)
- Invented by **S. Scott Crump (1989)**, commercialized by **Stratasys**; open-source variant = **FFF**
- **Three subsystems:** Feeder, Liquefier, Motion
- **Mass conservation:** $d_f^{2} \cdot v_f = d_n^{2} \cdot v_e$ (incompressible melt)
- **Hagen-Poiseuille for melt flow:** $\Delta P = 8 \mu L Q / (\pi R^{4})$
- **Bead width rule of thumb:** $w_b \approx 1.5 \, d_n$
- **Critical process parameters:** $T_n$ (180–300 °C), $t_L$ (0.05–0.4 mm), $\rho_i$ (5–100 %), raster angle, build orientation
- **FDM parts are ANISOTROPIC** — inter-layer bond strength is only 40–80 % of bulk (always quote this in exams)
- **Common materials:** PLA, ABS, PETG, TPU, Nylon, PC, PEEK (high-temp)
- **Typical applications:** Prototypes, jigs/fixtures, prosthetics, low-volume end-use parts

### DIW — Key Takeaways:
- DIW = **Material Extrusion + Rheological Control** (Viscous ink at room temp or slightly warmed)
- Also called **Robocasting / Micro-Robotic Deposition**
- **Yield stress** $\tau_y$ is the **defining rheological property** — enables shape retention
- **Governing rheology models:** Bingham ($\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$), Herschel-Bulkley ($\tau = \tau_y + K\dot{\gamma}^{n}$)
- **Critical shear rate:** $\dot{\gamma}_c = (\tau_y / K)^{1/n}$
- **Material diversity:** Ceramics, metals (as pastes), hydrogels, bio-inks, polymers, composites, cements
- **Pressure sensitivity:** $\Delta P \propto R^{-4}$ — sub-100 µm features require very high pressure
- **Post-processing is often essential:** Sintering (for ceramics → density > 95 %), debinding (for metals), crosslinking (for polymers)
- **Key applications:** Solid-state batteries, bioprinting (tissues/organs), printed electronics, technical ceramics, solar cells

### Equations to Memorize (KTU Minimum):
$$\underbrace{\frac{\pi d_f^{2}}{4} v_f = \frac{\pi d_n^{2}}{4} v_e}_{\text{FDM mass conservation}} \quad \underbrace{\Delta P = \frac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi R^{4}}}_{\text{Hagen-Poiseuille}} \quad \underbrace{\dot{\gamma}_c = \left(\frac{\tau_y}{K}\right)^{1/n}}_{\text{Yielding onset}}$$

### One-Liners for Last-Minute Revision:
- *FDM melts and extrudes; DIW flows and sets.*
- *FDM's weakness is weld lines; DIW's weakness is post-processing.*
- *FDM loves thermoplastics; DIW loves everything else.*
- *Anisotropy is the FDM curse; rheology is the DIW compass.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Material Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Additive Manufacturing (AM)**, **Material Properties** refer to the engineered characteristics (mechanical, thermal, physical, chemical, and surface) of a part produced through layer-by-layer deposition of material, as opposed to subtractive or formative manufacturing routes. These properties are intrinsically linked to the **process parameters** (laser power, scan speed, layer thickness, hatch spacing, build orientation, etc.), the **feedstock form** (powder, wire, resin, filament, sheet), and the resulting **microstructural evolution** (grain morphology, porosity distribution, residual stresses, phase composition).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEPET752 Module 2):**
> Material properties in AM describe the bulk and surface behaviour of parts whose internal architecture is governed by directional heat flow, cyclic thermal histories, and layer-wise bonding — making them fundamentally different from conventionally manufactured parts.

### 1.2 Core Property Categories in Additive Manufacturing

The properties of AM-built parts can be classified into **five primary domains**:

| Domain | Example Properties |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Tensile strength, yield strength, Young's modulus, fatigue life, hardness, impact toughness |
| **Physical** | Density, porosity, electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity |
| **Surface** | Roughness (Ra, Rz), wettability, reflectivity |
| **Microstructural** | Grain size, phase composition, crystallographic texture |
| **Chemical** | Corrosion resistance, oxidation behaviour, biocompatibility |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Layered Cake vs. the Carved Statue**
>
> Imagine two chefs asked to sculpt a swan:
> - **Chef A (Subtractive / Conventional)** carves it from a single block of marble. The grains are continuous, dense, and uniform in every direction.
> - **Chef B (Additive Manufacturing)** builds it layer by layer using a piping bag, with each layer fused (welded/bonded) to the previous one.
>
> If you pull Chef B's swan along the layer direction (parallel to the piping), it is strong — like peeling pages off a book lengthwise. If you pull it perpendicular to the layers (trying to "delaminate" the cake), it is weaker — like the book splitting at its glued spine.
>
> **This directional dependence of strength is called ANISOTROPY** — the signature property behaviour of AM parts.

### 1.4 Why Material Properties are Unique in AM

Unlike conventional manufacturing where the entire bulk is subjected to a single thermal/mechanical history, AM parts undergo:

1. **Cyclic localized thermal input** → repeated heating/cooling cycles
2. **Directional solidification** → grains grow along the build direction or scan direction
3. **Layer-wise bonding** → metallurgical (or chemical) interfaces between layers
4. **Trapped gas/shrinkage voids** → inherent porosity
5. **Residual thermal stresses** → due to steep thermal gradients ($\nabla T$)

These factors give AM parts a **process–structure–property triad** that is far more sensitive than in conventional manufacturing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Process–Structure–Property Triad

The material properties of an AM part are not independent of the manufacturing process — they emerge from a strict causal chain:

**Process Parameters** → **Thermal History & Solidification** → **Microstructure & Defects** → **Bulk/Surface Properties**

#### 2.1.1 Process Parameters That Dominate Property Formation

- **Laser Power ($P$)** — in W
- **Scan Speed ($v$)** — in mm/s
- **Hatch Spacing ($h$)** — distance between adjacent scan tracks in mm
- **Layer Thickness ($t$)** — in mm
- **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** — energy delivered per unit volume of powder

#### 2.1.2 Microstructural Outcomes

- **Equiaxed vs. columnar grains** (controlled by thermal gradient $G$ and solidification rate $R$)
- **Porosity** (lack-of-fusion voids and gas-entrapped pores)
- **Residual stress fields**
- **Texture / crystallographic preferred orientation**

### 2.2 Anisotropy in AM Parts

**Anisotropy** is the variation of a material property with direction. In AM, anisotropy arises because:

- Build layers act as 2D planes of bonding.
- Heat flows preferentially along the build direction (downward to the substrate) or along scan tracks.
- Grain morphology is elongated along the maximum thermal gradient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Favourite:**
> *"Explain anisotropy in AM parts."* (Asked frequently — 7 marks standard.)

**Mechanical anisotropy ratio:**
$$
A_{m} = \frac{X_{\parallel}}{X_{\perp}}
$$
where $X_{\parallel}$ and $X_{\perp}$ are the property values parallel and perpendicular to the build direction respectively. Ideally $A_{m} = 1$ for an isotropic material.

### 2.3 Porosity and Its Quantification

Porosity ($\phi$) is the ratio of void volume to the bulk apparent volume:

$$
\phi = \frac{V_{void}}{V_{bulk}} = 1 - \frac{\rho_{apparent}}{\rho_{theoretical}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why porosity matters:** Porosity is the single largest detractor from mechanical strength in AM parts. Even **1–2% porosity** can reduce fatigue life by an order of magnitude.

**Two principal types of porosity in AM:**

1. **Lack-of-fusion porosity** — irregular, sharp-edged voids from insufficient energy input → governed by VED.
2. **Gas-entrapped porosity** — spherical voids from trapped shielding gas or vaporization → governed by powder quality and chamber atmosphere.

### 2.4 Volumetric Energy Density (VED) — The Master Control Parameter

$$
VED = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad \left[ \text{J/mm}^{3} \right]
$$

where:
- $P$ = Laser power (W)
- $v$ = Scan speed (mm/s)
- $h$ = Hatch spacing (mm)
- $t$ = Layer thickness (mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU:**
> - Low VED → lack-of-fusion porosity ↑ → density ↓ → strength ↓
> - Optimal VED → near-full density (>99.5%) → peak strength
> - Excessively high VED → keyholing, vaporization, balling → strength ↓

### 2.5 Residual Stress in AM

Residual stress ($\sigma_{res}$) arises due to non-uniform thermal contraction as the melt pool rapidly cools and re-heats adjacent layers. A simplified thermal-gradient-based estimate is:

$$
\sigma_{res} \approx E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot f(\text{geometry})
$$

where:
- $E$ = Young's modulus
- $\alpha$ = Coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE)
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature difference between melt pool and substrate

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $VED = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | Volumetric Energy Density | $\text{J/mm}^{3}$ |
| 2 | $\phi = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{apparent}}{\rho_{theoretical}}$ | Porosity | dimensionless |
| 3 | $A_{m} = \dfrac{X_{\parallel}}{X_{\perp}}$ | Mechanical Anisotropy Ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\sigma_{res} \approx E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Residual Stress Estimate | MPa |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{\rho_{AM}}{\rho_{wrought}}$ | Relative Density | dimensionless |
| 6 | $Ra$ (arith.), $Rz$ (peak-to-valley) | Surface Roughness | $\mu m$ |
| 7 | $R_{a,side} \approx t \cdot \cot(\theta/2)$ | Side surface roughness (staircase effect) | $\mu m$ |
| 8 | $G/R$ ratio | Thermal gradient / solidification rate → grain morphology | K·s/mm² |
| 9 | $\sigma_{UTS,AM} = \sigma_{0} (1 - a \phi)$ | Strength vs. porosity (empirical) | MPa |

### 2.7 Engineering Utility

The property behaviour discussed above is critical in:

- **Aerospace** — qualification of L-PBF Ti-6Al-4V turbine blades requires anisotropy testing in three orthogonal directions.
- **Medical implants** — fatigue strength of lattice-structured hip stems depends on porosity and surface roughness.
- **Automotive** — weight-critical bionic brackets exploit property gradients achievable only via AM.
- **Tooling** — conformal cooling channels (impossible by CNC) require mechanical reliability under thermal cycling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Volumetric Energy Density (VED) Expression

**Starting concept:** VED is defined as the laser energy delivered per unit volume of powder melted.

**Step 1:** Power delivered per unit length of scan (linear energy density, LED):

$$
LED = \frac{P}{v} \quad \left[ \text{J/mm} \right]
$$

This is the energy input per millimetre of track length.

**Step 2:** The cross-sectional area of one scan track approximated as a rectangle of width $h$ (hatch spacing) and depth $t$ (layer thickness):

$$
A_{track} = h \cdot t \quad \left[ \text{mm}^{2} \right]
$$

**Step 3:** Volume melted per unit length of travel:

$$
V_{per\,length} = A_{track} = h \cdot t \quad \left[ \text{mm}^{3} / \text{mm} \right]
$$

**Step 4:** Volume energy density (energy per unit volume):

$$
VED = \frac{LED}{A_{track}} = \frac{P / v}{h \cdot t}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\, VED = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad [\text{J/mm}^{3}] \,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Doubling the layer thickness while keeping $P$, $v$, and $h$ constant halves the VED — making **layer thickness the most leveraged parameter** for energy control.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Porosity–Density Relationship

**Starting concept:** Density is mass per unit apparent volume (including voids).

**Step 1:** Let:
- $\rho_{th}$ = theoretical (fully dense) density
- $\rho_{app}$ = apparent density measured (e.g., by Archimedes)
- $V_{bulk}$ = total geometric volume
- $V_{solid}$ = volume occupied by material
- $V_{void}$ = volume of pores

$$
V_{solid} + V_{void} = V_{bulk}
$$

**Step 2:** Mass conservation:

$$
m = \rho_{th} \cdot V_{solid} = \rho_{app} \cdot V_{bulk}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $V_{solid}/V_{bulk}$:

$$
\frac{V_{solid}}{V_{bulk}} = \frac{\rho_{app}}{\rho_{th}}
$$

**Step 4:** By definition, porosity is the void fraction:

$$
\phi = \frac{V_{void}}{V_{bulk}} = 1 - \frac{V_{solid}}{V_{bulk}}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\, \phi = 1 - \frac{\rho_{app}}{\rho_{th}} \,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Staircase Effect Surface Roughness

When a feature is built at an inclined angle $\theta$ from the horizontal, the side surface exhibits a staircase pattern. The peak-to-valley height of one step equals the layer thickness $t$ projected onto the surface normal.

**Step 1:** The vertical step height is the layer thickness $t$.

**Step 2:** The horizontal run of one step depends on the inclination angle. For an inclined surface of angle $\theta$ from horizontal, the horizontal run for one layer is:

$$
L_{run} = \frac{t}{\tan \theta}
$$

**Step 3:** The peak-to-valley roughness measured normal to the surface is:

$$
Rz = \frac{t}{\sin \theta}
$$

**Step 4:** For typical KTU analysis, an approximate arithmetic roughness:

$$
\boxed{\, R_{a,\,stair} \approx t \cdot \cot(\theta/2) \quad [\mu m] \,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU application:** This is why AM surfaces are **always rougher on inclined/sloped surfaces** than on top/bottom faces. For $\theta = 90°$ (vertical wall), the staircase effect vanishes and roughness is governed by melt-pool boundary instabilities instead.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Process Parameters on Material Properties

| Property | ↑ Laser Power ($P$) | ↑ Scan Speed ($v$) | ↑ Hatch Spacing ($h$) | ↑ Layer Thickness ($t$) | ↑ Build Orientation ($\theta$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Density** | ↑ then ↓ (optimum) | ↓ at high $v$ | ↓ | ↓ | minor |
| **Tensile Strength (UTS)** | ↑ then ↓ | ↑ then ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ with vertical orientation (anisotropy) |
| **Surface Roughness (Ra)** | slight ↑ | ↑ (balling) | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ sharply at low $\theta$ |
| **Residual Stress** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | orientation-dependent |
| **Hardness** | non-monotonic | non-monotonic | ↓ | ↓ | depends on grain orientation |
| **Porosity** | ↓ then ↑ | ↑ sharply | ↑ | ↑ | minor |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU tip:** When asked *"How does scan speed affect material properties?"* — always answer in **three regimes**: (i) too low → over-melting, (ii) optimal → peak properties, (iii) too high → lack-of-fusion porosity.

---

### 3.5 Property Comparison: AM vs. Conventional Manufacturing (Ti-6Al-4V)

| Property | Wrought (forged/rolled) | L-PBF (AM, optimized) | L-PBF (AM, as-built) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density ($\rho$) | 4.43 g/cm³ | 4.40–4.43 | 4.20–4.35 |
| UTS | 950–1100 MPa | 950–1050 MPa | 800–950 MPa |
| Yield Strength (0.2%) | 880–920 MPa | 850–950 MPa | 700–850 MPa |
| Elongation | 10–15% | 8–14% | 2–8% |
| Surface Ra | 0.4–1.6 µm (machined) | 5–20 µm (as-built) | 10–40 µm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** AM parts in their **as-built** state are NOT drop-in replacements for wrought parts. Post-processing (HIP, machining, surface finishing) is mandatory for structural applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Process–Structure–Property Causal Chain in AM

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart LR
    A["Process Parameters"] --> B["Thermal History"]
    A --> C["Scan Strategy"]
    A --> D["Feedstock Form"]
    B --> E["Microstructure"]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F["Porosity and Defects"]
    E --> G["Grain Morphology"]
    E --> H["Residual Stress"]
    F --> I["Bulk Mechanical Properties"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    F --> J["Surface Properties"]
    G --> J
    H --> J

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Classification of Material Properties in AM

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart TB
    MP["Material Properties in AM"]
    MP --> M1["Mechanical"]
    MP --> M2["Physical"]
    MP --> M3["Surface"]
    MP --> M4["Microstructural"]
    MP --> M5["Chemical"]
    M1 --> M1a["Tensile Strength"]
    M1 --> M1b["Fatigue Life"]
    M1 --> M1c["Hardness"]
    M1 --> M1d["Impact Toughness"]
    M2 --> M2a["Density"]
    M2 --> M2b["Thermal Conductivity"]
    M2 --> M2c["Electrical Conductivity"]
    M3 --> M3a["Surface Roughness Ra"]
    M3 --> M3b["Wettability"]
    M3 --> M3c["Reflectivity"]
    M4 --> M4a["Grain Size"]
    M4 --> M4b["Phase Composition"]
    M4 --> M4c["Crystallographic Texture"]
    M5 --> M5a["Corrosion Resistance"]
    M5 --> M5b["Oxidation Behaviour"]
    M5 --> M5c["Biocompatibility"]

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Anisotropy in AM Build Direction

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart TB
    BD["Build Direction Z"]
    BD --> BD1["Layer 1"]
    BD --> BD2["Layer 2"]
    BD --> BD3["Layer N"]
    BD1 --> IF1["Inter-layer Interface"]
    BD2 --> IF2["Inter-layer Interface"]
    IF1 --> PR["Property Parallel to Layers: Higher Strength"]
    IF2 --> PN["Property Perpendicular to Layers: Lower Strength"]
    PR --> AR["Anisotropy Ratio Am Greater Than 1"]
    PN --> AR

### 4.4 Architecture: Porosity Formation Pathway in AM

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart LR
    P["Powder Bed or Melt Pool"] --> Q1["Low VED"]
    P --> Q2["Optimal VED"]
    P --> Q3["Excessive VED"]
    Q1 --> R1["Lack of Fusion Voids"]
    Q1 --> R2["Unmelted Particles"]
    Q2 --> R3["Near Full Density Above 99.5 percent"]
    Q3 --> R4["Keyholing Porosity"]
    Q3 --> R5["Vaporization Gas Pores"]
    R1 --> S["Reduced Mechanical Properties"]
    R2 --> S
    R4 --> S
    R5 --> S

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently test the **causal link between VED regimes and defect types**. Memorize the three regimes and their corresponding porosity outcomes.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Define **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** and state its SI-compatible unit in AM.

**Model Answer:**
VED is the amount of laser (or beam) energy delivered per unit volume of powder during the AM process. It is expressed as:

$$
VED = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad [\text{J/mm}^{3}]
$$

It governs the melt-pool size, density, and defect formation in powder-bed AM processes.

*(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 formula + 1 unit/role)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Distinguish between **lack-of-fusion porosity** and **gas-entrapped porosity** in AM.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Lack-of-Fusion Porosity | Gas-Entrapped Porosity |
|---|---|---|
| Shape | Irregular, sharp-edged | Spherical |
| Cause | Insufficient energy input | Trapped shielding gas / vaporization |
| Control | Increase VED | Improve powder quality, degassing |
| Location | Between layers/tracks | Random in melt pool |

*(3 marks: 2 differences across 2 attributes)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **process–structure–property relationship** in Laser Powder Bed Fusion (L-PBF) of metals. Discuss how the four primary process parameters influence the microstructure and resulting mechanical properties. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A Ti-6Al-4V part is being built using L-PBF with the following parameters:
- Laser power $P = 200$ W
- Scan speed $v = 800$ mm/s
- Hatch spacing $h = 0.10$ mm
- Layer thickness $t = 0.03$ mm

Calculate the **Volumetric Energy Density** and comment on whether it lies in the typical optimum range for Ti-6Al-4V (typical optimum: 60–120 J/mm³). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Process–structure–property chain in L-PBF:

1. **Process parameters** ($P$, $v$, $h$, $t$, scan strategy) determine the **melt pool geometry** and the **volumetric energy density**.
2. **Thermal history** — rapid heating (~10⁶ K/s) and cooling creates steep thermal gradients $G$ and high solidification rates $R$.
3. **Microstructure outcomes:**
   - Columnar prior-β grains aligned with build direction (high $G/R$).
   - Fine α' martensite or α+β Widmanstätten laths upon cooling.
   - Lack-of-fusion or keyhole pores depending on VED.
4. **Mechanical properties:**
   - High yield strength (grain refinement + martensite).
   - Reduced ductility and anisotropy due to texture.
   - Surface roughness 5–20 µm on as-built sidewalls.
5. The four key parameters control melt-pool overlap; low VED → unmelted regions → pores; high VED → vaporization → keyhole pores.

*(7 marks — distribute across 5 well-illustrated points.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
$P = 200$ W, $v = 800$ mm/s, $h = 0.10$ mm, $t = 0.03$ mm.

**Step 1:** Apply the VED formula:

$$
VED = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$
VED = \frac{200}{800 \times 0.10 \times 0.03}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute denominator:

$$
800 \times 0.10 \times 0.03 = 800 \times 0.003 = 2.4
$$

**Step 4:** Final VED:

$$
VED = \frac{200}{2.4} = 83.33 \;\; \text{J/mm}^{3}
$$

**Step 5:** Compare with the typical optimum (60–120 J/mm³):

Since $83.33 \in [60, 120]$ J/mm³, the VED **lies within the typical optimum range** for Ti-6Al-4V L-PBF.

**Comment:** At this VED, dense parts (>99.5%) with refined microstructure can be expected, provided the scan strategy ensures adequate overlap and the powder is properly dried.

*(7 marks: 2 formula + 2 substitution + 2 computation + 1 interpretation)*

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Define **anisotropy** in AM parts. With a neat sketch, describe how build orientation affects the tensile strength of an L-PBF metal part. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An AM stainless-steel (316L) coupon has an apparent density of 7.85 g/cm³ as measured by the Archimedes method, while its theoretical density is 7.98 g/cm³. Calculate the **porosity** and comment on the expected effect on fatigue life. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** **Anisotropy in AM parts:**

**Definition:** Anisotropy is the directional dependence of material properties — i.e., the property value varies with the direction of measurement relative to the build orientation.

**Mechanism:** Layer-wise bonding in AM creates weak inter-layer interfaces where lack-of-fusion defects concentrate. Grains grow preferentially along the build direction, producing a crystallographic texture.

**Effect on tensile strength:**

- Specimens loaded **perpendicular to build direction (vertical, Z-oriented)**: cracks must traverse many inter-layer interfaces → lower UTS.
- Specimens loaded **parallel to build direction (horizontal, XY-oriented)**: fracture path lies within a single layer → higher UTS.

The anisotropy ratio for Ti-6Al-4V L-PBF is typically $A_m \approx 1.05$–$1.15$.

**Sketch (text description):**

> Horizontal specimens (build layers parallel to load axis): high UTS.
> Vertical specimens (load normal to layer planes): lower UTS, fracture follows inter-layer boundaries.

*(7 marks: 2 definition + 3 mechanism + 2 diagram/description)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
$\rho_{app} = 7.85$ g/cm³, $\rho_{th} = 7.98$ g/cm³.

**Step 1:** Apply the porosity formula:

$$
\phi = 1 - \frac{\rho_{app}}{\rho_{th}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$
\phi = 1 - \frac{7.85}{7.98}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute ratio:

$$
\frac{7.85}{7.98} = 0.9837
$$

**Step 4:** Final porosity:

$$
\phi = 1 - 0.9837 = 0.0163 = 1.63\%
$$

**Step 5:** **Comment on fatigue life:**
Even a porosity of $\sim 1.6\%$ acts as a stress concentrator and crack-initiation site. For 316L, fatigue life can drop by **40–70%** compared to fully dense wrought material. **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)** is recommended to close pores and restore fatigue performance.

*(7 marks: 2 formula + 2 substitution + 1 numerical + 2 interpretation)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in "Material Properties" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing VED with linear energy density (LED)** — VED has units of J/mm³, LED has J/mm. Many students write the wrong formula.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — scan speed must be in mm/s, hatch in mm, layer thickness in mm for VED to come out in J/mm³.
> 3. **Stating anisotropy exists but not explaining WHY** — examiners expect mechanism (layer-wise bonding, grain texture).
> 4. **Missing the difference between lack-of-fusion and gas porosity** — both reduce density but their morphology and remediation are different.
> 5. **Comparing AM to wrought without specifying post-processing** — as-built AM is NOT equivalent to wrought.
> 6. **Not commenting on numerical answers** — a numerical question without an interpretive sentence loses 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **VED = P / (v·h·t)** is the master parameter; remember the unit (J/mm³) and the formula.
- **Porosity comes in two flavours**: irregular (lack-of-fusion) and spherical (gas-entrapped).
- **Anisotropy** arises from layer-wise bonding and directional grain growth; $A_m = X_\parallel / X_\perp$.
- **Surface roughness** depends on layer thickness and inclined-surface angle: $R_a \approx t \cdot \cot(\theta/2)$.
- **Residual stress** scales with $E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$; mitigated by preheating and scan strategy.
- **Mechanical strength** scales empirically as $\sigma_{UTS} = \sigma_0 (1 - a\phi)$ — porosity is the killer.
- **Process–Structure–Property triad** is the central theme of this module — always link process to microstructure to properties.
- **As-built ≠ wrought**: post-processing (HIP, machining, heat treatment, surface finishing) is mandatory for structural use.
- **Build orientation** strongly affects UTS, fatigue, surface roughness, and dimensional accuracy.
- **AM-specific property tests** include 3-directional tensile testing (XY, XZ, ZX) for anisotropy characterization.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Capability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Capability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Capability (Pc)** is a quantitative statistical measure of the inherent ability of a manufacturing process to produce output within specified tolerance limits, expressed as the ratio of the specification spread (tolerance width) to the natural process spread (six standard deviations, $6\sigma$).

In the context of **Additive Manufacturing (AM)**, Process Capability evaluates whether an AM system (e.g., FDM, SLA, SLS, DMLS) can consistently produce parts whose critical dimensions, surface roughness, density, and mechanical properties fall within the **design specification limits (USL – Upper Specification Limit, LSL – Lower Specification Limit)** as defined by the customer or the engineering drawing.

Mathematically, the simplest capability index is:

$$C_p = \frac{\text{Tolerance Width}}{\text{Process Spread}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

where $\sigma$ is the **standard deviation** of the process output.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Archer and the Target:**
> Imagine an archer shooting arrows at a circular target. The **target rings** represent the **specification limits (USL/LSL)**. The **arrows hitting the target** represent the manufactured parts. Now consider two archers:
>
> 1. **Archer A**: All arrows cluster tightly together in the center → high precision, low variation → **High Process Capability ($C_p$ large)**.
> 2. **Archer B**: Arrows scatter wildly across the field → low precision, high variation → **Low Process Capability ($C_p$ small)**.
>
> In **Additive Manufacturing**, the "archer" is the AM machine (e.g., a 3D printer), the "arrows" are the printed layers, and the "target" is the design tolerance. Process capability tells the engineer **"How well does the printer hit the target every time?"**

## 1.3 Why Process Capability Matters in AM

| AM-Specific Concern | Why Capability Matters |
|---|---|
| **Layer-by-layer variation** | Staircase effect, over-/under-extrusion create dimensional drift. |
| **Thermal distortion** | Residual stresses shift dimensions from nominal. |
| **Anisotropic properties** | Strength varies with build orientation → mechanical capability differs by direction. |
| **Porosity & density** | Affects fatigue life; statistical distribution of voids must be characterized. |
| **Post-processing variability** | Support removal, heat treatment, surface finishing introduce additional spread. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** AM processes are inherently more variable than CNC machining due to **~30–50 process parameters** (laser power, scan speed, hatch spacing, layer thickness, chamber temperature, etc.). Hence, process capability studies are **mandatory** before series production qualification.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Bold Definitions)

- **USL (Upper Specification Limit):** The maximum acceptable value of a quality characteristic.
- **LSL (Lower Specification Limit):** The minimum acceptable value of a quality characteristic.
- **Mean ($\mu$):** The central tendency (average) of the process output distribution.
- **Standard Deviation ($\sigma$):** A measure of process spread or variability.
- **Natural Process Limits (NPL):** The range $\mu \pm 3\sigma$, encompassing **99.73%** of output under a normal distribution.
- **Tolerance (T):** The total allowable variation, $T = USL - LSL$.
- **Pp / Ppk:** Preliminary process capability (short-term, preliminary study) — *used in KTU numerical problems*.
- **Cp / Cpk:** Long-term process capability indices — *the "true" capability reported to customers*.
- **Six Sigma Level:** A process operating with $\pm 6\sigma$ within half-tolerance, yielding **3.4 DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Statistical Foundation — Normal Distribution in AM

Most AM dimensional outputs (e.g., hole diameter, wall thickness) follow (or can be transformed to follow) a **Normal (Gaussian) Distribution** due to the **Central Limit Theorem**, which states that the sum of many small independent random errors (layer errors, thermal fluctuations, scanner jitter) tends toward normality.

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$$

The probability that a part falls **within specification** is:

$$P(LSL \leq X \leq USL) = \int_{LSL}^{USL} f(x)\,dx$$

For KTU problems, the standardized form (Z-transformation) is used:

$$Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

with critical Z-values being **Z = ±3** for the natural process limits.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Capability Evaluation

1. **Identify the Critical Quality Characteristic (CQC):** e.g., outer diameter of a 3D-printed shaft.
2. **Define Specification Limits:** Obtain USL and LSL from the engineering drawing (e.g., $\phi 20 \pm 0.1$ mm → USL = 20.1, LSL = 19.9).
3. **Collect Sample Data:** Measure **n ≥ 30** samples (typically **n = 50 or 125** subgroups of 5 for full studies) under stable conditions.
4. **Verify Normality:** Use a **Normal Probability Plot** or **Anderson-Darling test** ($p \geq 0.05$ → normal).
5. **Check Process Stability:** Construct **X-bar and R control charts**; remove out-of-control points.
6. **Estimate $\mu$ and $\sigma$:** $\bar{\bar{X}}$ for mean, and $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$ or $s$ (sample standard deviation) for variability.
7. **Compute Capability Indices:** $C_p$, $C_pk$, $C_{pm}$ as needed.
8. **Interpret Results:** Compare to industry benchmarks (e.g., automotive: $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 PEPET752 — Process Capability**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{T}{6\sigma}$ | Process potential (centering ignored) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $C_{pk} = \min\left(C_{pu},\, C_{pl}\right)$ | Process capability index (centering considered) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$ | One-sided capability (upper) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$ | One-sided capability (lower) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_{pm} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T_0)^2}}$ | Taguchi capability (target = $T_0$) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | Standard deviation from average range | same as data |
| 7 | $P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s}$ | Preliminary process performance | dimensionless |
| 8 | $P_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{3s},\, \dfrac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3s}\right)$ | Preliminary capability index | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Z_{bench} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma},\, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{\sigma}\right)$ | Sigma level (Z-score benchmark) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\% \text{Yield} = P(LSL \leq X \leq USL) \times 100$ | Process yield (using Z-tables) | % |
| 11 | $\text{DPMO} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times (1 - \text{Yield})$ | Defects per million opportunities | ppm |
| 12 | $\hat{\sigma} = s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | Sample standard deviation | same as data |
| 13 | $C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k)$ | Relation where $k = \dfrac{\vert T_0 - \mu\vert}{T/2}$ | dimensionless |

> **Constants for quick reference:**
> $d_2$ (n=5) = **2.326**, $d_2$ (n=2) = **1.128**, $d_2$ (n=3) = **1.693**.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Real-World Applications

1. **Machine Qualification:** Before approving a new AM system for aerospace parts (e.g., FAA / DGCA certification), $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ is often required.
2. **Supplier Selection:** OEMs compare $C_{pk}$ across multiple AM service bureaus to select vendors.
3. **Parameter Optimization:** DOE (Design of Experiments) is used to **maximize $C_{pk}$** by tuning laser power, scan speed, and hatch spacing.
4. **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ):** Low $C_{pk}$ → high scrap → non-viable for production; high $C_{pk}$ → near-zero defects.
5. **Industry 4.0 / Digital Twin:** Real-time $C_{pk}$ dashboards monitor AM machines in smart factories.

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Index

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $C_p$ alone is not enough:** $C_p$ only measures the **breadth** of the process relative to tolerance. A process can have $C_p = 2.0$ but be shifted far from the center, producing 50% scrap. **$C_{pk}$** corrects this by penalizing off-center processes. **$C_{pm}$** further penalizes deviation from the **target value $T_0$** (Taguchi's loss function philosophy).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Probability to $C_p$

The probability that a normally distributed output $X$ falls within the natural limits $\mu - 3\sigma$ and $\mu + 3\sigma$ is:

$$P(\mu - 3\sigma \leq X \leq \mu + 3\sigma) = P(-3 \leq Z \leq 3) = 0.9973$$

**Step 1:** The process spread (natural width) is:

$$\text{Process Spread} = (\mu + 3\sigma) - (\mu - 3\sigma) = 6\sigma$$

**Step 2:** The specification width (engineering tolerance) is:

$$\text{Specification Width} = USL - LSL = T$$

**Step 3:** Capability is defined as the ratio of **tolerance to process variation**:

$$C_p = \frac{\text{Tolerance Width}}{\text{Process Spread}} = \frac{T}{6\sigma}$$

**Step 4:** If the process mean $\mu$ is **not** at the midpoint $M = \frac{USL + LSL}{2}$, the one-sided indices are:

$$C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \qquad C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$$

**Step 5:** The two-sided index that accounts for centering is the minimum of the two:

$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Substituting $C_p = \dfrac{T}{6\sigma}$ and defining the **centering ratio $k$** as:

$$k = \frac{\vert T_0 - \mu \vert}{T/2}$$

where $T_0 = M$ is the target (mid-spec), we can show:

$$C_{pu} = C_p (1 + k), \qquad C_{pl} = C_p (1 - k)$$

**Step 7:** Therefore:

$$C_{pk} = \min\bigl[C_p(1+k),\, C_p(1-k)\bigr] = C_p(1 - \vert k\vert) = C_p(1 - k) \text{ for } k \geq 0$$

This is the **master relation** $C_{pk} = C_p(1-k)$ that examiners love to test.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** In a FDM process, the tensile strength of printed specimens is measured. The specification is **45 MPa ≤ Strength ≤ 55 MPa**. A sample of 100 specimens yields: $\bar{x} = 51.2$ MPa, $s = 1.8$ MPa. Compute $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $C_{pm}$ (target $T_0 = 50$ MPa), and the expected yield. State whether the process is capable.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify parameters**

$$USL = 55, \quad LSL = 45, \quad T = 10, \quad \mu \approx 51.2, \quad \sigma \approx 1.8, \quad T_0 = 50$$

**Step 2: Compute $C_p$**

$$C_p = \frac{T}{6\sigma} = \frac{10}{6 \times 1.8} = \frac{10}{10.8} = 0.926$$

**Step 3: Compute one-sided indices**

$$C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{55 - 51.2}{3 \times 1.8} = \frac{3.8}{5.4} = 0.704$$

$$C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{51.2 - 45}{3 \times 1.8} = \frac{6.2}{5.4} = 1.148$$

**Step 4: Compute $C_{pk}$**

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.704,\, 1.148) = 0.704$$

**Step 5: Compute $C_{pm}$**

$$C_{pm} = \frac{T}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T_0)^2}} = \frac{10}{6\sqrt{1.8^2 + (51.2 - 50)^2}}$$

$$= \frac{10}{6\sqrt{3.24 + 1.44}} = \frac{10}{6\sqrt{4.68}} = \frac{10}{6 \times 2.163} = \frac{10}{12.978} = 0.771$$

**Step 6: Compute yield (using Z-tables)**

$$Z_{USL} = \frac{55 - 51.2}{1.8} = 2.111, \quad Z_{LSL} = \frac{45 - 51.2}{1.8} = -3.444$$

From standard normal tables:
$$P(Z \leq 2.111) = 0.9826, \quad P(Z \leq -3.444) = 0.000286$$

$$\text{Yield} = 0.9826 - 0.000286 = 0.9823 \quad \text{or} \quad 98.23\%$$

$$\text{DPMO} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times (1 - 0.9823) = 17{,}700$$

**Step 7: Interpretation**

Since $C_{pk} = 0.704 < 1.00$, the process is **NOT capable** of meeting specification reliably. The process is shifted **toward the USL** (note $C_{pu} < C_{pl}$). **Recommendation:** Adjust process parameters to bring the mean closer to 50 MPa and reduce variability.

## 3.3 Capability Index Interpretation Matrix

| $C_{pk}$ Value | Process Status | Sigma Level (Z) | DPMO (approx.) | Typical Industry Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C_{pk} < 1.00$ | **Incapable** | $< 3\sigma$ | > 66,807 | Unacceptable; redesign needed |
| $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ | **Marginally capable** | $3\sigma$ | 66,807 | Existing process; monitor |
| $1.33 \leq C_{pk} < 1.67$ | **Capable** | $4\sigma$ | 6,210 | Automotive / General Industry |
| $1.67 \leq C_{pk} < 2.00$ | **Highly capable** | $5\sigma$ | 233 | Aerospace / Medical |
| $C_{pk} \geq 2.00$ | **Six-Sigma capable** | $6\sigma$ | 3.4 | World-class; safety-critical |

## 3.4 Derivation: DPMO and Sigma Level

**Step 1:** Start with the yield expression for a centered process:

$$\text{Yield} = P(-Z_{SL} \leq Z \leq Z_{SL})$$

**Step 2:** For $n\sigma$ capability (centered), $Z_{SL} = n$, so:

$$\text{Yield} = 2\Phi(n) - 1$$

where $\Phi$ is the standard normal CDF.

**Step 3:** Therefore:

$$\text{DPMO} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times \bigl[1 - (2\Phi(n) - 1)\bigr] = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 2\bigl[1 - \Phi(n)\bigr]$$

**Step 4:** For $n = 6$ (Six Sigma), Motorola's convention incorporates a **1.5σ shift** for long-term drift, giving:

$$\text{Yield}_{6\sigma} = 2\Phi(4.5) - 1 = 0.99999966$$

$$\text{DPMO}_{6\sigma} = 3.4$$

This is the famous **"3.4 DPMO Six Sigma"** benchmark.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: AM Process Capability Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **sequential workflow** of conducting a process capability study for an Additive Manufacturing system — covering data acquisition, statistical analysis, and qualification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Critical Quality Characteristic] --> B[Define Specification Limits USL LSL]
    B --> C[Setup AM Machine Calibrate Sensors]
    C --> D[Run Pilot Build n 100 Samples]
    D --> E[Measure Output Coordinate Measuring Machine]
    E --> F[Test for Normality Anderson Darling]
    F --> G{Is Data Normal}
    G -- No --> H[Apply Box Cox Transformation]
    H --> F
    G -- Yes --> I[Compute Mean and Std Deviation]
    I --> J[Construct Control Charts Xbar R]
    J --> K{Is Process Stable}
    K -- No --> L[Remove Assignable Causes]
    L --> C
    K -- Yes --> M[Compute Capability Indices]
    M --> N[Calculate Cp Cpk Cpm]
    N --> O[Compute Yield and DPMO]
    O --> P{Benchmark against Cpk 1.33}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Qualify Process for Production]
    P -- No --> R[DOE Optimize Parameters]
    R --> C
```

## 4.2 Concept Map: Relationship Between Capability Indices

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[Tolerance T] --> Cp
    S[Sigma sigma] --> Cp
    T --> Cpk
    M[Process Mean mu] --> Cpk
    Cp --> Cpk
    Cp --> Cpm
    T0[Target T0] --> Cpm
    M --> Cpm
    Cpk --> Q[Quality Decision]
    Cpm --> Q
    Q --> Accept[Accept Process]
    Q --> Reject[Reject Optimize]
```

## 4.3 AM-Specific Capability Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer Level Capability] --> P1[Surface Roughness Staircase]
    L1 --> P2[Layer Thickness Uniformity]
    P1 --> PartLevel
    P2 --> PartLevel
    PartLevel[Part Level Capability] --> P3[Dimensional Accuracy]
    PartLevel --> P4[Geometric Tolerances GDandT]
    P3 --> SystemLevel
    P4 --> SystemLevel
    SystemLevel[System Level Capability] --> P5[Mechanical Strength]
    SystemLevel --> P6[Density Porosity]
    P5 --> Batch
    P6 --> Batch
    Batch[Batch Production Capability] --> P7[Batch Consistency Cpk]
    Batch --> P8[Yield Rate DPMO]
```

## 4.4 Process Capability Decision Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Process Capability Study Initiated]) --> S1[Collect 125 subgroups of 5]
    S1 --> S2[Calculate Xbarbar and Rbar]
    S2 --> S3[Check Stability on Control Chart]
    S3 --> S4{Any Out of Control Points}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Investigate Special Causes]
    S5 --> S1
    S4 -- No --> S6[Compute Cp using sigma from Rbar d2]
    S6 --> S7[Compute Cpk]
    S7 --> S8{Cpk Greater than 1.33}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Capable Approve]
    S8 -- No --> S10{Cpk Greater than 1.00}
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Marginal Improve Slowly]
    S10 -- No --> S12[Incapable Redesign]
    S9 --> End([Production Release])
    S11 --> End
    S12 --> End
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Process Capability. Explain the significance of $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ indices in Additive Manufacturing.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Capability (Pc)** is a statistical measure of the ability of a manufacturing process to produce output within the design specification limits. It is quantified by indices that compare the **tolerance width** to the **natural process spread ($6\sigma$)**.

**$C_p$ (Process Capability Index):**
- Defined as $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$
- Measures the **potential capability** of a process, assuming perfect centering.
- A higher $C_p$ indicates that the process spread is much narrower than the tolerance, implying the process **could** meet specifications if properly centered.

**$C_{pk}$ (Process Capability Index, centered):**
- Defined as $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$
- Accounts for both process spread **and** deviation of the mean from the target.
- Provides a **realistic** assessment of process performance.

**Significance in AM:**
- AM processes involve many stochastic variables (laser power fluctuation, powder flow, thermal gradients) → inherent variability is high.
- $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ help determine whether an AM system is suitable for **functional** (load-bearing) vs. **prototype** applications.
- Aerospace and medical AM parts typically require $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$–$1.67$ for qualification.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is Six Sigma? State the relationship between DPMO and Sigma Level.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Sigma** is a quality management methodology that aims to reduce process defects to a level of **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**, corresponding to $\pm 6\sigma$ from the mean with a built-in **1.5σ long-term shift**.

**Relationship between DPMO and Sigma Level:**

| Sigma Level ($\sigma$) | DPMO (approx.) | Yield (%) |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | 308,537 | 69.1 |
| 3 | 66,807 | 93.3 |
| 4 | 6,210 | 99.38 |
| 5 | 233 | 99.977 |
| 6 | 3.4 | 99.99966 |

The **conversion formula** (for a centered process, no shift) is:

$$\text{DPMO} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 2\bigl[1 - \Phi(\sigma)\bigr]$$

For practical Six Sigma (with 1.5σ shift):

$$Z_{effective} = \sigma - 1.5$$

$$\text{DPMO} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 2\bigl[1 - \Phi(Z_{effective})\bigr]$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **ONE full question** (either A or B).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**In a Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) process, the density of printed nylon parts is specified as $1.10 \text{ g/cm}^3 \leq \rho \leq 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$. A study of 50 specimens yields: $\bar{x} = 1.158$ g/cm³, $s = 0.018$ g/cm³.**

**(a)** Compute $C_p$, $C_{pu}$, $C_{pl}$, and $C_{pk}$. (7 marks)

**(b)** If the target density is $T_0 = 1.15$ g/cm³, calculate $C_{pm}$ and determine the percentage yield and DPMO. State whether the process is acceptable for automotive application (requires $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$). (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Given: $USL = 1.20$, $LSL = 1.10$, $T = 0.10$, $\mu = 1.158$, $\sigma = 0.018$.

$$C_p = \frac{T}{6\sigma} = \frac{0.10}{6 \times 0.018} = \frac{0.10}{0.108} = 0.926$$

$$C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{1.20 - 1.158}{3 \times 0.018} = \frac{0.042}{0.054} = 0.778$$

$$C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{1.158 - 1.10}{0.054} = \frac{0.058}{0.054} = 1.074$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.778,\, 1.074) = 0.778$$

**Part (b):**

$$C_{pm} = \frac{T}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T_0)^2}} = \frac{0.10}{6\sqrt{0.018^2 + (1.158 - 1.15)^2}}$$

$$= \frac{0.10}{6\sqrt{0.000324 + 0.000064}} = \frac{0.10}{6\sqrt{0.000388}} = \frac{0.10}{6 \times 0.01970}$$

$$= \frac{0.10}{0.1182} = 0.846$$

**Yield calculation:**

$$Z_{USL} = \frac{1.20 - 1.158}{0.018} = 2.333, \quad Z_{LSL} = \frac{1.10 - 1.158}{0.018} = -3.222$$

From Z-tables: $P(Z \leq 2.333) = 0.9902$, $P(Z \leq -3.222) = 0.00064$.

$$\text{Yield} = 0.9902 - 0.00064 = 0.9896 = 98.96\%$$

$$\text{DPMO} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times (1 - 0.9896) = 10{,}400$$

**Conclusion:** $C_{pk} = 0.778 < 1.33$ → **Process is NOT capable** for automotive application. **Recommendation:** Conduct DOE to reduce variability (lower $\sigma$) and shift mean closer to $T_0 = 1.15$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Define Process Capability. Explain the difference between $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ with neat sketches of normal distributions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A FDM printer produces a bracket with a critical dimension specified as $25.00 \pm 0.15$ mm. 100 measurements yield $\bar{x} = 25.08$ mm and $s = 0.05$ mm. Calculate $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and the sigma level. Estimate yield and DPMO. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Capability** is the quantified ability of a process, operating under stable conditions, to produce output within specification limits.

**Differences (with sketches described):**

| Aspect | $C_p$ | $C_{pk}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $C_p = \dfrac{T}{6\sigma}$ | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma},\, \dfrac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ |
| Accounts for centering? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Use case | Theoretical potential | Actual real-world performance |
| Sketch | Bell curve centered in tolerance, narrow spread | Bell curve **off-center**, narrower on one side |
| Relation | Always $\geq C_{pk}$ | $C_{pk} = C_p(1 - k)$, where $k = \dfrac{\vert M - \mu\vert}{T/2}$ |

**Sketch description (verbal):**
- *$C_p$ diagram:* Symmetric bell curve inside the tolerance band; both tails well within USL/LSL.
- *$C_{pk}$ diagram:* Bell curve shifted to the right; right tail approaches USL, left tail far from LSL.

**Part (b):**

Given: $USL = 25.15$, $LSL = 24.85$, $T = 0.30$, $\mu = 25.08$, $\sigma = 0.05$.

$$C_p = \frac{0.30}{6 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.30}{0.30} = 1.00$$

$$C_{pu} = \frac{25.15 - 25.08}{3 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.07}{0.15} = 0.467$$

$$C_{pl} = \frac{25.08 - 24.85}{0.15} = \frac{0.23}{0.15} = 1.533$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.467,\, 1.533) = 0.467$$

**Sigma level (Z-bench):**

$$Z_{bench} = 3 \times C_{pk} = 3 \times 0.467 = 1.40 \quad (\text{rounded from } Z_{USL} = 1.4)$$

**Yield calculation:**

$$Z_{USL} = \frac{25.15 - 25.08}{0.05} = 1.40, \quad Z_{LSL} = \frac{24.85 - 25.08}{0.05} = -4.60$$

From Z-tables: $P(Z \leq 1.40) = 0.9192$, $P(Z \leq -4.60) \approx 0.0000021$.

$$\text{Yield} \approx 0.9192 = 91.92\%$$

$$\text{DPMO} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times (1 - 0.9192) = 80{,}800$$

**Conclusion:** $C_{pk} = 0.467 < 1.00$ → **Incapable process.** The mean is shifted toward USL; immediate corrective action required (re-calibrate printer, adjust extrusion multiplier).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (3–5 marks per error):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $C_p$ with $C_{pk}$:** $C_p$ is calculated using the **target** (mid-spec), $C_{pk}$ uses the **actual mean**. Marks deducted if students use $\mu = T_0$ when it's not.
> 2. **Forgetting to use $s$ vs. $\bar{R}/d_2$:** Preliminary studies use $s$; long-term control chart studies use $\bar{R}/d_2$. Mixing them → wrong $\sigma$ → wrong indices.
> 3. **Wrong Z-table lookup:** Always confirm whether Z is positive or negative; $P(Z \leq -3.22)$ is **not** the same as $P(Z \leq 3.22)$.
> 4. **Not stating the benchmark:** Writing "$C_{pk} = 0.78$" without concluding whether the process is acceptable for the application loses **interpretation marks** (1–2 marks).
> 5. **Unit errors in DPMO:** DPMO is dimensionless (per million); DPMO × 10⁻⁶ = defect probability. Don't write DPMO = 0.0104 — that is the probability, not DPMO.
> 6. **Skipping normality test:** In KTU descriptive answers, mentioning **Anderson-Darling** or **Normal Probability Plot** fetches extra marks.
> 7. **Mermaid node ID error:** Students drawing flowcharts must avoid reserved words (e.g., `end`, `start`) as node names.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Process Capability (PEPET752 Module 2)**

- **Definition:** Process Capability = ability of a stable process to produce output within specification limits.
- **Core formula:** $C_p = \dfrac{T}{6\sigma}$; $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl}) = C_p(1-k)$.
- **$C_p$ vs $C_{pk}$:** $C_p$ ignores centering; $C_{pk}$ accounts for it. **Always $C_{pk} \leq C_p$.**
- **Six Sigma:** 3.4 DPMO with 1.5σ shift; 99.99966% yield.
- **Benchmarks:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (general), $\geq 1.67$ (aerospace/medical).
- **AM Context:** High inherent variability due to ~30+ process parameters; capability studies are **mandatory** before production.
- **CQC Examples in AM:** Surface roughness ($R_a$), porosity (%), tensile strength (MPa), dimensional accuracy (mm), density (g/cm³), hardness (HV).
- **Tool for $\sigma$:** From control chart: $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$; From raw data: $s = \sqrt{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 / (n-1)}$.
- **Yield Formula:** Use Z-transformation and standard normal tables; $Z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$.
- **DPMO** = $10^6 \times (1 - \text{Yield})$.
- **$C_{pm}$:** Use when target value is non-central (Taguchi loss philosophy).
- **Sequence:** Identify CQC → Define USL/LSL → Collect data → Test normality → Check stability → Compute $\mu$, $\sigma$ → Calculate indices → Interpret.
- **AM-Specific Factors:** Build orientation, layer thickness, scan strategy, post-processing, chamber atmosphere (for metals).
- **Decision Rule:** $C_{pk} < 1$ → redesign; $1 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ → monitor; $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ → acceptable.
- **Related Concepts to Revise:** SPC (Statistical Process Control), DOE, R-chart, X-bar chart, Six Sigma DMAIC, Process Performance Index ($P_p$, $P_{pk}$).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Additive Manufacturing (AM) Applications** refer to the strategic deployment of layer-by-layer fabrication technologies (Vat Photopolymerization, Material Extrusion, Powder Bed Fusion, Directed Energy Deposition, Binder Jetting, Material Jetting, and Sheet Lamination) across diverse industrial verticals to manufacture functional prototypes, end-use production parts, tooling, biocompatible implants, and complex topological structures that are geometrically unachievable through conventional subtractive or formative manufacturing routes.

As per **ASTM F2792 - 12a** (Standard Terminology for Additive Manufacturing Technologies), the seven standardized AM process categories are application-agnostic; their **utility emerges only when mapped to functional requirements** such as part complexity, material class, mechanical performance, and economic batch volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEPET752 - Module 2):**
> The term *Applications* in this module specifically covers sector-wise case studies, the decision matrix for AM vs. conventional manufacturing, the role of AM in the **Industry 4.0** paradigm, and emerging domains such as **4D printing** and **bioprinting**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a traditional sculptor carving a statue from a solid marble block. Tons of marble become dust, and the geometry is limited by what the chisel can reach. Now imagine instead a sculptor who has a magical **3D pen** that can draw the statue in mid-air, layer by thin layer, using only the exact amount of material needed.

**That is Additive Manufacturing in a nutshell.** Material is *added* where it is needed, not removed from a larger block. The applications of AM are essentially the *stories* of where this "3D pen" has proven more powerful than the chisel.

| Real-World Analogy Element | AM Equivalent |
|---------------------------|---------------|
| Magical 3D pen | Additive Manufacturing system |
| Ink / filament | Polymer resin, metal powder, ceramic slurry |
| Air drawing | Layer-by-layer deposition (typically 20–100 µm thick) |
| Final statue | Functional prototype or end-use part |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Applications Matter in KTU Exams:**
> KTU frequently tests *application selection* — that is, given a real-world problem (e.g., "patient-specific cranial implant"), which AM process is best, and why? Mastering applications trains this decision-making skill.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in AM Applications

The following quantitative benchmarks are universally cited across KTU-approved references and ASTM/ISO AM standards:

- **Build Volume** — typically **50 mm × 50 mm × 50 mm** (desktop) up to **800 mm × 400 mm × 500 mm** (industrial).
- **Layer Thickness (Z-resolution)** — commonly **20 µm to 200 µm**.
- **Dimensional Accuracy** — **± 0.1 mm** to **± 0.3 mm** for most polymer AM systems.
- **Surface Roughness (Ra)** — **5 µm to 50 µm** as-built, often requiring post-processing.
- **Material Wastage** — typically **< 10 %** versus **> 90 %** for subtractive methods.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The AM Application Decision Logic

The decision to deploy AM versus a conventional process rests on a structured five-step engineering logic. Each step must be justified quantitatively or qualitatively.

### Step 1 — Complexity Assessment
- Evaluate the geometric complexity using the **Complexity Index (CI)**:

$$
CI = \frac{N_{features} \times D_{intricacy}}{M_{processability}}
$$

Where:
- $N_{features}$ = number of internal channels, lattices, or undercuts.
- $D_{intricacy}$ = degree of curvature and fine detail (dimensionless).
- $M_{processability}$ = ease of manufacturing by conventional means.

A higher CI strongly favours AM.

### Step 2 — Volume & Economic Viability
AM is economically superior when the **production volume is low to medium** (typically 1 to 10,000 units). For high-volume production (>100,000 units), injection molding remains dominant due to **lower per-unit cost** at scale.

### Step 3 — Material Compatibility
The chosen AM process must support the required material class:

| Material Class | Suitable AM Process |
|----------------|---------------------|
| Photopolymers (resins) | SLA, DLP, Material Jetting |
| Thermoplastics (ABS, PLA, Nylon) | FDM/FFF, SLS |
| Metals (Ti, Al, Inconel, SS 316L) | SLM, DMLS, EBM, DED |
| Ceramics (Al₂O₃, ZrO₂) | Binder Jetting, VPP, Robocasting |
| Biomaterials (hydrogels, living cells) | Extrusion Bioprinting, SLA Bioprinting |
| Composites | Markforged FDM, SLA with nano-reinforcement |

### Step 4 — Lead-Time Sensitivity
AM eliminates tooling, hence:

$$
t_{AM} = t_{design} + t_{printing} + t_{post-processing}
$$

$$
t_{conventional} = t_{tooling-fabrication} + t_{production} + t_{finishing}
$$

For low volumes, $t_{AM} \ll t_{conventional}$ due to **tooling lead time elimination** (often 4–12 weeks saved).

### Step 5 — Performance & Certification
End-use applications in aerospace/medical require **certified mechanical properties**. The relative density $\rho_{rel}$ of AM parts is critical:

$$
\rho_{rel} = \frac{\rho_{actual}}{\rho_{theoretical}} \times 100\,\%
$$

Typical values: SLM parts achieve **95 % – 99.9 %**, EBM parts achieve **95 % – 98 %**, while FDM parts achieve only **80 % – 95 %**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Application Context |
|---|-----------------|---------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Relative Density | $\rho_{rel} = \rho_{actual} \,/\, \rho_{theoretical}$ | 95 % – 99.9 % | Part quality assessment |
| 2 | Volumetric Build Rate (VBR) | $VBR = V_{part} \,/\, t_{build}$ | 1–50 cm³/hr | Productivity analysis |
| 3 | Cost per Part | $C_{part} = C_{machine} \cdot t_{build} + C_{material} + C_{labor}$ | Variable | Economic feasibility |
| 4 | Material Utilization Efficiency | $\eta = M_{final} \,/\, M_{input}$ | 70 % – 95 % | Sustainability metric |
| 5 | Layer-wise Stacking Time | $t_{total} = N_{layers} \cdot t_{layer}$ | Hours | Build time estimation |
| 6 | Complexity Index (Indicative) | $CI = f(geometry, features)$ | Dimensionless | Process selection |
| 7 | Surface Roughness Estimate | $R_a \approx k \cdot t_z$ | µm | Post-processing need |
| 8 | Energy Density (PBF) | $E_d = P \,/\, (v \cdot h \cdot t_z)$ | J/mm³ | Parameter optimization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Density Equation Decoded:**
> $P$ = Laser power (W), $v$ = scan speed (mm/s), $h$ = hatch spacing (mm), $t_z$ = layer thickness (mm). This is a recurring KTU 14-mark favourite.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative 1: Build Time Estimation for an FDM Part

**Problem Statement:** A cuboidal FDM part has dimensions **120 mm × 80 mm × 60 mm**. The nozzle diameter is **0.4 mm**, the layer thickness is **0.2 mm**, and the average print head traverse speed is **60 mm/s**. The infill percentage is **20 %**, and the machine spends **15 %** of total time in non-extrusion motions (travel, retraction, heating pauses).

**Step 1 — Compute the Total Build Height (Z-direction).**

$$
H_z = 60~\text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Number of Layers.**

$$
N_{layers} = \frac{H_z}{t_z} = \frac{60}{0.2}
$$

$$
N_{layers} = 300~\text{layers}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Effective Cross-Sectional Area per Layer.**

$$
A_{layer} = L \times W = 120 \times 80 = 9600~\text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Effective Extrusion Length per Layer.**

Assuming a single-wall perimeter with 20 % infill:

$$
L_{extrusion,layer} \approx A_{layer} \cdot \rho_{infill} \cdot k_{raster}
$$

Where $k_{raster}$ is the raster line density. For typical infill, let $k_{raster} = 0.05~\text{mm}^{-1}$.

$$
L_{extrusion,layer} = 9600 \times 0.20 \times 0.05 = 96~\text{m} = 96{,}000~\text{mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Extrusion Time per Layer.**

$$
t_{layer,extrusion} = \frac{L_{extrusion,layer}}{v} = \frac{96{,}000}{60}
$$

$$
t_{layer,extrusion} = 1600~\text{s} \approx 26.67~\text{min}
$$

**Step 6 — Include the Non-Extrusion Overhead.**

$$
t_{layer,total} = t_{layer,extrusion} \times (1 + 0.15)
$$

$$
t_{layer,total} = 1600 \times 1.15 = 1840~\text{s}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Total Build Time.**

$$
t_{total} = N_{layers} \times t_{layer,total} = 300 \times 1840
$$

$$
t_{total} = 552{,}000~\text{s} = 153.33~\text{hours} \approx 6.4~\text{days}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This theoretical value assumes ideal conditions. Real FDM builds include **first-layer adhesion delays**, **heat-up cycles**, and **material changeovers**, often adding 10 %–20 % to the theoretical time.

## 3.2 Derivative 2: Energy Density for SLM of Ti-6Al-4V

**Given:**
- Laser Power $P = 200~\text{W}$
- Scan Speed $v = 800~\text{mm/s}$
- Hatch Spacing $h = 0.1~\text{mm}$
- Layer Thickness $t_z = 0.03~\text{mm}$

**Step 1 — Apply the Volumetric Energy Density (VED) formula.**

$$
E_d = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t_z}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
E_d = \frac{200}{800 \times 0.1 \times 0.03}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
800 \times 0.1 \times 0.03 = 2.4~\text{mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the final energy density.**

$$
E_d = \frac{200}{2.4} = 83.33~\text{J/mm}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the process window for Ti-6Al-4V.**

The optimal VED for Ti-6Al-4V in SLM is reported in literature to lie between **60 J/mm³ and 120 J/mm³**. Our calculated value of **83.33 J/mm³** falls safely within this window, predicting a **dense part** (relative density > 99 %) with minimal keyhole porosity.

| VED Range (J/mm³) | Predicted Outcome |
|-------------------|-------------------|
| $< 40$ | Lack of fusion porosity |
| 60 – 120 | Optimal densification |
| $> 150$ | Keyhole porosity, vaporization |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: AM Process vs. Application Domain

| Application Domain | Recommended AM Process | Material | Key Justification |
|--------------------|------------------------|----------|-------------------|
| Aerospace structural brackets | SLM (DMLS) | Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel 718 | Topology optimization, weight saving |
| Patient-specific implants | SLM / EBM | Ti-6Al-4V, Co-Cr | Custom geometry from CT/MRI data |
| Dental crowns & bridges | DLP / Material Jetting | Co-Cr, ZrO₂, photopolymer resin | High accuracy, biocompatibility |
| Prototype visualisation | FDM / SLA | PLA, ABS, resin | Low cost, fast iteration |
| Automotive jigs & fixtures | FDM / Markforged | Nylon, carbon-fiber composite | Tooling cost elimination |
| Food & culinary art | Material Extrusion | Chocolate, sugar paste | Edible materials |
| Architectural models | Binder Jetting (plaster) | Gypsum, binder | Large build volume, color printing |
| Soft robotics & 4D printing | Material Jetting (multi-material) | Shape-memory polymers | Stimuli-responsive behavior |
| Tissue engineering scaffolds | Extrusion bioprinting | Hydrogels + living cells | Biomimetic porous architecture |
| Investment casting patterns | SLA / Material Jetting | Photopolymer wax-like resin | High resolution, burnout clean |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three medical applications of Additive Manufacturing and state the AM process typically used for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Patient-specific cranial implants** — built using **SLM (Selective Laser Melting)** with **Ti-6Al-4V**, derived from the patient's CT scan data to ensure anatomical fit (1 mark).
2. **Dental crowns and bridges** — fabricated using **DLP (Digital Light Processing)** with **biocompatible photopolymer resins** or **Co-Cr alloys via SLM**, providing sub-100 µm accuracy (1 mark).
3. **Surgical guides and anatomical models** — produced using **SLA or Material Jetting** with photopolymer resins, allowing surgeons to pre-plan complex operations (1 mark).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Define 4D printing. Give one example of an application.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** 4D printing is the fabrication of a 3D object using additive manufacturing technologies where the printed structure is designed to **change its shape, properties, or function over time** when subjected to an external stimulus such as temperature, humidity, light, pH, or magnetic field.

**Example (1 mark):** A 4D-printed **shape-memory polymer stent** that self-expands when exposed to body temperature after being delivered in a compact form through a minimally invasive catheter.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> In application-based 14-mark questions, students frequently lose marks by:
> (a) Naming the AM process without justifying *why* it suits the application (lose up to 3 marks).
> (b) Confusing SLM vs. DMLS vs. EBM — these are **not interchangeable**; clarify the energy source and typical material.
> (c) Failing to mention **post-processing requirements** for end-use parts.
> (d) Omitting **ASTM F2792** terminology.
> Always structure Part B answers as: *Application → Material → Process → Justification → Limitations.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the applications of Additive Manufacturing in the **aerospace industry**. Explain why topology optimization combined with metal AM has revolutionized aircraft component design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** process and explain its suitability for manufacturing aerospace turbine blades. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

Aerospace was one of the **earliest adopters of metal AM** because the industry values weight reduction, part consolidation, and performance over per-unit cost. Major applications include: **(1 mark)**

1. **Structural brackets and fittings** — e.g., Airbus A350 XWB uses SLM-printed **Ti-6Al-4V brackets** that are 30 %–55 % lighter than machined equivalents while maintaining equivalent strength. **(1 mark)**
2. **Bionic (organic) manifolds** — fuel and hydraulic lines with **lattice-internalized walls**, achieving complex curved flow paths impossible to machine. **(1 mark)**
3. **Engine components** — GE Aviation's **LEAP fuel nozzle**, originally an assembly of 18 parts, is now printed as **a single Co-Cr component** using DMLS, reducing weight by 25 % and improving fuel efficiency. **(1 mark)**
4. **Satellite and rocket components** — SpaceX and Relativity Space use DMLS for **Raptor engine injectors** and **Aeon rocket chambers**, cutting lead time drastically. **(1 mark)**
5. **Topology optimization synergy (1 mark):** Traditional brackets are designed within the constraints of milling; AM enables engineers to remove material from low-stress regions, yielding organic, bone-like geometries. The combination of **Generative Design + AM** has cut part mass by 30 %–60 % in certified aerospace parts. **(1 mark)**
6. **Spare parts on demand (MRO)** — instead of warehousing thousands of legacy parts, airlines can print them on demand at the airport, dramatically reducing inventory costs. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**SLM Process Description (4 marks):**

A thin layer of metal powder (typically **20 µm – 50 µm** thick) is spread across the build platform by a recoater blade. A high-power **ytterbium fiber laser (200 W – 1 kW)** selectively scans the cross-section defined by the sliced CAD model, fully melting the powder particles into a solid mass. The build platform descends by one layer thickness, a new powder layer is deposited, and the process repeats until the part is complete. The build chamber is purged with **argon or nitrogen** to maintain oxygen content below **100 ppm** to prevent oxidation.

**Sketch (1 mark):** A labelled diagram showing — powder bed, recoater, laser, galvanometer scanner, build platform, build chamber, and inert gas inlet/outlet.

**Suitability for Turbine Blades (2 marks):**
- SLM can produce **complex internal cooling channels** (conformal cooling) inside turbine blades, impossible to manufacture by casting or machining, leading to improved thermal management and engine efficiency.
- Compatible with **superalloys** such as **Inconel 718, Inconel 625, and CMSX-4** that withstand turbine operating temperatures.
- However, limitations include **residual stresses** requiring **hot isostatic pressing (HIP)** and **surface roughness** requiring post-machining of critical aerodynamic surfaces.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the major applications of AM in the **medical and dental fields**, with emphasis on **patient-specific implants** and **bioprinting**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A patient-specific titanium cranial implant is to be designed. Discuss the complete workflow from CT scan data acquisition to final implant delivery, identifying the AM process, material, and post-processing steps. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Patient-specific orthopedic implants** — hip, knee, and spinal cages manufactured using **SLM** with **Ti-6Al-4V**, derived from CT/MRI data for perfect anatomical fit (1 mark).
2. **Cranial and maxillofacial implants** — used to reconstruct skull defects from trauma or tumor resection (1 mark).
3. **Dental applications** — crowns, bridges, aligners, and surgical guides using DLP, Material Jetting, and SLM (1 mark).
4. **Prosthetics and orthotics** — custom-fit sockets and braces printed in lightweight polymers using FDM/SLS (1 mark).
5. **Surgical planning models** — anatomical replicas printed from patient scans to rehearse complex surgeries (1 mark).
6. **Bioprinting** — fabrication of **tissue scaffolds** and **organ-on-chip devices** using bio-inks (hydrogels loaded with living cells) via extrusion-based bioprinters; emerging goal is functional organ printing for transplantation (1 mark).
7. **Pharmaceutical printing** — personalized pills with controlled-release profiles produced via material jetting (1 mark).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Workflow:**

1. **Medical imaging** — CT scan of the patient's skull with slice thickness ≤ 0.5 mm for adequate resolution (1 mark).
2. **Image segmentation** — using software like **Mimics** or **3D Slicer**, the defect region is isolated, and the implant geometry is designed (1 mark).
3. **CAD modeling & design optimization** — implant is designed with a **lattice or porous back-surface** to encourage bone ingrowth (osseointegration) (1 mark).
4. **Process selection — SLM with Ti-6Al-4V (ELI grade)** — chosen for its biocompatibility, high strength-to-weight ratio, and ability to produce patient-specific geometry (1 mark).
5. **Printing & build preparation** — supports are added (away from critical surfaces), build orientation optimized, and SLM performed under **argon atmosphere** with oxygen < 100 ppm (1 mark).
6. **Post-processing** — support removal, **bead blasting** for surface finish, **heat treatment** for stress relief, **hot isostatic pressing (HIP)** for densification, and finally **sterilization** (autoclave/gamma irradiation) (1 mark).
7. **Quality assurance & surgical implantation** — dimensional inspection via CMM, surface analysis, and surgical placement by a neurosurgeon (1 mark).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **AM = Layer-by-layer material addition**, classified under **ASTM F2792** into seven process categories.
- **Aerospace** is dominated by **SLM/DMLS** for **Ti-6Al-4V** and **Inconel** components; key benefit is **weight reduction via topology optimization**.
- **Medical applications** include **patient-specific implants (SLM)**, **dental crowns (DLP/SLM)**, **surgical guides (SLA)**, and **bioprinting (extrusion-based)**.
- **Automotive** uses AM primarily for **jigs, fixtures, and low-volume customized parts**, not yet for mass-production engines.
- **4D printing** = 3D printing + **time-dependent shape change** in response to stimuli (heat, light, moisture, pH).
- **Bioprinting** uses **bio-inks** (hydrogels + living cells) and aims at **tissue engineering** and eventually **organ fabrication**.
- **Energy density formula:** $E_d = P \,/\, (v \cdot h \cdot t_z)$ — must lie in **process-specific optimal windows** for densification.
- **Build time** scales with **number of layers** and is heavily influenced by **infill %, raster pattern, and machine overhead**.
- **Relative density** is the gold-standard quality metric for AM metal parts; **≥ 99 %** is desirable for aerospace/medical.
- **Conventional manufacturing** still dominates for **high-volume production** (>100,000 units); AM dominates for **complex, low-volume, customized** parts.
- **Common KTU pitfall:** confusing **SLM vs. DMLS** (functionally similar, but DMLS is a trademark of EOS; SLM is generic terminology).
- **Post-processing** is **mandatory** for end-use AM parts — includes support removal, surface finishing, HIP, and inspection.
- **AM supports Industry 4.0** through **digital thread integration**, **on-demand manufacturing**, and **mass customization**.

---
