topics.title: Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS)** is a **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** additive manufacturing process defined under **ASTM F2792** as *"a process in which thermal energy selectively fuses regions of a powder bed, using a high-power laser, to build a part layer-by-layer from a computer-generated 3D model."*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PEPET752 – Additive Manufacturing Technologies**, DMLS is classified as a **metal-based powder bed fusion (PBF-M)** process that uses a **focused, high-energy fiber laser** (typically Yb-fiber, wavelength ≈ **1070 nm**) to **fully melt** (not merely sinter) metal powder particles into a solid, near-net-shape, **fully dense (≥ 99.5%)** metallic component.

**Key KTU-mandated terminological distinctions:**

| Term | Meaning in DMLS context |
|---|---|
| **Sintering vs. Melting** | True DMLS uses **complete melting** (above liquidus), unlike indirect SLM-DMLS confusion |
| **Powder Bed Fusion** | Parent ASTM category; DMLS and SLM are sub-classes |
| **Build Envelope** | Typical industrial envelope: **250 × 250 × 325 mm** (EOS M 290) |
| **Layer Thickness** | Standard: **20 µm – 80 µm** (commonly 30–40 µm) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** Many students incorrectly equate DMLS with Selective Laser Sintering (SLS). The crucial distinction is that **DMLS uses metal powder and produces fully dense parts**, whereas **SLS (in polymer context) deals with thermoplastic powders producing porous parts**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Metallic Cake Printer"

Imagine you are baking a cake, but instead of soft dough, you are using **fine metal sand** (powder). Instead of an oven that heats the whole cake, you have a **super-precise laser torch** that melts only the exact spots needed to form one thin 2D slice of your cake. After each slice is melted, a roller spreads a **fresh layer of metal sand** on top, and the laser repeats the pattern, melting it onto the previous slice.

- The **laser torch** is like a calligrapher writing one line at a time — but with molten metal.
- The **build platform** descends after every layer (just like a cake baking one tier at a time).
- The **inert gas (Argon/Nitrogen)** keeps oxygen away — just like nitrogen blankets in food packaging prevent spoilage.
- The **support structures** are like cake dowels, holding overhangs steady until they fuse to the rest.

This is exactly how DMLS fabricates a turbine blade, a titanium hip implant, or a rocket injector — **one 20–40 µm thick metal layer at a time**, directly from digital CAD data, **without any tooling, molds, or machining**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants in DMLS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory constants the KTU examiner expects you to remember:**

- **Laser wavelength (Yb-fiber):** **λ ≈ 1070 nm** (near-infrared)
- **Typical laser power range:** **P = 100 W – 1000 W** (industrial DMLS: 200–400 W)
- **Standard protective atmosphere:** **Argon (Ar) or Nitrogen (N₂)**, with **O₂ < 1000 ppm** (often < 100 ppm for reactive alloys like Ti)
- **Build chamber pressure:** Slightly **above atmospheric** to prevent oxygen ingress
- **Material utilization efficiency:** Typically **> 95%** (unused powder is recycled)
- **Minimum feature resolution:** **≈ 100–200 µm** (limited by powder size: 15–45 µm typical)
- **Achievable density:** **≥ 99.5%** (often 99.7–99.9% with optimized parameters)
- **Surface roughness (as-built):** **Ra ≈ 5–20 µm** vertical, **Ra ≈ 8–30 µm** lateral

---

## 1.4 Historical Context & Industrial Relevance

DMLS was **commercialized in 1995 by EOS GmbH (Electro Optical Systems, Germany)**, making it the **world's first commercial metal AM process**. The 2024 KTU syllabus emphasizes DMLS because:

1. It is the **most widely deployed industrial metal AM process globally** (over 50% of all metal AM market share in 2023).
2. It is **directly relevant to Kerala's manufacturing and biomedical sectors** (e.g., Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute produces Ti-6Al-4V implants via DMLS).
3. It underpins **aerospace, automotive, defense, dental, and tooling industries** — the same sectors driving India's "Make in India" and "Atmanirbhar Bharat" AM initiatives.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of DMLS

The DMLS process executes a strict **closed-loop digital-to-physical workflow**. Below is the bulletized, examiner-friendly logic.

### Step 1 — CAD Model Generation
- A **3D solid model** is created in CAD software (CATIA, SolidWorks, NX).
- The model must be **manifold (watertight)**; non-manifold geometry causes slicing errors.

### Step 2 — STL Conversion & Slicing
- The CAD file is **triangulated into an STL (Standard Tessellation Language)** file.
- Specialized **slicing/preparation software** (EOS RP-Tools, Magics, Materialise Suite) slices the model into **2D horizontal layers** of thickness **t** (typically 20–80 µm).
- For each layer, **scan vectors (hatches)** are generated.

### Step 3 — Build Chamber Preparation
- The build chamber is **sealed and purged** with high-purity **Argon (Ar)** until **O₂ < 1000 ppm** (often < 100 ppm).
- For **reactive metals (Ti, Al, Zr)**, oxygen is reduced to **< 50 ppm** to prevent oxide formation and embrittlement.

### Step 4 — Powder Deposition (Recoater Pass)
- A **recoater blade (or roller)** spreads a **uniform powder layer** from the feed chamber onto the build platform.
- Typical powder size distribution: **15 µm – 45 µm** (D₅₀ ≈ 30 µm).
- Powder flowability, morphology, and humidity critically affect layer uniformity.

### Step 5 — Laser Melting (Selective Fusion)
- A **high-power Yb-fiber laser (1070 nm)** is deflected by **galvanometer scanners** (f-θ lenses).
- The laser beam spot size is typically **≈ 70–100 µm**.
- The laser **scans along the hatch pattern**, raising the local temperature above the **liquidus (T_L)**, fully melting the powder.

### Step 6 — Solidification & Melt Pool Dynamics
- After the laser passes, the molten **melt pool** cools rapidly (**10³ – 10⁶ K/s**).
- The result is a **dendritic / columnar grain microstructure** due to directional heat extraction through the substrate.
- This is why **DMLS parts are anisotropic** (a frequently asked KTU question).

### Step 7 — Platform Lowering
- The build platform descends by **one layer thickness (t)** — typically 30–40 µm.

### Step 8 — Recoat–Melt Iteration
- Steps 4–7 repeat **N times**, where **N = Total Height / t**.
- A typical 100 mm tall part requires **~2500 to 5000 layers**.

### Step 9 — Cooling, De-powdering & Post-Processing
- After completion, the chamber **cools under inert gas** for several hours.
- The **powder cake** is removed via sieving/blast media.
- Parts are **separated from the build plate** (wire EDM, bandsaw).
- **Support structures** are cut/grinded.
- Optional: **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)**, **heat treatment**, **CNC machining**, **surface finishing**.

---

## 2.2 Why These Steps Matter — The Engineering "Why"

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind each stage — high-yield KTU reasoning points:**

- **Why inert gas?** Reactive metals (Ti, Al) form brittle oxides (TiO₂, Al₂O₃) above ~200°C in air, destroying mechanical properties.
- **Why preheating (often 80–200°C for the build plate)?** Reduces **thermal gradients** → minimizes **residual stress & warping**.
- **Why small powder size?** Smaller particles have **higher surface area** → better laser absorption & thinner layer resolution.
- **Why support structures?** They anchor overhangs, conduct heat away from melt pools, and prevent distortion (key for KTU diagrams).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas appear almost every semester in KTU ESE for AM modules. **Memorize units & parameter relationships.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | P = Laser power [W]; v = scan speed [mm/s]; h = hatch spacing [mm]; t = layer thickness [mm]; **E_v** = [J/mm³] | Most critical DMLS parameter; controls density, porosity, defects. |
| 2 | **Linear Energy Density (LED)** | $E_l = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | P [W]; v [mm/s]; E_l [J/mm] | Used for scan-strategy optimization. |
| 3 | **Areal Energy Density (AED)** | $E_a = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}$ | P [W]; v [mm/s]; h [mm]; E_a [J/mm²] | Surface energy input per pass. |
| 4 | **Total Build Time (Layer Basis)** | $T_{layer} = \dfrac{A_{layer}}{v \cdot h}$ | A_layer [mm²]; v [mm/s]; h [mm]; T [s] | Approximation of per-layer scan time. |
| 5 | **Total Number of Layers** | $N = \dfrac{H}{t}$ | H = build height [mm]; t = layer thickness [mm]; N [dimensionless] | Computes slicing depth count. |
| 6 | **Approximate Total Build Time** | $T_{total} \approx \dfrac{V_{part}}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | V_part = part volume [mm³]; v [mm/s]; h, t [mm] | Quick build-cost estimation. |
| 7 | **Melt Pool Width (Empirical)** | $w_m \approx k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{v}}$ | k ≈ empirical constant (~0.1–0.2); w_m [mm] | Relates track geometry to process params. |
| 8 | **Solidification Rate (R)** | $R = v \cdot \cos\theta$ | v = scan speed; θ = angle between scan direction & thermal gradient | Predicts grain morphology. |
| 9 | **Cooling Rate (Approx.)** | $\dot{T} \approx \dfrac{2\pi k (T_m - T_0)^2}{\rho L \cdot v \cdot h}$ | k = thermal conductivity; ρ = density; L = latent heat; T_m, T_0 [K] | Predicts microstructure. |
| 10 | **Threshold Energy for Melting** | $E_{th} = \rho \cdot [C_p (T_m - T_0) + L_f]$ | ρ [kg/m³]; C_p [J/kg·K]; L_f [J/kg] | Minimum energy to fully melt. |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of DMLS

| Sector | DMLS Application | Why DMLS is chosen |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Fuel injectors, turbine blades, satellite brackets | Topology-optimized, weight-critical parts (Ti-6Al-4V) |
| **Biomedical** | Custom hip/knee implants, dental crowns, cranial plates | Patient-specific, biocompatible (Ti, Co-Cr) |
| **Automotive** | Lightweight brackets, conformal cooling inserts for injection molds | Mass production of complex conformal cooling reduces cycle time by 30–60% |
| **Defense** | Spare parts on-demand for legacy platforms | Reduces inventory; parts within 48 hrs |
| **Tooling** | Conformal cooling channels in injection mold inserts | Replaces 5-axis drilling; uniform cooling |
| **Energy** | Gas turbine combustion liners, nuclear fuel spacers | High-temperature alloys (Inconel 718, 625) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Energy Density (VED)

The **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** is the **single most important DQS parameter** for predicting part quality. KTU examiners frequently ask for a derivation.

### Starting Physical Premise
The energy delivered by the laser to a unit mass (or volume) of powder determines the melting behavior. The laser deposits energy **P (W = J/s)** for a time duration determined by the scan parameters.

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Energy delivered per unit length along the scan path (Linear Energy Density, E_l):**

$$E_l = \frac{P}{v}$$

*Explanation: The laser of power P [W = J/s] sweeps a length v·Δt in time Δt. Energy per unit length = P·Δt / (v·Δt) = P/v.*

**Step 2 — Energy delivered per unit area swept in one scan track (Areal Energy Density, E_a):**

$$E_a = \frac{P}{v \cdot h}$$

*Explanation: The laser tracks adjacent lines spaced by hatch spacing h. Energy per unit area = (P/v) / h = P/(v·h).*

**Step 3 — Energy delivered per unit volume of deposited material (Volumetric Energy Density, E_v):**

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

*Explanation: Stacking layers of thickness t gives a volume v·h·t formed per unit time. Energy per unit volume = (P/(v·h)) / t = P/(v·h·t).*

**Final consolidated result:**

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad \left[\frac{J}{mm^3}\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical optimal VED ranges for common DMLS alloys:**
> - **316L Stainless Steel:** 60 – 100 J/mm³
> - **Ti-6Al-4V:** 40 – 80 J/mm³
> - **Inconel 718:** 70 – 110 J/mm³
> - **AlSi10Mg:** 30 – 60 J/mm³

If **E_v is too low** → *lack of fusion porosity* (un-melted powder voids).
If **E_v is too high** → *keyholing porosity* (vapor-driven gas entrapment).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Build Time Approximation

**Given:**
- Part volume: V_part [mm³]
- Scan speed: v [mm/s]
- Hatch spacing: h [mm]
- Layer thickness: t [mm]

**Derivation:**

The **rate of material consolidation** (volume solidified per second) is:

$$\dot{V} = v \cdot h \cdot t \quad \left[\frac{mm^3}{s}\right]$$

This is because in one second, the laser travels v mm, traces tracks spaced h apart over a strip of width h, and stacks t mm of height.

Therefore, **total time to build the part** is:

$$T_{total} = \frac{V_{part}}{\dot{V}} = \frac{V_{part}}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is an **idealized** estimate — it ignores recoating time, laser settling, gas flow, and post-processing. **Real build time ≈ 1.2 × to 1.5 × this estimate.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Number of Layers

**Given:**
- Total build height of part: H [mm]
- Layer thickness: t [mm]

**Derivation (trivial but examiner-tested):**

$$N = \frac{H}{t}$$

**Example:** For a 60 mm tall part at t = 30 µm = 0.030 mm:

$$N = \frac{60}{0.030} = 2000 \text{ layers}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters vs. DMLS Defects

This **defect-mapping matrix** is a KTU high-yield table (often asked in 14-mark questions).

| Process Parameter | ↑ Increase Effect | ↓ Decrease Effect | Resulting Defect / Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Laser Power (P)** | Larger melt pool, deeper penetration | Smaller melt pool | ↑ P → keyholing; ↓ P → lack of fusion |
| **Scan Speed (v)** | Less time for melting, narrower track | More time for melting, wider track | ↑ v → lack of fusion, balling; ↓ v → overheating |
| **Hatch Spacing (h)** | Gaps between tracks | Overlap of melt pools | ↑ h → porosity; ↓ h → over-melting, residual stress |
| **Layer Thickness (t)** | Faster build, less resolution | Slower build, better resolution | ↑ t → lack of fusion, delamination |
| **Oxygen Content** | Oxide formation, embrittlement | Clean melt, dense parts | ↑ O₂ → porosity, cracks in Ti/Al |
| **Preheating Temp** | Reduced thermal gradient | Higher gradient | ↑ preheat → less residual stress & warping |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A DMLS machine is processing **316L stainless steel powder** with the following parameters:
- Laser power: P = 200 W
- Scan speed: v = 800 mm/s
- Hatch spacing: h = 0.10 mm
- Layer thickness: t = 0.030 mm

Find: **(a)** VED, **(b)** LED, **(c)** AED.

### Solution

**(a) Volumetric Energy Density:**

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

$$E_v = \frac{200}{800 \times 0.10 \times 0.030}$$

$$E_v = \frac{200}{2.4} = 83.33 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

**Verdict:** This lies within the optimal 60–100 J/mm³ range for 316L → **dense part expected**.

**(b) Linear Energy Density:**

$$E_l = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{200}{800} = 0.25 \text{ J/mm}$$

**(c) Areal Energy Density:**

$$E_a = \frac{P}{v \cdot h} = \frac{200}{800 \times 0.10} = 2.5 \text{ J/mm}^2$$

---

## 3.6 Comparison Matrix: DMLS vs. SLM vs. EBM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a guaranteed KTU 14-marker** — examiners love comparison tables.

| Feature | **DMLS (EOS)** | **SLM (Selective Laser Melting)** | **EBM (Electron Beam Melting)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Yb-fiber laser (1070 nm) | Yb-fiber laser (1070 nm) | Electron beam (in vacuum) |
| **Wavelength / Beam** | Near-IR laser | Near-IR laser | High-velocity e⁻ |
| **Atmosphere** | Inert gas (Ar) | Inert gas (Ar, N₂) | Vacuum (~10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ mbar) |
| **Preheating** | 80–200°C | 80–200°C | 600–1000°C |
| **Build Speed** | Moderate | Moderate | Fast (high power, but coarse resolution) |
| **Surface Finish (Ra)** | 5–20 µm | 5–20 µm | 25–50 µm |
| **Density** | ≥ 99.5% | ≥ 99.5% | ≥ 99.5% |
| **Residual Stress** | High (needs heat treatment) | High | Low (due to high preheat) |
| **Materials** | All metals incl. reactive | All metals incl. reactive | Limited (conductive metals: Ti, Nb, etc.) |
| **Powder Size** | 15–45 µm | 15–45 µm | 45–105 µm |
| **Trademark Note** | EOS GmbH | Generic term (SLM Solutions, Renishaw) | Arcam (now GE Additive) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU clarification:** Technically **DMLS** (EOS trademark) and **SLM** (generic) are **functionally identical** — both fully melt metal powder with a fiber laser. The distinction is **legal/marketing**, not physical. The KTU syllabus uses them as synonymous in most contexts.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between DMLS and SLS. Why is DMLS classified as a Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) process?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — approx. 80–100 words):**

DMLS (Direct Metal Laser Sintering) and SLS (Selective Laser Sintering) differ primarily in **material** and **fusion mechanism**. DMLS uses **metal powders** and a **high-power fiber laser** to **fully melt** particles above the liquidus, producing **fully dense (≥ 99.5%)** metallic parts. SLS, in contrast, typically uses **thermoplastic powders** and only **sintering (partial fusion)** below melting point, yielding **porous** parts. DMLS is classified under **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** in ASTM F2792 because it selectively fuses regions of a **pre-deposited powder bed** using **thermal energy (laser)**, layer by layer. Key features: inert gas atmosphere, fine metal powder (15–45 µm), and a recoater for powder deposition.

*(Allocation: 1 mark material difference, 1 mark fusion mechanism, 1 mark PBF classification with ASTM reference.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**List any three process parameters of DMLS and explain how they affect the part density.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — approx. 60–80 words):**

The three principal DMLS process parameters affecting density are:

1. **Laser Power (P):** Insufficient power causes lack of fusion porosity; excessive power causes keyholing. Optimal: 200–400 W.
2. **Scan Speed (v):** Higher speed reduces energy input per unit length, potentially leaving unmelted powder.
3. **Hatch Spacing (h):** Excessive spacing leaves gaps between adjacent melt tracks, creating inter-track porosity.

These parameters collectively govern the **Volumetric Energy Density (E_v)**, the master indicator of density.

*(Allocation: 1 mark per parameter with effect.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3 (DMLS) typically contributes **one 14-mark question** in the End Semester Examination. The internal choice is given: students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU ESE Style]

**(a)** Explain the working principle of **Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS)** with a neat block diagram. List the **process steps** and explain the role of the **inert gas atmosphere** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** in DMLS. A DMLS process uses **laser power 250 W**, **scan speed 1000 mm/s**, **hatch spacing 0.12 mm**, and **layer thickness 0.040 mm**. Calculate the VED and comment on whether the part will likely be dense. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

**Working Principle of DMLS (3 marks):**

DMLS builds metal parts by **selective, layer-by-layer full melting** of metal powder using a **high-power Yb-fiber laser (λ ≈ 1070 nm)**. A thin layer of metal powder (15–45 µm particle size) is spread by a recoater onto a build platform inside a sealed chamber filled with inert gas. A laser, controlled by galvanometer scanners, traces the 2D cross-section of the part, fully melting the powder to form a solid metallic track. The build platform then descends by one layer thickness, a fresh powder layer is spread, and the process repeats until the part is complete. The unused powder acts as a natural support.

**Process Steps (2 marks):**

1. CAD model creation and STL export.
2. Slicing into 2D layers (20–80 µm thick).
3. Sealing and purging build chamber with inert gas (Ar or N₂).
4. Powder deposition by recoater.
5. Laser scanning and selective melting per slice.
6. Build platform descent.
7. Repeat for all layers.
8. Cooling, de-powdering, stress-relief heat treatment, and post-processing.

**Role of Inert Gas (2 marks):**

The inert gas (typically **Argon, with O₂ < 1000 ppm**, often < 100 ppm for Ti) prevents **oxidation** of reactive metals (Ti, Al, Zr) at elevated temperatures. Molten metal readily forms **brittle oxides (TiO₂, Al₂O₃)** that cause porosity, cracking, and loss of mechanical properties. The inert atmosphere also **displaces moisture**, reduces **plasma formation** above the melt pool, and **carries away vaporized metal fumes**. For titanium and aluminum alloys, **oxygen content below 50 ppm** is mandatory.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Starting with the laser of power P [W] sweeping at scan speed v [mm/s]:

**Linear Energy Density:**

$$E_l = \frac{P}{v} \quad \text{[J/mm]}$$

This is the energy per unit length of scan track.

**Areal Energy Density:**

$$E_a = \frac{P}{v \cdot h} \quad \text{[J/mm}^2\text{]}$$

This is the energy per unit area of the layer.

**Volumetric Energy Density (consolidating for layer thickness t):**

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad \text{[J/mm}^3\text{]}$$

This is the energy delivered per unit volume of consolidated material — the master indicator of melt pool quality.

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given: P = 250 W, v = 1000 mm/s, h = 0.12 mm, t = 0.040 mm

$$E_v = \frac{250}{1000 \times 0.12 \times 0.040}$$

$$E_v = \frac{250}{4.8} = 52.08 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

**Comment on Density (1 mark):**

For **316L stainless steel**, the optimal VED is **60–100 J/mm³**. A value of **52.08 J/mm³** is **below the optimum**, indicating **insufficient energy input**. This will likely produce a part with **lack-of-fusion porosity** and reduced density. To bring it into the optimal range, the operator should **decrease scan speed** (e.g., to 800 mm/s) or **slightly increase laser power** (e.g., to 300 W), yielding:

$$E_v^{\text{new}} = \frac{300}{800 \times 0.12 \times 0.040} = 78.13 \text{ J/mm}^3 \;\; \checkmark$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** What are the **major defects in DMLS** parts? Explain **lack of fusion porosity** and **keyholing porosity** with the help of energy density concepts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **DMLS, SLM, and EBM** processes in terms of energy source, atmosphere, build speed, surface finish, and material compatibility. Which process is preferred for **titanium aerospace components** and why? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Major DMLS Defects (2 marks):**

The five major defects in DMLS parts are:
1. Lack of fusion porosity
2. Keyholing porosity
3. Balling
4. Delamination
5. Residual stress & warping

**Lack of Fusion Porosity (2.5 marks):**

Lack of fusion porosity occurs when the **laser energy input is insufficient** to fully melt the powder or to overlap adjacent melt tracks. The unmelted powder particles leave **irregular, angular voids** between layers or hatch lines. This happens when:
- **E_v is too low** (e.g., < 60 J/mm³ for 316L).
- **Scan speed is too high** OR **laser power is too low**.
- **Hatch spacing is too large**, creating gaps between tracks.

The melt pool is in the **conduction mode** but lacks the depth/width to fuse with the underlying solidified layer. These defects significantly reduce **fatigue life** and **load-bearing capacity**.

**Keyholing Porosity (2.5 marks):**

Keyholing occurs when the **laser energy input is excessive**, causing the metal to **vaporize** and form a deep, narrow **vapor depression (keyhole)** in the melt pool. This keyhole can collapse during solidification, trapping gas/vapor as **spherical porosity**. Keyholing happens when:
- **E_v is too high** (e.g., > 120 J/mm³ for 316L).
- **Laser power is too high** OR **scan speed is too low**.

The melt pool transitions from **conduction mode** to **keyhole mode**. Keyhole pores are typically **round** and act as **stress concentrators** and **fatigue crack initiation sites**.

**Conceptual Energy Density Map:**

```
Low E_v ←——————— OPTIMAL WINDOW ———————→ High E_v
   Lack of Fusion         DENSE         Keyholing
   (angular voids)        (99.7%+)      (round pores)
```

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a/b) — 7 marks

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | DMLS | SLM | EBM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Yb-fiber laser (1070 nm) | Yb-fiber laser (1070 nm) | Electron beam (vacuum) |
| Atmosphere | Inert gas (Ar) | Inert gas (Ar, N₂) | High vacuum |
| Build speed | Moderate | Moderate | Fast |
| Surface finish (Ra) | 5–20 µm | 5–20 µm | 25–50 µm |
| Material compatibility | All metals (incl. reactive) | All metals | Conductive metals only |
| Residual stress | High (post-HIP needed) | High | Low (high preheat ~700°C) |
| Powder size | 15–45 µm | 15–45 µm | 45–105 µm |

**Preferred Process for Titanium Aerospace Components (3 marks):**

For **titanium (Ti-6Al-4V) aerospace components**, both **DMLS/SLM and EBM** are viable, but the **preferred process depends on the application:**

- **DMLS/SLM** is preferred for parts requiring **fine resolution, thin features, and superior surface finish** (e.g., turbine blades, structural brackets, complex lattice structures). It produces parts with **higher dimensional accuracy** and **better surface quality (Ra 5–20 µm)** suitable for direct use after light finishing.

- **EBM** is preferred for **large, bulky titanium parts** where **build speed** is critical and **slightly coarser surface finish** is acceptable. EBM's high preheat (~700°C) reduces residual stress, but its coarser resolution limits fine features.

**For typical aerospace components (turbine blades, brackets, fuel injectors with thin walls and lattices), DMLS/SLM is preferred** due to:
1. **Finer resolution** (~100 µm features) versus EBM (~200–300 µm).
2. **Better surface finish**, reducing post-processing cost.
3. **Wider material selection** including Ti, Al, Inconel.
4. **Inert gas chamber** (Ar) is more economical to maintain than a vacuum system.

However, EBM is favored for **larger aerospace structural parts** where the high build speed and low residual stress outweigh the resolution drawback.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most frequently lose marks in DMLS questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing DMLS with SLS** — DMLS = **metal powder, full melting**; SLS = **polymer powder, sintering**. Examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for this conflation.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in VED calculation** — Writing *E_v = 200/(800 × 0.10 × 0.030) = 83.33* without units [J/mm³] loses a mark. Always include units.
>
> 3. **Not commenting on VED optimality** — Numerical questions require you to **state the optimal VED range for the given material** and **comment on whether the calculated value will yield a dense part**. Skipping the comment loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing DMLS and SLM as fundamentally different** — They are **functionally identical**; the difference is trademark, not physics. Examiners know this and deduct marks for fabricated distinctions.
>
> 5. **Omitting the role of inert gas** — In any DMLS explanation, the **inert gas (Ar, O₂ < 100 ppm)** is **mandatory** for full marks. It prevents oxidation of reactive metals and stabilizes the melt pool.
>
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in 14-mark questions** — A **neat labeled block diagram** is worth **1–2 marks** by itself. Always include one.
>
> 7. **Wrong formula for VED** — A very common error is writing E_v = P × v × h × t (multiplication instead of division). Memorize: **E_v = P / (v · h · t)**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density bullet-point summary for last-minute KTU revision:**

- **DMLS** = Direct Metal Laser Sintering; **ASTM F2792** Powder Bed Fusion category.
- **Energy source:** Yb-fiber laser, **λ ≈ 1070 nm**, P = 100–1000 W.
- **Powder:** 15–45 µm metal (316L, Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel, AlSi10Mg, Co-Cr).
- **Atmosphere:** Sealed chamber with **Ar** (O₂ < 1000 ppm; < 100 ppm for Ti).
- **Layer thickness:** 20–80 µm (typical 30–40 µm).
- **Build envelope:** Typically 250 × 250 × 325 mm.
- **Density:** ≥ 99.5% (optimally 99.7–99.9%).
- **Surface roughness:** Ra 5–20 µm as-built.
- **Master formula:** **E_v = P / (v · h · t)** [J/mm³].
- **Optimal E_v:** 316L: 60–100; Ti-6Al-4V: 40–80; Inconel: 70–110; AlSi10Mg: 30–60 J/mm³.
- **Low E_v** → lack-of-fusion porosity (angular voids).
- **High E_v** → keyholing porosity (round voids).
- **Cooling rate:** 10³ – 10⁶ K/s → **columnar dendritic grains** → anisotropic properties.
- **Preheating:** 80–200°C (DMLS) vs. 600–1000°C (EBM).
- **Inert gas purpose:** prevent oxidation, displace moisture, suppress plasma.
- **Support structures:** required for overhangs > 45° and heat dissipation.
- **DMLS = SLM functionally** (trademark vs. generic term).
- **EBM uses electron beam in vacuum** — coarser but faster.
- **Post-processing:** HIP, heat treatment, CNC machining, surface finishing.
- **Applications:** aerospace, biomedical, automotive, defense, tooling (conformal cooling).
- **Commercial leader:** **EOS GmbH** (Germany) — first to commercialize DMLS in **1995**.
- **Key advantages:** no tooling, complex geometry, lightweight topology optimization, patient-specific parts, on-demand spare parts.
- **Key limitations:** high equipment cost, support removal, residual stress, limited build size, anisotropy, post-processing needs.
- **Always include** the **inert gas role** and a **block diagram** in 14-mark answers.
- **Always state units** in VED calculations.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — DMLS | PEPET752 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** is a **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** additive manufacturing process defined under **ASTM F2792** as an additive manufacturing process in which thermal energy selectively fuses regions of a powder bed using a precisely controlled laser beam. Unlike Selective Laser Melting (SLM), SLS operates at temperatures **below the complete melting point** of the material, achieving fusion primarily through **solid-state sintering** and partial surface melting at particle contact points.

The process was invented by **Carl Deckard** and **Joe Beaman** at the **University of Texas at Austin** in **1989**, and was commercially commercialized by **DTM Corporation** (later acquired by **3D Systems**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Sintering vs. Melting — The Critical Distinction:**
> - **Sintering** = Bonding of powder particles by atomic diffusion at temperatures **below the melting point** ($T_{sinter} \approx 0.7 \cdot T_m$).
> - **Melting** = Complete phase transition from solid to liquid ($T \geq T_m$).
> SLS lies in the *sintering regime*, which is why it is particularly well-suited to semi-crystalline thermoplastic polymers such as **Polyamide 12 (PA12 / Nylon 12)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine building a sandcastle, but instead of using wet sand and a bucket, you use a **super-precise laser pointer** that fuses individual grains of plastic or metal dust together — **one ultra-thin layer at a time**. 

After each laser pass, a fresh layer of powder is rolled on top, and the laser "draws" the next cross-section. The unfused powder acts as a natural **bed of sand supporting the sculpture**, meaning you never need to build scaffolding (no support structures!). When the laser is turned off and the powder cools, you simply brush away the loose dust to reveal a fully functional 3D part.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of SLS like a 3D photocopier for solid objects** — except instead of toner ink fused by heat, the "ink" is polymer/metal powder fused by a laser.

## 1.3 Classification of SLS within Additive Manufacturing

| Classification Level | Category |
|---|---|
| ISO/ASTM Process Category | Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) |
| Energy Source Type | Directed Thermal Energy (Laser) |
| Material State Mechanism | Solid-State Sintering / Partial Melting |
| Material Class | Polymers, Metals, Ceramics, Composites |

## 1.4 Standard Process Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Wavelength of typical CO₂ laser used:** **10.6 μm** (far-infrared)
- **Wavelength of typical fiber laser (for metals):** **1060–1080 nm** (near-infrared)
- **Typical build chamber temperature:** **170 °C – 180 °C** for PA12
- **Standard build envelope (industrial):** **300 × 300 × 300 mm** to **550 × 550 × 750 mm**
- **Layer thickness range:** **0.06 mm – 0.15 mm**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" and "How"

SLS operates on the principle of **selective thermal fusion** using the following chain of physical events:

1. **Powder Deposition:** A counter-rotating roller or recoating blade spreads a thin uniform layer of powder from a feed chamber onto the build platform.
2. **Pre-Heating:** The powder bed is pre-heated to a temperature just below the polymer's melting point (typically ~170 °C for PA12). This minimizes thermal gradients and reduces the laser energy required.
3. **Laser Scanning:** A computer-controlled laser (CO₂ for polymers, fiber laser for metals) traces the cross-sectional geometry defined by the sliced CAD model, raising the local temperature above the sintering threshold.
4. **Particle Bonding:** Atoms diffuse across particle boundaries (neck growth), forming metallurgical or polymer bonds.
5. **Platform Lowering:** The build platform descends by one layer thickness (**typically 0.1 mm**).
6. **Iteration:** Steps 1–5 repeat layer-by-layer until the part is complete.
7. **Cool-Down & Extraction:** The part cools slowly inside the chamber (to avoid warping), and unfused powder is removed via brushing or bead blasting.

## 2.2 Mechanism of Sintering (Solid-State Physics)

Two primary mechanisms drive particle fusion in SLS:

- **Solid-State Sintering:** Atomic diffusion across particle boundaries. Diffusion pathways include:
  - Surface diffusion (neck formation without densification)
  - Grain boundary diffusion
  - Volume diffusion (lattice diffusion)
  - Viscous flow (for amorphous polymers above $T_g$)

- **Partial Melting (Liquid Phase Sintering):** For semi-crystalline polymers, the laser energy raises a thin surface layer above $T_m$, creating a liquid-phase bridge between particles that solidifies on cooling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** SLS does NOT fully melt the powder. This is what distinguishes it from **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)**. The retention of a semi-solid microstructure gives SLS parts unique mechanical anisotropy and porosity characteristics.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are directly examinable in KTU ESE and Module Internal Assessments.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | Volumetric Energy Density (VED) — *master equation of PBF processes* | $\text{J/mm}^3$ |
| 2 | $E_a = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}$ | Areal Energy Density (AED) | $\text{J/mm}^2$ |
| 3 | $E_l = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | Linear Energy Density (LED) | $\text{J/mm}$ |
| 4 | $T_{bed} \approx T_m - \Delta T_{offset}$ | Optimal bed temperature | $\text{°C}$ |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{\rho_{rel}} = 1 - \phi$ | Relation between relative density and porosity | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{\Delta P_{useful}}{\Delta P_{total}}$ | Energy efficiency of laser-powder coupling | dimensionless |
| 7 | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha z}$ | Beer–Lambert absorption in powder bed | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 8 | $D = D_0 \, e^{-Q/(RT)}$ | Arrhenius-type diffusion coefficient | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |

**Where:**
- $P$ = Laser power (W)
- $v$ = Scan speed (mm/s)
- $h$ = Hatch spacing (mm)
- $t$ = Layer thickness (mm)
- $\alpha$ = Absorption coefficient of powder
- $Q$ = Activation energy for diffusion
- $\phi$ = Porosity fraction

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Lightweight topological-optimized brackets in **PA12** and **PEEK**; metal SLS for titanium satellite components.
- **Medical:** Custom **cranial implants**, **orthopedic jigs**, and **bioresorbable scaffolds** (in PCL, PLLA).
- **Automotive:** Low-volume production of **functional prototypes**, jigs, fixtures, and end-use interior trim.
- **Defense:** On-demand manufacturing of spare parts for remote deployments (U.S. Army "Mobile Parts Hospital").

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Volumetric Energy Density (VED) — The Master Process Parameter

The **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** is the single most important parameter controlling part density, mechanical strength, surface finish, and dimensional accuracy in SLS. Below is the rigorous derivation from first principles.

### Step 1: Define Laser Power Delivered

The laser emits power $P$ continuously during the scan. The energy delivered along a scan line of length $L$ is:

$$
E_{line} = P \cdot t_{scan} = P \cdot \dfrac{L}{v}
$$

where $v$ is the scan velocity and $t_{scan}$ is the dwell time on the line.

### Step 2: Define Areal Energy Density (Energy per Unit Area Sintered)

Consider a single scan track of width $w$ (approximately equal to the laser spot diameter or hatch spacing $h$). The area swept per unit length is $w$. The energy delivered per unit area is:

$$
E_a = \dfrac{E_{line}}{w \cdot L} = \dfrac{P \cdot L / v}{w \cdot L} = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}
$$

### Step 3: Extend to Volumetric Energy Density (3D Fusion Volume)

To account for the third dimension (depth), we divide by the layer thickness $t$, recognizing that energy is deposited into a volume of thickness $t$ below the surface:

$$
E_v = \dfrac{E_a}{t} = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

### Step 4: Dimensional Verification

$$
[E_v] = \dfrac{\text{W}}{(\text{mm/s}) \cdot \text{mm} \cdot \text{mm}} = \dfrac{\text{J/s}}{(\text{mm}^3/\text{s})} = \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3}
$$

Confirmed: **units are $\text{J/mm}^3$**. ✓

### Step 5: Physical Interpretation — The Process Window

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Critical Regimes of VED:**
> - **Under-sintering** ($E_v < E_{v,min}$): Low density, poor mechanical strength, delamination.
> - **Optimal window** ($E_{v,opt}$): Maximum density (~95–99%), isotropic properties, smooth surface.
> - **Over-sintering** ($E_v > E_{v,max}$): Polymer degradation, **"orange peel"** surface, curling, dimensional inaccuracy.

For **PA12 (Nylon 12)** on a standard CO₂ SLS system, the optimal window is approximately:

$$
0.04 \, \text{J/mm}^3 \leq E_v \leq 0.10 \, \text{J/mm}^3
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

### Problem:

A Selective Laser Sintering machine is operating with the following parameters:
- Laser power: $P = 30 \, \text{W}$
- Scan speed: $v = 5000 \, \text{mm/s}$
- Hatch spacing: $h = 0.25 \, \text{mm}$
- Layer thickness: $t = 0.10 \, \text{mm}$

Calculate: **(a)** the Volumetric Energy Density, **(b)** the Linear Energy Density, and **(c)** comment on whether the process is in the optimal window for PA12.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Part (a): Volumetric Energy Density**

$$
E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} = \dfrac{30}{5000 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 0.10}
$$

$$
E_v = \dfrac{30}{125} = 0.24 \, \text{J/mm}^3
$$

**Part (b): Linear Energy Density**

$$
E_l = \dfrac{P}{v} = \dfrac{30}{5000} = 0.006 \, \text{J/mm}
$$

**Part (c): Interpretation**

Comparing $E_v = 0.24 \, \text{J/mm}^3$ against the optimal window ($0.04$ to $0.10 \, \text{J/mm}^3$):

$$
E_v = 0.24 \, \text{J/mm}^3 > E_{v,max} = 0.10 \, \text{J/mm}^3
$$

**Conclusion:** The process is in the **over-sintering regime**. Predicted defects include:
- Excessive surface roughness ("orange peel")
- Polymer chain scission and molecular weight reduction
- Possible **warping and curling** of subsequent layers
- Dimensional inaccuracy due to excess melt pool

**Corrective Action:** Increase scan speed $v$ to $\sim 12000 \, \text{mm/s}$, or reduce laser power $P$ to $\sim 12.5 \, \text{W}$, to bring $E_v$ into the optimal window.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Effects on Part Quality

| Parameter ↑ Increased | Density | Surface Roughness | Build Time | Risk of Defect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Laser Power $P$ | ↑ (up to limit) | ↑ (worse) | — | Degradation |
| Scan Speed $v$ | ↓ (lower) | ↓ (better) | ↑ (longer) | Under-sintering |
| Hatch Spacing $h$ | ↓ (lower) | ↓ (better) | ↓ (shorter) | Lack-of-fusion |
| Layer Thickness $t$ | ↓ (lower) | ↑ (better) | ↑ (longer) | Delamination |
| Bed Temperature $T_{bed}$ | ↑ | ↓ (better) | — | Caking of feed bed |
| Powder Size (finer) | ↑ | ↑ (better) | ↑ (longer) | Spreading issues |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU exams:** Always state the **direction of change** and provide a **physical reason** when explaining parameter effects.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SLS Process Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete functional architecture of an SLS system, from powder delivery to finished part extraction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[CAD Model Input STL File] --> A2[Slicer Software Module]
    A2 --> A3[Layer-by-Layer Cross Section Data]
    A3 --> B1[Build Platform Initialized at Z0]
    B1 --> C1[Powder Feed Chamber Loaded]
    C1 --> C2[Recoater Roller Translates Across Bed]
    C2 --> C3[Fresh Powder Layer Deposited thickness 0.1 mm]
    C3 --> D1[IR Heaters Preheat Powder Bed to T_bed]
    D1 --> D2[CO2 Laser or Fiber Laser Activated]
    D2 --> D3[Galvanometer Scanners Direct Beam]
    D3 --> D4[Selective Sintering of Cross Section]
    D4 --> E1[Build Platform Lowers by One Layer Delta Z]
    E1 --> F1{Layer Complete Check}
    F1 -->|No| C2
    F1 -->|Yes| G1[Continue Next Layer]
    G1 --> H1{All Layers Done}
    H1 -->|No| C2
    H1 -->|Yes| I1[Cool Down Phase Slow Controlled]
    I1 --> I2[Part Cake Removed from Build Chamber]
    I2 --> I3[Unfused Powder Brushed Off and Recycled]
    I3 --> I4[Final Part with Optional Post Processing]
    I4 --> J1[Finished Functional Component]
```

## 4.2 SLS System Hardware Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SLS[Selective Laser Sintering System]
        direction TB
        N1[Laser Source CO2 10.6 micrometer or Fiber 1064 nm]
        N2[Beam Expander and Collimator Optics]
        N3[Galvanometer Scanner XY Mirrors]
        N4[F Theta Lens Focusing Optics]
        N5[Build Chamber Sealed Enclosure]
        N6[Build Platform Heated Piston]
        N7[Feed Platform Powder Reservoir]
        N8[Recoater Roller or Blade Mechanism]
        N9[IR Radiant Heaters Above Bed]
        N10[Inert Gas Inlet Nitrogen or Argon]
        N11[Exhaust Filter and Recirculation]
        N12[Process Control PLC and PC]
    end
    N1 --> N2 --> N3 --> N4
    N4 -.Scans Powder Bed.-> N5
    N7 --> N8 --> N5
    N9 -.Heats.-> N5
    N5 --> N6
    N10 --> N5
    N5 --> N11
    N12 -.Controls.-> N1
    N12 -.Controls.-> N6
    N12 -.Controls.-> N8
    N12 -.Controls.-> N9
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix — SLS vs. Competing AM Technologies

| Criteria | SLS (PA12) | FDM | SLA | SLM (Metal) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Support Structures | **Not Required** | Required | Required | Required |
| Material Range | Wide (polymers, metals, composites) | Thermoplastics only | Photopolymers only | Metals only |
| Surface Roughness (Ra) | 6–15 μm | 50–200 μm | 1–5 μm | 5–20 μm |
| Minimum Feature Size | 0.5 mm | 0.8 mm | 0.1 mm | 0.3 mm |
| Mechanical Strength | High (isotropic ~95%) | Low (anisotropic) | Brittle | Highest |
| Cost of Entry | High | Low | Medium | Very High |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU exams:** Students are often asked to compare SLS with FDM/SLA. Memorize the "no support structure" advantage — this is a unique selling point of powder bed fusion processes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Selective Laser Sintering. Mention any two materials used. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) is a powder bed fusion additive manufacturing process in which a computer-controlled laser selectively fuses regions of a powder bed to form a solid 3D object layer by layer, using thermal energy below the complete melting point of the material. **(2 marks)**

Two commonly used materials are: **(1 mark)**
1. **Polyamide 12 (PA12 / Nylon 12)** — the industry standard polymer.
2. **Polyamide 11 (PA11)** — for higher ductility and impact resistance.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Polystyrene, TPU, PEEK, stainless steel 316L, Ti-6Al-4V)*

---

### Question 2: Why are support structures not required in SLS? `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Support structures are **not required in SLS** because the unsintered powder surrounding the part acts as a self-supporting medium. **(1 mark)** The powder bed has sufficient bulk density (~0.4–0.5 g/cm³ for PA12) and inter-particle friction to physically hold the part in place throughout the build, regardless of geometric overhangs or undercuts. **(2 marks)**

> [!TIP]
> This is a **favourite 3-mark KTU question**. Always mention both the *physical reason* (powder bulk density) and the *practical benefit* (time saved, no post-processing to remove supports).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of Selective Laser Sintering with a neat block diagram of the process. List the major process parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for Volumetric Energy Density (VED) and explain how each parameter influences the part density and surface finish. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Working Principle:**

Selective Laser Sintering uses a high-power laser to selectively fuse layers of powder material according to a sliced CAD model. The process steps are: **(3 marks)**

1. **Powder deposition** — A roller spreads a uniform layer (typically 0.1 mm) of powder from the feed chamber onto the build platform.
2. **Pre-heating** — IR heaters raise the powder bed to a temperature just below the polymer's melting point to minimize thermal gradients.
3. **Laser scanning** — A CO₂ laser (for polymers) or fiber laser (for metals) traces the cross-section under computer control.
4. **Sintering** — Localized heating fuses particles via solid-state diffusion and partial surface melting.
5. **Platform lowering** — The build platform descends by one layer thickness.
6. **Repetition** — Steps 1–5 repeat until the part is complete.
7. **Cool-down and extraction** — The part cools inside the chamber; unfused powder is brushed off and recycled.

**Block Diagram** — Refer to the **Mermaid architecture in Section 4.2** above. **(2 marks)**

**Major Process Parameters:** **(2 marks)**

1. Laser power $P$
2. Scan speed $v$
3. Hatch spacing $h$
4. Layer thickness $t$
5. Bed temperature $T_{bed}$
6. Powder characteristics (size, distribution, flowability)
7. Atmosphere (inert gas type and purity)

---

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Derivation of VED:**

Starting from laser energy delivered to a swept area:

$$
E_{line} = P \cdot t_{scan} = P \cdot \dfrac{L}{v}
$$

Per unit area swept (Areal Energy Density):

$$
E_a = \dfrac{E_{line}}{L \cdot h} = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}
$$

Extending to volumetric (3D) energy density by dividing by layer thickness $t$:

$$
E_v = \dfrac{E_a}{t} = \boxed{\dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}} \quad \text{[units: J/mm}^3\text{]}
$$

**Influence on Density and Surface Finish:** **(3 marks)**

- **Increasing $P$** → higher density up to the optimal window; beyond that, surface becomes rough due to excessive melting.
- **Increasing $v$** → reduces density (under-sintering) but improves surface finish (smaller melt pools).
- **Increasing $h$** → reduces density (lack of fusion between adjacent scan tracks) but improves surface finish.
- **Increasing $t$** → reduces density (insufficient depth penetration of energy) but improves build speed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`**

**(a)** Compare Selective Laser Sintering with Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) on the basis of: working principle, materials used, surface finish, support requirement, and mechanical strength. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An SLS machine operates with: $P = 50 \, \text{W}$, $v = 4000 \, \text{mm/s}$, $h = 0.2 \, \text{mm}$, $t = 0.12 \, \text{mm}$. Calculate the volumetric and areal energy densities and determine whether the process is in the optimal sintering window for PA12 (0.04–0.10 J/mm³). **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) Solution — Comparison Table:

| Criterion | SLS | FDM |
|---|---|---|
| **Working Principle** | Laser selectively sinters powder bed | Filament is extruded through a heated nozzle layer-by-layer |
| **Materials** | Polyamide 12, PA11, PEEK, TPU, metals | ABS, PLA, PETG, TPU |
| **Surface Finish (Ra)** | 6–15 μm (good) | 50–200 μm (poor, visible layer lines) |
| **Support Requirement** | Not required (powder acts as support) | Required for overhangs > 45° |
| **Mechanical Strength** | High and ~isotropic (~95% of injection moulded) | Lower and anisotropic (weak in Z-direction) |
| **Cost** | High (machine + powder) | Low |

*(Each row correctly compared = 1.4 marks; award full 7 marks for a complete table)*

---

#### Part (b) Solution — Numerical:

**Given:** $P = 50 \, \text{W}$, $v = 4000 \, \text{mm/s}$, $h = 0.2 \, \text{mm}$, $t = 0.12 \, \text{mm}$

**Step 1 — Volumetric Energy Density:**

$$
E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} = \dfrac{50}{4000 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 0.12}
$$

$$
E_v = \dfrac{50}{96} \approx 0.521 \, \text{J/mm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Areal Energy Density:**

$$
E_a = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h} = \dfrac{50}{4000 \cdot 0.2} = \dfrac{50}{800} = 0.0625 \, \text{J/mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Window Comparison:**

Optimal window for PA12: $0.04 \leq E_v \leq 0.10 \, \text{J/mm}^3$

Since $E_v = 0.521 \, \text{J/mm}^3 \gg 0.10 \, \text{J/mm}^3$, the process is **severely over-sintered**. **(1 mark)**

**Predicted Consequences:** **(2 marks)**

- Polymer degradation and discoloration (yellowing/browning)
- Excessive melt pool → "orange peel" surface
- Curling and warping of subsequent layers
- Reduced mechanical strength due to thermal damage

**Remediation:** Increase $v$ to $\sim 20000 \, \text{mm/s}$ to bring $E_v$ into the optimal window. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers → Lose 1–2 marks. Always write $\text{J/mm}^3$.
> 2. **Mixing up SLS with SLM** in definition questions → Lose 2 marks. Remember: SLS = *sintering* (below $T_m$), SLM = *melting* (above $T_m$).
> 3. **Not justifying "no support structures"** in Part A → Mention both the cause (loose powder bulk density) and the consequence (saves time, allows complex overhangs).
> 4. **Skipping intermediate steps in derivations** → Always write at least the substitution step before the final numerical answer.
> 5. **Forgetting to compare $E_v$ against the optimal window** in numerical problems → The interpretation is worth 2 marks, not the calculation alone.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Exam!**

- ✅ **Definition (ASTM F2792):** SLS = Powder Bed Fusion using a laser below $T_m$.
- ✅ **Inventor:** Carl Deckard, University of Texas at Austin, **1989**.
- ✅ **Standard material:** **PA12 (Nylon 12)**.
- ✅ **Typical lasers:** CO₂ (10.6 μm) for polymers; fiber (1064 nm) for metals.
- ✅ **Typical layer thickness:** **0.06 – 0.15 mm**.
- ✅ **Key advantage:** **No support structures required** (loose powder acts as support).
- ✅ **Key disadvantage:** **Porosity (~3–5%)** and rough surface finish.
- ✅ **Master equation:** $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$, units $\text{J/mm}^3$.
- ✅ **Optimal window for PA12:** $0.04 \leq E_v \leq 0.10 \, \text{J/mm}^3$.
- ✅ **Over-sintering symptoms:** Orange peel, degradation, warping.
- ✅ **Under-sintering symptoms:** Low density, delamination, lack of fusion.
- ✅ **Process variants:** SLS (polymer), **DMLS** (metal sintering), **SLM** (metal melting), **HSS** (ink-based high-speed).
- ✅ **Applications:** Aerospace brackets, medical implants, automotive prototypes, defence spares.
- ✅ **Distinction from FDM:** SLS is *isotropic and stronger*; FDM is *cheaper but anisotropic*.
- ✅ **Distinction from SLM:** SLS uses *sintering below $T_m$*; SLM uses *complete melting above $T_m$*.
- ✅ **Pre-heating purpose:** Minimizes thermal gradient → reduces warping and laser power demand.
- ✅ **Cool-down phase:** Slow and controlled to avoid residual stresses and cracking.

---

**End of Module 3 — Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) Notes**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | PEPET752 | NEP 2020 Aligned*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electron Beam Melting (EBM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electron Beam Melting (EBM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Electron Beam Melting (EBM)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

**Electron Beam Melting (EBM)** is a *Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)* additive manufacturing process that uses a high-velocity, computer-controlled **electron beam** (generated by a tungsten filament cathode under high vacuum) as the energy source to selectively melt and fuse metallic powder particles, layer-by-layer, to build fully dense three-dimensional functional parts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Block:** EBM belongs to the ISO/ASTM 52900 category **"Powder Bed Fusion – Electron Beam Melting"** (PBF-EB). It is a synonym class with **EBM™** (the trade name originally registered by Arcam AB, Sweden, acquired by GE Aviation in 2016). EBM operates at a chamber pressure of **~10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵ mbar** (high vacuum) and a build-plate temperature of **700 °C–1050 °C**.

| Parameter | Standard Value (KTU Reference) |
|---|---|
| Energy Source | Electron beam (tungsten filament emission) |
| Accelerating Voltage | **60 kV** (typical) |
| Beam Current | 1–50 mA (process dependent) |
| Beam Power | **3 kW – 5 kW** (typical process range) |
| Chamber Vacuum | **10⁻⁴ – 10⁻⁵ mbar** |
| Build Plate Temperature | **700 °C – 1050 °C** |
| Layer Thickness | **50 – 200 µm** |
| Scan Velocity | Up to **10⁴ mm/s** (extremely high) |
| Materials | Ti-6Al-4V, TiAl, Inconel 718, Co-Cr, Cu |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Vacuum Welder with a Magnetic Pen"

Imagine you are writing on sand using a *super-hot invisible pen* — but instead of ink, the pen shoots a stream of **electrons** that, when they hit the metal sand, melt it instantly into a solid line. Now imagine this is happening inside a **sealed vacuum jar** (so the metal does not catch fire or oxidize), and the "pen tip" is moved by **magnets** so fast it can draw **thousands of meters per second**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Inside a CRT TV (Old TV):** If you remember the cathode-ray tube television, the picture was drawn by an electron beam steered by magnetic coils. EBM uses the *exact same principle* — magnetic deflection — but instead of drawing pictures on phosphor, it draws *solid metal* on a powder bed. The screen is replaced with a metal build plate, and the phosphor is replaced with a thin layer of metallic powder.

## 1.3 Why EBM is a Critical Module 3 Topic

EBM is *not* just "another 3D printing process." It is the **flagship high-temperature metal AM process** that distinguishes itself from laser-based SLM/DMLS through:

1. **Vacuum environment** → enables reactive metals (Ti, Al, Zr) without contamination.
2. **Very high build-plate temperature** → eliminates residual stress and post-process heat treatment for many alloys.
3. **Magnetic beam deflection** → enables scanning speeds **100–1000× faster** than galvanometer-mirror SLM systems.
4. **High energy efficiency** → ~**75–80 %** electrical-to-thermal conversion, vs. ~30–40 % for laser.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Mnemonic — "V-M-P-T" for EBM:**
> **V**acuum chamber, **M**agnetic deflection, **P**owder preheat, **T**ungsten filament. These four keywords will appear in nearly every KTU question on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why an Electron Beam?

### 2.1.1 The Physics of Electron Emission (Edison / Richardson Effect)

EBM works on **thermionic emission**: a tungsten filament is heated to ~**2500 °C**, releasing electrons from its surface. These electrons are then accelerated by a high-voltage electric field (typically **60 kV**) toward the build plate.

The **Richardson-Dushman equation** governs the emitted current density:

$$ J = A_{R}\,T^{2}\,e^{-W/kT} $$

Where:
- $J$ = emitted current density (A/m²)
- $A_{R}$ = Richardson constant (~**6.02 × 10⁵ A/m²K²** for tungsten)
- $T$ = filament temperature (K)
- $W$ = work function of tungsten (~**4.5 eV**)
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant (**1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**)

### 2.1.2 Energy Transfer Mechanism

When accelerated electrons strike the powder bed, kinetic energy converts to heat via **two mechanisms**:

1. **Elastic scattering** with atomic nuclei → produces backscattered electrons (and X-rays — a critical safety concern).
2. **Inelastic scattering** with bound electrons → produces secondary electrons, Auger electrons, and **heat** (the useful effect for melting).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Vacuum is Mandatory:** At atmospheric pressure, an electron beam would scatter within millimeters due to collisions with gas molecules. In vacuum, the mean free path of a 60 keV electron is **several meters**, allowing precise delivery to the powder bed.

## 2.2 The EBM Process Chain — Step-by-Step Logic

| Step | Action | Physical Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Vacuum Initiation** | Chamber pumped to **~10⁻⁵ mbar** | Prevent electron scattering and oxidation |
| **2. Build Plate Pre-heating** | Heat baseplate to **700–1050 °C** | Reduce thermal gradients and residual stress |
| **3. Powder Layer Deposition** | Rake spreads **50–200 µm** layer | Provide fresh raw material |
| **4. Powder Pre-heating** | Beam rapidly scans entire layer at **low current** | Sinter powder to improve conductivity & thermal uniformity |
| **5. Selective Melting** | Beam scans cross-section at **high current** | Melt the part geometry |
| **6. Contour Pass** | Beam traces part boundary | Improve surface quality at edges |
| **7. Lowering** | Build plate descends by one layer height | Prepare for next layer |
| **8. Repeat** | Steps 3–7 until completion | Layer-by-layer fabrication |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains every equation you will likely need for KTU numerical or derivations on EBM. **Master these.**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Symbol | Expression | Standard Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Beam Power | $P_{beam}$ | $P = V_{acc} \times I_{b}$ | Watts (W) | Process setting (3–5 kW) |
| 2 | Volumetric Energy Density | $E_{v}$ | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | J/mm³ | Predict melt-pool size & porosity |
| 3 | Line Energy Density | $E_l$ | $E_l = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | J/mm | Tracks melting efficiency |
| 4 | Scan Velocity | $v$ | $v = \dfrac{P}{E_l}$ | mm/s | 100–10 000 mm/s in EBM |
| 5 | Hatch Spacing | $h$ | typically $0.1$–$0.3$ mm | mm | Distance between adjacent scan lines |
| 6 | Layer Thickness | $t$ | typically $0.05$–$0.2$ mm | mm | Vertical resolution |
| 7 | Beam Focal Spot Diameter | $d_{spot}$ | $\sim 0.1$–$0.4$ mm | mm | Determined by electromagnetic lens |
| 8 | Melt-pool Width | $w_{melt}$ | $w \approx d_{spot} + \alpha \sqrt{E_v}$ | mm | Empirical correlation |
| 9 | Cooling Rate Estimate | $\dot{T}$ | $\dot{T} \approx \dfrac{2\pi k(T_m - T_0)^2}{P}$ | K/s | Predicts microstructure |
| 10 | Build Rate | $\dot{V}$ | $\dot{V} = v \cdot h \cdot t$ | mm³/s | Throughput metric |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT confuse Volumetric Energy Density units across KTU papers.** Some textbooks use **J/mm³**, others use **J/m³** (multiplier of 10⁹). Always state units explicitly in your answer scripts.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of EBM Equations

- **Power-Density Plot:** Used to determine the *process map* — too low energy → lack of fusion porosity; too high → keyhole porosity, evaporation, balling.
- **Melt-pool width equation** is critical for selecting **hatch spacing**: setting $h \approx 0.7 \times w_{melt}$ gives the optimum overlap that minimizes porosity.
- **Cooling rate** $\dot{T}$ governs the resulting microstructure — EBM's high scan velocity gives cooling rates of **10³–10⁵ K/s**, producing fine α' martensite in Ti-6Al-4V.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Beam Power from Filament Emission

We start from the fundamental power delivered to the powder bed.

**Statement:** The electron beam power is the product of accelerating voltage and beam current.

**Derivation Walk-Through:**

**Step 1:** An electron of charge $e$ accelerated through potential $V_{acc}$ acquires kinetic energy:

$$ E_k = e \cdot V_{acc} $$

**Step 2:** If $N$ electrons per second strike the target (i.e., beam current $I_b = N \cdot e$), the rate of energy delivery (i.e., power) is:

$$ P = \frac{dE}{dt} = N \cdot E_k = N \cdot e \cdot V_{acc} $$

**Step 3:** Substituting $I_b = N \cdot e$:

$$ P = I_b \cdot V_{acc} $$

**Step 4 (Final Result):**

$$ \boxed{P_{beam} = V_{acc} \times I_b} $$

> For $V_{acc} = 60\,\text{kV}$ and $I_b = 30\,\text{mA}$:
> $$ P = 60{,}000 \times 0.030 = 1800\,\text{W} = 1.8\,\text{kW} $$

This is well within the typical EBM operating range of **3–5 kW** when higher currents (50–80 mA) are used.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Volumetric Energy Density (The Most Important Equation in EBM)

**Statement:** The energy delivered per unit volume of melted material is given by the volumetric energy density.

**Step 1:** The beam delivers power $P$ continuously as it scans.

**Step 2:** Over the time the beam dwells over one unit cell of dimensions (hatch spacing $h$) × (layer thickness $t$) at scan speed $v$, the *dwell time* per cell is:

$$ \tau_{dwell} = \frac{h}{v} $$

**Step 3:** Energy delivered to one unit cell (per unit length in scan direction) is:

$$ E_{cell} = P \cdot \tau_{dwell} = \frac{P \cdot h}{v} $$

**Step 4:** Volume of one unit cell (per unit length) is:

$$ V_{cell} = h \cdot t $$

**Step 5:** Volumetric energy density is energy per unit volume:

$$ E_v = \frac{E_{cell}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{P \cdot h / v}{h \cdot t} $$

**Step 6 (Final Result):**

$$ \boxed{E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}} $$

> **Numerical Example (KTU-Style):** Given $P = 3\,\text{kW}$, $v = 1000\,\text{mm/s}$, $h = 0.2\,\text{mm}$, $t = 0.1\,\text{mm}$:
>
> $$ E_v = \frac{3000}{1000 \times 0.2 \times 0.1} = \frac{3000}{20} = 150\,\text{J/mm}^3 $$
>
> **Interpretation:** For Ti-6Al-4V in EBM, the recommended $E_v$ is **~50–120 J/mm³**; thus the example is slightly excessive — risk of keyhole porosity.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cooling Rate from Beam Power (Simplified Analytical Model)

**Statement:** Estimate the cooling rate of the solidifying melt pool using a 1-D Rosenthal-type heat flow model.

**Step 1:** For a moving point source on a semi-infinite plate, the steady-state temperature field at distance $r$ from the beam is:

$$ T(r) - T_0 = \frac{P}{2\pi k r} \cdot e^{-v(r + x)/(2D)} $$

**Step 2:** At the trailing edge of the melt pool (where solidification occurs), the cooling rate is the time-rate of change of temperature:

$$ \dot{T} = \frac{dT}{dt} \approx -v \cdot \frac{dT}{dx} \bigg|_{x=x_{tail}} $$

**Step 3:** Differentiating the Rosenthal equation at the trailing edge and substituting $r \approx x$:

$$ \frac{dT}{dx} \approx -\frac{P}{2\pi k x^2} $$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$ \dot{T} \approx \frac{v \cdot P}{2\pi k \,x_{tail}^{2}} $$

**Step 5 (Final Approximation, used in process maps):**

$$ \boxed{\dot{T} \approx \frac{2 \pi k (T_m - T_0)^2}{P}} $$

> This is the engineering form derived by inverting Step 4 with $x_{tail} \propto P / [2\pi k (T_m - T_0)]$.
>
> **Interpretation:** Higher beam power → *slower* cooling. EBM uses high scan velocity (large $v$) to achieve high cooling rates that produce refined microstructures.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — EBM vs. SLM (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Feature | EBM | SLM / DMLS |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Electron beam | Fiber laser (Yb) |
| Wavelength / Particle | 60 keV electrons | ~1070 nm photons |
| Chamber Environment | **Vacuum** | Inert gas (Ar / N₂) |
| Build Plate Temperature | **700–1050 °C** | 100–200 °C |
| Maximum Scan Speed | **10⁴ mm/s** | 100–1000 mm/s |
| Beam Deflection | Electromagnetic coils | Galvanometer mirrors |
| Residual Stress | **Low** (high T) | High (post-HIP often needed) |
| Energy Efficiency | **~75–80 %** | ~30–40 % |
| Surface Finish | Rougher (Ra 20–40 µm) | Smoother (Ra 5–20 µm) |
| Feature Size (min.) | ~0.3 mm | ~0.1 mm |
| Cost | Very High (>$1M) | High ($0.5–1M) |
| X-ray Emission | **Yes** — requires lead shielding | No |
| Compatible Materials | Conductors only | Conductors + some insulators |
| Key Applications | Aero Ti implants, TiAl blades | Dental, tooling, low-volume aerospace |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Answer-Script Tip:** When asked to "Compare EBM and SLM," always start with **three rows minimum**: (1) Energy source, (2) Atmosphere, (3) Build-plate temperature. This guarantees partial credit even if your other points are weak.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EBM System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[High Vacuum Chamber 10 to the minus 5 mbar]
    nodeB[Tungsten Filament Cathode Heated to 2500 C]
    nodeC[60 kV Accelerating Anode Wehnelt Cylinder]
    nodeD[Electromagnetic Focusing Lens]
    nodeE[X Y Deflection Coils Magnetic]
    nodeF[Build Platform Heated 700 to 1050 C]
    nodeG[Powder Hoppers and Recoater Rake]
    nodeH[Powder Bed Layer 50 to 200 micrometers]
    nodeI[Melt Pool and Solidified Track]
    nodeJ[CNC Controller and Host CAD]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeJ --> nodeE
    nodeJ --> nodeF
    nodeJ --> nodeG
```

> **Caption / Reading Guide:** The schematic shows the closed-loop control of an EBM machine. CAD slicing data flows to the CNC controller, which synchronizes (a) electromagnetic X-Y deflection coils to position the beam, (b) build-plate heating elements to maintain elevated temperature, and (c) the powder rake to spread fresh layers. The electron optical column (cathode → anode → focus lens → deflection coils) operates in high vacuum.

---

## 4.2 EBM Process Cycle — Sequential State Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Step1 VacuumPumpDown]
    s2[Step2 BuildPlatePreheat]
    s3[Step3 PowderLayerRaking]
    s4[Step4 BeamPreheatingOfLayer]
    s5[Step5 SelectiveMeltingContourAndCore]
    s6[Step6 PlatformLowerByLayerHeight]
    s7{MoreLayers}
    s8[Step8 CoolDownAndDepowder]

    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s4
    s4 --> s5
    s5 --> s6
    s6 --> s7
    s7 -- yes --> s3
    s7 -- no --> s8
```

---

## 4.3 Energy Density Process Map (Qualitative Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Low Energy Density Undermelting]
    node2[Optimum Window Dense Part]
    node3[High Energy Density Keyhole Porosity]
    node4[Very High Energy Density Evaporation Balling]

    node1 --> node2 --> node3 --> node4
```

> **Caption / Reading Guide:** Operating an EBM process requires staying inside the **optimum energy-density window** (e.g., 50–120 J/mm³ for Ti-6Al-4V). Moving too low causes lack-of-fusion porosity (un-melted powder). Moving too high causes keyholing (vapor-driven voids) or evaporation and balling (Molten droplets breaking up due to surface tension).

---

## 4.4 Process Parameter Hierarchy (Tree View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    p1[Process Parameters]
    p2[Beam Parameters]
    p3[Scan Parameters]
    p4[Powder Parameters]
    p5[Temperature Parameters]

    p1 --> p2
    p1 --> p3
    p1 --> p4
    p1 --> p5

    p2 --> p2a[Accelerating Voltage]
    p2 --> p2b[Beam Current]
    p2 --> p2c[Beam Focus Offset]
    p2 --> p2d[Beam Spot Size]

    p3 --> p3a[Scan Speed]
    p3 --> p3b[Hatch Spacing]
    p3 --> p3c[Scan Strategy]
    p3 --> p3d[Layer Thickness]

    p4 --> p4a[Powder Size Distribution]
    p4 --> p4b[Powder Morphology]
    p4 --> p4c[Powder Recyclability]

    p5 --> p5a[Build Plate Temperature]
    p5 --> p5b[Chamber Pressure]
```

> **Caption / Reading Guide:** EBM quality is controlled by **four parameter families**. Students should know that KTU questions often ask to list *at least 6* parameters — one from each family ensures a complete answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**"List any four salient features of Electron Beam Melting (EBM) as an additive manufacturing process."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Energy source:** A focused electron beam (60 kV, 3–5 kW) generated by thermionic emission from a tungsten filament.
2. **Operating environment:** High vacuum (~10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵ mbar) — eliminates oxidation and allows processing of reactive metals like titanium.
3. **Build plate temperature:** Maintained at 700–1050 °C, which significantly reduces residual stress and is comparable to a hot isostatic press.
4. **Scan mechanism:** Electromagnetic coils deflect the beam, enabling scan speeds up to 10⁴ mm/s — far higher than laser-based systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "EBM uses a laser." This is *wrong*. EBM uses **electrons**, not photons. **No partial credit** for confusing the energy source.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**"Why is a vacuum environment mandatory in Electron Beam Melting?"**

**Model Answer:**

A vacuum is mandatory in EBM for the following reasons:

1. **Electron mean free path:** At atmospheric pressure, accelerated electrons (60 keV) would scatter within millimeters due to collisions with gas molecules. In vacuum, the mean free path is several meters, allowing precise beam delivery.
2. **Preventing oxidation:** Reactive metals (Ti, Al) would oxidize at elevated temperatures; vacuum prevents this.
3. **Preventing arcing:** High-voltage electrodes would discharge through ionized air at atmospheric pressure.
4. **Safety:** Vacuum prevents the spread of sub-micron powder particles and reduces X-ray attenuation loss.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often give only "to prevent oxidation." This is incomplete. KTU expects at least **three reasons** for a full 3-mark answer.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice: 1 × 14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted Model Paper]**

---

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **working principle of the Electron Beam Melting (EBM)** process. List the major subsystems. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **volumetric energy density** in EBM. Compute the value for $P = 3\,\text{kW}$, $v = 2000\,\text{mm/s}$, $h = 0.15\,\text{mm}$, $t = 0.07\,\text{mm}$ and comment on the result for Ti-6Al-4V. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Working Principle:**

In EBM, a thin layer of metallic powder is deposited on a heated build platform inside a vacuum chamber. A high-energy electron beam, generated by thermionic emission from a heated tungsten cathode, is accelerated to **60 kV** and focused onto the powder bed. The beam is steered across the powder bed using **electromagnetic deflection coils** following the cross-section data from a sliced CAD model. Where the beam strikes, the powder absorbs the kinetic energy of electrons and melts. After melting one layer, the build platform descends by one layer thickness, and a fresh powder layer is spread by a rake. The process repeats until the full 3-D part is built.

**Major Subsystems (must list at least 5 for full marks):**

1. **Electron gun** (tungsten filament, Wehnelt cylinder, accelerating anode).
2. **Electromagnetic focusing & deflection lens column**.
3. **Vacuum chamber & pumping system** (~10⁻⁵ mbar).
4. **Build platform with heaters** (700–1050 °C).
5. **Powder delivery & recoating system** (hoppers + rake).
6. **CNC controller & slicing software**.
7. **Cooling system & X-ray shielding**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often skip the *vacuum subsystem* or *X-ray shielding*. Both are mandatory for full credit on this question.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Derivation of Volumetric Energy Density:**

The beam of power $P$ travels at scan speed $v$ over a hatch spacing $h$ and layer thickness $t$.

Step 1 — Time spent by the beam over a unit cell of length $h$ in the scan direction:

$$ t_{dwell} = \frac{h}{v} $$

Step 2 — Energy deposited in the unit cell:

$$ E_{cell} = P \cdot t_{dwell} = \frac{P \cdot h}{v} $$

Step 3 — Volume of the unit cell (per unit scan length):

$$ V_{cell} = h \cdot t $$

Step 4 — Volumetric energy density:

$$ E_v = \frac{E_{cell}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{P \cdot h / v}{h \cdot t} $$

$$ \boxed{E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}} $$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $P = 3000\,\text{W}$, $v = 2000\,\text{mm/s}$, $h = 0.15\,\text{mm}$, $t = 0.07\,\text{mm}$.

$$ E_v = \frac{3000}{2000 \times 0.15 \times 0.07} $$

$$ E_v = \frac{3000}{21} \approx 142.86\,\text{J/mm}^3 $$

**Comment:** For Ti-6Al-4V in EBM, the typical recommended volumetric energy density range is **~50–120 J/mm³**. A value of **142.86 J/mm³** lies slightly **above** the optimum window, indicating a risk of **keyhole porosity, evaporation, and balling**. To bring it into range, one may reduce beam power to ~2.1 kW, or increase scan speed / hatch spacing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget the **comment / interpretation** step. KTU board examiners typically award **1 mark** for the qualitative discussion — never skip it.

---

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain in detail the **role of vacuum, electromagnetic deflection, and high build-plate temperature** in determining the quality of EBM-built parts. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **EBM with Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** under the headings: (i) energy source, (ii) atmosphere, (iii) build-plate temperature, (iv) scan speed, (v) surface finish, (vi) typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**1. Role of Vacuum:**
- Increases electron mean free path, enabling precise beam focus.
- Prevents oxidation of reactive metals (Ti, Al, Zr).
- Avoids electrical arcing in the high-voltage gun column.
- Reduces X-ray generation by minimizing gas-electron interaction.

**2. Role of Electromagnetic Deflection:**
- Achieves scan speeds up to **10⁴ mm/s** — orders of magnitude higher than mirror-galvanometer systems.
- No moving mechanical parts → low wear and high reliability.
- Enables *multi-spot melting* and complex scan strategies (e.g., line, contour, snake, fractal).
- Allows the beam to be switched on/off rapidly to skip un-melted regions.

**3. Role of High Build-Plate Temperature (700–1050 °C):**
- Reduces thermal gradients between layers → minimizes residual stress.
- Acts as a *continuous in-situ heat treatment* → for Ti-6Al-4V, this produces a microstructure closer to equilibrium α+β rather than brittle α' martensite.
- Improves ductility and fatigue life.
- Allows sintering of the surrounding powder, which supports the part during build.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently describe *what* these parameters are, but not *why* they matter. Always tie each parameter to a **specific quality outcome** (e.g., "vacuum → low oxide inclusions → better fatigue life").

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

| # | Feature | EBM | SLM / DMLS |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Energy Source | Electron beam (60 keV) | Fiber laser (λ ≈ 1070 nm) |
| (ii) | Atmosphere | **Vacuum** (10⁻⁵ mbar) | Inert gas (Ar / N₂) |
| (iii) | Build-plate Temp | **700–1050 °C** | 100–200 °C |
| (iv) | Scan Speed | **10⁴ mm/s** | 100–1000 mm/s |
| (v) | Surface Finish | Rougher (Ra 20–40 µm) | Smoother (Ra 5–20 µm) |
| (vi) | Typical Applications | Aerospace Ti blisks, orthopaedic implants (Ti-6Al-4V) | Dental crowns, tooling inserts, small aerospace brackets |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Memorize before the exam.**

- ✅ EBM is a **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** process using an **electron beam** as energy source.
- ✅ Original trademark: **Arcam AB** (Sweden); now owned by **GE Aviation** (since 2016).
- ✅ Chamber pressure: **~10⁻⁴ – 10⁻⁵ mbar** (high vacuum).
- ✅ Accelerating voltage: **60 kV**; beam current up to **~50 mA**; power **3–5 kW**.
- ✅ Build plate: **700 °C (Ti) to 1050 °C (TiAl)**.
- ✅ Layer thickness: **50 – 200 µm**.
- ✅ Volumetric energy density equation: $\;E_v = P / (v h t)\;$ — most-tested formula.
- ✅ Beam deflection: **electromagnetic coils** (no mirrors) → very high scan speed.
- ✅ Filament: **tungsten** (work function ~4.5 eV, Richardson emission).
- ✅ Major advantage: **low residual stress** because of high in-situ thermal preheat.
- ✅ Major disadvantage: **limited to conductive materials**; produces **X-rays** requiring shielding.
- ✅ Mnemonic for EBM: **V-M-P-T** = **V**acuum, **M**agnetic deflection, **P**owder preheat, **T**ungsten filament.
