- ✅ For KTU numerals: always compute $E_v$ and state whether it falls in the **optimum window** for the chosen material.
- ✅ Always mention **3 reasons** for vacuum (mean free path + oxidation prevention + arcing prevention).
- ✅ Comparison with SLM — minimum **6 rows** for a 7-mark comparison answer.
- ✅ Cooling rate ~**10³ – 10⁵ K/s** → produces refined / martensitic microstructure in Ti-6Al-4V.
- ✅ Common EBM materials: **Ti-6Al-4V, TiAl, Inconel 718, Co-Cr (ASTM F75)**.
- ✅ Key applications: **GE Catalyst engine (TiAl blades), orthopaedic hip cups, spinal cages**.
- ✅ Safety note: EBM systems require **lead-shielded enclosures** for X-ray protection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selective Laser Melting (SLM) and Multijet Fusion (MJF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selective Laser Melting (SLM) and Multijet Fusion (MJF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Selective Laser Melting (SLM)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** is a powder bed fusion (PBF) additive manufacturing process in which a high-power focused laser beam is used to selectively and **fully melt** metallic powders (layer by layer) inside an oxygen-controlled build chamber, producing near-fully-dense (>99% theoretical density) functional metallic parts with mechanical properties comparable to wrought materials.

The process is governed by **ASTM F2792** standard, classifying SLM under the category *"Powder Bed Fusion – Energy Source: Laser Beam."*

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are decorating a cake, layer by layer. But instead of icing, you sprinkle a thin layer of fine metallic sand. Then, instead of using a piping bag, you use a highly concentrated "laser pen" that is so powerful it can instantly melt that sand into a solid metallic line. You repeat this hundreds or thousands of times — each new layer fuses perfectly with the previous one — until a fully metallic 3D object emerges from the sand bed. That, in essence, is SLM.

### 1.2 Multijet Fusion (MJF)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Multijet Fusion (MJF)** is a proprietary powder-bed additive manufacturing technology developed by **Hewlett-Packard (HP)** in which a thermal inkjet-style printhead selectively deposits a **fusing agent** (and a **detailing agent**) onto a polymer powder bed, followed by uniform exposure to **infrared (IR) radiation** that fuses the targeted regions. It belongs to the broader family of **Multi Jet Modeling** processes.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of MJF like printing a color photograph on paper using an inkjet printer — except the "paper" is a bed of polymer powder and the "ink" is a chemical fusion agent. After printing the agent pattern on one powder layer, you pass a "flashlight" of infrared light over the entire surface. Wherever the fusion agent was printed, the powder melts and solidifies; everywhere else, the powder stays loose (and is later recycled). Layer by layer, the part builds up with no laser scanning required.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Constants

| Parameter (Standard Metric) | SLM | MJF |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Ytterbium fiber laser (typically **1070 nm** wavelength) | Infrared (IR) heating lamps (~**2.5 µm – 10 µm** range) |
| **Atmosphere** | Inert gas (Argon / Nitrogen), O₂ < **500 ppm** | Air (open chamber, no inert gas needed) |
| **Typical Layer Thickness** | **20 – 50 µm** | **70 – 100 µm** |
| **Powder Type** | Metal (SS 316L, Ti-6Al-4V, AlSi10Mg, Inconel 718) | Thermoplastic polymers (PA12, PA11, TPU) |
| **Typical Achievable Density** | **>99%** (theoretical) | **~95%** (lower than SLS/SLM metals) |
| **Build Volume Range** | **100×100×100 mm** (lab) to **800×400×500 mm** (industrial) | **380×284×380 mm** (HP Jet Fusion 4200) |

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight**
> In KTU 2024 Module 3, SLM and MJF are treated as **benchmark powder-bed processes** — SLM represents the "metal laser-based" track while MJF represents the "polymer non-laser" track of modern PBF technology.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Selective Laser Melting (SLM) — Detailed Working Principle

SLM converts digital CAD geometry into physical parts through the following operational chain:

1. **CAD Model Preparation**
   - The 3D model is converted to **STL** format and sliced into 2D cross-sectional layers using software (e.g., Magics, Materialise, Siemens NX).

2. **Build Chamber Preparation**
   - The build chamber is sealed and purged with **high-purity Argon or Nitrogen** until oxygen content falls below **500 ppm** (often below **100 ppm** for reactive metals like Ti).
   - A build platform (substrate plate) is heated to a pre-set temperature (typically **80 – 200 °C**) to reduce thermal gradients.

3. **Powder Deposition**
   - A recoater blade or roller spreads a uniform layer of metal powder (typical particle size **15 – 45 µm**) across the build platform.

4. **Laser Melting**
   - A **fiber laser** (200 W – 1 kW) is directed by galvanometer scanners and focused by F-theta lenses onto the powder bed.
   - The laser selectively scans the 2D cross-section, **fully melting** the powder into a solid metal track.

5. **Layer-wise Build**
   - The build platform descends by one layer thickness (typically **30 µm**), and steps 3–4 repeat.

6. **Cooling and Depowdering**
   - After completion, the chamber cools under inert atmosphere (slow cooling to avoid residual stress).
   - Excess powder is removed via vacuuming or brushing.

**Why and How of SLM:**

- **Why a laser?** A laser provides spatially precise, high-energy-density input capable of fully melting high-melting-point metals (e.g., **Titanium = 1668 °C**).
- **Why an inert atmosphere?** Reactive metals (Ti, Al) would oxidize instantly, forming brittle oxides. Argon displaces oxygen and prevents oxidation and porosity.

### 2.2 SLM Process Parameters and Energy Density

The single most critical metric in SLM is **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)**, denoted $E_v$:

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Laser Power (W)
- $v$ = Scan Speed (mm/s)
- $h$ = Hatch Spacing (mm) — distance between two adjacent scan tracks
- $t$ = Layer Thickness (mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical VED for SS 316L** lies in the range **60 – 100 J/mm³**.
> Below this range → lack of fusion porosity.
> Above this range → keyhole porosity, vaporization, and balling defects.

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
Engineers at aerospace firms (e.g., GE Aviation, Airbus) use VED optimization charts to qualify **fuel nozzle brackets, turbine blades, and structural brackets** for flight-critical applications.

### 2.3 Multijet Fusion (MJF) — Detailed Working Principle

MJF uses **two inkjet-style printheads** working in tandem:

1. **Fusing Agent** — A black carbon-based ink that absorbs IR radiation strongly, causing localized melting where it is jetted.
2. **Detailing Agent** — A clear ink that prevents powder fusion at part boundaries, ensuring sharp edges and high dimensional accuracy.

**Step-by-Step Operation:**

1. **Powder spreading:** A layer of polymer powder (e.g., **PA12**, particle size **~60 µm**) is spread across the build platform.
2. **Agent jetting:** Printheads (with **~1200 nozzles per inch**) deposit fusing agent onto the cross-section pixels and detailing agent around the part edges.
3. **IR fusing:** The entire layer is exposed to IR lamps, raising temperature of agent-deposited regions above the polymer melting point (~**190 °C** for PA12).
4. **Cooling and lowering:** The platform descends, a new powder layer is spread, and the cycle repeats.
5. **Depowdering:** After cooling, unfused powder is vacuumed and reused (typical **refresh rate = 20%** new powder).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E_v$ | Volumetric Energy Density (SLM) | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | J/mm³ |
| $P$ | Laser Power | Input parameter | W |
| $v$ | Scan Speed | Input parameter | mm/s |
| $h$ | Hatch Spacing | Distance between scan lines | mm |
| $t$ | Layer Thickness | Z-resolution | mm |
| $Q_{IR}$ | IR Energy Flux Density (MJF) | $\dfrac{E_{IR}}{A \cdot t_{exp}}$ | J/cm² |
| $\rho_{rel}$ | Relative Density | $\dfrac{\rho_{built}}{\rho_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$ | % |
| $\eta_{build}$ | Build Efficiency | $\dfrac{V_{part}}{V_{total} + V_{waste}} \times 100\%$ | % |
| $R_{melt}$ | Melting Rate (SLM) | $R_{melt} = \dfrac{m_{melted}}{\Delta t}$ | g/s |
| $C_{cool}$ | Cooling Rate Estimate | $C_{cool} \approx \dfrac{2 \cdot k \cdot \Delta T}{h^2}$ | K/s |
| $\sigma_{res}$ | Residual Stress (SLM) | $\sigma_{res} \propto E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | MPa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Units:** Always maintain SI consistency. A common KTU examination pitfall is mixing **µm** with **mm** in the VED formula, leading to an answer that is off by a factor of 1000.

**Real-World Engineering Utility of MJF:**
- Functional prototypes with injection-molded-like isotropic mechanical properties (e.g., HP Jet Fusion 5200 prints **up to 10,000 parts/week** in production settings).
- Custom orthopedic devices, footwear midsoles (Adidas uses a similar concept), and automotive interior components.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — SLM vs MJF Side-by-Side

| Analytical Dimension | SLM | MJF |
|---|---|---|
| **ASTM F2792 Category** | Powder Bed Fusion – Laser | Material Jetting / Multi Jet Fusion (Hybrid) |
| **Energy Source** | Fiber laser (single-spot scanning) | IR lamps (area-wise flood exposure) |
| **State of Fusion** | Full melting (liquid phase) | Partial melting (sintering-like) |
| **Powder Material** | Metals (single alloy or pre-alloyed) | Thermoplastics (PA12, PA11, TPU, PP) |
| **Atmosphere** | Inert gas (Ar / N₂) | Ambient air |
| **Scan Strategy** | Vector scan lines (hatch pattern) | Raster scan of full layer (per-pixel agents) |
| **Thermal Profile** | Localized, steep gradient | Bulk, mild gradient |
| **Surface Roughness (Ra)** | **5 – 20 µm** (as-built) | **5 – 15 µm** (better than SLS) |
| **Mechanical Anisotropy** | Moderate (depends on build direction) | Very low (near-isotropic) |
| **Build Time per cm³** | Slow (laser point-by-point) | Fast (area exposure) |
| **Cost Driver** | Inert gas + laser maintenance | Printhead consumable + powder refresh |

### 3.2 Derivation of Volumetric Energy Density (VED) for SLM

**Step 1: Energy delivered per unit length of scan track**

The laser delivers power $P$ (W = J/s) while scanning at speed $v$ (mm/s). The energy per unit length of scan is:

$$E_{length} = \frac{P}{v} \quad [\text{J/mm}]$$

**Step 2: Energy delivered per unit area of a single scan track**

The width of a melted track is approximately equal to the hatch spacing $h$ (mm). So energy per unit area of one scan layer becomes:

$$E_{area} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h} \quad [\text{J/mm}^2]$$

**Step 3: Energy delivered per unit volume of melted material**

A single layer has thickness $t$ (mm). Multiplying the denominator by $t$ converts area-based energy into volumetric energy:

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad [\text{J/mm}^3]$$

**Numerical Illustration — KTU-style Problem Walkthrough:**

> **Problem:** An SLM machine melts SS 316L powder using laser power $P = 200$ W, scan speed $v = 800$ mm/s, hatch spacing $h = 0.1$ mm, and layer thickness $t = 0.03$ mm. Calculate the Volumetric Energy Density and comment on whether it falls in the optimum range for SS 316L (60 – 100 J/mm³).

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Substitute values into the VED equation.

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numeric values.

$$E_v = \frac{200}{800 \times 0.1 \times 0.03}$$

**Step 3:** Compute denominator.

$$800 \times 0.1 \times 0.03 = 2.4$$

**Step 4:** Final evaluation.

$$E_v = \frac{200}{2.4} = 83.33 \quad \text{J/mm}^3$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation.

Since **83.33 J/mm³** lies in the range **60 – 100 J/mm³**, the parameter set is **optimum for SS 316L**, yielding a near-fully-dense part with minimal porosity.

### 3.3 Derivation of Build Rate for SLM

The volumetric build rate $R_{build}$ (mm³/s) depends on scan speed, hatch spacing, and layer thickness:

$$R_{build} = v \cdot h \cdot t \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{s}]$$

**For the above problem:**

$$R_{build} = 800 \times 0.1 \times 0.03 = 2.4 \quad \text{mm}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to cm³/hr:

$$R_{build} = 2.4 \times 3600 / 1000 = 8.64 \quad \text{cm}^3/\text{hr}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Expression for Residual Stress in SLM

Residual stress arises from steep thermal gradients during rapid solidification. A first-order approximation:

$$\sigma_{res} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot f$$

Where:
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of the material (MPa)
- $\alpha$ = Coefficient of thermal expansion (1/K)
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature gradient between melt pool and substrate (K)
- $f$ = Constraint factor (0 to 1, depending on substrate clamping)

**For SS 316L:**
- $E \approx 193$ GPa
- $\alpha \approx 17 \times 10^{-6}$ /K
- $\Delta T \approx 1500$ K (melt pool ~2500 K, substrate ~1000 K)

$$\sigma_{res} = 193{,}000 \cdot (17 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 1500 \cdot 1 \approx 4921.5 \quad \text{MPa}$$

This extremely high theoretical value illustrates why **residual stress** is one of the most critical defects in SLM, mitigated through:
- **Substrate preheating**
- **Scan strategy optimization** (e.g., checkerboard, island, or chess pattern)
- **Post-process heat treatment** (stress relief at 600 – 700 °C)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SLM Process Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Model STL File] --> B[Slicing Software Layer Generation]
    B --> C[Build Chamber Purging with Argon Gas]
    C --> D[Recoater Spreads Metal Powder Layer]
    D --> E[Galvanometer Scanner Directs Fiber Laser]
    E --> F[Laser Fully Melts Powder Cross Section]
    F --> G[Build Platform Descends by Layer Thickness]
    G --> D
    G --> H[Cooling Stage Under Inert Gas]
    H --> I[Depowdering and Stress Relief]
    I --> J[Finished SLM Metal Part]
```

**Node Interpretation:**
- **A → B:** STL file is sliced into 2D layers (typical 30 µm thickness).
- **B → C:** Oxygen level reduced below 500 ppm to prevent oxidation.
- **C → D:** Powder layer uniformity is critical (target ±5 µm variation).
- **D → E:** Recoater spreads; laser scan is initiated.
- **E → F:** Full melting — distinguishes SLM from Selective Laser Sintering (SLS), which only sinters.
- **F → G:** Layer-by-layer build proceeds.
- **G → H:** Cycle repeats 100s to 1000s of times.
- **H → I:** Slow cooling to prevent cracking.
- **I → J:** Final part is removed and post-processed.

### 4.2 MJF Process Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Model STL File] --> B[Slicing and Layer Generation]
    B --> C[Powder Bed Initialized with PA12]
    C --> D[Recoater Spreads Polymer Powder]
    D --> E[Fusing Agent Printhead Jets Pattern]
    E --> F[Detailing Agent Printhead Jets Edges]
    F --> G[IR Lamps Flood Expose Layer]
    G --> H[Fused Areas Solidify on Cooling]
    H --> I[Build Platform Descends]
    I --> D
    I --> J[Cooling and Depowdering]
    J --> K[Finished MJF Polymer Part]
```

**Node Interpretation:**
- **A → B:** Slicing produces 2D pixel patterns per layer.
- **B → C:** Powder bed is preheated near polymer melting point.
- **C → D:** Uniform powder spreading is essential.
- **D → E:** Fusing agent (carbon black + water + surfactants) is jetted onto the part cross-section.
- **E → F:** Detailing agent (water + glycol) is jetted around part boundaries to control thermal spread.
- **F → G:** IR lamps melt the agent-marked regions.
- **G → H:** Cooled regions solidify.
- **H → I:** Platform descends by one layer thickness (~80 µm).
- **I → J:** Unfused powder is removed and recycled.
- **J → K:** Final part has near-isotropic properties.

### 4.3 SLM Build Chamber Components — Functional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fiber Laser Source 1070 nm] --> B[Galvanometer Scanner XY Mirrors]
    B --> C[F Theta Focusing Lens]
    C --> D[Build Chamber Window]
    D --> E[Powder Bed Build Platform]
    E --> F[Recoater Blade Powder Hopper]
    F --> E
    E --> G[Inert Gas Inlet Argon Supply]
    E --> H[Exhaust and Filtration System]
    H --> I[Oxygen Sensor below 500 ppm]
```

### 4.4 MJF Printhead Subsystem — Functional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Fusing Agent Reservoir] --> B[Fusing Agent Printhead 1200 nozzles]
    C[Detailing Agent Reservoir] --> D[Detailing Agent Printhead 1200 nozzles]
    B --> E[Drop on Demand Piezoelectric Actuation]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Powder Bed Pixelwise Deposition]
    F --> G[IR Lamp Bank Halogen Type]
    G --> H[Fused Layer]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q1. Distinguish between Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) and Selective Laser Melting (SLM).** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- **SLS** sinters (heats below melting point) polymer or metal powders, leaving a porous structure (typical density 85 – 95%).
- **SLM** fully melts metal powders, producing near-fully-dense (>99%) metallic parts.
- SLS often requires post-processing infiltration; SLM parts are functionally complete after depowdering.
- SLM requires inert atmosphere; SLS for polymers may run in air.
- SLM is restricted to a narrower range of weldable, single-alloy powders, whereas SLS accommodates blended metal powders.

---

**Q2. List any three advantages of Multijet Fusion over conventional Selective Laser Sintering for polymer parts.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Faster build time** — IR area exposure vs. point-by-point laser scanning.
2. **Better surface finish** — detailing agent sharpens edges, yielding **Ra ≈ 5 – 15 µm**.
3. **Near-isotropic mechanical properties** — uniform area-wise heating avoids directional grain structure.
4. *Bonus:* No support structures needed in MJF; surrounding unfused powder acts as natural support.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU ESE Model Question – Module 3 Internal Choice]**
> *(Note: As per KTU 2024 scheme, students answer ONE question of 14 marks from the Module 3 internal choice pair.)*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** with a neat block diagram. List any four process parameters that influence part density. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Working Principle:**
   - CAD model → STL → slicing into 2D layers.
   - Inert chamber (Ar / N₂, O₂ < 500 ppm).
   - Recoater spreads metal powder (15 – 45 µm).
   - Fiber laser (1070 nm, ~200 W – 1 kW) selectively scans the cross-section.
   - Powder **fully melts** into a solid metallic track.
   - Platform descends by layer thickness (20 – 50 µm), cycle repeats.
   - After build: slow cooling → depowdering → stress relief heat treatment.

2. **Process Parameters Influencing Density:**
   - Laser Power ($P$)
   - Scan Speed ($v$)
   - Hatch Spacing ($h$)
   - Layer Thickness ($t$)

   The combined effect is captured by **Volumetric Energy Density** $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$.

**(b)** For SS 316L powder processed in an SLM machine, calculate the volumetric energy density if $P = 250$ W, $v = 1000$ mm/s, $h = 0.12$ mm, and $t = 0.04$ mm. Comment on the suitability for SS 316L (optimum range 60 – 100 J/mm³). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Substitute values.

$$E_v = \frac{250}{1000 \times 0.12 \times 0.04}$$

**Step 2:** Compute denominator.

$$1000 \times 0.12 \times 0.04 = 4.8$$

**Step 3:** Final result.

$$E_v = \frac{250}{4.8} = 52.08 \quad \text{J/mm}^3$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.
Since **52.08 J/mm³ < 60 J/mm³**, the parameter set is **below optimum**, likely leading to **lack-of-fusion porosity** and reduced mechanical strength. Recommendation: reduce scan speed or increase laser power.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Describe the working of **Multijet Fusion (MJF)** with the role of fusing agent and detailing agent. Mention at least three advantages of MJF. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Working Principle:**
   - A thin layer of polymer powder (e.g., PA12) is spread on the build platform.
   - Inkjet-style printheads selectively jet **fusing agent** (carbon-based, IR-absorbing) onto the cross-section pixels.
   - Simultaneously, **detailing agent** (water + glycol) is jetted around part edges to control thermal bleed.
   - IR lamps flood-expose the entire layer, melting only the agent-marked regions.
   - Platform descends, new powder layer spread, cycle repeats.
   - After build, unfused powder is recycled (refresh rate ~20% new powder).

2. **Role of Fusing Agent:** Absorbs IR energy and lowers the effective melting point of the polymer locally.

3. **Role of Detailing Agent:** Prevents over-fusion at edges, ensuring sharp dimensional accuracy.

4. **Three Advantages of MJF:**
   - **Faster build time** due to area-wise IR exposure.
   - **Near-isotropic mechanical properties**.
   - **No support structures** required; unfused powder acts as natural support.
   - **Better surface finish** with detailing agent.

**(b)** Compare **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** and **Multijet Fusion (MJF)** on the basis of energy source, material compatibility, density achievable, and build atmosphere. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (Comparison Table):**

| Basis | SLM | MJF |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Ytterbium fiber laser (1070 nm) | Infrared (IR) lamps, area-wise exposure |
| **Material Compatibility** | Metals (SS 316L, Ti-6Al-4V, AlSi10Mg, Inconel) | Thermoplastics (PA12, PA11, TPU, PP) |
| **Achievable Density** | >99% theoretical (fully dense) | ~95% theoretical (sintered/dense) |
| **Build Atmosphere** | Inert (Argon / Nitrogen, O₂ < 500 ppm) | Ambient air |
| **Typical Layer Thickness** | 20 – 50 µm | 70 – 100 µm |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in SLM / MJF Questions**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in VED Calculation:** Many students write $h = 100$ µm instead of $h = 0.1$ mm. Always convert µm → mm before substituting into $E_v = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$ or you will get a 1000× error.
> 2. **Confusing SLS with SLM:** SLS = sintering (below melting); SLM = full melting. Examiners deduct 1 mark per question where this distinction is wrong.
> 3. **Omitting Atmosphere Details for SLM:** A complete SLM answer must mention the **inert atmosphere** and the **oxygen threshold (<500 ppm)**. Skipping this is a 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Forgetting Detailing Agent in MJF:** Many students describe only the fusing agent. Detailing agent is **mandatory** for full marks in MJF questions.
> 5. **No Diagram Penalty:** KTU examiners specifically award marks for "neat labelled diagrams." A textual-only answer for a "with neat diagram" question loses 2 marks typically.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — SLM and MJF**

- **SLM** = Powder Bed Fusion with **laser** that **fully melts** **metal** powder → **>99% dense** metallic parts.
- **MJF** = Powder Bed Fusion with **IR lamps** + **fusing & detailing agents** → **~95% dense** polymer parts.
- **Volumetric Energy Density (VED):** $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ — optimum for SS 316L is **60 – 100 J/mm³**.
- **Layer thickness:** SLM uses **20 – 50 µm**, MJF uses **70 – 100 µm**.
- **Atmosphere:** SLM requires **inert gas** (O₂ < 500 ppm); MJF operates in **air**.
- **Critical SLM defects:** lack of fusion porosity (low VED), keyhole porosity (high VED), balling, residual stress.
- **Critical MJF parameters:** fusing agent saturation, detailing agent positioning, IR exposure time, powder refresh ratio (~20%).
- **ASTM F2792 classification:** SLM = Powder Bed Fusion; MJF = Material Jetting (often cited as hybrid).
- **Mechanical anisotropy:** SLM = moderate; MJF = near-isotropic.
- **Industrial applications:** SLM → aerospace brackets, medical implants, conformal cooling inserts. MJF → functional prototypes, batch polymer production, customized consumer goods.
- **Common KTU examination keywords:** "powder bed fusion," "volumetric energy density," "fusing agent," "detailing agent," "inert atmosphere," "build rate."
- **Always convert µm → mm** before any numerical substitution into the VED formula.
- **Always draw a block diagram** for full-marks SLM/MJF descriptive answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Phenomena
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process Phenomena

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Process Phenomena" in Additive Manufacturing?

In the context of Additive Manufacturing (AM), **Process Phenomena** refers to the set of coupled physical, thermal, chemical, metallurgical, and fluid-mechanical events that occur *simultaneously* inside and around the small active zone (the "process zone") where material is being joined, melted, sintered, or cured to build a part layer-by-layer.

Unlike conventional (subtractive) manufacturing where the cutting zone is well-understood and largely steady-state, AM processes operate with **highly localized, transient, and non-equilibrium** events occurring in a matter of **microseconds to milliseconds**, inside volumes often smaller than **1 mm³**. These events collectively determine the final part's:

- **Density** and internal porosity
- **Residual stresses** and distortion
- **Microstructure** and grain morphology
- **Surface roughness**
- **Mechanical properties** (strength, fatigue, hardness)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> *"Process phenomena in additive manufacturing encompass the multi-physics events — heat transfer, mass transfer, fluid flow, solidification, phase transformations, and residual stress evolution — that govern the quality, microstructure, and mechanical performance of additively manufactured parts across all seven ISO/ASTM 52900 process categories."*

---

## 1.2 The Five Coupled Phenomena (Core Pillar Concept)

Almost every AM process (except material jetting of droplets) involves **five coupled physical phenomena** occurring concurrently:

| # | Phenomenon | Typical Driver | Key Outcome Controlled |
|---|------------|----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Heat Transfer** | Energy source (laser, electron beam, arc, heated nozzle) | Melt pool size, thermal gradient, cooling rate |
| 2 | **Mass Transfer & Fluid Flow** | Surface tension, Marangoni forces, recoil pressure, buoyancy | Track geometry, wetting, balling |
| 3 | **Solidification & Phase Change** | Latent heat release, undercooling | Grain structure, dendrite spacing |
| 4 | **Chemical Reactions / Kinetics** | Photopolymerization (UV), binder infiltration, oxidation | Curing depth, cross-link density |
| 5 | **Stress Evolution** | Thermal contraction, phase-volume change, prior-layer constraint | Residual stress, warpage, cracking |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Welding Analogy:**
> Think of an AM process as performing **thousands of miniature spot-welds in rapid succession**, each on top of the previous one, while a thin new layer of powder/resin is spread. Just as a bad spot weld leaves a weak joint, a poorly controlled process phenomenon (e.g., insufficient melting, balling, or excessive residual stress) leaves a defect that propagates layer-by-layer. The "micro-weld" you make today determines the strength of the entire 1000-layer part you build tomorrow.

---

## 1.3 Why Process Phenomena Matter (The "Hidden Variable")

A KTU examiner often tests whether students understand that **process parameters (P, V, h, t)** alone do not determine part quality — the **process phenomena** they induce *do*. For example:

- Two different machines with the same laser power **P = 200 W** and scan speed **v = 800 mm/s** can produce vastly different parts if one has a powder with **higher absorptivity** or the build plate has **different thermal conductivity**.

This is why AM research focuses heavily on **in-situ monitoring** (pyrometers, high-speed cameras, melt-pool thermography) — to *observe* the phenomena, not just the inputs.

---

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following constants and metrics are repeatedly invoked in AM process-phenomena calculations and are **high-yield for KTU exams**:

- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** σ = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**
- **Emissivity** (typical for Ti-6Al-4V at T_melt ≈ 1923 K): ε ≈ **0.4 – 0.6**
- **Latent heat of fusion** for common AM metals:
  - Ti-6Al-4V: L_f ≈ **286 kJ/kg**
  - SS 316L: L_f ≈ **272 kJ/kg**
  - Inconel 718: L_f ≈ **227 kJ/kg**
- **Surface tension of molten metals** (at melting point): γ ≈ **1.0 – 1.8 N/m**
- **Beer-Lambert absorption length** for laser in metal powder: ℓ ≈ **(1/R)·ln(1/(1-A))** (a derived relation for effective penetration)
- **Glass transition temperature** (Tg) and **melting temperature** (Tm) of polymers — fundamental to FFF/FDM phenomena.
- **Dimensionless numbers**: **Marangoni number (Ma)**, **Reynolds number (Re)**, **Peclet number (Pe)** — used to characterize the dominant driving force inside the melt pool.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Heat Transfer Phenomena (The Dominant Phenomenon in PBF, DED, EBM)

Heat transfer in AM occurs through **three simultaneous modes**:

### 2.1.1 Conduction (Fourier's Law)

Heat flow through solid material (built layers, substrate, powder bed):

$$q_{cond} = -k \nabla T$$

Where:
- **q_cond** = conductive heat flux (W/m²)
- **k** = thermal conductivity of the material (W/m·K)
- **∇T** = temperature gradient (K/m)

For powder beds, **k_eff** (effective conductivity) is dramatically lower than the solid bulk — typically **1% to 10%** of bulk value — because the powder contains trapped gas/vacuum. This is why the **first few layers** are critical: heat cannot escape downward easily into the loose powder.

### 2.1.2 Convection (Newton's Law of Cooling)

Heat carried away by shielding gas (Argon, Nitrogen) flowing across the build:

$$q_{conv} = h_c \,(T_s - T_{\infty})$$

Where:
- **h_c** = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), typically **10–200 W/m²·K** in AM chambers
- **T_s** = surface temperature, **T_∞** = ambient/gas temperature

> [!NOTE]
> In Selective Laser Melting (SLM), the **recoil pressure** from vaporization accelerates vapor **upward** (away from melt pool), and this vapor plume actually *opposes* the downward shielding-gas flow, creating complex **counter-flow convection** patterns.

### 2.1.3 Radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann Law)

Dominant at high temperatures, particularly for **Electron Beam Melting (EBM)** where surface temperatures can exceed **2500 K**:

$$q_{rad} = \varepsilon \,\sigma \,(T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$$

Where:
- **ε** = emissivity (0 to 1)
- **σ** = **Stefan-Boltzmann constant = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**
- **T_s, T_sur** = absolute temperatures (K)

---

## 2.2 Mass Transfer & Fluid Flow inside the Melt Pool

Once a metal powder absorbs enough energy to melt, the resulting **molten pool** behaves like a tiny, highly dynamic, capillary-driven fluid system. The dominant forces are:

### 2.2.1 Surface Tension (Capillary Force)
Surface tension **γ** tries to **minimize surface area** → drives the melt pool into a spherical shape. The **Laplace pressure** across a curved interface is:

$$\Delta P_{Laplace} = \gamma \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \right)$$

### 2.2.2 Marangoni (Thermo-capillary) Convection
Surface tension varies with temperature. If **dγ/dT < 0** (most metals: γ decreases as T increases), fluid flows from **hot center → cold edges**, creating outward flow. This is the **Marangoni effect**, characterized by the dimensionless **Marangoni number**:

$$Ma = \frac{\vert d\gamma/dT \vert \cdot \Delta T \cdot L}{\mu \cdot \alpha}$$

Where:
- **ΔT** = temperature difference across pool
- **L** = characteristic length (melt pool radius)
- **μ** = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- **α** = thermal diffusivity (m²/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surfactants Matter:** Adding trace elements like **Sulphur (S)** to a steel powder reverses **dγ/dT** to positive, causing **inward Marangoni flow**, which deepens the melt pool and improves keyhole stability. This is exploited in **maraging steel (18Ni300)** AM.

### 2.2.3 Recoil Pressure
When the local temperature exceeds the **boiling point**, vaporization creates a recoil force pushing the melt pool downward, often producing a deep **keyhole**:

$$P_{recoil} = \frac{P_0}{\sqrt{T_s/T_b}} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{L_v \cdot M}{R_g}\left(\frac{1}{T_b} - \frac{1}{T_s}\right)\right)$$

Where **L_v** = latent heat of vaporization, **M** = molar mass, **R_g** = universal gas constant.

### 2.2.4 Buoyancy (Gravity-driven)
Weaker than Marangoni but always present. Driven by density difference:

$$\Delta P_{buoy} = \rho g \beta \Delta T$$

Where **β** = thermal expansion coefficient.

---

## 2.3 Solidification Phenomena (Microstructure Control)

Solidification is governed by two key local variables:

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | What it Controls |
|-----------|--------|------|------------------|
| **Thermal Gradient** | G | K/m | Direction of heat flow |
| **Solidification Growth Rate** | R | m/s | Speed of the solid-liquid interface |

The product **G × R** = **Cooling Rate** (K/s) — controls **grain size**:

$$\lambda_{DAS} = A \cdot (G \cdot R)^{-n}$$

Where **λ_DAS** = dendrite arm spacing (μm), and **A, n** are material constants (for Ti-6Al-4V: A ≈ 80, n ≈ 0.33).

The ratio **G/R** controls the **solidification morphology**:

| G/R Ratio | Morphology | Typical AM Location |
|-----------|------------|---------------------|
| **Very High** | Planar front | First layers on substrate |
| **High** | Cellular | Lower bulk of melt pool |
| **Medium** | Columnar dendritic | Most of melt pool in SLM |
| **Low** | Equiaxed dendritic | Top of melt pool / EBM |

---

## 2.4 Constitutional Supercooling Criterion (KSU/JNTU/KTU Favourite)

A solidification front becomes **unstable** (forming dendrites/cells) when:

$$\frac{G}{R} < \frac{m \, C_0 \, (1-k_0)}{D_L \, k_0}$$

Where:
- **m** = liquidus slope in phase diagram (K/wt%)
- **C₀** = initial solute concentration
- **k₀** = partition coefficient
- **D_L** = solute diffusivity in liquid (m²/s)

Violation of this criterion → dendrites, segregation, hot cracking.

---

## 2.5 The "Balling" Phenomenon (Classic AM Defect)

**Balling** is the break-up of a continuous molten track into a row of spherical balls due to **Rayleigh-Plateau capillary instability**. It occurs when:

- Scan speed is too **high** (track too thin, surface tension dominates)
- Scan speed is too **low** (excessive heat, oxidation, wetting failure)
- Oxygen content in chamber is too **high** (oxide film prevents wetting)

The classical **Rayleigh criterion** for a cylindrical liquid jet:

$$\lambda_{crit} = \pi \sqrt{2} \cdot d_{jet}$$

A liquid cylinder longer than **λ_crit** breaks into spheres. In AM, this happens when **track length / track width > ~3.5**.

---

## 2.6 Residual Stress — The "Invisible Killer"

During cooling, the just-solidified layer wants to **contract** (thermal strain = α·ΔT) but is restrained by the cooler, stiffer substrate/previous layers. This generates **thermal stress**:

$$\sigma_{thermal} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot f(constraint)$$

Where **E** = Young's modulus, **α** = coefficient of thermal expansion.

If **σ_thermal > Yield Strength** → **plastic deformation** is locked in, producing **residual stress** that manifests as **warpage** and **delamination** (especially in SLM of Ti-6Al-4V and Inconel 718).

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Typical Use in AM |
|---|---------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **q = k∇T** | Fourier conduction | Heat flow in substrate/powder |
| 2 | **q = h_c(T_s − T_∞)** | Newton cooling | Shielding gas effect |
| 3 | **q = εσ(T⁴ − T_sur⁴)** | Stefan-Boltzmann radiation | EBM, high-T SLM |
| 4 | **Ma = \|dγ/dT\|·ΔT·L/(μ·α)** | Marangoni number | Marangoni flow strength |
| 5 | **Pe = v·L/α** | Peclet number | Advection vs diffusion in melt pool |
| 6 | **Re = ρvL/μ** | Reynolds number | Flow regime (laminar/turbulent) |
| 7 | **G·R** = Cooling rate | Microstructure | Dendrite spacing |
| 8 | **G/R** = Morphology map | Solidification mode | Columnar vs equiaxed |
| 9 | **σ_th = E·α·ΔT** | Thermal stress | Residual stress estimate |
| 10 | **λ_DAS = A·(G·R)⁻ⁿ** | Dendrite arm spacing | Microstructure prediction |
| 11 | **A = 1 − R_reflected/R_incident** | Absorptivity | Laser-matter coupling |
| 12 | **I(z) = I₀·exp(−βz)** | Beer-Lambert | Laser penetration in powder |

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU exam, you can draw the **Solidification Map (G vs R)** — it is a *single diagram* worth **4–5 marks** if you label axes, units, regimes, and AM process positions correctly. Practice it!

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Beer-Lambert Absorption of Laser Energy in Powder Bed

When a laser penetrates a powder bed, intensity decays exponentially. Let us derive the **effective absorption length**.

Let **I(z)** = intensity at depth z inside the powder bed, and **I₀** = incident intensity at z = 0.

The elementary absorption in an infinitesimal slice **dz** is proportional to the intensity at that depth and the powder's absorption coefficient **β**:

$$dI = -\beta \cdot I(z) \cdot dz$$

This is a first-order linear ODE. Rearranging:

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -\beta \, dz$$

Integrating both sides from **z = 0** to **z**:

$$\int_{I_0}^{I(z)} \frac{dI}{I} = -\beta \int_0^z dz$$

The left side integrates to **ln(I(z)) − ln(I₀) = ln(I(z)/I₀)**. The right side is simply **−βz**.

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I(z)}{I_0}\right) = -\beta \, z$$

Exponentiating:

$$\boxed{I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\beta z}}$$

This is the **Beer-Lambert Law** for laser attenuation in powder.

**Engineering Utility:** In SLM/DED, a typical absorption coefficient for a metal powder bed (e.g., 316L stainless steel at 1070 nm fiber laser wavelength) is **β ≈ 4 × 10⁵ m⁻¹**, giving an **optical penetration depth δ = 1/β ≈ 2.5 μm**. This means nearly all laser energy is deposited within the **top 5–10 μm** of the powder layer — which is why powder layer thickness (typically 20–50 μm) must be carefully controlled.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cooling Rate in terms of Process Parameters

In Laser Powder Bed Fusion (L-PBF), the cooling rate at the solid-liquid interface can be derived from first principles using the **Rosenthal thin-plate solution** for a moving point heat source.

For a Gaussian laser beam moving with scan speed **v** along the x-axis on a semi-infinite plate, the steady-state temperature field far from the heat source is:

$$T - T_0 = \frac{P}{2 \pi k \, r} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{v \, (x + r)}{2 \alpha}\right)$$

Where:
- **P** = laser power (W)
- **k** = thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
- **α** = thermal diffusivity (m²/s)
- **r** = radial distance from beam center (m)
- **v** = scan speed (m/s)
- **T₀** = initial/substrate temperature (K)

The **thermal gradient** at the solid-liquid interface (ahead of the beam, where solidification begins) is obtained by differentiating T along the scan direction at the melt-pool trailing edge.

For the trailing edge, the rate of cooling is:

$$\dot{T} = \frac{dT}{dt}\bigg|_{interface} = -G \cdot v$$

Where **G** is the thermal gradient at the interface (K/m) and **v** is the scan speed. Substituting the gradient form from Rosenthal:

$$\boxed{\dot{T} \approx \frac{2 \pi k \, (T_m - T_0)^2}{P / v}}$$

Rearranged for the classic KTU form:

$$\boxed{\dot{T} \approx \frac{2 \pi k \, v \, (T_m - T_0)^2}{P}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- **Higher v (scan speed)** → **Higher cooling rate** ✓
- **Higher P (laser power)** → **Lower cooling rate** ✓
- **Higher k (conductivity)** → **Higher cooling rate** (heat escapes faster) ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **Ti-6Al-4V** with k ≈ 7 W/m·K, T_m − T₀ ≈ 1850 K, P = 200 W, v = 1 m/s:
>
> $$\dot{T} \approx \frac{2 \pi \times 7 \times 1 \times (1850)^2}{200} \approx 7.5 \times 10^5 \text{ K/s} = 0.75 \text{ MK/s}$$
>
> This matches experimentally measured cooling rates in SLM (~10⁵ – 10⁶ K/s), confirming the derivation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Marangoni Flow Velocity

We derive the characteristic velocity **v_Ma** of fluid flow inside a melt pool driven purely by surface tension gradient.

Surface tension gradient force per unit area (at the free surface):

$$F_{Ma} = \frac{d\gamma}{dT} \cdot \nabla T$$

This force is balanced by **viscous shear stress** in the boundary layer of thickness **δ**:

$$\tau_{visc} = \mu \cdot \frac{v_{Ma}}{\delta}$$

The thermal boundary layer thickness scales as **δ ~ √(α·L/v₀)** (where v₀ is some reference velocity), but for an order-of-magnitude estimate, take **δ ≈ L** (melt pool depth).

Setting **F_Ma = τ_visc**:

$$\frac{d\gamma}{dT} \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{L} = \mu \cdot \frac{v_{Ma}}{L^2}$$

Solving for **v_Ma**:

$$\boxed{v_{Ma} = \frac{\vert d\gamma/dT \vert \cdot \Delta T \cdot L}{\mu}}$$

This is the **characteristic Marangoni velocity**. The Marangoni number **Ma** is obtained by multiplying by L/α:

$$Ma = \frac{v_{Ma} \cdot L}{\alpha} = \frac{\vert d\gamma/dT \vert \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^2}{\mu \cdot \alpha}$$

(You may see both forms in textbooks — they are equivalent definitions depending on the characteristic length used.)

**Numerical Example:** For SS 316L melt pool with L = 100 μm = 10⁻⁴ m, ΔT = 200 K, |dγ/dT| = 10⁻⁴ N/(m·K), μ = 5 × 10⁻³ Pa·s:

$$v_{Ma} = \frac{10^{-4} \times 200 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.4 \text{ m/s}$$

This is **extremely fast** compared to the scan speed (0.1–1 m/s) — explaining why Marangoni flow dominates melt-pool dynamics in SLM.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Critical Scan Speed for Balling Instability

The Rayleigh-Plateau instability wavelength for a cylindrical jet of diameter **d_jet** is:

$$\lambda_{crit} = \pi \, d_{jet}$$

For instability to develop in a time **t_solidification** (the time the melt stays liquid), the surface perturbation must grow faster than solidification front moves:

$$t_{RP} < t_{solidification}$$

The characteristic Rayleigh-Plateau growth time is:

$$t_{RP} \sim \frac{\mu \, d_{jet}}{\gamma}$$

Solidification time (approximating the melt pool as cooling from T_m to T_solidus at cooling rate Ṫ):

$$t_{solidification} = \frac{T_m - T_{solidus}}{\dot{T}}$$

Substituting the cooling-rate expression:

$$t_{solidification} = \frac{(T_m - T_{solidus}) \cdot P}{2 \pi k \, v \, (T_m - T_0)^2}$$

Setting **t_RP = t_solidification** and solving for **v**:

$$\boxed{v_{critical} = \frac{(T_m - T_{solidus}) \cdot P}{2 \pi k \cdot \mu \cdot d_{jet} / \gamma \cdot (T_m - T_0)^2}}$$

This gives a **lower bound** on scan speed — below this, balling occurs due to **excessive oxidation and wetting failure** (slow scan = more oxygen exposure).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process Phenomena vs AM Process Family

This matrix is **highly examinable** and frequently appears in KTU Part B questions:

| Process Family | Dominant Heat Source | Primary Heat Transfer Mode | Dominant Fluid Flow Force | Main Solidification Feature | Typical Cooling Rate | Main Defect from Poor Phenomena Control |
|----------------|----------------------|----------------------------|---------------------------|----------------------------|----------------------|------------------------------------------|
| **SLM / DED (Laser)** | Fiber/Disc laser | Conduction + Radiation (at edges) | Marangoni + Recoil | Columnar dendrites, epitaxial growth | 10⁵ – 10⁶ K/s | Balling, porosity, residual stress |
| **EBM (Electron Beam)** | Electron beam (vacuum) | Conduction + **Strong radiation** | Marangoni (weaker, due to high T) | Coarser columnar grains | 10³ – 10⁴ K/s | Swirl, charging, smoke |
| **DED (Plasma / Arc)** | Plasma / TIG arc | Conduction + Convection (shielding gas) | Buoyancy + Marangoni | Coarse dendrites, multi-layer HAZ | 10² – 10³ K/s | Lack of fusion, distortion |
| **FDM / FFF** | Heated nozzle (conduction) | Conduction (polymer) | Polymer rheology (viscous flow) | Glass transition (no melt for semi-crystalline) | 10⁰ – 10¹ K/s | Warping, delamination, under-extrusion |
| **SLA / DLP (Vat)** | UV light | Photon absorption (Beer-Lambert) | None (solid polymer) | Photo-polymerization (cross-linking) | N/A (curing) | Over-curing, under-curing, stair-step |
| **Binder Jetting** | Heated printhead (binder) | Conduction (powder-binder wetting) | Capillary infiltration | Binder setting + sintering (post) | Slow | Powder spreading failure, bleed |
| **Material Jetting** | UV + heat | Photopolymerization + thermal | Droplet spreading | Curing + solidification | N/A | Satellite droplets, coffee ring |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example (KTU Numerical Style)

**Problem (Typical 14-mark derivation):**
*"A Ti-6Al-4V part is being built by Selective Laser Melting using a fiber laser of power 250 W and scan speed 1.2 m/s. The powder bed has thermal conductivity k = 7 W/m·K, T_m = 1923 K, T_0 = 353 K (preheated substrate), T_solidus = 1878 K. Estimate (a) cooling rate, (b) dendrite arm spacing if A = 80 μm·(K/s)^n and n = 0.33, and (c) comment on the resulting microstructure."*

### Solution:

**(a) Cooling Rate** (using formula from §3.2):

$$\dot{T} = \frac{2 \pi k \, v \, (T_m - T_0)^2}{P}$$

Substituting:

$$\dot{T} = \frac{2 \pi \times 7 \times 1.2 \times (1923 - 353)^2}{250}$$

$$\dot{T} = \frac{2 \pi \times 7 \times 1.2 \times (1570)^2}{250}$$

$$\dot{T} = \frac{2 \pi \times 7 \times 1.2 \times 2{,}464{,}900}{250}$$

$$\dot{T} = \frac{52.78 \times 2{,}464{,}900}{250}$$

$$\dot{T} \approx \frac{1.301 \times 10^{8}}{250} \approx 5.2 \times 10^{5} \text{ K/s}$$

**(b) Dendrite Arm Spacing:**

$$\lambda_{DAS} = A \cdot (\dot{T})^{-n}$$

$$\lambda_{DAS} = 80 \cdot (5.2 \times 10^{5})^{-0.33}$$

Compute exponent: **−0.33** of **5.2 × 10⁵**:

$$\log_{10}(5.2 \times 10^5) = 5.716$$

$$-0.33 \times 5.716 = -1.886$$

$$10^{-1.886} = 0.0130$$

So:

$$\lambda_{DAS} = 80 \times 0.0130 \approx 1.04 \text{ μm}$$

**(c) Microstructure Commentary:**

- Cooling rate of ~10⁶ K/s is **typical of SLM** (rapid solidification).
- DAS of ~1 μm is **much finer** than casting (~50–200 μm) or even wrought material.
- Result: **fine cellular/columnar β-Ti dendrites** with α' martensite on cooling — high strength, low ductility (typical AM Ti-6Al-4V behavior, requiring HIP for property optimization).

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q:** Define the **Marangoni effect** in the context of additive manufacturing. State how it influences the melt pool dynamics in laser powder bed fusion.

**Model Answer:**

The **Marangoni effect** (also called thermo-capillary convection) is the fluid flow inside a molten pool driven by a **surface tension gradient along the free surface**, which arises because surface tension **γ** varies with temperature according to **dγ/dT**.

In laser-based powder bed fusion (L-PBF):
- Most metals have **dγ/dT < 0** (surface tension decreases as temperature rises). This causes fluid to flow from the **hot center** of the melt pool toward the **cooler edges**, producing an **outward Marangoni flow** that results in a **wide, shallow melt pool**.
- If **surfactant elements** like **sulphur or oxygen** are present (above critical concentration), the sign of **dγ/dT reverses to positive**, causing **inward Marangoni flow**, which produces a **narrow, deep melt pool** (closer to keyhole mode).
- Marangoni flow is the **dominant fluid flow mechanism** in L-PBF melt pools, often overpowering buoyancy by an order of magnitude, and is characterized by the dimensionless **Marangoni number (Ma)**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q:** Explain the **balling phenomenon** in laser powder bed fusion. List any **three process parameters** whose improper selection leads to balling.

**Model Answer:**

**Balling** is a process-phenomena defect in L-PBF where a continuous molten track, instead of remaining as a smooth line, **breaks up into a series of discrete spherical or ellipsoidal balls** due to the **Rayleigh-Plateau capillary instability**. The molten metal, attempting to minimize its surface energy, contracts into spheres because the cylindrical track geometry is hydrodynamically unstable when its length-to-diameter ratio exceeds a critical value (≈ **π√2 ≈ 4.44**).

The three process parameters whose improper selection causes balling are:

1. **Excessively high scan speed** — the melt pool becomes too long and thin; surface tension dominates over wetting, causing the track to pinch off into spheres.

2. **Excessively low scan speed (or excessive laser power)** — the melt pool stays liquid for too long, allowing oxide layers (in metals like Ti, Al) to form on the surface, preventing proper wetting of the underlying substrate.

3. **Insufficient energy density** (combination of low power + high speed) — leads to **lack of fusion** between successive tracks, where the molten material cannot adequately wet the previously solidified layer.

Other contributing factors include **high oxygen content in the build chamber**, **poor powder flowability**, and **improper powder layer thickness**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**Q:** With the aid of neat diagrams, describe in detail the **various process phenomena** occurring during **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** of metal powders. Explain how these phenomena collectively determine the final part quality.

#### Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the heat transfer modes, melt pool formation, and fluid flow phenomena inside the SLM melt pool.**

#### Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the solidification phenomena, microstructure evolution, residual stress development, and key defects (balling, porosity) arising from improper control of these phenomena.**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### (a) Heat Transfer and Melt Pool Phenomena (7 Marks)

**Heat Transfer Modes (3 marks):**

In SLM, a high-power fiber laser (typically 200–500 W, wavelength 1060–1070 nm) interacts with the metal powder bed. Once energy is absorbed, three modes of heat transfer operate simultaneously:

1. **Conduction (Fourier's Law):** Heat flows from the melt pool into the previously solidified layers and the substrate. Because the loose powder has very low effective thermal conductivity (**k_eff** is about 1–10% of bulk solid), most heat flows **downward into the previously solidified bulk material**. The conductive heat flux is:

$$q_{cond} = -k \nabla T$$

2. **Convection (Newton's Law):** The shielding gas (Argon or Nitrogen) flowing across the build removes heat through forced convection:

$$q_{conv} = h_c \,(T_s - T_{\infty})$$

A counter-flow develops because the vapor plume (from keyhole vaporization) rises and opposes the downward shielding gas.

3. **Radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann Law):** At high temperatures (>2000 K for Ti alloys), surface radiation becomes significant:

$$q_{rad} = \varepsilon \,\sigma \,(T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$$

**Melt Pool Formation and Fluid Flow (4 marks):**

The absorbed laser energy melts a small volume of powder, forming a **teardrop-shaped melt pool** roughly 100–200 μm wide and 50–100 μm deep. Inside this pool, four fluid-flow forces compete:

- **Marangoni (thermo-capillary) force** — the dominant flow, governed by **dγ/dT**. Outward flow (most metals) → wide/shallow pool. Inward flow (S-rich steels) → narrow/deep pool.
- **Recoil pressure** from vaporization → pushes melt downward, can create **keyhole porosity** if too strong.
- **Buoyancy** from density difference → weak secondary flow.
- **Laplace (capillary) pressure** → drives wetting of underlying layer.

The relative dominance is given by dimensionless numbers:
- **Ma** (Marangoni number, typically 10² – 10⁴ in SLM)
- **Re** (Reynolds number, typically 10–1000, indicating laminar to mild turbulent)
- **Pe** (Peclet number, Pe >> 1, indicating advection dominates diffusion)

The shape of the melt pool is determined by the balance of energy input (Power/Speed) and heat loss (conduction into substrate).

---

#### (b) Solidification, Microstructure, Residual Stress, and Defects (7 Marks)

**Solidification Phenomena (3 marks):**

As the laser beam moves away, the trailing edge of the melt pool cools rapidly. Solidification occurs with two key local parameters:
- **Thermal Gradient G (K/m)** — direction of heat extraction
- **Solidification Growth Rate R (m/s)** — speed of the S/L interface

The **cooling rate Ṫ = G·R** (typically 10⁵ – 10⁶ K/s in SLM) determines the dendrite arm spacing:

$$\lambda_{DAS} = A \cdot (G \cdot R)^{-n}$$

The ratio **G/R** determines the solidification morphology per the **constitutional supercooling criterion**:

$$\frac{G}{R} < \frac{m \, C_0 \, (1-k_0)}{D_L \, k_0} \Rightarrow \text{dendritic}$$

In SLM, conditions favor **columnar dendritic growth** with epitaxial alignment to the build direction, often producing **anisotropic mechanical properties**.

**Residual Stress (2 marks):**

On cooling, the just-solidified layer contracts by thermal strain **ε = α·ΔT** but is constrained by the cooler substrate, generating tensile stress:

$$\sigma_{thermal} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

If this exceeds the **yield strength** at high temperature, plastic strain is locked in, producing **residual stress** that manifests as **warpage, delamination, and cracking**. Remedies include **preheating (200–400 °C)**, **scan strategy optimization** (e.g., chessboard, island scanning), and **support structures**.

**Key Defects (2 marks):**

- **Balling** — capillary instability breaking the track into spheres (covered in Part A).
- **Porosity** — three types:
  (i) **Lack-of-fusion porosity** (irregular shape, from insufficient energy density)
  (ii) **Keyhole porosity** (spherical, from excessive energy density → deep keyhole collapses)
  (iii) **Gas porosity** (small, spherical, from entrapped shielding gas or powder gas)

Control of these defects requires careful balancing of the **volumetric energy density (VED)**:

$$VED = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad [\text{J/mm}^3]$$

where P = laser power, v = scan speed, h = hatch spacing, t = layer thickness. Typical optimum range for Ti-6Al-4V is **40–80 J/mm³**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q:** Explain the **solidification phenomena** in additive manufacturing processes in detail. With the help of a **G vs R solidification map**, discuss how process parameters control the final microstructure.

#### Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the relationship between cooling rate, dendrite arm spacing, and process parameters. Show how cooling rate depends on laser power and scan speed.**

#### Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks
**Draw and explain the G vs R solidification map. Mark on it the typical operating regimes of (i) SLM, (ii) EBM, (iii) DED, and (iv) investment casting. Discuss how constitutional supercooling affects the resulting microstructure.**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### (a) Derivation of Cooling Rate and DAS (7 Marks)

**Starting from Rosenthal's equation** for a moving point heat source in a semi-infinite plate:

$$T - T_0 = \frac{P}{2 \pi k \, r} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{v(x + r)}{2 \alpha}\right)$$

**Step 1:** The thermal gradient **G** at the solid-liquid interface (along scan direction at trailing edge of melt pool) is:

$$G = \left.\frac{dT}{dx}\right|_{interface} = \frac{2 \pi k (T_m - T_0)^2}{P / v} \cdot \frac{1}{v} \cdot (\text{geometric factor})$$

**Step 2:** At the interface, the solidification front velocity equals the scan speed (in the limit of small solidification angle): **R ≈ v**.

**Step 3:** Therefore, the **cooling rate** is:

$$\boxed{\dot{T} = G \cdot R \approx \frac{2 \pi k \, v \, (T_m - T_0)^2}{P}}$$

**Step 4:** The **secondary dendrite arm spacing (DAS)** is empirically related to cooling rate via:

$$\lambda_{DAS} = A \cdot (\dot{T})^{-n}$$

Substituting Ṫ:

$$\boxed{\lambda_{DAS} = A \cdot \left(\frac{P}{2 \pi k \, v \, (T_m - T_0)^2}\right)^{n}}$$

**Step 5:** Numerical substitution for Ti-6Al-4V (A = 80 μm·(K/s)^n, n = 0.33, k = 7 W/m·K, T_m − T₀ = 1570 K, P = 200 W, v = 1 m/s):

$$\dot{T} = \frac{2 \pi \cdot 7 \cdot 1 \cdot (1570)^2}{200} \approx 5.4 \times 10^5 \text{ K/s}$$

$$\lambda_{DAS} = 80 \cdot (5.4 \times 10^5)^{-0.33} \approx 1.0 \text{ μm}$$

This explains why SLM Ti-6Al-4V exhibits **ultra-fine microstructures** with DAS ~1 μm (compared to ~50 μm in cast material).

---

#### (b) Solidification Map and Constitutional Supercooling (7 Marks)

**G vs R Solidification Map** (Draw the following in exam):

```
   G (K/m)  ▲
             │
   10⁶       │      ┃ Planar
             │      ┃
   10⁵       │      ┃    ┃ Cellular
             │      ┃    ┃
   10⁴       │ SLM  ┃    ┃   ┃ Columnar Dendrites
             │  ●───┃────┃───┃
   10³       │      ┃    ┃   ┃    ┃ Equiaxed Dendrites
             │      ┃    ┃   ┃    ┃
   10²       │      ┃ EBM        DED ●
             │      ┃  ●          ┃
             │      ┃            ┃
   10¹       │      ┃      Investment Casting ●
             │      ┃            ┃
             └──────────────────────────► R (m/s)
             10⁻⁴  10⁻³  10⁻²  10⁻¹  10⁰
```

**Position of processes:**

| Process | Approx. G (K/m) | Approx. R (m/s) | Morphology |
|---------|-----------------|-----------------|------------|
| **Investment Casting** | 10¹ – 10² | 10⁻⁴ – 10⁻³ | Coarse columnar / equiaxed (50–500 μm DAS) |
| **DED (laser cladding)** | 10³ – 10⁴ | 10⁻³ – 10⁻² | Coarse columnar |
| **EBM** | 10³ – 10⁴ | 10⁻³ – 10⁻² | Columnar with equiaxed top |
| **SLM** | 10⁵ – 10⁶ | 10⁻² – 10⁻¹ | Fine columnar with cellular substructure |

**Constitutional Supercooling** (3 marks):

A planar solidification front remains stable only when:

$$\frac{G}{R} \geq \frac{m \, C_0 \, (1-k_0)}{D_L \, k_0}$$

In SLM, due to the very high scan speed (R is large), the ratio **G/R** is small, and the constitutional supercooling criterion is **violated**. This is why SLM microstructures are predominantly **dendritic** rather than planar. The same violation produces **segregation** of alloying elements and can cause **hot cracking** in alloys like Inconel 718 if not properly controlled.

In contrast, in **investment casting**, slow cooling gives a high G/R ratio → planar or cellular solidification possible, producing coarser but more homogeneous structures.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "G × R" with "G / R":** G × R = Cooling rate. G / R = Solidification morphology map. Many students mix these up — **this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical problems:** Always write **units** (K/m, m/s, K/s) for G, R, Ṫ. KTU examiners specifically check for unit consistency.
>
> 3. **Wrong direction of Marangoni flow:** Most metals have dγ/dT < 0 → **outward** flow (NOT inward). Only surfactant-rich alloys have inward flow. **Wrong direction = 1 mark lost**.
>
> 4. **Confusing *conduction mode* vs *keyhole mode* melt pools:** Conduction mode is **wide and shallow**, with porosity only from lack-of-fusion. Keyhole mode is **narrow and deep**, prone to gas-entrapped spherical porosity. Do not interchange.
>
> 5. **Omitting the G vs R diagram** when asked about microstructure — this alone is 3–4 marks.
>
> 6. **Using "powder size" instead of "particle size distribution"** — always use the precise technical term.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### 🔑 Core Concepts (Must Memorize)

- [ ] **Five coupled phenomena**: Heat transfer, mass transfer/fluid flow, solidification, chemical reactions, stress evolution.
- [ ] **Beer-Lambert Law**: I(z) = I₀·e^(−βz) — laser absorption in powder bed.
- [ ] **Marangoni number**: Ma = |dγ/dT|·ΔT·L/(μ·α) — dominant fluid-flow driver.
- [ ] **Rayleigh-Plateau criterion**: λ_crit = π√2·d_jet — onset of balling.
- [ ] **Cooling rate**: Ṫ = G·R ≈ 2πk·v·(T_m − T₀)²/P
- [ ] **DAS formula**: λ_DAS = A·(G·R)⁻ⁿ — predicts microstructural fineness.
- [ ] **Constitutional supercooling**: G/R < m·C₀·(1−k₀)/(D_L·k₀) → unstable planar front → dendrites.
- [ ] **Stefan-Boltzmann constant** σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴
- [ ] **Emissivity** of typical AM metals at T_melt: ε ≈ 0.4–0.6

### 🧠 Key Phenomena by AM Process

- [ ] **SLM / DED**: Marangoni + recoil pressure dominate; cooling rate 10⁵–10⁶ K/s; columnar dendritic microstructure.
- [ ] **EBM**: Radiation dominant (vacuum); coarser microstructure due to slower cooling (10³–10⁴ K/s).
- [ ] **FDM/FFF**: Polymer rheology + crystallization (semi-crystalline) or glass transition (amorphous); warping is the main residual-stress issue.
- [ ] **SLA/DLP**: Beer-Lambert UV penetration; curing depth controlled by exposure energy (E = I·t).
- [ ] **Binder Jetting**: Capillary infiltration of binder; post-process sintering causes shrinkage (~20%).

### 🛠️ Engineering Levers (Parameter-to-Phenomena Mapping)

| Phenomenon | Primary Process Lever |
|------------|----------------------|
| Melt pool size | Laser power P, Scan speed v |
| Marangoni flow direction | Sulphur / Oxygen content |
| Cooling rate | P/v ratio (energy density) |
| Residual stress | Preheat T, scan strategy, support structures |
| Porosity | Volumetric energy density (VED) |
| Surface roughness | Layer thickness t, hatch spacing h |

### ⚠️ Common Defects — Phenomena Origin

| Defect | Phenomena Responsible |
|--------|----------------------|
| Lack-of-fusion porosity | Insufficient energy / poor wetting |
| Keyhole porosity | Excess energy / recoil pressure collapse |
| Balling | Rayleigh-Plateau instability + oxidation |
| Cracking | Residual stress > yield strength |
| Warpage | Thermal contraction mismatch |
| Anisotropic strength | Columnar grain alignment with build direction |

### 📚 Frequently Asked in KTU (PYQ Pattern)

1. **Define Marangoni effect** (3 marks) — almost every KTU exam since 2020.
2. **Sketch and explain G vs R solidification map** (7 marks) — appears in both Part A and Part B.
3. **Differentiate SLM and EBM based on process phenomena** (7 marks) — extremely common.
4. **Explain balling, its causes, and remedies** (3–7 marks).
5. **Derive cooling rate from Rosenthal equation** (7 marks).
6. **Discuss residual stress in AM and methods to mitigate** (7 marks).
7. **Constitutional supercooling in AM** (3–7 marks) — high-yield topic.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** In the KTU 14-mark Part B question, always structure your answer as: **(1) Diagram (2 marks)** → **(2) Definitions (2 marks)** → **(3) Mechanism explanation (4 marks)** → **(4) Process-parameter mapping (3 marks)** → **(5) Engineering implications/remedies (3 marks)**. This 2-2-4-3-3 pattern matches the KTU marking rubric and ensures you don't lose marks for poor structure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Powder Handling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Powder Handling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Powder Handling in Additive Manufacturing** is defined as the integrated set of operations, procedures, and engineering controls that govern the **production, characterization, storage, conditioning, transportation, recycling, and safe disposal** of particulate feedstock materials (metallic, polymeric, or ceramic powders) used in powder-bed fusion (PBF), directed energy deposition (DED), and binder jetting AM processes.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, powder handling is recognized as a **cross-functional discipline** that directly influences part density, mechanical properties, surface finish, repeatability, and operator safety. It is a **Process Map (P-Map)** level input under ASTM F3434 and ISO/ASTM 52900.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Bakery Flour Model 🍞**
>
> Imagine running a high-end artisan bakery where the *flour* is your raw material.
> - You **source** it carefully (powder production via atomization).
> - You **inspect** the grain size and moisture (powder characterization).
> - You **store** it in sealed, dry, temperature-controlled silos (powder storage).
> - You **sift** it before every use to remove lumps (sieving/conditioning).
> - You **recycle** surplus flour but never blindly (powder reuse limits).
> - You **protect** it from humidity, contamination, and pests (contamination control).
> - You handle **fine combustible dust** with care (explosion safety, similar to flour-dust explosions in real bakeries).
>
> In AM, the powder is your "flour" — only the consequences of mishandling are far costlier because the powder typically costs **₹ 8,000 – 40,000 per kg** for aerospace-grade Ti-6Al-4V or Inconel 718.

## 1.3 Why Powder Handling is a Critical Module Topic

| Driver | Engineering Impact |
|---|---|
| Powder cost (esp. Ti, Ni-superalloys) | Recycling efficiency directly affects part economics |
| Powder morphology & PSD | Determines flowability, layer uniformity, and final density |
| Reactive metals (Ti, Al, Mg) | Fire / explosion risk — requires argon-purged glove boxes |
| Contamination (O, N, moisture pickup) | Causes porosity, cracking, and embrittlement |
| Health hazards (inhalable fines < 10 µm) | Respiratory diseases (e.g., cobalt lung, hard-metal disease) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard reference metrics used universally:
> - **PSD range for LPBF:** **15 – 45 µm** (typical), **10 – 63 µm** (broader)
> - **Apparent density of Ti-6Al-4V powder:** **~ 2.55 g/cc**
> - **Hall Flow rate target:** **< 30 s for 50 g** (ASTM B213)

---

## 1.4 Scope of Powder Handling (5 Sub-Domains)

1. **Powder Production** — atomization, mechanical comminution, chemical synthesis.
2. **Powder Characterization** — physical, chemical, rheological testing.
3. **Powder Storage & Conditioning** — silos, sieving, drying, blending.
4. **Powder Feeding & In-Process Flow** — recoater hoppers, DED powder feeders.
5. **Powder Recycling, Safety & Disposal** — sieving, blending ratios, MIE/MEC analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step 1 — Powder Production Methods

The **feedstock origin** dictates every downstream property. KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes four methods:

### (a) Gas Atomization (GA)
- Molten metal stream impinged by high-pressure **inert gas (Ar / N₂)** jet.
- Produces **spherical, satellite-rich** powders.
- Most common for LPBF/EBM.
- Typical yield (15–45 µm) ≈ **60 – 75 %**.

### (b) Plasma Atomization (PA) / Plasma Rotating Electrode Process (PREP)
- Wire or melt fed into plasma torch / rotating electrode arc.
- Yields **highly spherical, low-satellite, low-oxide** powders.
- Used for **Ti, Nb, reactive metals**.

### (c) Water Atomization (WA)
- Uses high-pressure **water jets** — cheaper, but powder is **irregular, high-oxygen**.
- Suitable for **binder jetting** and **MIM**, not LPBF.

### (d) Mechanical Alloying / Milling
- High-energy ball milling — produces **non-spherical, work-hardened** powder.
- Used for **ODS steels, Al-Si alloys** in DED / cold spray.

## 2.2 Step 2 — Powder Characterization (The 7 Pillars)

> [!NOTE]
> Characterization is performed in two phases:
> - **Pre-use (incoming QC)** — sieving, PSD, morphology, chemistry, Hall flow.
> - **In-use (in-process QC)** — sieving, O/N content, blend-ratio tracking.

| # | Property | Test Method | Standard | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Particle Size Distribution (PSD) | Laser diffraction, sieve | ISO 13320, ASTM B214 | Layer thickness, recoat uniformity |
| 2 | Particle Morphology | SEM imaging, image analysis | ASTM B822 | Flowability, packing density |
| 3 | Apparent Density (AD) | Hall flowmeter cup | ASTM B212 | Layer mass consistency |
| 4 | Tap Density (TD) | Tap volumeter (3000 taps) | ASTM B527 | Packing efficiency |
| 5 | Hall Flow Rate (HFR) | Hall funnel + 50 g powder | ASTM B213 | Recoater speed selection |
| 6 | Hausner Ratio (HR) | TD / AD | — | Flow regime indicator |
| 7 | Carr Index (CI) | (TD − AD) / TD | ASTM D6393 | Compressibility indicator |

## 2.3 Step 3 — Storage, Conditioning & Sieving

- Powders are stored in **vacuum-sealed containers, argon-purged glove boxes, or climate-controlled silos (T < 25 °C, RH < 5 %)**.
- **Sieve cut-off** typically **63 µm** for LPBF and **100 µm** for DED.
- **Vacuum sieving** under inert gas is preferred for reactive metals.
- **Drying** of polymer powders (PA12, TPU) at **80 – 100 °C** for 4–6 h to drive off moisture (which causes *steam-pop* porosity in SLS).

## 2.4 Step 4 — In-Process Powder Feed Systems

- **LPBF / SLM:** gravity / vibratory hopper → **recoater blade or roller** spreading powder across the build plate.
- **EBM:** powder hoppers above and below the build rake (gravity-driven).
- **DED (laser cladding, blown powder):** **powder feeder** (e.g., Twin-10, Twin-12) with **carrier gas (Ar)** at **2 – 8 L/min**, powder feed rate **1 – 30 g/min**.

## 2.5 Step 5 — Recycling, Reuse & Disposal

- **Reuse Limit:** typically **10 – 30 cycles** before discard, governed by:
  - **Oxygen pickup** (O > 0.15 wt% in Ti is rejected).
  - **Nitrogen pickup** (N > 0.05 wt% in Ti is rejected).
  - **PSD drift** (D50 shift > 5 µm is rejected).
- **Refresh ratio:** typically **30 – 70 % new powder blended with recycled**.
- **Disposal:** Inert-encapsulated landfill for Ti/Al; **high-temperature oxidation + landfill** for steels.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in KTU ESE numericals & Part A derivations.**

| Symbol | Formula | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hausner Ratio (HR)** | $HR = \dfrac{\rho_{tap}}{\rho_{apparent}}$ | Tap density / Apparent density | dimensionless |
| **Carr Index (CI)** | $CI = \dfrac{\rho_{tap} - \rho_{apparent}}{\rho_{tap}} \times 100$ | Compressibility index | **%** |
| **Hall Flow Rate (HFR)** | $HFR = \dfrac{50\,g\, \text{of powder}}{t}$ | Time for 50 g to flow through Hall funnel | **s / 50 g** |
| **Sieve Retention %** | $R_i = \dfrac{m_i}{m_{total}} \times 100$ | Mass retained on sieve $i$ | **%** |
| **Cumulative Passing %** | $P(d) = 100 - \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | % powder passing sieve of size $d$ | **%** |
| **Specific Surface Area (SSA)** | $SSA = \dfrac{6}{\rho_p \cdot d_{50}}$ | For spherical particles | **m²/kg** |
| **Powder Layer Thickness** | $t_L = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.0) \times d_{max}$ | Recommended layer thickness | **µm** |
| **Sphericity** | $\Psi = \dfrac{4\pi A}{P^2}$ | Area / perimeter based | **0 → 1** |
| **Aspect Ratio (AR)** | $AR = \dfrac{d_{max}}{d_{min}}$ | For non-spherical particles | **≥ 1** |
| **Minimum Explosible Concentration** | $MEC \approx 50 - 500\,g/m^3$ | Empirical — fine metal dust | **g/m³** |
| **Minimum Ignition Energy** | $MIE \approx 1 - 100\,mJ$ | Powder-specific (Ti ≈ 10 mJ) | **mJ** |
| **Kst Explosion Index** | $K_{st} = \left(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3}$ | Dust explosion severity | **bar·m/s** |

### Engineering Interpretation of HR & CI

| HR Value | CI (%) | Flow Character | AM Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.00 – 1.11 | < 11 | Excellent | Ideal |
| 1.12 – 1.18 | 11 – 15 | Good | Acceptable |
| 1.19 – 1.25 | 16 – 20 | Fair | Marginal |
| 1.26 – 1.34 | 21 – 25 | Passable | Needs blending |
| 1.35 – 1.45 | 26 – 31 | Poor | Reject |
| > 1.46 | > 32 | Very Poor | Reject |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (Ti-6Al-4V LPBF):** Powder cost ~ ₹ 30,000/kg → 30 % savings via recycling translates to **₹ 9,000/kg** recovered cost.
- **Medical implants (Co-Cr, ASTM F75):** powder purity > 99.9 % ensures biocompatibility (ISO 5832-4).
- **Automotive (binder jetting of 316L):** bulk powder reuse up to 5× reduces per-part cost by 40 %.
- **Defence (Inconel 718 DED):** on-site powder recovery with sieving + blending extends mission-readiness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hausner Ratio & Carr Index from First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting principle:** A powder's bulk volume $V$ can be expressed as a function of its tapped state.

Let a known mass $m$ of powder occupy a volume $V_0$ when *freely poured* (apparent volume) and $V_{tap}$ after *standardized tapping* (tap volume, where $V_{tap} \le V_0$).

By definition:

$$
\rho_{apparent} = \frac{m}{V_0}
$$

$$
\rho_{tap} = \frac{m}{V_{tap}}
$$

Since $V_0 \ge V_{tap}$, we conclude $\rho_{tap} \ge \rho_{apparent}$.

**Define Hausner Ratio (HR):**

$$
HR = \frac{\rho_{tap}}{\rho_{apparent}}
$$

$$
HR = \frac{\dfrac{m}{V_{tap}}}{\dfrac{m}{V_0}} = \frac{V_0}{V_{tap}}
$$

$$
\boxed{HR = \frac{V_0}{V_{tap}}}
$$

**Define Carr Index (CI) as the relative reduction in volume:**

$$
CI = \frac{V_0 - V_{tap}}{V_0}
$$

$$
CI = 1 - \frac{V_{tap}}{V_0} = 1 - \frac{1}{HR}
$$

$$
\boxed{CI = \left(1 - \frac{1}{HR}\right) \times 100\,\%}
$$

These two equations are **mathematically interlocked** — knowing one gives the other.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Surface Area (SSA) of a Spherical Powder

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting principle:** For a monodisperse sphere of diameter $d$, surface $A$ and volume $V_s$ are related through $d$.

For one particle:

$$
A = \pi d^2
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi d^3}{6}
$$

Per unit mass of powder (mass of one particle $= \rho_p \cdot V_s$):

$$
SSA = \frac{A}{\rho_p \cdot V_s}
$$

$$
SSA = \frac{\pi d^2}{\rho_p \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6}} = \frac{6}{\rho_p \cdot d}
$$

$$
\boxed{SSA = \frac{6}{\rho_p \cdot d}}
$$

**For a polydisperse powder**, use Sauter mean diameter $d_{32}$:

$$
d_{32} = \frac{\sum n_i d_i^3}{\sum n_i d_i^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{SSA = \frac{6}{\rho_p \cdot d_{32}}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Layer Thickness Rule (Powder-Bed Fusion)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical principle:** To ensure **complete melting** of a single layer by the energy source, the layer thickness $t_L$ must be **larger than the largest particle** (otherwise the largest particles "stand up" through the layer).

Let $d_{max}$ be the maximum particle size in the PSD.

A safe empirical rule (validated for LPBF, EBM, SLS):

$$
\boxed{t_L = k \cdot d_{max}, \quad k \in [1.5,\, 2.0]}
$$

For laser PBF, $k \approx 1.5$; for EBM, $k \approx 2.0$ (coarser powder, 45–105 µm).

**Numerical example (KTU-style):** A Ti-6Al-4V powder has $d_{max} = 45\, \mu m$ for an LPBF system. What is the safe layer thickness?

$$
t_L = 1.5 \times 45 = 67.5\,\mu m
$$

Rounded to a KTU-acceptable setting: **$t_L = 60\text{–}70\,\mu m$**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — PSD from Sieve Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Method:** Stack sieves from largest (top) to smallest (bottom). Pour 100 g of powder. Weigh residue on each sieve.

Let $m_i$ = mass retained on sieve $i$, $m_{total} = \sum m_i$ (≈ 100 g after loss).

**Retention on sieve $i$:**

$$
R_i = \frac{m_i}{m_{total}} \times 100 \quad [\%]
$$

**Cumulative retention** up to sieve $i$:

$$
CR_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} R_j
$$

**Cumulative passing** (undersize) through sieve $i$:

$$
P_i = 100 - CR_i = 100 - \sum_{j=1}^{i} R_j \quad [\%]
$$

The sieve at which $P_i = 50\,\%$ defines the **median particle size $d_{50}$** (by interpolation if needed).

### KTU-Standard Worked Example

A 200 g Ti-6Al-4V sample is sieve-analyzed. Results:

| Sieve (µm) | Mass Retained $m_i$ (g) | $R_i$ (%) | $CR_i$ (%) | $P_i$ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 100 | 6 | 3.0 | 3.0 | 97.0 |
| 75 | 18 | 9.0 | 12.0 | 88.0 |
| 63 | 32 | 16.0 | 28.0 | 72.0 |
| 45 | 84 | 42.0 | 70.0 | 30.0 |
| 25 | 50 | 25.0 | 95.0 | 5.0 |
| Pan | 10 | 5.0 | 100.0 | 0.0 |
| **Total** | **200** | **100** | — | — |

**Find $d_{50}$ (size at 50 % passing):**

- 50 % passing lies between 63 µm ($P = 72\%$) and 45 µm ($P = 30\%$) — interpolate on log scale:

$$
d_{50} = 45 \times \left(\frac{63}{45}\right)^{\frac{50-30}{72-30}}
$$

$$
d_{50} = 45 \times 1.4^{0.476}
$$

$$
d_{50} = 45 \times 1.178
$$

$$
\boxed{d_{50} \approx 53.0\,\mu m}
$$

This is consistent with typical Ti-6Al-4V LPBF PSD (45 – 63 µm range).

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Carr Index & Flow Regime

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle:** Carr Index measures the **% volume reduction** from free-pour to tapped state — a direct proxy for inter-particle friction.

For a powder with:

- $V_0 = 100\,cc$ at free pour
- $V_{tap} = 78\,cc$ after 3000 taps

Compute:

$$
HR = \frac{V_0}{V_{tap}} = \frac{100}{78} = 1.282
$$

$$
CI = \left(1 - \frac{1}{HR}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - 0.780\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{CI = 21.97\,\% \approx 22\,\%}
$$

**Interpretation (from KTU table):** $CI = 22\%$ → **Passable flow** → needs blending or sieving before LPBF.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Powder Selection Decision Tree

> [!NOTE]
> Use this matrix during KTU case-study questions to justify your powder choice.

| Process | Powder Size (µm) | Sphericity | Apparent Density Ratio | Preferred Alloy Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LPBF / SLM | 15 – 45 | > 0.9 | > 0.55 | Ti, Inconel, 316L, AlSi10Mg |
| EBM | 45 – 105 | > 0.85 | > 0.58 | Ti-6Al-4V, TiAl |
| DED (blown powder) | 45 – 150 | > 0.8 | > 0.50 | Inconel 718, Stellite, Waspaloy |
| Binder Jetting | 5 – 45 (fine) | > 0.7 | > 0.45 | 316L, 17-4PH, M2 tool steel |
| SLS (polymer) | 40 – 100 | irregular OK | > 0.40 | PA12, PA11, TPU, PEEK |
| Material Extrusion (FDM) | Pellets, not powder | — | — | ABS, PLA, PEEK pellets |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
