# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three powder production methods used in Additive Manufacturing and state one distinguishing feature of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Gas Atomization** — Molten metal is disintegrated by high-velocity inert gas (Ar/N₂). Produces **spherical powders**, ideal for LPBF.
2. **Plasma Rotating Electrode Process (PREP)** — A rotating metal bar is melted by plasma arc; centrifugal force ejects droplets. Produces **highly spherical, satellite-free** powder for reactive metals like Ti.
3. **Water Atomization** — Molten metal impinged by water jets. Produces **irregular, high-oxygen powder**, suitable for binder jetting and metal injection molding.

**(3 marks: 1 mark per method + 0.5 mark per feature)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Carr Index of a powder? State the flow classification for CI = 18%.**

**Model Answer:**

> Carr Index (CI) is the **percentage reduction in powder volume** from the free-poured state to the tapped state, indicating flowability.

$$
CI = \frac{\rho_{tap} - \rho_{apparent}}{\rho_{tap}} \times 100\,\%
$$

For **CI = 18%** (i.e., between 16 – 20%), the flow is classified as **"Fair"** — **marginally acceptable** for AM and may require blending or sieving.

**(3 marks: 1.5 for definition + 1 for formula + 0.5 for classification)**

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete powder lifecycle** in Laser Powder Bed Fusion (LPBF), starting from raw stock to disposal. Discuss the role of sieving, characterization, and recycling at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A batch of Ti-6Al-4V powder has:
- Apparent density = **2.45 g/cc**
- Tap density = **2.72 g/cc**
- Maximum particle size $d_{max} = 50\,\mu m$

Compute the **Hausner Ratio**, **Carr Index**, and recommend a **safe layer thickness** for LPBF. Classify the flow. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — Raw Stock & Production:**
- Raw Ti sponge + Al-V master alloy → vacuum arc remelting (VAR) → inert gas atomization (Ar, 30–60 bar).
- Output: spherical powder with satellites; **yield in 15–45 µm range ≈ 60–70%**.

**Step 2 — Incoming Quality Control:**
- **PSD** (laser diffraction, ISO 13320).
- **Morphology** (SEM, ASTM B822).
- **Chemistry** (O/N/H, ICP-OES).
- **Flow** (Hall flowmeter, ASTM B213).

**Step 3 — Storage:**
- Vacuum-sealed containers / Ar-purged glove boxes; RH < 5 %.

**Step 4 — Build Process:**
- Hopper → recoater blade → spread layer → laser melt → descend platform → repeat.
- Unmelted powder is **recovered** by vacuuming.

**Step 5 — Recycling:**
- Sieve at **63 µm** → check O/N content → blend 30–70 % fresh → return to process.

**Step 6 — Disposal:**
- Spent powder (O > 0.15 wt%) → encapsulated landfill or sent to MIM/binder jetting (down-cycling).

**Block Diagram (Reference):**

```
Raw Stock → Atomization → Incoming QC → Sieve → Storage
            → Recoater → LPBF Build → Powder Recovery
            → Sieve → Reuse Limit Check → Blend / Reject
            → Disposal
```

**(7 marks: 1 mark per stage + 2 marks for role of sieving/characterization/recycling)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Given:**
- $\rho_{apparent} = 2.45\,g/cc$
- $\rho_{tap} = 2.72\,g/cc$
- $d_{max} = 50\,\mu m$

**Step 1 — Compute Hausner Ratio:**

$$
HR = \frac{\rho_{tap}}{\rho_{apparent}} = \frac{2.72}{2.45}
$$

$$
\boxed{HR = 1.110}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Carr Index:**

$$
CI = \left(\frac{\rho_{tap} - \rho_{apparent}}{\rho_{tap}}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
CI = \left(\frac{2.72 - 2.45}{2.72}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
CI = \left(\frac{0.27}{2.72}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{CI = 9.93\,\% \approx 10\,\%}
$$

**Step 3 — Flow Classification:**

| Metric | Value | Class |
|---|---|---|
| HR = 1.11 | < 1.12 | **Excellent** |
| CI = 10 % | < 11 % | **Excellent** |

→ **Excellent flow** — directly usable for LPBF without further blending.

**Step 4 — Layer Thickness (using $k = 1.5$ for LPBF):**

$$
t_L = 1.5 \times d_{max} = 1.5 \times 50 = 75\,\mu m
$$

$$
\boxed{t_L = 75\,\mu m}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
This powder is **premium grade** (excellent flow, no frictional losses), enabling a relatively thick layer of 75 µm and high build rates.

**(7 marks: 2 for HR, 2 for CI, 1 for classification, 2 for layer thickness)**

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Discuss the **safety hazards** associated with handling fine metal powders in AM. With a block diagram, explain the **explosion pentagon** and list at least four mitigation strategies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 250 g sample of Inconel 718 powder is subjected to sieve analysis. The retained masses are:

| Sieve (µm) | 100 | 75 | 63 | 45 | 25 | Pan |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mass retained (g) | 8 | 25 | 50 | 110 | 45 | 12 |

Determine the **median particle size $d_{50}$**, the **specific surface area** (assume $\rho_p = 8.19\,g/cc$), and comment on the suitability of this powder for **DED**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Safety Hazards in AM Powder Handling:**

1. **Dust Explosion** — fine powders (< 63 µm) form explosive clouds. **MIE for Ti ≈ 10 mJ** (a static spark from a human body can ignite it).
2. **Fire** — reactive metals (Ti, Al, Mg) burn in air.
3. **Inhalation** — sub-10 µm particles cause **respiratory diseases** (cobalt lung, hard-metal disease, fibrosis).
4. **Skin/Eye Irritation** — metal dust contact.
5. **Reactivity with Water** — Mg, Al react exothermically.

**Explosion Pentagon (5 elements):**

```
   1. COMBUSTIBLE POWDER (Fuel)
   2. OXYGEN (Oxidizer)
   3. IGNITION SOURCE
   4. CONFINEMENT
   5. DISPERSION
```

All 5 must be present simultaneously. Remove any **one** → no explosion.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Combustible Powder] --> EX{Dust Explosion}
    A2[Oxygen] --> EX
    A3[Ignition Source] --> EX
    A4[Confinement] --> EX
    A5[Dispersion] --> EX
    EX --> M1[Inert Blanket Ar N2]
    EX --> M2[Grounding and Bonding]
    EX --> M3[Explosion Vents]
    EX --> M4[HRR Limiting Design]
    style A1 fill:#e74c3c,color:#ffffff
    style A2 fill:#e74c3c,color:#ffffff
    style A3 fill:#e74c3c,color:#ffffff
    style A4 fill:#e74c3c,color:#ffffff
    style A5 fill:#e74c3c,color:#ffffff
    style EX fill:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
    style M1 fill:#27ae60,color:#ffffff
    style M2 fill:#27ae60,color:#ffffff
    style M3 fill:#27ae60,color:#ffffff
    style M4 fill:#27ae60,color:#ffffff
```

**Mitigation Strategies:**

1. **Inert gas blanketing** (Ar/N₂) in sieving, storage, and process chambers.
2. **Grounding & bonding** of all equipment to dissipate static charge.
3. **Explosion vents** on silos (rupture discs) to safely release pressure.
4. **MIE-aware design** — eliminate sparks, hot surfaces, and static.
5. **Operator PPE** — respirators (P100), anti-static clothing, gloves.
6. **LEV (Local Exhaust Ventilation)** — capture dust at source.

**(7 marks: 2 for hazards, 2 for pentagon, 2 for mitigations, 1 for diagram)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Step 1 — Build the Sieve Table:**

| Sieve (µm) | $m_i$ (g) | $R_i$ (%) | $CR_i$ (%) | $P_i$ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 100 | 8 | 3.2 | 3.2 | 96.8 |
| 75 | 25 | 10.0 | 13.2 | 86.8 |
| 63 | 50 | 20.0 | 33.2 | 66.8 |
| 45 | 110 | 44.0 | 77.2 | 22.8 |
| 25 | 45 | 18.0 | 95.2 | 4.8 |
| Pan | 12 | 4.8 | 100.0 | 0.0 |
| **Total** | **250** | **100.0** | — | — |

**Step 2 — Locate $d_{50}$:**

50 % passing lies between 63 µm ($P = 66.8\%$) and 45 µm ($P = 22.8\%$).

Geometric (log-scale) interpolation:

$$
d_{50} = 45 \times \left(\frac{63}{45}\right)^{\frac{50 - 22.8}{66.8 - 22.8}}
$$

$$
d_{50} = 45 \times 1.4^{0.618}
$$

$$
d_{50} = 45 \times 1.247
$$

$$
\boxed{d_{50} \approx 56.1\,\mu m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Specific Surface Area (SSA):**

Using $d_{50} = 56.1\,\mu m = 56.1 \times 10^{-6}\,m$ and $\rho_p = 8.19\,g/cc = 8190\,kg/m^3$:

$$
SSA = \frac{6}{\rho_p \cdot d_{50}}
$$

$$
SSA = \frac{6}{8190 \times 56.1 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
SSA = \frac{6}{0.4595}
$$

$$
\boxed{SSA \approx 13.06\,m^2/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Suitability for DED:**

DED (blown powder) typically uses **45 – 150 µm** powder. With $d_{50} \approx 56\,\mu m$ and $d_{max} = 100\,\mu m$, this powder is **well within the DED range**. SSA is moderate (13 m²/kg) — fine enough for rapid heating, coarse enough to avoid nozzle clogging. **Verdict: Suitable for DED.**

**(7 marks: 2 for $d_{50}$, 2 for SSA, 1 for conversion, 2 for DED suitability)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks ⚠️**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — $g/cc \to kg/m^3$ in SSA calculation. A common 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Using arithmetic mean instead of geometric** interpolation for $d_{50}$ — KTU wants log-scale interpolation across sieve sizes.
> 3. **Mixing up HR and CI formulas** — HR = tap/apparent; CI uses the *difference*. Don't invert.
> 4. **Ignoring the explosion pentagon's 5th element** (Dispersion) — many students miss this; it is mandatory for full marks.
> 5. **Not justifying layer thickness** with a $k$ value — always state $k = 1.5$ (LPBF) or $2.0$ (EBM).
> 6. **Drawing powder lifecycle but missing the recycling loop** — full-marks diagrams must show **feedback arrows**.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Powder Handling Module 3**

- [x] **Powder production** = atomization (gas/plasma/water) or mechanical (milling). **Gas atomization** is the AM workhorse.
- [x] **LPBF powder PSD** = **15 – 45 µm**; **EBM** = 45 – 105 µm; **DED** = 45 – 150 µm.
- [x] **Sphericity** $\Psi > 0.9$ is the AM target; $\Psi = 1$ is a perfect sphere.
- [x] **Hausner Ratio** $HR = \rho_{tap}/\rho_{apparent}$; **CI** = $(1 - 1/HR) \times 100$.
- [x] **HR < 1.18 / CI < 15 %** → **Good flow** → AM-acceptable.
- [x] **Sieve cutoff** for LPBF recycling = **63 µm**; for DED = **100 µm**.
- [x] **Layer thickness rule:** $t_L = 1.5 \times d_{max}$ (LPBF), $2.0 \times d_{max}$ (EBM).
- [x] **Storage** = Ar-purged, RH < 5 %, vacuum-sealed, fire-suppressed.
- [x] **Drying polymer powders (PA12)** at 80 – 100 °C for 4 – 6 h before SLS.
- [x] **Ti rejection criteria:** O > 0.15 wt%, N > 0.05 wt%, D50 drift > 5 µm.
- [x] **Reuse cycles:** typically 10 – 30; refresh ratio 30 – 70 % new powder.
- [x] **Explosion Pentagon** has **5 elements** (combustible + O₂ + ignition + confinement + dispersion).
- [x] **MIE of Ti ≈ 10 mJ**; **MEC ≈ 50 – 500 g/m³**.
- [x] **PPE:** P100 respirator, anti-static clothing, gloves, eye protection.
- [x] **DED powder feed rate:** 1 – 30 g/min; carrier gas (Ar) 2 – 8 L/min.
- [x] **Sphericity formula** $\Psi = 4\pi A / P^2$; **Aspect Ratio** $AR = d_{max}/d_{min}$.
- [x] **Standards to memorize:** ASTM B213 (Hall flow), B212 (apparent density), B527 (tap density), ISO 13320 (PSD laser), ASTM F3434 (AM powder P-Map).
- [x] **Specific Surface Area** $SSA = 6/(\rho_p \cdot d_{50})$ — use $d_{32}$ (Sauter mean) for polydisperse.
- [x] **Powder cost hierarchy:** Ti/Al/Ni superalloys >> steels > polymers — drives recycling economics.
- [x] **Always show units** in KTU numericals; marks are deducted for missing or wrong units.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Capabilities in Additive Manufacturing (AM)** refer to the quantifiable range of operational parameters, performance boundaries, and functional characteristics that define what a specific AM process can achieve. This includes build envelope dimensions, dimensional accuracy, surface roughness, minimum feature size, supported materials, production speed, and post-processing requirements.

According to the **ASTM F2792** standard terminology, process capabilities define the envelope of what a manufacturing system can reliably produce under specified conditions. In AM, these capabilities are bounded by:

- **Geometric Complexity:** Maximum overhang angles, minimum wall thickness, internal channels
- **Dimensional Accuracy:** Typically in the range of **±0.1 mm to ±0.5 mm** depending on the process
- **Surface Finish (Ra):** Varies from **5 µm (SLA)** to **200 µm (FDM)**
- **Build Volume:** From **50 mm³ (Micro-AM)** to **>1 m³ (Large-format AM)**
- **Material Throughput:** Typically measured in **cm³/hour** or **grams/hour**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of AM processes as a Swiss Army Knife Workshop:** Each AM technology is like a different specialized tool in a craftsman's workshop. Vat Photopolymerization (SLA/DLP) is your precision magnifying glass—excellent for fine details but limited in size. Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) is your reliable hammer—versatile and affordable but rough. Selective Laser Melting (SLM) is your high-temperature forge—powerful for metals but expensive. **Process capabilities** tell you exactly which tool (process) to pick for your specific job (application).

Just as a carpenter wouldn't use a chainsaw to hammer a nail, an engineer must understand the capabilities of each AM process to make informed decisions. The "process capability envelope" is the unique fingerprint that distinguishes one AM technology from another.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Process Capabilities

> [!TIP]
> **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) used to evaluate AM process capabilities:**
> - **Dimensional Tolerance (mm)** — Deviation from nominal dimensions
> - **Surface Roughness Ra (µm)** — Arithmetic mean roughness
> - **Build Rate (cm³/h)** — Volume of material deposited per hour
> - **Layer Thickness (µm)** — Z-axis resolution per layer
> - **Minimum Feature Size (µm)** — Smallest producible geometric feature
> - **Material Density (% theoretical)** — Relative density of printed parts

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Process Capabilities

The capability of an AM process is governed by a multi-dimensional parameter space. Each process has unique boundaries that constrain the final part quality. Let us break this down systematically:

### 2.1.1 Dimensional Accuracy and Tolerance

Dimensional accuracy is influenced by:

1. **Mechanical precision** of motion systems (linear rails, ball screws)
2. **Thermal contraction** during solidification or curing
3. **Layer stacking errors** accumulated over height
4. **Material shrinkage** upon phase change

The general relationship for tolerance accumulation:

$$\Delta_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \delta_i$$

Where $\Delta_{total}$ is the cumulative tolerance and $\delta_i$ is the error contribution from layer $i$.

### 2.1.2 Build Rate Calculation

The build rate $R$ of an AM process can be expressed as:

$$R = \frac{V_{build}}{t_{build}} = \frac{L \times W \times H}{t_{layer} \times N_{layers}}$$

Where:
- $V_{build}$ = Build volume (cm³)
- $t_{build}$ = Total build time (hours)
- $L, W, H$ = Length, Width, Height of build envelope
- $t_{layer}$ = Time per layer
- $N_{layers}$ = Total number of layers

### 2.1.3 Surface Roughness Model

For layered manufacturing, theoretical surface roughness depends on layer thickness $h_t$ and part orientation angle $\theta$:

$$R_a^{theoretical} = h_t \times \cos(\theta)$$

### 2.1.4 Material Utilization Efficiency

$$\eta_{material} = \frac{m_{final\_part}}{m_{total\_material}} \times 100\%$$

This is critical for expensive materials like Titanium Ti-6Al-4V or Inconel 718.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units** | **Applicable Process** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Build Rate | $R = \frac{L \times W \times H}{t_{layer} \times N_{layers}}$ | cm³/h | All AM processes |
| Theoretical Roughness | $R_a = h_t \times \cos(\theta)$ | µm | All layer-based AM |
| Material Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{m_{part}}{m_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Powder bed, Binder jet |
| Energy Density | $E_d = \frac{P}{v \times h_t \times t}$ | J/mm³ | SLM, DMLS, EBM |
| Layer Count | $N = \frac{H}{h_t}$ | dimensionless | All |
| Shrinkage Compensation | $L_{cad} = L_{part}(1 + S)$ | mm | All |
| Volumetric Capacity | $V_c = L \times W \times H$ | mm³ or cm³ | All |
| Aspect Ratio Limit | $AR_{max} = \frac{H_{feature}}{t_{min}}$ | dimensionless | FDM, SLA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Density Equation** — The most critical formula in powder bed fusion processes:
> $$E_d = \frac{P}{v \times h_t \times t}$$
> Where $P$ is laser power (W), $v$ is scan speed (mm/s), $h_t$ is hatch spacing (mm), and $t$ is layer thickness (mm). **Typical optimum range: 40-100 J/mm³ for SLM of Ti-6Al-4V.**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why Process Capabilities Matter in Industry:**
> - **Aerospace:** Selecting SLM/EBM for topology-optimized Ti-6Al-4V brackets
> - **Medical:** Using SLA for patient-specific surgical guides with **±0.05 mm accuracy**
> - **Automotive:** Using Binder Jetting for large sand molds and metal casting patterns
> - **Tooling:** Using DMLS for conformal cooling channels in injection mold inserts
> - **Jewelry:** Using DLP/SLA for lost-wax casting patterns with **Ra < 10 µm**

Process capability matching is the cornerstone of **Design for Additive Manufacturing (DfAM)**. The engineer must select the right process based on the part's functional requirements, material needs, geometric complexity, and economic constraints.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Build Time Estimation

Let us derive the total build time for a typical **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** operation, considering scan patterns and recoating time.

### Given Parameters:

- Build envelope: $L = 250$ mm, $W = 250$ mm, $H = 300$ mm
- Layer thickness: $h_t = 30$ µm $= 0.030$ mm
- Laser power: $P = 200$ W
- Scan speed: $v = 800$ mm/s
- Hatch spacing: $h_s = 0.12$ mm
- Recoating time per layer: $t_r = 12$ s
- Laser scan time per layer: $t_s$ (depends on area)

### Step 1: Calculate Number of Layers

The total number of layers is determined by dividing the build height by layer thickness:

$$N_{layers} = \frac{H}{h_t}$$

Substituting values:

$$N_{layers} = \frac{300 \text{ mm}}{0.030 \text{ mm}}$$

$$N_{layers} = 10{,}000 \text{ layers}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Effective Layer Area

Assuming the part occupies approximately 30% of the build platform (packing density $\phi = 0.30$):

$$A_{layer} = L \times W \times \phi$$

$$A_{layer} = 250 \times 250 \times 0.30$$

$$A_{layer} = 18{,}750 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 3: Calculate Scan Track Length

The total scan length per layer is the area divided by hatch spacing (each hatch is one scan pass):

$$L_{scan} = \frac{A_{layer}}{h_s}$$

$$L_{scan} = \frac{18{,}750 \text{ mm}^2}{0.12 \text{ mm}}$$

$$L_{scan} = 156{,}250 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Laser Scan Time per Layer

The time to scan one layer is the scan length divided by scan speed:

$$t_s = \frac{L_{scan}}{v}$$

$$t_s = \frac{156{,}250 \text{ mm}}{800 \text{ mm/s}}$$

$$t_s = 195.3 \text{ s per layer}$$

### Step 5: Calculate Time per Layer Including Recoating

$$t_{layer} = t_s + t_r$$

$$t_{layer} = 195.3 + 12$$

$$t_{layer} = 207.3 \text{ s/layer}$$

### Step 6: Total Build Time

$$t_{build} = N_{layers} \times t_{layer}$$

$$t_{build} = 10{,}000 \times 207.3 \text{ s}$$

$$t_{build} = 2{,}073{,}000 \text{ s}$$

### Step 7: Convert to Hours

$$t_{build} = \frac{2{,}073{,}000}{3600} \text{ h}$$

$$t_{build} \approx 575.8 \text{ hours} \approx 24 \text{ days}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This derivation shows why SLM is expensive for large parts. The dominant time contributor is the **laser scan time** ($t_s$), not recoating. Increasing hatch spacing or reducing part density can dramatically reduce build time.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: AM Process Capabilities Comparison

| **Capability** | **FDM** | **SLA** | **SLS** | **SLM/DMLS** | **EBM** | **Binder Jet** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dimensional Accuracy | ±0.5 mm | ±0.1 mm | ±0.3 mm | ±0.1 mm | ±0.2 mm | ±0.2 mm |
| Surface Roughness Ra | 50-200 µm | 5-20 µm | 20-50 µm | 10-30 µm | 20-40 µm | 30-80 µm |
| Min. Layer Thickness | 100 µm | 25 µm | 80 µm | 20-50 µm | 50 µm | 50-100 µm |
| Min. Feature Size | 500 µm | 100 µm | 300 µm | 150 µm | 200 µm | 500 µm |
| Build Rate (cm³/h) | 10-50 | 5-30 | 15-40 | 5-15 | 10-30 | 50-200 |
| Material Density | 80-95% | 95-100% | 90-98% | 95-99.9% | 98-99.5% | 60-90% (pre-sinter) |
| Max Overhang Angle | 45° | 30° | 45° | 45° | 45° | 45° |
| Support Structure | Required | Required | Not Required | Required | Required | Not Required |
| Material Waste | Low | Low | Medium | Low | Low | High |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

> **Define "Process Capability" in the context of Additive Manufacturing. List any four key process capability parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

Process capability in Additive Manufacturing refers to the range of operational parameters and performance characteristics that define what a specific AM process can achieve, including geometric, material, and economic limits.

**Four key process capability parameters:**
1. Dimensional accuracy and tolerance (typically **±0.1 mm to ±0.5 mm**)
2. Surface roughness (Ra values from **5 µm to 200 µm**)
3. Build envelope dimensions
4. Build rate (volume of material deposited per hour)
5. Minimum feature size
6. Material compatibility and density

**[Marks Distribution: Definition – 1 mark | Any 4 parameters – 2 marks]**

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

> **What is meant by Energy Density in powder bed fusion processes? Write the formula and state its typical optimum range for SLM of Ti-6Al-4V.**

**Model Answer:**

Energy Density ($E_d$) is the amount of laser energy delivered per unit volume of powder in powder bed fusion processes. It is a critical process capability parameter that determines the quality of the melt pool and final part density.

$$E_d = \frac{P}{v \times h_t \times t}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Laser Power (W)
- $v$ = Scan Speed (mm/s)
- $h_t$ = Hatch Spacing (mm)
- $t$ = Layer Thickness (mm)

**Typical optimum range for SLM of Ti-6Al-4V: 40 to 100 J/mm³**

Values below this range cause **lack of fusion porosity**, while values above cause **keyholing porosity** and material evaporation.

**[Marks Distribution: Definition – 1 mark | Formula – 1 mark | Range – 1 mark]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** — Option 1

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]

> **Explain in detail the various process capability parameters used to characterize an AM process. Discuss dimensional accuracy, surface roughness, build rate, and minimum feature size with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

Process capability parameters define the performance envelope of an AM process. The major parameters are:

**1. Dimensional Accuracy:**
- Measures how closely the printed part matches the CAD model
- Influenced by machine precision, thermal effects, and layer stacking errors
- Example: **SLA achieves ±0.1 mm** while **FDM achieves ±0.5 mm**
- Critical for assembly-fit components and medical implants

**2. Surface Roughness (Ra):**
- Measured as arithmetic mean deviation of surface profile
- Depends on layer thickness and staircase effect
- Formula: $R_a = h_t \times \cos(\theta)$ where $\theta$ is the part orientation angle
- Example: **SLA: 5-20 µm**, **FDM: 50-200 µm**

**3. Build Rate:**
- Volume of material deposited per unit time
- Formula: $R = \frac{V_{build}}{t_{build}}$
- Example: **Binder Jetting: 50-200 cm³/h**, **SLM: 5-15 cm³/h**

**4. Minimum Feature Size:**
- Smallest geometric feature reliably produced
- Depends on laser spot size, nozzle diameter, or pixel resolution
- Example: **SLA: 100 µm**, **FDM: 500 µm**, **PolyJet: 14 µm**

**[Marks Distribution: Each parameter – 1.5 marks with example | Introduction – 1 mark]**

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]

> **A part of dimensions 100 mm × 80 mm × 50 mm is to be manufactured using SLM with a layer thickness of 30 µm. The laser scan speed is 1000 mm/s and hatch spacing is 0.10 mm. Calculate the total number of layers and the approximate build time assuming 25% packing density and 10 seconds recoating time per layer.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Number of Layers**

$$N = \frac{H}{h_t} = \frac{50 \text{ mm}}{0.030 \text{ mm}} = 1{,}667 \text{ layers}$$

**Step 2: Effective Layer Area**

$$A_{layer} = L \times W \times \phi = 100 \times 80 \times 0.25 = 2{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Total Scan Length per Layer**

$$L_{scan} = \frac{A_{layer}}{h_s} = \frac{2{,}000}{0.10} = 20{,}000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Laser Scan Time per Layer**

$$t_s = \frac{L_{scan}}{v} = \frac{20{,}000}{1000} = 20 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5: Total Time per Layer**

$$t_{layer} = t_s + t_r = 20 + 10 = 30 \text{ s/layer}$$

**Step 6: Total Build Time**

$$t_{build} = N \times t_{layer} = 1{,}667 \times 30 = 50{,}010 \text{ s}$$

$$t_{build} = \frac{50{,}010}{3600} \approx 13.89 \text{ hours}$$

**[Marks Distribution: N calculation – 2 marks | Scan time – 2 marks | Total time – 3 marks]**

---

### Question B **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** — Option 2

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]

> **Compare the process capabilities of FDM, SLA, and SLM processes in terms of dimensional accuracy, surface finish, materials supported, build volume, and typical applications.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **FDM** | **SLA** | **SLM** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dimensional Accuracy | ±0.5 mm | ±0.1 mm | ±0.1 mm |
| Surface Finish Ra | 50-200 µm | 5-20 µm | 10-30 µm |
| Materials | Thermoplastics (ABS, PLA, Nylon) | Photopolymers, Ceramics | Metals (Ti, Al, Steel, Inconel) |
| Typical Build Volume | 200×200×300 mm | 145×145×175 mm | 250×250×300 mm |
| Min. Layer Thickness | 100-300 µm | 25-100 µm | 20-50 µm |
| Support Structure | Required | Required | Required |
| Typical Applications | Prototyping, Concept models, Hobbyist | Dental, Jewelry, Medical guides | Aerospace, Medical implants, Tooling |
| Relative Cost | Low | Medium | Very High |
| Production Speed | Fast | Slow | Very Slow |

**Discussion:**
- **FDM** is best for low-cost, fast prototyping with thermoplastics
- **SLA** dominates in applications requiring high surface quality and intricate details
- **SLM** is the gold standard for functional metal parts with high mechanical performance

**[Marks Distribution: Comparison table – 5 marks | Discussion – 2 marks]**

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]

> **Explain the concept of volumetric energy density in SLM/DMLS processes. Discuss the effects of low and high energy density on part quality with reference to porosity and mechanical properties.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Volumetric Energy Density ($E_d$) quantifies the energy input per unit volume of metal powder during SLM/DMLS. It is the master parameter that controls melt pool dynamics and final part quality.

$$E_d = \frac{P}{v \times h_t \times t}$$

**Effects of Low Energy Density (< 40 J/mm³):**
- Insufficient melting of powder particles
- **Lack of fusion porosity** (unmelted regions)
- Weak inter-layer bonding
- Reduced mechanical strength and fatigue life
- Increased surface roughness due to partially melted particles

**Effects of High Energy Density (> 100 J/mm³):**
- Excessive energy input causes material evaporation
- **Keyholing porosity** (gas pockets trapped in deep melt pools)
- Balling phenomenon due to surface tension instability
- Residual stresses and distortion
- Potential for element vaporization in alloys

**Optimum Range (40-100 J/mm³ for Ti-6Al-4V):**
- Dense parts with > 99.5% theoretical density
- Strong metallurgical bonding between layers
- Balanced mechanical properties (UTS > 900 MPa for Ti-6Al-4V)
- Minimal defects and good fatigue performance

**Conclusion:**
Energy density must be carefully optimized for each material system. For Ti-6Al-4V the optimum is typically 60-80 J/mm³; for AlSi10Mg it is around 40-60 J/mm³.

**[Marks Distribution: Definition and formula – 2 marks | Low ED effects – 2 marks | High ED effects – 2 marks | Conclusion – 1 mark]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing µm, mm, and cm in build rate calculations. Always convert to a **single consistent unit system** (preferably mm) before substitution.
>
> 2. **Wrong Energy Density Formula:** Some students write $E_d = P \times v \times h_t \times t$ (multiplication instead of division). **Remember:** Higher power and lower speed increase energy density.
>
> 3. **Confusing Build Rate vs Build Time:** Build rate has units of cm³/h, while build time has units of hours. They are reciprocally related, not the same.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Recoating Time:** In build time calculations, students often forget to add the recoating/dwell time per layer. **Always include $t_r$.**
>
> 5. **Forgetting Packing Density:** Using 100% platform coverage is unrealistic. Use realistic packing density (typically 20-40%) for accurate estimations.
>
> 6. **Stating Tolerance Ranges Incorrectly:** Do NOT write "very accurate" or "high accuracy." Always quote specific numerical values like **±0.1 mm**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Process Capabilities:**
>
> ☐ **Energy Density formula:** $E_d = \frac{P}{v \times h_t \times t}$ — units **J/mm³**, optimum range **40-100 J/mm³** for Ti-6Al-4V
>
> ☐ **Build Rate formula:** $R = \frac{L \times W \times H}{t_{layer} \times N_{layers}}$ — units **cm³/h**
>
> ☐ **Theoretical Surface Roughness:** $R_a = h_t \times \cos(\theta)$ — layer thickness × cos(orientation angle)
>
> ☐ **Number of Layers:** $N = \frac{H}{h_t}$ — total height divided by layer thickness
>
> ☐ **Build Time formula:** $t_{build} = N \times (t_s + t_r)$ where $t_s$ is scan time and $t_r$ is recoating time
>
> ☐ **Key Accuracy Values to Memorize:**
>   - SLA: **±0.1 mm**, Ra **5-20 µm**
>   - FDM: **±0.5 mm**, Ra **50-200 µm**
>   - SLM: **±0.1 mm**, Ra **10-30 µm**
>
> ☐ **Lack of Fusion vs Keyholing:** Low ED → Lack of fusion; High ED → Keyholing
>
> ☐ **Process Capability Triangle:** Accuracy ↔ Speed ↔ Cost (mutual trade-offs)
>
> ☐ **Material Density Targets:** SLM > 99%, FDM 80-95%, Binder Jet 60-90% (pre-sintering)
>
> ☐ **ASTM F2792** is the standard classification for AM processes
>
> ☐ **Support structures** are required for FDM, SLA, SLM, EBM (NOT for SLS, Binder Jet)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
