topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Comparison

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Additive Manufacturing (AM), **Comparison** refers to the systematic, parametric evaluation of the seven standardized AM process categories defined by **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021** — Vat Photopolymerization (VPP), Material Jetting (MJT), Binder Jetting (BJT), Material Extrusion (MEX), Powder Bed Fusion (PBF), Sheet Lamination (SHL), and Directed Energy Deposition (DED). The comparison is performed across qualitative and quantitative parameters such as *dimensional accuracy, surface roughness, build rate, material compatibility, part complexity, post-processing requirements, and cost-per-part*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, students are expected to draw a **comparative study** of major AM processes, identifying their suitability for polymers, metals, ceramics, and composites, and justifying process selection for a given engineering application.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you need to build a sandcastle. You could:
- **Squeeze** sand through a nozzle (like FDM extruding plastic).
- **Spray** water and melt the sand grains with a laser (like Selective Laser Sintering).
- **Drop** a binding glue droplet by droplet to fuse sand (like Binder Jetting).
- **Cure** liquid sand-resin using a UV flashlight through a mask (like SLA).

Each method gives a different finish, strength, speed, and cost. The AM "Comparison" topic is exactly this — understanding *which "sandcastle-building" approach fits which engineering problem*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants / Standard Metrics in AM Comparison:**
> - **Dimensional Tolerance Range:** ±0.1 mm to ±0.5 mm (typical AM range)
> - **Layer Thickness Range:** **16 µm to 500 µm** (process-dependent)
> - **Standard Reference:** **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021** (Additive Manufacturing – General Principles – Fundamentals and Vocabulary)

### 1.3 Why Compare AM Processes?

Different AM processes are NOT interchangeable. A process optimized for polymers (e.g., FDM) cannot directly fabricate a titanium aerospace bracket — that requires **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)**. Comparison is the engineer's tool for **process selection**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Standardized AM Process Families (ISO/ASTM 52900)

| # | Process Family | Commercial Examples | State of Feed Material |
|---|----------------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Vat Photopolymerization (VPP) | SLA, DLP | Liquid photopolymer resin |
| 2 | Material Jetting (MJT) | PolyJet, MultiJet | Liquid photopolymer / wax droplets |
| 3 | Binder Jetting (BJT) | ExOne, voxeljet | Powder + liquid binder |
| 4 | Material Extrusion (MEX) | FDM / FFF | Thermoplastic filament / pellets |
| 5 | Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) | SLS, SLM, EBM, DMLS | Polymer or metal powder |
| 6 | Sheet Lamination (SHL) | LOM, UAM | Paper / metal foils / tapes |
| 7 | Directed Energy Deposition (DED) | LMD, EBF³ | Powder or wire feedstock |

### 2.2 Comparison Parameters (The "Comparison Axes")

To compare two AM processes systematically, engineers evaluate them across the following axes:

1. **Material compatibility** — polymer, metal, ceramic, composite, bio-material.
2. **Dimensional accuracy & tolerance** — usually expressed in **±µm** or **±mm**.
3. **Surface roughness (Ra)** — measured in **µm**.
4. **Build volume / envelope** — in mm³ or cm³.
5. **Build rate / speed** — cm³/h or g/h.
6. **Layer thickness (Z-resolution)** — typically **16 µm to 500 µm**.
7. **Support structure requirement** — yes / no / sacrificial.
8. **Post-processing intensity** — curing, depowdering, HIP, machining.
9. **Equipment & per-part cost** — capital vs. operating expenditure.
10. **Part complexity** — overhangs, internal channels, lattice structures.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **key quantitative relationships** used in AM process comparison problems.

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Typical Units | Notes |
|-----------|--------|---------------------|---------------|-------|
| Build Time (estimated) | $T_{build}$ | $T_{build} = \dfrac{V_{part}}{R_{build} \cdot \eta}$ | hours (h) | $\eta$ = process efficiency factor (0.3 – 0.8) |
| Part Volume | $V_{part}$ | $V_{part} = \dfrac{m_{part}}{\rho_{material}}$ | mm³ or cm³ | $\rho$ = material density (g/cm³) |
| Volumetric Build Rate | $R_{build}$ | $R_{build} = A_{layer} \cdot \dfrac{h_{layer}}{t_{layer}}$ | cm³/h | $h_{layer}$ = layer thickness, $t_{layer}$ = time per layer |
| Surface Roughness (approx.) | $R_a$ | $R_a \approx k \cdot h_{layer}$ | µm | $k$ = process factor (0.1 SLA, 0.4 FDM, 0.2 SLS) |
| Energy Density (PBF/DED) | $E_d$ | $E_d = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | J/mm³ | $P$ = laser power, $v$ = scan speed |
| Cost-per-Part | $C_p$ | $C_p = C_{mat} + C_{machine}\cdot t_{build} + C_{post}$ | ₹ or USD | Sum of material, machine-time, post-processing |
| Material Utilization Efficiency | $\eta_{mat}$ | $\eta_{mat} = \dfrac{m_{part}}{m_{consumed}} \times 100\%$ | % | BJT/SLS: ~90 %, FDM: ~100 % (single material) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real industries (aerospace, dental, automotive), the **cost-per-part formula** and the **energy density formula** are the two most decisive comparison tools — they decide whether a part is *physically possible* and *economically viable* in a given AM process.

### 2.4 Comparative Process Logic (Why / How)

- **VPP (SLA/DLP):** Liquid resin is cured **layer-by-layer** by UV light → ultra-smooth surface, **best for prototypes and dental models**.
- **MJT (PolyJet):** Hundreds of inkjet nozzles deposit **micro-droplets of photopolymer** that are instantly UV-cured → supports **multi-material and full-color parts**.
- **BJT:** A printhead selectively deposits a **liquid binder** onto a powder bed → no heat distortion, but parts are **porous and require infiltration/sintering**.
- **MEX (FDM/FFF):** A thermoplastic filament is **melted and extruded** through a heated nozzle → cheapest, most accessible, **lowest accuracy**.
- **PBF (SLS / SLM / EBM / DMLS):** A high-power laser or electron beam **selectively melts/sinters** powder → **highest mechanical strength**, dominant in **metal AM**.
- **SHL (LOM):** Sheets of material (paper, metal foil) are **bonded and cut** layer by layer → low-cost, used for **large patterns and prototypes**.
- **DED (LMD / EBF³):** Focused energy source (laser/electron beam) **melts feedstock** as it is deposited → enables **repair, coating, and large metal parts** (turbine blades, ship propellers).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative Example 1 — Build Time Estimation for an FDM Part

**Problem Setup:** A part has volume $V_{part} = 80{,}000$ mm³. FDM layer thickness is $h_{layer} = 0.2$ mm. Layer area is $A_{layer} = 30{,}000$ mm². Time per layer $t_{layer} = 45$ s. Process efficiency $\eta = 0.6$. Calculate the estimated build time.

**Step 1 — Write the volumetric build rate formula.**

$$R_{build} = A_{layer} \cdot \frac{h_{layer}}{t_{layer}}$$

**Step 2 — Convert units consistently.**

$$A_{layer} = 30{,}000 \text{ mm}^2, \quad h_{layer} = 0.2 \text{ mm}, \quad t_{layer} = 45 \text{ s} = \frac{45}{3600} \text{ h} = 0.0125 \text{ h}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$R_{build} = 30{,}000 \times \frac{0.2}{0.0125} \text{ mm}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator and denominator separately.**

$$30{,}000 \times 0.2 = 6{,}000$$
$$\frac{6{,}000}{0.0125} = 480{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 5 — Convert mm³/h → cm³/h (1 cm³ = 1000 mm³).**

$$R_{build} = \frac{480{,}000}{1000} = 480 \text{ cm}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the build-time formula with efficiency factor.**

$$T_{build} = \frac{V_{part}}{R_{build} \cdot \eta}$$

**Step 7 — Convert part volume to cm³.**

$$V_{part} = \frac{80{,}000}{1000} = 80 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 8 — Final calculation.**

$$T_{build} = \frac{80}{480 \times 0.6} = \frac{80}{288} = 0.278 \text{ h} \approx 16.67 \text{ minutes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This build time is realistic for an FDM machine printing a small plastic bracket. The same part in **SLS** would take **~45 min** (slower layer time), but **SLA would take ~25 min** (faster layer time but more post-curing).

---

### 3.2 Derivative Example 2 — Cost-per-Part Comparison (FDM vs. SLM)

**Given:**
- Material density: $\rho_{ABS} = 1.04$ g/cm³ (FDM); $\rho_{Ti-6Al-4V} = 4.43$ g/cm³ (SLM).
- Part mass: $m_{part} = 83.2$ g (same volume assumed for comparison).
- ABS filament cost: $C_{mat,FDM} = ₹4$/g.
- Ti powder cost: $C_{mat,SLM} = ₹150$/g.
- Machine rate: $C_{machine} = ₹500$/h (FDM); $₹4000$/h (SLM).
- Build time: $t_{build,FDM} = 0.28$ h; $t_{build,SLM} = 1.2$ h.
- Post-processing: $C_{post,FDM} = ₹50$; $C_{post,SLM} = ₹800$.

**Step 1 — Material cost FDM.**

$$C_{mat,FDM} = 83.2 \times 4 = ₹332.8$$

**Step 2 — Machine time cost FDM.**

$$C_{machine,FDM} = 500 \times 0.28 = ₹140$$

**Step 3 — Total FDM cost.**

$$C_{p,FDM} = 332.8 + 140 + 50 = ₹522.8$$

**Step 4 — Material cost SLM.**

$$C_{mat,SLM} = 83.2 \times 150 = ₹12{,}480$$

**Step 5 — Machine time cost SLM.**

$$C_{machine,SLM} = 4000 \times 1.2 = ₹4800$$

**Step 6 — Total SLM cost.**

$$C_{p,SLM} = 12{,}480 + 4800 + 800 = ₹18{,}080$$

**Step 7 — Cost ratio.**

$$\frac{C_{p,SLM}}{C_{p,FDM}} = \frac{18{,}080}{522.8} \approx 34.6$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The SLM metal part is **~34× more expensive** than the FDM plastic part. This single calculation justifies why FDM is used for *concept models* and SLM is reserved for *functional, load-bearing, end-use metal parts*.

---

### 3.3 Master Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Parameter | FDM (MEX) | SLA (VPP) | SLS (PBF-Polymer) | SLM (PBF-Metal) | DED (Metal) | PolyJet (MJT) | Binder Jetting (BJT) |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|-------------|---------------|----------------------|
| Material | Thermoplastics (ABS, PLA, Nylon) | Photopolymer resin | Nylon (PA12), TPU | Stainless steel, Ti, Al alloys | Ti, Inconel, St. steel | Photopolymer, wax | Sand, metal, ceramic powder |
| Dimensional Accuracy | $\pm 0.2$ mm | $\pm 0.1$ mm | $\pm 0.3$ mm | $\pm 0.05$ – $0.1$ mm | $\pm 0.5$ mm | $\pm 0.02$ mm | $\pm 0.2$ mm (before infiltration) |
| Surface Roughness Ra | $10$ – $25$ µm | $0.5$ – $5$ µm | $5$ – $15$ µm | $5$ – $20$ µm | $15$ – $40$ µm | $0.5$ – $2$ µm | $20$ – $50$ µm |
| Layer Thickness | $100$ – $300$ µm | $16$ – $100$ µm | $80$ – $120$ µm | $20$ – $80$ µm | $250$ – $500$ µm | $14$ – $32$ µm | $100$ – $200$ µm |
| Build Volume (typical) | $200 \times 200 \times 300$ mm | $1450 \times 750 \times 800$ mm | $300 \times 300 \times 400$ mm | $400 \times 400 \times 400$ mm | $2000 \times 1500 \times 1000$ mm | $490 \times 390 \times 200$ mm | $800 \times 500 \times 400$ mm |
| Supports Required | Yes (essential) | Yes (minimal) | No | Yes (essential) | No (multi-axis) | Yes (water-soluble) | No |
| Post-Processing | Sanding, painting | UV curing, support removal | Depowdering | Stress relief, HIP, CNC, blasting | Machining, HIP | Support removal, UV cure | Depowdering, infiltration, sintering |
| Typical Application | Concept models, jigs, fixtures | Dental, jewelry, prototypes | Functional plastic parts, low-batch production | Aerospace brackets, dental implants, medical implants | Repair, large turbine parts, cladding | Full-color prototypes, medical models | Sand casting patterns, large metal parts |
| Relative Cost-per-Part | **Low** | Medium | Medium | **High** | **Very High** | High | Medium-Low |
| Mechanical Strength | Anisotropic, low | Brittle | Near-isotropic, good | Near-isotropic, excellent | Excellent (wrought-like after HIP) | Moderate | Low–Medium (porous) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Selection Decision Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Application] --> B{Is the part a final\nfunctional part?}
    B -- No, concept only --> C{Plastic prototype?}
    C -- Yes --> D{Do you need full color\nor multi-material?}
    D -- Yes --> E1[PolyJet / MJT]
    D -- No --> E2{Do you need ultra-smooth\nsurface finish?}
    E2 -- Yes --> E3[SLA / DLP]
    E2 -- No --> E4[FDM / MEX]
    C -- No, metal required --> F1[DMLS / SLM]
    B -- Yes, functional --> G{Is the part a polymer?}
    G -- Yes --> H1[SLS - Nylon/PA12]
    G -- No, metal --> I{Is the part large\nor requires repair?}
    I -- Yes --> J1[DED / LMD]
    I -- No, small/medium --> K1[SLM / EBM / DMLS]
    A --> L{Is cost the\nprimary driver?}
    L -- Yes --> M1[FDM or Binder Jetting]
    L -- No, quality first --> N1[SLM or SLA]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Comparison Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input1[Design STL File] --> PreProcess[Slicing and\nSupport Generation]
    PreProcess --> Process1[FDM Extrusion]
    PreProcess --> Process2[SLA UV Curing]
    PreProcess --> Process3[SLS Laser Sintering]
    PreProcess --> Process4[SLM Laser Melting]
    PreProcess --> Process5[PolyJet Inkjetting]
    Process1 --> Post1[Support Removal\nSanding]
    Process2 --> Post2[UV Curing\nSupport Removal]
    Process3 --> Post3[Depowdering\nBead Blasting]
    Process4 --> Post4[HIP CNC Machining\nStress Relief]
    Process5 --> Post5[Water Jet Support Removal\nUV Curing]
    Post1 --> Metric1[Accuracy Low\nCost Low]
    Post2 --> Metric2[Accuracy High\nCost Medium]
    Post3 --> Metric3[Accuracy Medium\nStrength Good]
    Post4 --> Metric4[Accuracy High\nStrength Excellent]
    Post5 --> Metric5[Accuracy Very High\nMulti-material Capable]
    Metric1 --> Compare[(Comparison Matrix)]
    Metric2 --> Compare
    Metric3 --> Compare
    Metric4 --> Compare
    Metric5 --> Compare
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Every AM process follows a common *digital → slice → process → post-process* workflow. The right-most block ("Comparison Matrix") is the conceptual destination of all process data. This is the **functional comparison architecture** that a KTU examiner expects students to draw in ESE answers.

### 4.3 AM Process Classification Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AM[Additive Manufacturing] --> Pol[Polymer Based]
    AM --> Met[Metal Based]
    AM --> Cer[Ceramic Based]
    AM --> Com[Composite Based]
    Pol --> Pol1[VPP - SLA]
    Pol --> Pol2[MJT - PolyJet]
    Pol --> Pol3[MEX - FDM]
    Pol --> Pol4[PBF - SLS]
    Pol --> Pol5[SHL - LOM]
    Met --> Met1[PBF - SLM DMLS EBM]
    Met --> Met2[DED - LMD EBF3]
    Met --> Met3[SHL - UAM]
    Met --> Met4[BJT - Metal Binder Jetting]
    Cer --> Cer1[BJT - Sand Ceramic]
    Cer --> Cer2[VPP - Photopolymer+ceramic]
    Cer --> Cer3[PBF - Indirect ceramic]
    Com --> Com1[MEX - Fiber Reinforced Filament]
    Com --> Com2[DED - Wire Arc Additive]
    Com --> Com3[Multi-material MJT]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define **Vat Photopolymerization (VPP)** and name two commercial processes belonging to this family.

> **Model Answer:** Vat Photopolymerization (VPP) is an AM process in which a vat of **liquid photopolymer resin** is selectively cured layer-by-layer using a **UV light source** (laser or projector). The two main commercial variants are: (i) **Stereolithography (SLA)** — which uses a UV **laser** to trace each cross-section, and (ii) **Digital Light Processing (DLP)** — which uses a **digital projector** to cure an entire layer at once.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] List **four key parameters** used to compare additive manufacturing processes.

> **Model Answer:** The four key comparison parameters are: (i) **Dimensional accuracy / tolerance**, (ii) **Surface roughness (Ra)**, (iii) **Build rate / speed**, and (iv) **Material compatibility** (polymers / metals / ceramics / composites).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note to Students:** KTU ESE Module-internal choice questions carry 14 marks, typically split as **7 + 7** (two sub-parts). Attempt the choice in which you are strongest.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Compare **Material Extrusion (FDM)** and **Powder Bed Fusion (SLS)** based on the following parameters: working principle, materials, support structure requirement, surface finish, accuracy, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company wants to manufacture a **custom titanium hip implant**. Justify, with reasoning, which AM process family you would select, citing at least **three supporting arguments**. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Answer (a):**
>
> | Parameter | FDM (MEX) | SLS (PBF) |
> |-----------|-----------|-----------|
> | **Working Principle** | Thermoplastic filament is melted and extruded through a heated nozzle onto a build platform, layer by layer. | A CO₂ laser selectively sinters (does not fully melt) polymer powder particles in a powder bed. |
> | **Materials** | ABS, PLA, PETG, Nylon, TPU. | Polyamide (PA12, PA11), TPU, polypropylene, PS. |
> | **Supports** | **Required** for overhangs > 45°; made of same material (wasteful) or break-away. | **Not required** — unsintered powder itself acts as the support. |
> | **Surface Finish (Ra)** | $10$ – $25$ µm (visible stair-stepping). | $5$ – $15$ µm (smoother, no layer lines as visible). |
> | **Dimensional Accuracy** | $\pm 0.2$ mm (lower). | $\pm 0.3$ mm but better isotropic strength. |
> | **Typical Applications** | Concept models, jigs, fixtures, low-cost prototypes. | Functional plastic parts, small-batch production, hinges, snap-fits. |
>
> **Verdict:** SLS is preferred for **functional end-use parts**; FDM is preferred for **low-cost prototypes**.

> **Model Answer (b):**
>
> For a **custom titanium hip implant**, the best process family is **Powder Bed Fusion – Selective Laser Melting (PBF-SLM)**.
>
> **Argument 1 — Biocompatible Material Compatibility:** SLM is one of the few AM processes capable of processing **biocompatible titanium alloys (Ti-6Al-4V ELI)** with wrought-equivalent mechanical properties post-HIP. FDM cannot process titanium, and SLA cannot achieve the required fatigue strength.
>
> **Argument 2 — Custom Complex Geometry:** Patient-specific implants require **lattice structures, trabecular surfaces, and complex internal channels** for bone in-growth. SLM can manufacture these **without part-specific tooling**, unlike CNC machining or casting.
>
> **Argument 3 — Density and Strength:** SLM achieves **> 99.5% density** after Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP), meeting the **ASTM F1108** standard for surgical implants. DED cannot match this density, and BJT requires post-sintering infiltration that compromises biocompatibility.
>
> **Conclusion:** **SLM** is the optimal process for custom titanium hip implants due to its material compatibility, geometric freedom, and certified mechanical performance.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** Differentiate between **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** and **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)**. List **two advantages** and **two limitations** of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An aerospace component requires **wall thickness of 1 mm with internal cooling channels of 0.5 mm diameter**. Identify the most suitable AM process and explain why traditional processes fail. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Answer (a):**
>
> | Aspect | DED | PBF |
> |--------|-----|-----|
> | **Feedstock** | Powder or wire injected through a nozzle. | Pre-laid powder bed. |
> | **Energy Source** | Laser, electron beam, or plasma arc. | Laser (SLM/DMLS/SLS) or electron beam (EBM). |
> | **Build Environment** | Open atmosphere or inert chamber. | Sealed inert chamber (argon / nitrogen). |
> | **Feature Size** | Larger features, multi-axis deposition. | Finer features, high resolution. |
> | **Surface Finish** | Rougher ($15$ – $40$ µm Ra). | Smoother ($5$ – $20$ µm Ra). |
>
> **DED — Advantages:** (i) Can deposit on **existing parts** (repair applications). (ii) Suitable for **very large parts** (multi-meter scale).
> **DED — Limitations:** (i) **Lower dimensional accuracy**. (ii) Requires **extensive post-machining**.
>
> **PBF — Advantages:** (i) **Higher accuracy and surface finish**. (ii) **No support structures** (powder bed supports part).
> **PBF — Limitations:** (i) **Limited build volume** (typical max 400 × 400 × 400 mm). (ii) **Slow** due to layer-by-layer powder spreading.

> **Model Answer (b):**
>
> The most suitable AM process is **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** under the **PBF family**.
>
> **Why traditional processes fail:**
> 1. **CNC Machining** cannot machine **0.5 mm internal cooling channels** — the tool cannot reach inside a hollow 1 mm wall.
> 2. **Casting** cannot create such fine, internal, tortuous paths without expensive soluble cores.
> 3. **Injection Molding** is uneconomical for a single aerospace component.
>
> **Why SLM succeeds:**
> 1. SLM builds the part **layer by layer from metal powder**, naturally creating **complex internal channels and conformal cooling** that no other process can achieve.
> 2. SLM achieves **> 99% density** in metals like Inconel and Ti-6Al-4V, satisfying aerospace fatigue requirements.
> 3. The **unsintered powder inside the channels can be removed** post-build using compressed air or ultrasound — making complex internal geometry achievable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Do NOT confuse SLS (polymer sintering) with SLM (metal melting).** Many students interchange them and lose 2–3 marks instantly.
> - **Do NOT state "AM does not require post-processing."** ALL metal AM parts require **stress relief, support removal, and often HIP** — mentioning this in your answer shows examiner-level maturity and earns full marks.
> - **Do NOT skip the "Why NOT traditional" reasoning** in process selection questions. KTU examiners specifically allocate 2–3 marks for the *negative reasoning* (why other processes fail).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Comparison):**

- [x] AM has **7 process families** per **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021**: VPP, MJT, BJT, MEX, PBF, SHL, DED.
- [x] **VPP (SLA/DLP)** = best surface finish, used for **dental, jewelry, prototypes**.
- [x] **MEX (FDM)** = cheapest, lowest accuracy, **support structures mandatory**.
- [x] **PBF (SLS/SLM/EBM/DMLS)** = highest strength; **SLS = polymer, SLM = full metal melting**.
- [x] **DED (LMD/EBF³)** = **repair and large parts**, multi-axis deposition, lower resolution.
- [x] **MJT (PolyJet)** = only process with **multi-material + full color** capability.
- [x] **BJT (Binder Jetting)** = **no support, no heat distortion**, but porous → needs infiltration/sintering.
- [x] **SHL (LOM/UAM)** = **cheapest large parts**, low accuracy, paper or metal foil feedstock.
- [x] **Accuracy ranking (highest → lowest):** PolyJet > SLM > SLA > SLS > DED > FDM > BJT (as-sintered).
- [x] **Surface roughness ranking (best → worst):** SLA/PolyJet > SLM > SLS > DED > FDM > BJT.
- [x] **Build time formula:** $T_{build} = \dfrac{V_{part}}{R_{build} \cdot \eta}$.
- [x] **Energy density formula:** $E_d = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ (J/mm³).
- [x] **Cost-per-part = material + machine-time + post-processing.**
- [x] **Always justify your process selection** with material, accuracy, complexity, AND cost reasoning in ESE answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SLM Process: Laser Material Interaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** SLM Process: Laser Material Interaction ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** is a powder-bed fusion (PBF) additive manufacturing process in which a high-energy-density laser beam (typically a **fiber laser at 1060–1080 nm** or a **Nd:YAG laser at 1064 nm**) is used to fully melt and fuse metallic powder particles (layer thickness typically **20–100 µm**) into a solid, near-fully-dense (relative density > **99%**) three-dimensional part in a layer-by-layer fashion under an inert atmosphere (Argon or Nitrogen, O₂ < **500 ppm**). **Laser–Material Interaction** in SLM refers to the coupled physical phenomena — *photon absorption, heat generation, melt-pool hydrodynamics, mass transport, Marangoni convection, vaporization, and re-solidification* — that occur within an extremely short interaction window (typically **10⁻⁶ to 10⁻³ seconds**) when the focused laser spot strikes the powder bed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The interaction governs the *melt pool geometry, density, microstructure, residual stress, and mechanical properties* of the printed part. It is the single most important phenomenon controlling SLM part quality. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Welding a Pencil Dot of Sand" Imagine you have a tiny heap of dry sand (metallic powder) sitting on a metal plate, and you hold a magnifying glass focusing bright sunlight (the laser) onto a single grain. In less than a millisecond, that one grain absorbs the light, heats up, melts into a tiny puddle, and fuses with the plate beneath. Now imagine doing this 10,000 times per second while sweeping the light across a thin layer of sand, then sprinkling another layer on top and repeating — millions of times — to "draw" a solid 3D object out of thin air. That is exactly what happens in SLM. The **laser-material interaction** is the micro-event (the single grain melting) that, when repeated billions of times, becomes the macro-process. ### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Constants (Bolded) | Parameter | Typical Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | Fiber laser wavelength | **λ = 1060–1080 nm** | Strongly absorbed by metals | | Absorptivity of solid metal at 1064 nm | **A_solid ≈ 0.30–0.40** | For stainless steel 316L | | Absorptivity of powder bed | **A_powder ≈ 0.60–0.80** | Higher due to multiple reflections | | Melt pool temperature | **T_melt ≈ 1450 °C** (for 316L) | Above solidus | | Vaporization temperature | **T_vap ≈ 2900 °C** (for Fe) | Triggers keyhole | | Thermal diffusivity of steel | **α_th ≈ 4 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** | Governs cooling rate | | Laser spot diameter | **d = 50–200 µm** | Defines track width | | Layer thickness | **t = 20–100 µm** | Defined by recoater | > [!NOTE] > **Why wavelength matters:** Most metals reflect > 90% of CO₂ laser radiation (λ = 10.6 µm) but absorb 30–70% of fiber laser radiation (λ ≈ 1.06 µm). Hence, **fiber/YAG lasers are universally used in SLM for metals** — never CO₂ lasers. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Sequential Stages of Laser-Material Interaction The interaction is not a single event — it is a tightly coupled chain of four physical stages occurring in microseconds: 1. **Stage 1 — Photon Absorption (10⁻⁹ s):** - Laser photons strike the powder bed. - Photons are absorbed by free electrons in the metal (inverse Bremsstrahlung absorption for metals). - Electron gas is heated to very high temperatures (electron temperature Tₑ). - Governing law: **Beer–Lambert Law** $I(z) = I_0 e^{-\alpha z}$. 2. **Stage 2 — Heat Generation & Conduction (10⁻⁸ s):** - Hot electrons transfer energy to the metal lattice via electron-phonon collisions. - Heat then diffuses into the bulk by **Fourier conduction**. - Governing law: **3D Transient Heat Equation** $\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + Q(x,y,z,t)$. 3. **Stage 3 — Phase Change & Melt Pool Dynamics (10⁻⁶ s):** - Powder reaches melting point → solid-to-liquid transition (latent heat of fusion absorbed). - Melt pool forms, sustained by balance between laser energy input and heat losses. - **Marangoni convection** (surface-tension-driven flow) circulates molten metal. - **Buoyancy convection** also contributes but is secondary. - If energy density is too high → **vaporization → keyhole formation** (deep, narrow vapor cavity). 4. **Stage 4 — Solidification & Microstructure Formation (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³ s):** - As the laser moves away, the melt pool cools extremely fast. - Cooling rate R = **10³–10⁸ K/s** (much faster than casting). - Dendritic / cellular microstructure forms, with grain size inversely proportional to cooling rate. ### 2.2 Energy Density — The Master Process Parameter The single most important "knob" combining all process inputs is the **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)**: $$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$ where: - $P$ = laser power (W) - $v$ = scan speed (mm/s) - $h$ = hatch spacing (mm) - $t$ = layer thickness (mm) **Typical optimal VED range for 316L stainless steel: 60–120 J/mm³** | Regime | Energy Density | Result | |---|---|---| | Low VED | < 50 J/mm³ | Lack-of-fusion porosity, unmelted powder | | Optimal VED | 60–120 J/mm³ | > 99.5% density, good mechanical properties | | Excessive VED | > 200 J/mm³ | Keyholing, vaporization pores, balling | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v h t}$ | Volumetric Energy Density | J/mm³ | Process window optimization | | 2 | $E_l = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | Linear Energy Density | J/mm | Track-level energy input | | 3 | $E_s = \dfrac{P}{v h}$ | Areal Energy Density | J/mm² | 2D scan strategy analysis | | 4 | $I(z) = I_0 e^{-\alpha z}$ | Beer–Lambert absorption law | W/m² | Optical penetration depth | | 5 | $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}$ | Optical penetration depth | m | Depth of photon absorption | | 6 | $R = \dfrac{G \cdot V}{...}$ (cooling rate) | $R \approx \dfrac{2\pi k (T_m - T_0)^2}{P/v \cdot h}$ | K/s | Microstructure prediction | | 7 | $G = \dfrac{2 k (T_m - T_0)}{Q r}$ | Thermal gradient (G) | K/m | Affects grain morphology | | 8 | $Ma = \dfrac{\Delta \sigma \cdot L}{\mu \cdot \alpha_{th}}$ | Marangoni number | — | Melt pool flow intensity | | 9 | $q'' = \alpha_s I_0$ | Absorbed heat flux at surface | W/m² | Boundary condition for heat eqn | | 10 | $P_c = \dfrac{2 \pi k (T_m - T_0)}{V}$ | Critical power for keyhole | W | Keyhole threshold | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Exam Tip:** For the absolute value of variables (e.g., $\vert T - T_0 \vert$), always use `\vert` or `\mid` inside LaTeX, never the bare pipe character, so the markdown table parser never breaks. ### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Laser-Material Interaction Theory - **Process parameter selection:** Predicts optimal P, v, h, t before a single build. - **Defect diagnosis:** Identifies whether porosity is *lack-of-fusion* (low VED) or *keyhole* (high VED). - **Multi-material printing:** Predicts compatibility of dissimilar powders. - **In-situ monitoring:** Sets the threshold for optical/thermal monitoring systems (e.g., pyrometers, melt-pool cameras). - **Residual stress mitigation:** Drives the need for substrate preheating (to reduce thermal gradient G). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Volumetric Energy Density (VED) from First Principles **Goal:** Derive the master equation $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v h t}$. **Step 1 — Power delivered per unit track length:** The laser moves at scan speed $v$ (mm/s) with power $P$ (W). The energy delivered per mm of track is: $$E_l = \frac{P}{v} \quad \text{[J/mm]}$$ **Step 2 — Energy delivered per unit area of the scanned surface:** Each scan track is separated by hatch spacing $h$ (mm). The 2-D area covered per mm of track is $1 \times h = h$ mm². So the areal energy density is: $$E_s = \frac{E_l}{h} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h} \quad \text{[J/mm}^2\text{]}$$ **Step 3 — Energy delivered per unit volume:** The laser melts through the full layer thickness $t$ (mm). The 3-D volume corresponding to the area $h$ over depth $t$ is $1 \cdot h \cdot t$ mm³. So the volumetric energy density is: $$E_v = \frac{E_s}{t} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad \text{[J/mm}^3\text{]}$$ This is the most-quoted equation in any KTU SLM question. **Box this in your exam answer.** --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Optical Penetration Depth (Beer–Lambert) **Goal:** Show that $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}$. The intensity of laser radiation at depth $z$ inside a metal obeys: $$\frac{dI}{dz} = -\alpha I$$ Separating variables: $$\frac{dI}{I} = -\alpha \, dz$$ Integrating from $z = 0$ (surface intensity $I_0$) to depth $z$: $$\int_{I_0}^{I(z)} \frac{dI}{I} = -\alpha \int_{0}^{z} dz$$ This gives: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{I(z)}{I_0}\right) = -\alpha z$$ Exponentiating: $$I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha z}$$ **Definition of penetration depth** $\delta$: it is the depth at which intensity falls to $1/e$ (≈ 36.8%) of its surface value. So $I(\delta) = I_0 / e$: $$\frac{I_0}{e} = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha \delta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-1} = e^{-\alpha \delta}$$ $$\alpha \delta = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta = \frac{1}{\alpha}$$ For 316L stainless steel with a fiber laser ($\lambda = 1064$ nm), typical values: $\alpha \approx 6 \times 10^7$ m$^{-1}$, giving $\delta \approx 17$ nm — i.e., the laser is essentially a **surface heat source** for metals. > [!NOTE] > **Exam insight:** This is why SLM heat-transfer models treat the laser as a *surface boundary condition* (Gaussian heat flux), not as a volumetric source. Only for transparent media (polymers, ceramics at certain λ) does volumetric absorption matter. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Cooling Rate in SLM **Goal:** Derive $R \approx \dfrac{2\pi k (T_m - T_0)^2}{E_l}$. For a moving point heat source (Goldak's model), the thermal cycle cooling rate at the rear of the melt pool is: $$R = \frac{2\pi k (T_m - T_0)^2}{q/v}$$ where $q$ is the absorbed power $\approx \alpha_s P$ and $k$ is thermal conductivity. Substituting $E_l = P/v$: $$R = \frac{2\pi k (T_m - T_0)^2}{E_l}$$ Substituting 316L numbers: $k = 25$ W/(m·K), $T_m = 1450$ °C, $T_0 = 25$ °C, $E_l = 0.2$ J/mm = 200 J/m: $$R = \frac{2\pi (25)(1425)^2}{200} \approx 1.6 \times 10^6 \ \text{K/s}$$ This confirms the **mega-Kelvin-per-second cooling rate** that gives SLM its ultra-fine microstructures (grain size ~ 1–10 µm) compared to casting (~ 100 µm). --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Keyhole Threshold Power **Goal:** Find the laser power at which evaporation begins and a keyhole forms. A keyhole forms when the recoil pressure from vaporization $P_r$ equals the hydrostatic pressure of the molten pool plus surface tension. A simplified energy balance yields: $$P_c = \frac{\pi k (T_v - T_0)}{\alpha_s} \cdot d$$ For 316L with $T_v \approx 2900$ °C, $k = 25$ W/(m·K), $\alpha_s = 0.4$, $d = 100$ µm: $$P_c = \frac{\pi (25)(2875)}{0.4} (100 \times 10^{-6}) \approx 56 \ \text{W}$$ This is the *minimum* power for keyhole in a 100 µm spot. In practice, **typical SLM operates at 100–400 W with 50–200 µm spots**, placing the process right on the **conduction-to-keyhole transition boundary** — and that is why process control is so critical. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Defect vs. Process Condition | Defect Type | Appearance | VED Regime | Laser Condition | Root Cause | |---|---|---|---|---| | Lack-of-fusion | Irregular, elongated pores between tracks | Low (E_v < 60 J/mm³) | Insufficient penetration | Insufficient energy; tracks don't wet the layer below | | Keyhole porosity | Spherical pores, often with entrapped gas | High (E_v > 200 J/mm³) | Excessive vaporization | Recoil pressure → deep cavity collapses | | Balling | Bead breaks into spheres, high surface roughness | Very high scan speed or low power | Rapid solidification | Plateau–Rayleigh instability of molten cylinder | | Cracking | Linear, along scan direction | High thermal gradient G | Insufficient preheat | Tensile stress > material strength at high T | | Delamination | Layer separation | Extreme local shrinkage | Steep T gradient | Residual stress exceeds inter-layer bond | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram 1 — SLM Process Hardware Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[FIBER LASER
1064 nm] --> A2[BEAM EXPANDER] A2 --> A3[SCANNING
GALVANOMETER X] A2 --> A4[SCANNING
GALVANOMETER Y] A3 --> A5[F THETA LENS] A4 --> A5 A5 --> A6[POWDER BED
20 TO 100 um] A6 --> A7[MELT POOL] A7 --> A8[SOLIDIFIED TRACK] A6 --> A9[RECOATER BLADE] A6 --> A10[BUILD PLATE] A6 --> A11[INERT GAS
ARGON OR N2] A7 --> A12[PYROMETER
TEMPERATURE] A7 --> A13[MELT POOL
CAMERA] ``` ### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Four-Stage Laser–Material Interaction Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[STAGE 1
PHOTON ABSORPTION
10 TO NEG 9 s] S2[STAGE 2
HEAT CONDUCTION
10 TO NEG 8 s] S3[STAGE 3
PHASE CHANGE
AND MELT POOL
10 TO NEG 6 s] S4[STAGE 4
SOLIDIFICATION
10 TO NEG 4 TO NEG 3 s] S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S2 --> P1[BEER LAMBERT
ABSORPTION] S3 --> P2[MARANGONI
CONVECTION] S3 --> P3[BUOYANCY
CONVECTION] S3 --> P4[KEYHOLE
FORMATION] S4 --> P5[DENDRITIC
GRAIN GROWTH] S4 --> P6[RESIDUAL
STRESS BUILD UP] ``` ### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Energy Density Decision Map (Process Window) ```mermaid flowchart TD INPUT[INPUT
P POWER
V SPEED
H HATCH
T LAYER] INPUT --> CALC[CALCULATE
E_v = P / V H T] CALC --> Q1{E_v BELOW
50 J/mm3} CALC --> Q2{E_v BETWEEN
60 AND 120 J/mm3} CALC --> Q3{E_v ABOVE
200 J/mm3} Q1 --> R1[DEFECT
LACK OF FUSION
POROSITY] Q2 --> R2[OPTIMAL
DENSITY OVER 99.5%
GOOD PROPERTIES] Q3 --> R3[DEFECT
KEYHOLE PORES
BALLING
CRACKING] ``` ### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Melt Pool Heat Transfer Modes ```mermaid flowchart TB HTQ[HEAT INPUT
FROM LASER] --> M1[CONDUCTION
DOWN TO SUBSTRATE] HTQ --> M2[CONVECTION
INSIDE MELT POOL] HTQ --> M3[RADIATION
FROM SURFACE] M2 --> M2A[MARANGONI FLOW
SURFACE TENSION DRIVEN] M2 --> M2B[BUOYANCY FLOW
DENSITY DRIVEN] M1 --> LOSS1[HEAT LOSS TO BUILD PLATE] M3 --> LOSS2[HEAT LOSS TO AMBIENT GAS] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]** Define Volumetric Energy Density (VED) in SLM. Why is it considered the master process parameter? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Volumetric Energy Density is the laser energy delivered per unit volume of the powder bed, defined as: $$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$ where $P$ is laser power (W), $v$ is scan speed (mm/s), $h$ is hatch spacing (mm), and $t$ is layer thickness (mm). It is the *master* parameter because it **integrates all four primary process inputs into a single scalar** that directly predicts melt pool size, density, defect type (lack-of-fusion vs. keyhole), and ultimately the mechanical properties of the printed part. For 316L stainless steel, the optimal window is **60–120 J/mm³**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]** Distinguish between **conduction mode** and **keyhole mode** melting in SLM. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Conduction Mode | Keyhole Mode | |---|---|---| | Energy density | Low–Moderate | High | | Melt pool shape | Shallow, wide, hemispherical | Deep, narrow, vapor-filled cavity | | Aspect ratio (depth/width) | < 0.5 | > 1.0 | | Vaporization | Negligible | Significant | | Resulting porosity | Lack-of-fusion if too shallow | Entrapped gas pores if cavity collapses | | Preferred for | High-density parts, fine features | Deep penetration, thick layers | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part A):** Students often write a one-line answer like "conduction is shallow, keyhole is deep" and lose 1.5 marks. Always provide a **tabular or comparative** answer with at least 3 distinguishing criteria to score full 3 marks. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A — Choice 1 [14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model QP]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the hardware architecture of an SLM machine. Identify the role of the F-theta lens and the inert gas system. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the four sequential stages of laser–material interaction in SLM. State the typical time scale and governing physics for each stage. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) SLM Hardware Architecture (7 marks):** The main components, in the order of the laser beam path, are: 1. **Fiber laser source** ($\lambda = 1064$ nm, 100–1000 W) — emits the high-brightness beam. 2. **Beam expander** — collimates and enlarges the beam to match scanner aperture. 3. **Scanning galvanometers (X and Y)** — two fast rotating mirrors steer the beam across the powder bed at speeds up to **10 m/s**. 4. **F-theta lens** — focuses the beam onto a flat plane (the powder bed) and ensures the spot size is constant regardless of scan position (otherwise the focal spot would distort at edges). Spot diameter typically **50–200 µm**. 5. **Powder bed & recoater blade** — a wiper spreads fresh powder at layer thickness 20–100 µm. 6. **Build platform** — moves down by one layer thickness after each solidified layer. 7. **Inert gas system** — floods the chamber with Argon or Nitrogen to keep O₂ < 500 ppm, **preventing oxidation** of reactive metals (Ti, Al) and reducing spatter. 8. **Process monitoring** — pyrometer + melt-pool camera for in-situ quality control. *(Award: 1 mark per major component, 1 mark for F-theta lens role, 1 mark for inert gas role.)* --- **(b) Four Stages of Laser–Material Interaction (7 marks):** | Stage | Time Scale | Governing Physics | Outcome | |---|---|---|---| | 1. Photon Absorption | ~10⁻⁹ s | Inverse Bremsstrahlung, Beer–Lambert | Free electrons heated, surface energy deposited | | 2. Heat Conduction | ~10⁻⁸ s | Fourier heat equation, 3D transient | Lattice temperature rises; thermal gradient G forms | | 3. Phase Change & Melt Pool | ~10⁻⁶ s | Latent heat of fusion, Marangoni & buoyancy convection | Liquid pool forms; Marangoni flows drive homogenization | | 4. Solidification | ~10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³ s | Nucleation & growth, rapid cooling 10⁶ K/s | Fine dendritic microstructure, residual stress built up | *(Award: 1.5 marks per stage with time scale + governing physics + outcome.)* --- #### **Question B — Choice 2 [14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model QP]** **(a)** Derive the expression for Volumetric Energy Density $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v h t}$ used in SLM. A build is carried out with $P = 200$ W, $v = 800$ mm/s, $h = 0.1$ mm, $t = 0.03$ mm. Calculate the VED and comment on whether the resulting part is likely to be defect-free if the optimum VED for 316L is 80 J/mm³. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the Beer–Lambert law, derive the expression for optical penetration depth $\delta = 1/\alpha$. A 316L powder bed has an absorption coefficient $\alpha = 6 \times 10^7$ m⁻¹. Calculate $\delta$ and comment on what this value implies about the heat source modelling. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation + Numerical (7 marks):** *Derivation (4 marks):* Starting with power $P$ (W) and scan speed $v$ (mm/s), the linear energy density is: $$E_l = \frac{P}{v}$$ When tracks are spaced by hatch $h$ (mm), the 2-D areal energy density is: $$E_s = \frac{P}{v \cdot h}$$ When the laser melts through layer thickness $t$ (mm), the 3-D volumetric energy density is: $$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad \text{[J/mm}^3\text{]}$$ *Numerical substitution (2 marks):* $$E_v = \frac{200}{800 \times 0.1 \times 0.03} = \frac{200}{2.4} \approx 83.3 \ \text{J/mm}^3$$ *Comment (1 mark):* Since 83.3 J/mm³ lies within the optimal window of 60–120 J/mm³ for 316L, the part is **expected to be near-full-density with minimal defects**. --- **(b) Beer–Lambert Derivation + Numerical (7 marks):** *Derivation (4 marks):* The differential form of Beer's law is: $$\frac{dI}{dz} = -\alpha I$$ Separating and integrating from surface ($I_0$ at $z=0$) to depth $z$: $$\int_{I_0}^{I(z)} \frac{dI}{I} = -\alpha \int_0^z dz$$ $$\ln\!\left(\frac{I(z)}{I_0}\right) = -\alpha z$$ $$I(z) = I_0 e^{-\alpha z}$$ At the penetration depth, $I(\delta) = I_0 / e$: $$e^{-1} = e^{-\alpha \delta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta = \frac{1}{\alpha}$$ *Numerical (2 marks):* $$\delta = \frac{1}{6 \times 10^7} = 1.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{m} = 16.7 \ \text{nm}$$ *Comment (1 mark):* Since $\delta \approx 17$ nm is **negligibly small** compared to typical powder size (20–50 µm) and melt-pool depth (50–100 µm), the laser behaves as a **surface heat source**. Hence, in heat-transfer modelling, the laser is applied as a *surface Gaussian flux boundary condition*, not as a volumetric heat source. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):** > 1. **Units trap:** Many students write $E_v$ in J/m³ or mix mm and m units — always **check unit consistency** before submitting. The KTU standard is **J/mm³** when $P$ is in W and $v$, $h$, $t$ are in mm/s, mm, mm. > 2. **Beer–Lambert mistake:** A common error is to set $I(\delta) = 0$ instead of $I(\delta) = I_0/e$. The penetration depth is the **1/e decay length**, not the depth of zero intensity. > 3. **Marangoni confusion:** Students often write that Marangoni flow is caused by gravity — it is **surface-tension-gradient-driven**, not buoyancy-driven. Both exist, but Marangoni dominates in SLM due to steep temperature gradients. > 4. **Keyhole sign error:** Some students report keyhole as "good for density" — wrong. Keyholing *increases* porosity due to entrapped vapor, even though it increases local penetration. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-page Revision Bullets — Print This Before the Exam** - **SLM** = Powder Bed Fusion using a **fiber laser (1064 nm)** to fully melt metallic powders layer by layer in an **inert atmosphere** (O₂ < 500 ppm). - **Master equation:** $E_v = P / (v h t)$ in **J/mm³**; optimal for 316L = 60–120 J/mm³. - **Four stages of interaction:** Absorption → Conduction → Phase Change → Solidification; time scales 10⁻⁹ s to 10⁻³ s. - **Beer–Lambert law:** $I(z) = I_0 e^{-\alpha z}$; penetration depth $\delta = 1/\alpha$. For metals at 1064 nm, $\delta \approx 10$–$30$ nm → treat laser as **surface heat source**. - **Wavelength rule:** Use **fiber/Nd:YAG (~1 µm) for metals**; CO₂ (~10.6 µm) is reflected and unusable. - **Two melting modes:** Conduction (shallow, defect-free) vs. Keyhole (deep, vapor-filled, defect-prone). - **Cooling rate:** $10^5$–$10^7$ K/s → ultra-fine dendritic microstructure (grain size 1–10 µm). - **Marangoni convection** dominates melt-pool flow; driven by **surface tension gradient** $\partial \sigma / \partial T$. - **Hatch strategies:** Stripe, chessboard, island — chosen to reduce **residual stress** by shortening scan vector length. - **Three classic defects:** Lack-of-fusion (low VED), Keyhole porosity (high VED), Balling (very high speed, low power). - **In-situ monitoring:** Pyrometer (T), melt-pool camera (size, shape), photodiode (plasma intensity). - **Preheating** (substrate 100–200 °C) reduces thermal gradient G, mitigates cracking and distortion. - **Absorptivity** of powder bed (0.6–0.8) >> absorptivity of solid bulk (0.3–0.4) due to multiple internal reflections. - **Exam hot list:** $E_v$ formula, Beer–Lambert derivation, Conduction vs. Keyhole table, Four stages table. - **Always include units in numerical answers**, and explicitly state the regime (optimal, lack-of-fusion, keyhole) when commenting on VED values.

topics.title: Effect of Laser Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Laser Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Laser Parameters in Powder Bed Fusion (PBF):** The set of process variables governing the interaction between the laser beam and the metallic/ polymeric powder bed in **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)**, **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)**, and **Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS)** processes. The four primary controllable parameters are **Laser Power (P)**, **Scan Speed (v)**, **Hatch Spacing (h)**, and **Layer Thickness (t)**. Their combined effect is quantified through the **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)**.

The interaction physics follows the classic Beer-Lambert absorption model where laser intensity attenuates through the powder bed depth, governed by the **absorption coefficient (α)** of the material at the laser's wavelength. Most industrial SLM systems use a **Ytterbium fiber laser** operating at **1070 nm wavelength** (near-infrared) with powers typically in the range **100 W to 1 kW**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Campfire Story:**
> Imagine you are trying to melt a row of butter cubes (think of butter cubes as powder particles). You hold a magnifying glass (the laser) focusing sunlight on them.
>
> - If you move your hand **very slowly** over the butter (low scan speed), the butter burns and turns brown — this is **excessive energy input** leading to evaporation and keyholing.
> - If you wave the magnifying glass **too fast** (high scan speed), the butter barely softens — this is **insufficient energy**, leading to lack-of-fusion porosity.
> - The **gap between adjacent passes** (hatch spacing) decides if you miss any butter cubes between lines.
> - The **thickness of butter layer** you spread (layer thickness) decides how much material the laser must penetrate.
>
> The "sweet spot" — where butter melts just enough to fuse with neighbors without burning — is what AM engineers call the **process window**. Mastering this is the entire art of laser parameter tuning.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Laser Wavelength (λ):** typically **1070 nm** for Yb-fiber lasers used in SLM.
- **Beam Spot Diameter:** typically **50 µm – 200 µm**.
- **Absorptivity (A):** **0.3 – 0.7** for metals at 1070 nm.
- **Standard Energy Density Range:** **40 – 120 J/mm³** for metals like **316L stainless steel**, **Ti-6Al-4V**, **Inconel 718**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breakdown of Each Laser Parameter

### 2.1.1 Laser Power (P) — measured in Watts (W)

- **Physical Role:** Determines the amount of energy delivered per unit time to the powder bed.
- **Why it matters:** Higher P creates a deeper and wider melt pool. Too low → incomplete melting. Too high → keyholing, evaporation, spatter.
- **Typical Range for metals:** **100 W – 400 W**; for high-temperature alloys like Inconel 718, up to **1000 W**.

### 2.1.2 Scan Speed (v) — measured in mm/s

- **Physical Role:** Linear velocity of the laser spot across the powder surface.
- **Why it matters:** Inversely proportional to energy density. Higher v → shallower melt pool, faster solidification (finer grains).
- **Typical Range:** **200 mm/s – 2000 mm/s** depending on the material.

### 2.1.3 Hatch Spacing (h) — measured in µm

- **Physical Role:** Lateral distance between two adjacent laser scan tracks.
- **Why it matters:** Controls overlap between consecutive scan vectors. Insufficient overlap → inter-track porosity.
- **Typical Range:** **50 µm – 200 µm** (often ~ **0.5 × to 0.7 ×** of the melt pool width).

### 2.1.4 Layer Thickness (t) — measured in µm

- **Physical Role:** Vertical height of powder spread by the recoater.
- **Why it matters:** Determines depth of melting required per pass. Thicker layers → higher energy demand and risk of lack-of-fusion defects.
- **Typical Range:** **20 µm – 80 µm**.

## 2.2 The Energy Density Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Volumetric Energy Density (VED):** A scalar quantity representing the energy delivered per unit volume of powder. It is the most widely used single-parameter metric in KTU board questions for predicting melt pool behavior.

**Two common definitions are used in literature:**

1. **Area Energy Density (AED):** Useful for studying single-track behavior.

$$\text{AED} = \frac{P}{v}$$

2. **Volumetric Energy Density (VED):** Most cited in KTU exam answers.

$$\text{VED} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

Where:
- P = Laser power (W)
- v = Scan speed (mm/s)
- h = Hatch spacing (mm)
- t = Layer thickness (mm)
- Units of VED: **J/mm³**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Typical Range (Metals) | Effect of ↑ Increase |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Laser Power | P | W | 100 – 1000 | Deeper melt pool, more evaporation |
| Scan Speed | v | mm/s | 200 – 2000 | Shallower melt, finer grains |
| Hatch Spacing | h | µm | 50 – 200 | Less inter-track overlap |
| Layer Thickness | t | µm | 20 – 80 | Higher energy demand |
| Area Energy Density | AED | J/mm | $\frac{P}{v}$ | ↑ melts more per unit length |
| Volumetric Energy Density | VED | J/mm³ | $\frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | ↑ risk of keyholing |
| Melt Pool Depth | d | µm | 30 – 150 | ↑ with P, ↓ with v |
| Solidification Rate | R | K/s | $10^{5}$ – $10^{7}$ | ↑ with v |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Energy Density is **NOT** a universal process quality predictor. Two completely different parameter combinations can yield the same VED but produce vastly different microstructures. Always discuss **individual parameters** in long-answer questions, not just VED.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

These parameters directly control:
- **Densification** (target ≥ 99.5 % for aerospace-grade SLM parts)
- **Mechanical properties** (UTS, fatigue life, hardness)
- **Surface roughness** (Ra typically **5 – 20 µm** as-built)
- **Residual stresses** and distortion
- **Build time** and cost economics

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Volumetric Energy Density from First Principles

Let us rigorously derive the VED expression from energy conservation.

**Step 1 — Energy delivered by laser per unit time:**

$$E_{\text{delivered}} = P \cdot \Delta t$$

Where Δt is the time duration of laser exposure over a defined volume.

**Step 2 — Time taken for laser to traverse one scan track length L:**

$$\Delta t = \frac{L}{v}$$

**Step 3 — Energy delivered over one scan track of length L:**

$$E_{\text{track}} = P \cdot \frac{L}{v}$$

**Step 4 — Volume of material melted by that single track:**

The melted region forms a rectangular prism of length L, width h (hatch spacing), and depth t (layer thickness):

$$V_{\text{track}} = L \cdot h \cdot t$$

**Step 5 — Energy per unit volume (VED):**

$$\text{VED} = \frac{E_{\text{track}}}{V_{\text{track}}} = \frac{P \cdot \dfrac{L}{v}}{L \cdot h \cdot t}$$

$$\boxed{\text{VED} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}}$$

**Step 6 — Unit verification:**

$$\text{VED} = \frac{\text{J/s}}{(\text{mm/s}) \cdot \text{mm} \cdot \text{mm}} = \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^{3}}$$

Confirmed.

## 3.2 Derivation of Melt Pool Depth (Rosenthal-Type Approximation)

For a moving point heat source on a semi-infinite plate, the steady-state melt pool depth **d** can be approximated as:

$$\begin{aligned}
d &\approx \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot A \cdot P}{\pi \cdot \rho \cdot C_{p} \cdot v \cdot (T_{m} - T_{0})}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Where:**
- A = Absorptivity of material (dimensionless, typically **0.3 – 0.6** for metals at 1070 nm)
- ρ = Density of powder (kg/m³)
- C_p = Specific heat capacity (J/kg·K)
- T_m = Melting temperature (K)
- T_0 = Initial powder bed temperature (K)

**Step-by-step interpretation:**

1. **Numerator (A · P):** Higher absorptivity and power increase energy coupled into the material.
2. **Denominator (ρ · C_p · v · ΔT):** Higher scan speed means less dwell time, hence shallower penetration. Higher thermal mass requires more energy.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A SLM machine processes **316L stainless steel** powder with the following parameters: P = **200 W**, v = **800 mm/s**, h = **0.1 mm**, t = **0.03 mm**. Calculate the Volumetric Energy Density and comment on the expected porosity regime.

**Solution:**

Given:
- P = 200 W
- v = 800 mm/s
- h = 0.1 mm
- t = 0.03 mm

**Step 1 — Apply VED formula:**

$$\text{VED} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

$$\text{VED} = \frac{200}{800 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 0.03}$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator:**

$$\begin{aligned}
800 \cdot 0.1 &= 80 \\
80 \cdot 0.03 &= 2.4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Final VED:**

$$\text{VED} = \frac{200}{2.4} = 83.33 \; \text{J/mm}^{3}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret:** A VED of **~83 J/mm³** lies within the optimal process window for 316L stainless steel (typically **60 – 100 J/mm³**), predicting **densification > 99 %** with minimal keyholing porosity.

## 3.4 Process Defect Map — Analytical Matrix

| Defect Type | Cause (Parameter Effect) | Microstructural Signature |
|---|---|---|
| **Lack of Fusion** | Low P, high v, large h, large t → low VED (< 40 J/mm³) | Irregular, large pores at inter-track boundaries |
| **Keyholing** | High P, low v → very high VED (> 150 J/mm³) | Spherical pores from entrapped vapor |
| **Balling Effect** | High v + unstable melt pool | Discontinuous, beaded scan tracks |
| **Delamination** | Excessive residual stress from high P | Cracks parallel to build plane |
| **Cracking** | High thermal gradient (high v, low t) | Solidification cracks in alloys like Inconel |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Laser Parameter Interaction Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Laser Source 1070 nm] --> B[Galvanometer Scanners]
    B --> C[F-Theta Lens Focusing]
    C --> D[Powder Bed Surface]
    D --> E[Melt Pool Formation]
    E --> F[Solidification and Layer Bonding]
    
    P1[Laser Power P] --> E
    P2[Scan Speed v] --> E
    P3[Hatch Spacing h] --> E
    P4[Layer Thickness t] --> E
    
    E --> Q1{Energy Density Regime}
    Q1 -->|Low VED| R1[Lack of Fusion Porosity]
    Q1 -->|Optimal VED| R2[Dense Part 99.5 percent]
    Q1 -->|High VED| R3[Keyholing and Evaporation]
    
    R1 --> S1[Low UTS and Fatigue]
    R2 --> S2[Aerospace Grade Quality]
    R3 --> S3[Spatter and Surface Defects]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Process Parameter Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Parameter Set]
        I1[Power P]
        I2[Scan Speed v]
        I3[Hatch Spacing h]
        I4[Layer Thickness t]
    end
    
    INPUT --> CALC[VED Calculator]
    CALC --> REGIME{Process Window Check}
    
    REGIME -->|Below Window| WARN1[Increase P or reduce v]
    REGIME -->|Within Window| OK[Stable Build]
    REGIME -->|Above Window| WARN2[Reduce P or increase v]
    
    WARN1 --> CALC
    WARN2 --> CALC
    OK --> OUT[Qualified Part]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Explanation (Textual Description of Melt Pool Geometry)

Since melt pool cross-sections are physical micrographs, we describe the conceptual geometry here for KTU diagram-type questions:

```
       ←—— Hatch Spacing (h) ——→
       ┌─────────────────────┐    ↑
       │  ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  │    │
       │  ░░ MELT POOL ░░░░  │    │ Layer Thickness (t)
       │  ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  │    │
       └─────────────────────┘    ↓
       ───────────────────────── (Scan Direction → v)
```

- **Width** of pool ≈ 1.5 × to 2 × the laser spot diameter.
- **Depth** governed by Rosenthal equation (derived in §3.2).
- **Aspect Ratio (depth/width)** determines conduction-mode vs keyhole-mode melting.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Define Volumetric Energy Density (VED) in the context of Selective Laser Melting and write its SI-coherent unit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Volumetric Energy Density is the amount of laser energy delivered per unit volume of powder bed in a SLM process. It is the most common scalar used to characterize the laser-matter interaction regime.
>
> $$\text{VED} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$
>
> Where P = laser power, v = scan speed, h = hatch spacing, t = layer thickness.
>
> **Unit (1 mark):** **J/mm³** (since W = J/s and mm/s cancels with mm·mm).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]`

**Distinguish between "lack of fusion" porosity and "keyholing" porosity in SLM-built parts.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Lack of Fusion | Keyholing |
|---|---|---|
| **Cause** | Insufficient energy (low VED) | Excessive energy (high VED) |
| **Pore Shape** | Irregular, sharp-edged | Spherical, smooth |
| **Location** | Inter-track / inter-layer | Random within track |
| **Remedy** | ↑ P, ↓ v, ↓ h, ↓ t | ↓ P, ↑ v |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> - Students frequently write only the VED formula without explaining **individual** parameter effects → **lose 4–5 marks**.
> - Confusing **AED** (J/mm) with **VED** (J/mm³) → **lose 2 marks**.
> - Not providing **numerical example** in derivation-type questions → **lose 3 marks**.
> - Forgetting to mention **units** in the final answer → **lose 1 mark**.

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style — 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the effect of the following laser parameters on the melt pool geometry and resulting density of SLM-built parts:
  (i) Laser Power
  (ii) Scan Speed
  (iii) Hatch Spacing
  (iv) Layer Thickness

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for Volumetric Energy Density from first principles. An SLM machine is set with P = **285 W**, v = **950 mm/s**, h = **0.12 mm**, t = **0.04 mm**. Calculate the VED and state whether the part is likely to be in the **lack-of-fusion** or **keyholing** regime for Ti-6Al-4V powder.

**(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Effect of Each Parameter (7 marks: ~1.75 each)

**(i) Laser Power (P):**
- ↑ P → larger and deeper melt pool.
- Insufficient P → unmelted powder particles remain → porosity.
- Excessive P → vaporization of metal → recoil pressure → keyholing, spatter.

**(ii) Scan Speed (v):**
- ↑ v → shorter interaction time → shallower melt pool.
- Very high v → balling effect (Rayleigh instability breaks molten track into droplets).
- Low v → excessive energy → evaporation.

**(iii) Hatch Spacing (h):**
- ↑ h → gaps between scan tracks → inter-track porosity.
- Optimal h ≈ 0.5 × to 0.7 × melt pool width for adequate re-melting overlap.

**(iv) Layer Thickness (t):**
- ↑ t → laser must penetrate deeper → risk of lack of fusion at layer interfaces.
- ↓ t → higher build time, finer resolution, better surface finish.

---

#### Part (b) — Derivation + Numerical (7 marks: 4 derivation + 3 numerical)

**Derivation (4 marks):** Refer to §3.1 of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation leading to:

$$\boxed{\text{VED} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}}$$

**Numerical Solution (3 marks):**

Given:
- P = 285 W
- v = 950 mm/s
- h = 0.12 mm
- t = 0.04 mm

$$\text{VED} = \frac{285}{950 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 0.04}$$

**Step-by-step:**

$$\begin{aligned}
950 \cdot 0.12 &= 114 \\
114 \cdot 0.04 &= 4.56 \\
\text{VED} &= \frac{285}{4.56} = 62.5 \; \text{J/mm}^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** For Ti-6Al-4V, the optimal VED window is approximately **55 – 90 J/mm³**. The computed value of **62.5 J/mm³** falls **within the optimal window** → predicted densification **> 99 %**, no keyholing, no lack of fusion.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style — 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Discuss the concept of the **process window** in Selective Laser Melting. Sketch and explain the relationship between energy density and part porosity, identifying the zones of lack-of-fusion, optimal processing, and keyholing.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **316L stainless steel** part is built with the following two parameter sets:
  - **Set 1:** P = **150 W**, v = **1200 mm/s**, h = **0.08 mm**, t = **0.03 mm**
  - **Set 2:** P = **350 W**, v = **400 mm/s**, h = **0.15 mm**, t = **0.05 mm**
  
  Calculate the VED for both sets. Explain why two different parameter sets yielding similar VED values can produce **different** mechanical and microstructural outcomes.

**(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Process Window Concept (7 marks)

**Concept (3 marks):** The process window is the bounded region in the parameter space (P, v, h, t) within which the melt pool is stable, the part achieves > 99 % density, and defects are minimized.

**Porosity vs VED Curve (4 marks):**

```
Porosity (%)
   ↑
   │   \          / Keyholing
   │    \        /
   │     \      /
   │      \____/  ← Optimal Plateau (<1%)
   │       /
   │      / Lack of Fusion
   │_____/
   └──────────────────────→ VED (J/mm³)
       40    80   120   200
```

- **Below ~ 50 J/mm³:** Lack of fusion dominates → irregular pores.
- **~ 60 – 100 J/mm³ for 316L:** Optimal density plateau.
- **Above ~ 150 J/mm³:** Keyholing → spherical pores from vapor entrapment.

---

#### Part (b) — VED Calculation and Critical Comparison (7 marks)

**Set 1 Calculation (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{VED}_1 &= \frac{150}{1200 \cdot 0.08 \cdot 0.03} \\
&= \frac{150}{2.88} \\
&= 52.08 \; \text{J/mm}^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Set 2 Calculation (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{VED}_2 &= \frac{350}{400 \cdot 0.15 \cdot 0.05} \\
&= \frac{350}{3.0} \\
&= 116.67 \; \text{J/mm}^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Critical Analysis (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two VED values differ significantly (**52 vs 117 J/mm³**), but even if they had been numerically close, the microstructural outcomes would still differ because:
>
> 1. **Set 1** has high scan speed → finer grains → higher hardness but lower ductility.
> 2. **Set 2** has slow scan speed → coarser grains, longer dwell time → different phase formation.
> 3. **Melt pool depth/width ratio** differs because the **individual parameter magnitudes** govern heat flow, not just VED.
> 4. **Thermal history** (cooling rate ~ $10^{5}$ to $10^{7}$ K/s) is parameter-specific, not VED-specific.
>
> **Conclusion:** VED is a useful **first-order** indicator, but **not** a sufficient descriptor of part quality. Individual parameter optimization is mandatory in industrial AM.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Effect of Laser Parameters**
>
> - **Four primary parameters:** P (W), v (mm/s), h (mm), t (mm).
> - **VED formula:** $\text{VED} = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ → Unit: **J/mm³**.
> - **AED formula:** $\text{AED} = \dfrac{P}{v}$ → Unit: **J/mm**.
> - **↑ P** → deeper melt pool, risk of evaporation; **↓ P** → lack of fusion.
> - **↑ v** → shallower pool, finer grains; **↓ v** → keyholing.
> - **↑ h** → inter-track porosity; **optimal h** ≈ 0.5× – 0.7× melt pool width.
> - **↑ t** → risk of lack-of-fusion at layer interfaces.
> - **Optimal VED for 316L:** ~ 60 – 100 J/mm³; **Ti-6Al-4V:** ~ 55 – 90 J/mm³; **Inconel 718:** ~ 50 – 80 J/mm³.
> - **Laser type:** Yb-fiber, **λ = 1070 nm**, **A ≈ 0.3 – 0.7**.
> - **Cooling rates:** $10^{5}$ – $10^{7}$ K/s (orders of magnitude higher than casting).
> - **Three defect regimes:** lack-of-fusion (< 40 J/mm³), optimal (~ 60 – 100), keyholing (> 150).
> - **VED alone is insufficient** — always discuss individual parameters in KTU 14-mark answers.
> - **Melt pool depth** can be estimated via Rosenthal equation: $d \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2 A P}{\pi \rho C_p v (T_m - T_0)}}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Melting Modes - Conduction and Keyhole Mode of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Melting Modes - Conduction and Keyhole Mode of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In laser-based Additive Manufacturing (AM) processes such as **Laser Powder Bed Fusion (L-PBF)** and **Laser Directed Energy Deposition (L-DED)**, the laser beam interacts with the metallic powder/substrate, generating a localized **melt pool**. Depending on the magnitude of **volumetric energy density (VED)** delivered by the laser, the melt pool operates in one of two fundamentally distinct regimes:

- **Conduction Mode:** A shallow, hemispherical melt pool where heat transfer into the substrate is governed predominantly by **Fourier conduction** through the liquid metal. The maximum surface temperature of the melt pool remains **below the boiling point** of the alloy.
- **Keyhole Mode:** A deep, narrow, and elongated melt pool where the local **intensity of the laser beam exceeds the vaporization threshold** of the material. The recoil pressure generated by **evaporated metal vapor** displaces the liquid, creating a vapor cavity (the "keyhole") that penetrates deep into the substrate.

The transition between these two modes is governed by a dimensionless or process-parameter criterion relating laser intensity, scan speed, and the thermophysical properties (boiling point, absorptivity, thermal diffusivity) of the material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The melting mode is the foundational concept that determines **defect formation** (lack-of-fusion vs. keyhole porosity), **melt pool geometry**, **microstructural evolution**, and ultimately the **mechanical performance** of the printed part. Examiner questions frequently test the *causal relationship* between energy density and the resulting melting regime.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine heating a slab of butter with a kitchen torch:

- **Conduction Mode (Gentle Heating):** Hold the torch far away. The surface of the butter melts slowly into a wide, shallow puddle. Heat flows into the butter purely by conduction — no smoke, no bubbling, no sputtering. The melt pool is *broad and shallow* (depth $\ll$ width).

- **Keyhole Mode (Aggressive Heating):** Hold the torch very close. The surface temperature spikes above butter's vaporization/smoke point. Vapors jet out, pushing the molten butter downward, creating a **deep, narrow cavity** — exactly the geometry of a keyhole drilled in a door. The melt pool is *deep and narrow* (depth $\gg$ width).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Parallel:** The same physics governs **laser welding** — conduction-mode welds are wide and shallow (good for thin sheets), while keyhole-mode welds are deep and narrow (used for thick-section joining). In AM, the engineer must deliberately select the regime: conduction mode is preferred for **stable, low-defect** builds, while keyhole mode enables **higher build rates** but risks porosity.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Volumetric Energy Density (VED):** typically **30–100 J/mm³** for conduction mode; **> 100 J/mm³** can trigger keyhole.
- **Laser Power (P):** typically **100–500 W** (fiber laser, $\lambda$ ≈ **1070 nm**) for L-PBF of steels/titanium.
- **Beam Diameter (d):** typically **50–200 μm** in L-PBF.
- **Scan Speed (v):** typically **400–1500 mm/s** in L-PBF.
- **Boiling Point of Ti-6Al-4V:** ≈ **3287 °C** (a benchmark alloy).
- **Boiling Point of Stainless Steel 316L:** ≈ **2900 °C**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conduction Mode — Operational Breakdown

- **Energy input is moderate:** The absorbed laser intensity $I_0 = A \cdot P/(\pi r_b^2)$ is below the vaporization threshold.
- **Melt pool geometry:** Approximately **hemispherical / elliptical**, with depth $h_p \ll$ width $w_p$. Typical aspect ratio $AR = h_p / w_p \approx 0.3$–$0.5$.
- **Heat transfer mechanism:** Heat is dissipated into the substrate by **Fourier conduction**. Marangoni (surface-tension-driven) convection also redistributes heat within the pool, but no vaporization occurs.
- **Surface temperature:** $T_{surface} < T_{boil}$. No recoil pressure.
- **Defect signature:** If energy is *too low*, **lack-of-fusion (LoF) defects** appear — irregular, unmelted powder pockets between layers.
- **Advantages:** Stable melt pool, low porosity, low residual stress, predictable solidification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Why:** In conduction mode, the absorbed laser energy heats a thin surface layer. Because the heat flux is sub-vaporization, the liquid metal remains in contact with the solid. The melt-pool boundary is essentially an **isotherm** at the alloy's melting point — hence the shallow, broad, hemispherical shape.

### 2.2 Keyhole Mode — Operational Breakdown

- **Energy input is high:** The local intensity exceeds the **vaporization threshold** of the alloy.
- **Vaporization & recoil pressure:** The metal vapor escapes the melt pool axially, generating a **recoil pressure** ($P_{recoil}$) on the order of **10⁴–10⁵ Pa** that depresses the liquid surface.
- **Keyhole formation:** A **vapor cavity** forms and grows downward. Multiple reflections of the laser beam off the cavity walls (called **multiple Fresnel absorption** or **keyhole absorption**) cause the beam to penetrate **5–10× deeper** than in conduction mode.
- **Melt pool geometry:** **Deep, narrow, finger-like** cavity. Aspect ratio $AR = h_p / w_p \approx 1.0$–$2.0$ or higher.
- **Defect signature:** If the keyhole becomes unstable or collapses, **keyhole porosity** (spherical gas pores) and **balling** defects form.
- **Advantages:** Higher build rates, deeper fusion, suitable for high-reflectivity materials at sufficient power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Why:** The keyhole is essentially a **micro-welding cavity**. Vapor jets outward, and the Bernoulli-like pressure balance between recoil pressure, hydrostatic pressure, and surface tension determines the cavity depth. The depth is dynamically unstable — small perturbations cause the keyhole to "wobble," trapping gas bubbles that become porosity.

### 2.3 Transition Criterion Between Modes

The most widely accepted academic criterion is the **normalized enthalpy threshold**, proposed by **Egan & Paul (2016)** and refined for AM by **King et al.**:

$$\frac{\Delta H}{h_{s}} = \frac{A P}{(\pi)^{3/2} \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot T_{boil} \cdot \sqrt{\kappa \cdot d^3 \cdot v}}$$

- **Conduction mode:** $\Delta H / h_s \lesssim 5$–$7$
- **Keyhole mode:** $\Delta H / h_s \gtrsim 7$–$10$

A simpler engineering rule of thumb uses **linear energy density** $E_L = P/v$:

- **Conduction:** $E_L \lesssim 0.2$–$0.4$ J/mm (material-dependent)
- **Keyhole:** $E_L \gtrsim 0.4$–$0.8$ J/mm

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Absorbed Laser Intensity | $I_0 = A \cdot P / (\pi r_b^2)$ | W/m² | Power absorbed per unit beam area |
| 2 | Linear Energy Density | $E_L = P / v$ | J/mm | Energy delivered per unit scan length |
| 3 | Volumetric Energy Density (VED) | $E_V = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$ | J/mm³ | Energy delivered per unit volume of powder |
| 4 | Beer–Lambert Absorption in Powder | $I(z) = I_0 \cdot \exp(-\alpha z)$ | W/m² | Intensity decays exponentially with depth in powder bed |
| 5 | Melt Pool Aspect Ratio | $AR = h_p / w_p$ | dimensionless | Distinguishes conduction (low AR) from keyhole (high AR) |
| 6 | Normalized Enthalpy (King/Egan) | $\Delta H / h_s = AP / (\pi^{3/2} \rho c_p T_{boil} \sqrt{\kappa d^3 v})$ | dimensionless | Mode transition criterion |
| 7 | Recoil Pressure | $P_{recoil} \approx 0.54 \, P_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{L_v M}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{boil}} - \dfrac{1}{T_s}\right)\right)$ | Pa | Pressure that drives keyhole cavity |
| 8 | Fourier Heat Conduction | $q = -k \, dT/dx$ | W/m² | Governs conduction-mode heat flow |
| 9 | Thermal Diffusivity | $\kappa = k / (\rho c_p)$ | m²/s | Rate of heat diffusion into substrate |
| 10 | Keyhole Depth (empirical) | $h_k \approx \dfrac{A P}{\pi \rho c_p (T_{boil} - T_0) v} \cdot \dfrac{1}{r_b}$ | μm | Approximate cavity depth |

Where:
- $A$ = absorptivity, $P$ = laser power, $r_b$ = beam radius, $v$ = scan speed
- $h$ = hatch spacing, $t$ = layer thickness
- $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient of powder, $z$ = depth
- $\rho$ = density, $c_p$ = specific heat, $T_{boil}$ = boiling temperature
- $k$ = thermal conductivity, $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporization, $M$ = molar mass
- $R$ = universal gas constant, $P_0$ = ambient pressure, $T_s$ = surface temperature

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Aerospace Ti-6Al-4V brackets are typically printed in **conduction mode** to minimize keyhole porosity (which causes fatigue failure). Conversely, **Inconel 718** aerospace nozzles may use a deliberately *near-keyhole* regime for productivity, with process parameters tightly controlled to avoid instability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Volumetric Energy Density (VED) from First Principles

The **volumetric energy density** is the most fundamental process-window metric in AM. We derive it from the definition of energy per unit volume of material processed.

**Step 1 — Laser Energy Delivered per Unit Time:**
The laser delivers power $P$ (W = J/s). The energy delivered per unit time is simply $P$.

**Step 2 — Time Spent Heating a Unit Length of Track:**
The laser scans at speed $v$ (mm/s). The time the beam spends over a unit length (1 mm) of the track is:

$$t_{unit} = \frac{1 \text{ mm}}{v} = \frac{1}{v} \quad [\text{s/mm}]$$

**Step 3 — Energy per Unit Length of Track (Linear Energy Density):**
Multiplying power by time per unit length:

$$E_L = P \cdot t_{unit} = \frac{P}{v} \quad [\text{J/mm}]$$

**Step 4 — Cross-Sectional Area of the Melt Track per Pass:**
Each track has a width approximately equal to the **hatch spacing** $h$ (mm), and a depth approximately equal to the **layer thickness** $t$ (mm). The cross-sectional area is:

$$A_{track} = h \cdot t \quad [\text{mm}^2]$$

**Step 5 — Volume per Unit Length:**
Multiplying cross-sectional area by unit length:

$$V_{unit} = h \cdot t \cdot 1 = h \cdot t \quad [\text{mm}^3]$$

**Step 6 — Final VED Expression:**
Dividing linear energy density by the unit volume:

$$E_V = \frac{E_L}{V_{unit}} = \frac{P / v}{h \cdot t}$$

$$\boxed{E_V = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad [\text{J/mm}^3]}$$

**Interpretation:** VED captures the *total* energy deposited into a unit cell of the powder bed. A VED too low → lack of fusion. A VED too high → keyhole porosity. The *sweet spot* for conduction-mode defect-free printing is typically **40–80 J/mm³** for Ti-6Al-4V.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Beer–Lambert Absorption Within the Powder Bed

In L-PBF, the laser does not hit a flat solid surface — it penetrates a porous powder bed. The intensity decays exponentially with depth, following the Beer–Lambert law.

**Step 1 — Differential Form of Absorption:**
The fractional intensity lost in an infinitesimal layer $dz$ is proportional to the local intensity $I(z)$ and the absorption coefficient $\alpha$ (m⁻¹) of the powder bed:

$$\frac{dI}{dz} = -\alpha \, I(z)$$

**Step 2 — Separation of Variables:**
Divide both sides by $I(z)$ and multiply by $dz$:

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -\alpha \, dz$$

**Step 3 — Integration from Surface to Depth z:**
