> **Explanation:** Total fabrication time = (number of layers) × (time to scan one layer). Convert seconds to hours for practicality.

**Step 3 — Total Cycle Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{cycle} &= t_{build} + t_{post} \\[4pt]
t_{cycle} &= 50 + 1.5 \\[4pt]
t_{cycle} &= 51.5\ \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Approximately **2.15 days** for one aerospace bracket, including post-processing.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Conformal Cooling Insert Cycle Time Reduction

**Given Scenario (KTU Model Problem):**
An injection mold for a plastic gear has:
- Conventional straight cooling channel cycle time: $t_{conv} = 60$ s
- Heat removal rate (conventional): $\dot{Q}_{conv} = 1.2$ kW
- Conformal cooling insert (DMLS AlSi10Mg): Heat removal rate increased by **1.8×**

**Step 1 — Conformal Heat Removal**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{conformal} &= 1.8 \times \dot{Q}_{conv} \\[4pt]
\dot{Q}_{conformal} &= 1.8 \times 1.2\ \text{kW} \\[4pt]
\dot{Q}_{conformal} &= 2.16\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Cycle Time Reduction (Inverse Proportionality)**

Assuming cooling time dominates the cycle, $t \propto \frac{1}{\dot{Q}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{conformal} &= \frac{t_{conv}}{1.8} \\[4pt]
t_{conformal} &= \frac{60}{1.8} \\[4pt]
t_{conformal} &= 33.33\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Time Saved Per Cycle**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t &= t_{conv} - t_{conformal} \\[4pt]
\Delta t &= 60 - 33.33 \\[4pt]
\Delta t &= 26.67\ \text{s/cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual Time Saved (Assuming 100,000 parts/year)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_{annual} &= \Delta t \times 100{,}000 \\[4pt]
\Delta t_{annual} &= 26.67 \times 100{,}000 \\[4pt]
\Delta t_{annual} &= 2{,}667{,}000\ \text{s} \\[4pt]
\Delta t_{annual} &\approx 30.87\ \text{days}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This single derivation justifies why **conformal cooling inserts** are the #1 application of metal AM in the plastics industry — **30+ days of production time recovered annually**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Break-Even Analysis — AM vs. Injection Molding

**Given Scenario:**
- AM cost per part (material + machine + labor): $C_{AM} = ₹2{,}500$
- Injection molding material + machining cost: $C_{IM} = ₹400$
- Injection molding tooling cost: $C_{tooling} = ₹15{,}00{,}000$

**Step 1 — Cost Equations**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{AM}^{total}(n) &= n \times 2500 \\[4pt]
C_{IM}^{total}(n) &= 15{,}00{,}000 + n \times 400
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Setting Costs Equal (Break-Even)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n \times 2500 &= 15{,}00{,}000 + n \times 400 \\[4pt]
2500n - 400n &= 15{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
2100n &= 15{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
n_{BE} &= \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{2100} \\[4pt]
n_{BE} &\approx 714\ \text{units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Economic Interpretation**

For $n < 714$ units → **AM is cheaper**
For $n > 714$ units → **Injection molding is cheaper**

> **Application Link:** This is why AM dominates **low-volume, high-customization** applications like medical implants, aerospace brackets, and bespoke automotive parts.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (KTU Style)

| Application | Material | Recommended AM Process | Key Reason | Limitation |
|-------------|----------|------------------------|------------|------------|
| **Aero turbine blade** | Inconel 718 | DED / SLM | Complex cooling channels, high temp | Post-machining needed |
| **Patient hip implant** | Ti-6Al-4V | SLM / EBM | Patient-specific lattice, biocompatible | Requires HIP post-processing |
| **Dental crown** | Co-Cr / ZrO₂ | DLP / Binder Jetting | High accuracy, biocompatible | Surface finishing required |
| **Hearing aid shell** | Photopolymer resin | SLA | Mass customization from ear scan | Limited to photopolymer |
| **Engine bracket** | AlSi10Mg | SLM | Topology optimization, light-weighting | Cost at high volume |
| **Conformal cooling insert** | 316L SS | DMLS | Complex internal channels | Powder removal challenges |
| **Architectural model** | PLA / Concrete | FDM / Contour Crafting | Large-scale, low cost | Low mechanical strength |
| **Jewelry pattern** | Castable resin | SLA | High detail, smooth surface | Indirect (needs casting) |
| **Surgical guide** | Biocompatible resin | SLA / DLP | Anatomical accuracy | Single-use sterilization |
| **Satellite bracket** | Ti / Al | SLM | Lightweight, vibration-resistant | Outgassing testing required |
| **Food (chocolate)** | Cocoa butter | Material Extrusion | Food-safe, customized shapes | Limited to soft materials |
| **PCB prototype** | Conductive ink | Material Jetting / DIW | Embedded circuits | Limited conductivity |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`
**"List any three biomedical applications of Additive Manufacturing and the AM process used for each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**

1. **Patient-specific orthopedic implants** (hip, knee, cranial) — Manufactured via **SLM using Ti-6Al-4V powder** due to its high specific strength and biocompatibility.
2. **Dental crowns and bridges** — Produced using **DLP/SLA with ceramic-filled photopolymer resin**, offering high accuracy (~50 μm) and bio-inert surfaces.
3. **Surgical guides and anatomical models** — Built using **SLA with biocompatible resin**, enabling pre-operative planning and patient-specific drill guides.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this fetches full marks:** Each point includes **(a) the application**, **(b) the process**, and **(c) the material justification** — exactly the structure KTU examiners reward.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, S4 Model Paper]`
**"Explain the concept of conformal cooling inserts in injection molding and how AM enables it."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Conformal cooling inserts are **tool inserts with internal cooling channels that follow the contour of the mold cavity**, rather than running in straight lines. AM — specifically **DMLS using 316L stainless steel or maraging steel** — enables the fabrication of these complex internal geometries that are impossible to manufacture by conventional drilling or EDM.

**Benefits:**
- **1.8× higher heat removal** rate (as derived in Section 3.2)
- **~30–40% reduction** in cycle time
- **Improved part quality** with reduced warpage and sink marks

*(1 mark — concept; 1 mark — AM enabling; 1 mark — quantitative benefit)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]`

**"Discuss in detail the various applications of Additive Manufacturing in the aerospace industry. Explain with examples how AM has revolutionized aircraft component design and manufacturing."**

#### Part (a) — Aerospace Structural and Functional Applications (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The aerospace industry was one of the **earliest adopters of metal AM** due to its demand for lightweight, high-strength, and geometrically complex parts. Major applications include:

**(i) Topology-Optimized Brackets and Structural Fittings**
- **Process:** SLM / DMLS using Ti-6Al-4V or AlSi10Mg
- **Example:** Airbus A350 XWB cabin bracket — **30% lighter** than conventionally machined part with no compromise in load-bearing capacity
- **Benefit:** Weight reduction directly translates to fuel savings (~$1M/year per kg removed per aircraft)

**(ii) Combustion Chamber Liners and Injector Heads**
- **Process:** DED (Laser Metal Deposition) with Inconel 718
- **Example:** GE Aviation LEAP engine fuel nozzle — **20 parts consolidated into 1**
- **Benefit:** 25% fuel efficiency improvement; eliminates 800 brazed joints

**(iii) Bionic / Lattice Structures for Thermal Management**
- **Process:** SLM with graded lattice infill
- **Example:** Heat exchangers with **gyroid TPMS (Triply Periodic Minimal Surface)** lattices
- **Benefit:** Surface-area-to-volume ratio 5–10× higher than conventional fins

**(iv) Repair and Refurbishment of High-Value Components**
- **Process:** DED / Laser Cladding
- **Example:** Turbine blade tip restoration; landing gear overhaul
- **Benefit:** Extends part life, reduces MRO costs by up to **60%**

*(Breakdown: 4 applications × ~1.75 marks each = 7 marks)*

#### Part (b) — Revolution in Design & Manufacturing Paradigm (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Design Freedom — Topology Optimization**
Engineers can now specify **load paths** and let algorithms generate organic, organic-shaped structures that mimic bone trabeculae. Result: weight reduction of **30–70%** for the same stiffness.

**(ii) Part Consolidation**
Traditional assemblies of 10–20 components are replaced by **single monolithic prints**. Reduces assembly error, fastener count, and inspection overhead.

**(iii) Supply Chain Disruption — Digital Inventory**
Aircraft manufacturers now store parts as **CAD files**, not physical stock. On-demand printing of spares at remote airfields.

**(iv) Certification Challenges**
- Standards: **ASTM F3301** (post-processing), **AMS 7000 series** (process spec)
- Each part requires **extensive mechanical testing** (tensile, fatigue, creep, fracture toughness)

**(v) Material Range**
Titanium alloys, nickel superalloys, aluminum alloys (AlSi10Mg, Scalmalloy), and even refractory metals (W, Ta) are now processable.

*(Breakdown: 5 sub-points × ~1.4 marks each = 7 marks)*

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]`

**"Explain the role of Additive Manufacturing in biomedical engineering. Discuss in detail the materials, processes, and case studies for implants, prosthetics, and tissue engineering scaffolds."**

#### Part (a) — Implants and Prosthetics (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Orthopedic Implants**
- **Material:** Ti-6Al-4V (ASTM F136), Co-Cr alloys (ASTM F75)
- **Process:** SLM / EBM
- **Case Study:** Patient-specific **acetabular cup** for hip replacement — designed from CT scan, fabricated with porous lattice for **osseointegration**
- **Key Properties:** $\sigma_y \approx 880$ MPa, biocompatible, corrosion-resistant

**(ii) Dental Implants and Crowns**
- **Material:** Co-Cr, ZrO₂, Ti
- **Process:** DLP (resin), Binder Jetting (metal/ceramic), SLM (metal)
- **Case Study:** Same-day crown manufacturing — scan → design → print in <2 hours

**(iii) Craniomaxillofacial (CMF) Implants**
- **Process:** SLM with patient-specific CT-derived geometry
- **Example:** Custom cranial plates for trauma patients — fit accuracy <0.2 mm

**(iv) Prosthetic Sockets and Limbs**
- **Process:** FDM (for low-cost), SLS / MJF (for functional)
- **Benefit:** Custom-fit sockets improve comfort and biomechanics for amputees

*(Breakdown: 4 case studies × ~1.75 marks each = 7 marks)*

#### Part (b) — Tissue Engineering Scaffolds and Bioprinting (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Scaffold Design Requirements**
- **Porosity:** 60–80% for cell infiltration
- **Pore size:** 200–500 μm for vascularization
- **Biodegradability:** Match tissue regeneration rate (months–years)
- **Surface chemistry:** Promote cell adhesion (e.g., hydroxyapatite coating)

**(ii) Scaffold Fabrication Methods**
- **SLM with biodegradable polymers:** PCL, PLGA
- **Indirect printing:** Print sacrificial mold → cast scaffold
- **Bioprinting:** Co-deposit living cells with hydrogel bioink

**(iii) Materials Used**
- **Synthetic polymers:** PLA, PLGA, PCL
- **Natural polymers:** Collagen, gelatin, chitosan, alginate
- **Bioactive ceramics:** Hydroxyapatite (HA), tricalcium phosphate (TCP)
- **Hybrid bio-inks:** GelMA, Gelatin methacrylate

**(iv) Case Studies**
- **Ear cartilage scaffold:** 3D-printed auricle using patient stem cells (Cornell / Weill Cornell research)
- **Bone scaffold:** PCL/HA composite for alveolar bone regeneration
- **Vascularized tissue:** Sacrificial bio-ink (Pluronic F127) creates perfusable channels

**(v) Regulatory Pathway**
- **FDA 21 CFR 1271** governs HCT/Ps (Human Cells, Tissues, Cellular Products)
- **EU ATMP Regulation 1394/2007** classifies scaffold-based products as Advanced Therapy Medicinal Products

*(Breakdown: 5 sub-points × ~1.4 marks each = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Naming the process without justifying it:** Saying *"SLA is used in dental"* without explaining *why* (resolution, biocompatibility, surface finish) loses 1–2 marks per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing SLM vs. DMLS:** SLM is the trademark of SLM Solutions; DMLS is EOS's term — both are Powder Bed Fusion of metals.
> 3. **Ignoring post-processing:** Almost every AM part requires support removal, heat treatment (stress relief), and surface finishing. Examiners often deduct marks for omission.
> 4. **Mixing up ASTM/ISO standards:** ASTM F42 → ISO/ASTM 52900 (renamed in 2015). Use the current name.
> 5. **Numerical errors in break-even / build-time derivations:** Carry units throughout. A missing unit string costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 6. **Generic answers like "AM is fast and cheap":** Replace with **quantified statements** — *"AM reduces weight by 30% and consolidates 20 parts into 1"*.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Summary — Pin This on Your Wall:**

**🔷 Aerospace:**
- Ti-6Al-4V via SLM for brackets; Inconel 718 via DED for engine parts
- GE LEAP nozzle: 20 parts → 1 part
- Fuel savings = direct revenue driver

**🔷 Medical:**
- Ti-6Al-4V for implants; Co-Cr for dental; PCL/PLA for scaffolds
- Patient-specific geometry from CT/MRI data
- Standards: ASTM F136, F75, FDA 21 CFR 1271

**🔷 Automotive:**
- AlSi10Mg for brackets; PEEK/ULTEM for functional prototypes
- Conformal cooling: 1.8× heat removal, ~30% cycle time cut

**🔷 Tooling:**
- DMLS 316L inserts; SLA castable resin for investment patterns
- Break-even vs. injection molding: ~714 units (typical)

**🔷 Architecture (AEC):**
- Contour Crafting for concrete; FDM for scale models
- Large-scale printers (e.g., 10 m × 10 m × 40 m)

**🔷 Jewellery:**
- SLA castable resin → lost-wax casting → precious metal
- Resolution <25 μm enables intricate micro-features

**🔷 Food & Emerging:**
- Chocolate, sugar paste via material extrusion
- Conductive inks via DIW for PCB prototyping

**🔷 Universal Selection Rule:**
*Application → Material → Process → Post-Processing → Certification*

**🔷 Key Numerical Knobs:**
- Build Time: $t_{build} = h/z_{layer} \times t_{layer}$
- Energy Density: $E_v = P / (v \cdot h \cdot z_{layer})$
- Break-even: $n_{BE} = C_{tooling}/(C_{IM} - C_{AM})$

**🔷 Top Standards to Memorize:**
- ISO/ASTM **52900** (Process Categories)
- ASTM **F3301** (PBF Post-Processing)
- ASTM **F136** (Ti-6Al-4V for surgical implants)
- ASTM **F75** (Co-Cr for surgical implants)
