topics.title: Laser Engineered Net Shaping (LENS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Laser Engineered Net Shaping (LENS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Laser Engineered Net Shaping (LENS)** is a **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** based additive manufacturing process that uses a high-power laser beam as the focused heat source to melt metallic powder (or wire) fed coaxially through a deposition head, building near-net-shape parts layer-by-layer on a substrate under controlled atmosphere (typically argon or nitrogen).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Highlight)**
> LENS is classified under **ASTM F2792-12a** as a *Directed Energy Deposition (DED)* process. The technology was originally developed at **Sandia National Laboratories, USA** and later commercialized by **Optomec Inc.** The acronym stands for **L**aser **E**ngineered **N**et **S**haping.

| Terminology | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** | A category of AM in which focused thermal energy is used to fuse materials as they are deposited |
| **Net Shaping** | Manufacturing a part close to its final shape, minimizing post-machining |
| **Coaxial Nozzle** | Nozzle geometry where powder streams and laser beam converge at a common focal point |
| **Near-Net-Shape** | Part requiring only minor finishing operations |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are decorating a cake with an icing pen that extrudes soft icing, but instead of icing you are squirting **fine metal powder**, and instead of just letting it sit, a **super-hot laser torch instantly melts it into solid steel** the moment it touches the cake (the substrate). Move the pen slightly sideways — a thin strip of solid metal is now welded there. Move it up a tiny bit — repeat. Layer by layer, the cake turns into a **solid metal sculpture**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 30-Second Intuition**: LENS = *Laser-powered 3D metal printing where powder is sprayed and melted simultaneously in mid-air, then solidified on a platform to grow a part upward*.

**Key Standard Metrics Used in LENS:**
- **Laser Power (P):** typically **100 W to 4 kW** (often Nd:YAG or fiber laser at ~1064 nm)
- **Powder Feed Rate (ṁ):** **1 g/min to 30 g/min** per nozzle
- **Scan Speed (v):** **5 mm/s to 25 mm/s**
- **Layer Thickness (t):** **0.1 mm to 0.5 mm**
- **Spot Diameter (d):** **0.5 mm to 3 mm**
- **Chamber Oxygen Level:** kept **< 50 ppm** (oxygen acts as a contaminant in reactive metals like Ti)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Structured Logic

LENS operates on a four-stage synchronized sequence:

1. **Substrate Preparation:** A metal build plate (similar or dissimilar material) is mounted on a numerically controlled (CNC) stage or robotic arm inside an inert-atmosphere chamber.
2. **Powder Delivery:** Powder particles are entrained in a carrier gas (Argon) and pneumatically conveyed through multi-port coaxial nozzles that converge with the laser beam.
3. **Laser Interaction & Melt Pool Formation:** The focused laser strikes the substrate, creating a **molten pool** (~1500 °C – 3000 °C depending on material). Powder particles impinge on this pool, get absorbed, and fully melt.
4. **Layer-by-Layer Deposition:** After one layer solidifies, the Z-axis (or deposition head) increments by one layer thickness. The process repeats until the part is built.

### 2.2 System Components (Mandatory KTU Diagram Point)

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Laser Source** | Generates the high-energy beam (fiber/Nd:YAG/CO₂) |
| 2 | **Beam Delivery Optics** | Focuses and guides laser via mirrors/fibers |
| 3 | **Powder Feeder** | Precisely meters powder via rotating disk or auger |
| 4 | **Coaxial Nozzle** | Merges powder stream and laser beam at common focal point |
| 5 | **Carrier Gas System** | Transports powder (Argon/N₂); also shields melt pool |
| 6 | **CNC Stage / Robotic Arm** | Provides 4–5 axis motion |
| 7 | **Inert Atmosphere Chamber** | Prevents oxidation |
| 8 | **Process Controller & Sensors** | Monitors melt pool temperature, height |
| 9 | **Substrate / Build Plate** | Base on which part is deposited |
| 10 | **Cooling System** | Removes excess heat to avoid distortion |

### 2.3 Key Process Parameters

The LENS process is governed by **four primary process parameters** that control quality, density, and dimensional accuracy:

$$
P \rightarrow \text{Laser Power (W)} \quad ; \quad v \rightarrow \text{Scan Speed (mm/s)}
$$
$$
\dot{m} \rightarrow \text{Powder Feed Rate (g/min)} \quad ; \quad h \rightarrow \text{Hatch Spacing (mm)}
$$
$$
t \rightarrow \text{Layer Thickness (mm)} \quad ; \quad d \rightarrow \text{Spot Diameter (mm)}
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Equations for KTU Board Exam — Memorize These**

| # | Equation | Formula | Units | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** | $\eta = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | J/mm³ | Most critical LENS formula; controls density & defects |
| 2 | **Linear Energy Density (LED)** | $\Lambda = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | J/mm | Measures energy per unit length of bead |
| 3 | **Powder Catch Efficiency** | $\eta_c = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{deposited}}{\dot{m}_{supplied}} \times 100\%$ | % | Actual material retained vs. sprayed |
| 4 | **Volumetric Deposition Rate** | $R_v = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho}$ | mm³/s | Volume of material built per second |
| 5 | **Deposition Width** | $w \approx d + 2\cdot\delta$ | mm | Bead width approx. equals spot + spread |
| 6 | **Melt Pool Aspect Ratio** | $AR = \dfrac{D}{Z}$ | dimensionless | Depth (D) to width (Z) ratio; indicates conduction vs. keyhole mode |
| 7 | **Specific Energy per Unit Mass** | $E_m = \dfrac{P}{\dot{m}}$ | J/g | Energy input normalized by mass flow |
| 8 | **Number of Layers** | $N = \dfrac{H_{part}}{t}$ | dimensionless | Total layers for part of height $H_{part}$ |
| 9 | **Build Time (Ideal)** | $T_{build} = \dfrac{V_{part}}{R_v}$ | s | Ignoring inter-layer dwell time |
| 10 | **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) Width** | $w_{HAZ} \approx k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\alpha \cdot P}{v \cdot \rho \cdot c_p}}$ | mm | Influenced by thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ |

Where:
- $\rho$ = density of powder material (g/cm³)
- $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity (mm²/s)
- $c_p$ = specific heat capacity (J/g·K)
- $k$ = empirical constant (~2.0 – 3.5)
- $\delta$ = lateral spread of melt (~0.1 – 0.3 mm)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

LENS finds critical applications in:
- **Aerospace:** Titanium and Inconel turbine blade repair, blisk (blade-integrated disk) manufacturing
- **Biomedical:** Custom patient-specific Ti-6Al-4V orthopedic implants
- **Defense:** Hard-facing of armor with functionally graded materials (FGM)
- **Tooling:** Repair of high-value forging dies and molds
- **Multi-Material Components:** Depositing stainless steel on copper substrates for thermal management
- **Hybrid Manufacturing:** Integration with CNC milling on the same platform (e.g., DMG MORI LASERTEC)

> [!TIP]
> **Why LENS over SLM?** SLM (Selective Laser Melting) cannot easily build **multi-material** or **large-volume parts** because powder bed cannot be easily changed. LENS excels here due to **multi-hopper powder feeders**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Volumetric Energy Density (VED)

**Starting Concept:** Energy density represents the amount of laser energy delivered per unit volume of deposited material. It is the single most-cited parameter in KTU AM examinations.

**Step 1:** Energy delivered per unit length of the deposited track is the *Linear Energy Density*:

$$
\Lambda = \frac{P}{v} \quad \text{[J/mm]}
$$

**Step 2:** This energy spreads across a cross-sectional area of the deposited bead. For a rectangular cross-section approximation (valid for a single track):

$$
A_{bead} = w \times t \approx h \times t
$$

where the bead width $w$ is approximately equal to the hatch spacing $h$.

**Step 3:** Energy per unit volume is then the linear energy divided by the cross-sectional area:

$$
\eta = \frac{\Lambda}{A_{bead}} = \frac{P / v}{h \cdot t}
$$

**Step 4:** Final canonical form of Volumetric Energy Density:

$$
\boxed{\eta = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad [\text{J/mm}^3]}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- Higher $\eta$ → more energy per volume → denser part, but risk of **vaporization / keyhole porosity**
- Lower $\eta$ → less energy → risk of **lack of fusion (LoF) defects** and weak bonding
- Optimal range for Ti-6Al-4V: **$\eta \approx 60$ – $120 \text{ J/mm}^3$**

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Build Time

**Step 1:** Volume of material required for the part (assuming 100% density):

$$
V_{part} = \text{Surface Area}_{XY} \times H_{part}
$$

**Step 2:** Volumetric deposition rate from powder feed:

$$
R_v = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{s}]
$$

**Step 3:** Theoretical minimum build time ignoring dwell:

$$
\boxed{T_{build} = \frac{V_{part}}{R_v} = \frac{V_{part} \cdot \rho}{\dot{m}}}
$$

**Step 4:** Including inter-layer dwell time $\tau_d$ for cooling:

$$
T_{total} = T_{build} + N \cdot \tau_d
$$

$$
T_{total} = \frac{V_{part} \cdot \rho}{\dot{m}} + \frac{H_{part}}{t} \cdot \tau_d
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A LENS machine is used to deposit **Inconel 718** powder (density $\rho = 8.19 \text{ g/cm}^3$). The process parameters are:
> - Laser Power: $P = 400 \text{ W}$
> - Scan Speed: $v = 10 \text{ mm/s}$
> - Hatch Spacing: $h = 0.4 \text{ mm}$
> - Layer Thickness: $t = 0.25 \text{ mm}$
> - Powder Feed Rate: $\dot{m} = 8 \text{ g/min}$
>
> Calculate:
> (a) Volumetric Energy Density
> (b) Linear Energy Density
> (c) Specific Energy per unit mass
> (d) Theoretical deposition rate in mm³/s
> (e) Time to build a part of volume $V = 5000 \text{ mm}^3$

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Part (a): Volumetric Energy Density**

Substituting directly into the canonical formula:

$$
\eta = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} = \frac{400}{10 \times 0.4 \times 0.25}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{400}{1.0} = 400 \text{ J/mm}^3
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This value (400 J/mm³) is **excessively high** for Inconel 718 — typical optimum is **60 – 100 J/mm³**. The part would suffer **keyholing and vaporization**. A student should reduce power or increase scan speed.

**Part (b): Linear Energy Density**

$$
\Lambda = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{400}{10} = 40 \text{ J/mm}
$$

**Part (c): Specific Energy per Unit Mass**

First, convert feed rate to g/s:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{8}{60} = 0.1333 \text{ g/s}
$$

$$
E_m = \frac{P}{\dot{m}} = \frac{400}{0.1333} = 3000 \text{ J/g}
$$

**Part (d): Theoretical Deposition Rate**

Convert density: $\rho = 8.19 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 8.19 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/mm}^3$

$$
R_v = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{0.1333}{8.19 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
R_v = \frac{0.1333}{0.00819} = 16.28 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Part (e): Build Time**

$$
T_{build} = \frac{V_{part}}{R_v} = \frac{5000}{16.28}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{build} \approx 307.1 \text{ seconds} \approx 5.12 \text{ minutes}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: LENS vs. Competing DED Methods

| Parameter | **LENS (Laser + Powder)** | **EBM-DED (Electron Beam)** | **Plasma Arc DED** | **Wire Arc DED** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Laser (fiber/Nd:YAG) | Electron beam | Plasma arc | GMAW/GTAW arc |
| Feedstock | Powder | Powder / Wire | Powder | Wire |
| Power Range | 0.1 – 4 kW | 1 – 15 kW | 1 – 10 kW | 2 – 20 kW |
| Atmosphere | Inert gas chamber | Vacuum | Inert shield | Inert shield |
| Accuracy (typical) | ± 0.05 mm | ± 0.10 mm | ± 0.3 mm | ± 0.5 mm |
| Surface Roughness | 4 – 10 µm Ra | 10 – 20 µm Ra | 15 – 40 µm Ra | 30 – 100 µm Ra |
| Build Rate | Medium (1–10 cm³/min) | High (10–60 cm³/min) | Very High | Very High |
| Multi-material | Excellent | Limited | Good | Limited |
| Cost | High | Very High | Medium | Low |
| Best For | Repair, multi-material | Titanium, large parts | Hardfacing | Large structural parts |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: LENS Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Laser Source Nd YAG Fiber 1064 nm] --> B[Beam Delivery Optics Mirrors Lenses]
    B --> C[Coaxial Deposition Nozzle]
    D[Powder Hopper 1 Material A] --> E[Powder Feeder Rotating Disk]
    F[Powder Hopper 2 Material B Optional] --> E
    G[Carrier Gas Argon or Nitrogen] --> E
    E --> C
    C --> H[Melt Pool on Substrate]
    I[Substrate Build Plate] --> H
    H --> J[Solidified Metal Layer]
    J --> K[Z axis Increment layer thickness t]
    K --> L{Next Layer?}
    L -- Yes --> C
    L -- No --> M[Finished Near Net Shape Part]
    N[CNC Stage or 5 axis Robotic Arm] --> I
    O[Inert Atmosphere Chamber O2 less than 50 ppm] -.encloses.-> H
    P[Closed Loop Sensors Pyrometer CCD Camera] -.monitors.-> H
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Layer-by-Layer Deposition Logic

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Controller
    participant L as Laser
    participant PF as Powder Feeder
    participant N as Nozzle
    participant S as Substrate
    participant Q as Quality Sensor

    C->>L: Activate laser at Power P
    C->>PF: Start powder flow at feed rate m
    PF->>N: Powder + carrier gas stream
    L->>S: Focused beam strikes surface
    N->>S: Powder enters melt pool
    S->>S: Melting and fusion
    Q-->>C: Thermal feedback
    C->>S: Move XY to next scan vector
    Note over S: One full layer deposited
    C->>S: Increment Z by layer thickness t
    Note over C: Loop until part complete
```

### 4.3 Process Parameter Influence Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Increase Laser Power P] --> A1[Higher Energy Density]
    B[Increase Scan Speed v] --> B1[Lower Energy Density]
    C[Increase Hatch h] --> C1[Lower Energy Density]
    D[Increase Layer t] --> D1[Lower Energy Density]
    A1 --> X{Defect Type}
    B1 --> X
    C1 --> X
    D1 --> X
    X -- Excess Energy --> Y[Keyhole Porosity Vaporization]
    X -- Insufficient Energy --> Z[Lack of Fusion Porosity Delamination]
    X -- Optimal Range --> W[Dense Part greater than 99.5 percent]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Define Laser Engineered Net Shaping (LENS). Mention any two distinguishing features of LENS compared to Selective Laser Melting (SLM).**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
LENS is a Directed Energy Deposition (DED) additive manufacturing process in which a high-power laser beam is used to melt metal powder injected through a coaxial nozzle, building parts layer-by-layer on a substrate within an inert atmosphere chamber.

**Distinguishing Features (any two):**

| S.No | LENS | SLM |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Powder is *injected* through nozzles (DED) | Powder is *pre-spread* in a bed (PBF) |
| 2 | Can easily deposit **multiple materials** (multi-hopper) | Limited to single material per build |
| 3 | Suitable for **repair and coating** of large parts | Mainly used for small intricate parts |
| 4 | No support structures needed for overhangs | Requires support structures |
| 5 | High deposition rate, lower resolution | Lower rate, higher resolution |

*(3 Marks: 1 for definition + 1 each for two features)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List the four primary process parameters of LENS and write the formula for Volumetric Energy Density (VED).**

**Model Answer:**

The four primary process parameters are:
1. **Laser Power (P)** in Watts
2. **Scan Speed (v)** in mm/s
3. **Hatch Spacing (h)** in mm
4. **Layer Thickness (t)** in mm

**Volumetric Energy Density:**

$$
\eta = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad \left[\frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3}\right]
$$

*(3 Marks: 0.5 each for parameter + 1.5 for formula with units)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice Note:** KTU 2024 scheme requires students to answer **EITHER** Question A OR Question B in full (both 14 marks).

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of LENS with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the coaxial nozzle and inert atmosphere. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A LENS system deposits Ti-6Al-4V powder ($\rho = 4.43 \text{ g/cm}^3$) with the following parameters: $P = 300 \text{ W}$, $v = 12 \text{ mm/s}$, $h = 0.35 \text{ mm}$, $t = 0.20 \text{ mm}$, $\dot{m} = 6 \text{ g/min}$. Calculate:
- (i) Volumetric Energy Density
- (ii) Linear Energy Density
- (iii) Build time for a part volume of 3000 mm³ **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle:**

LENS uses a focused laser beam (fiber or Nd:YAG, ~1064 nm) to melt metallic powder delivered coaxially through a multi-port nozzle. The powder-laden carrier gas (Argon) converges with the laser at a focal point on the substrate, creating a small molten pool (~ few mm³). The substrate is mounted on a CNC table (4-axis or 5-axis). After one layer solidifies, the Z-axis is incremented by layer thickness $t$, and the process repeats.

**Role of Coaxial Nozzle:**
- Allows **5-axis deposition** (rotational symmetry about laser axis)
- Eliminates directional bias of single-side powder injection
- Enables deposition on **non-planar / curved surfaces** — critical for repair
- Multiple powder streams converge at common focal point for uniform melt pool

**Role of Inert Atmosphere:**
- Prevents **oxidation** of reactive metals (Ti, Al, Mg) at melt-pool temperatures
- Maintains **oxygen level < 50 ppm** (sometimes < 10 ppm for aerospace Ti)
- Prevents **nitrogen pick-up** which embrittles titanium
- Shields laser optics from metal vapor contamination

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.1)

*(7 Marks: 3 for principle, 2 for nozzle, 2 for inert atmosphere + diagram)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Volumetric Energy Density:**

$$
\eta = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} = \frac{300}{12 \times 0.35 \times 0.20}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{300}{0.84} \approx 357.14 \text{ J/mm}^3
$$

**(ii) Linear Energy Density:**

$$
\Lambda = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{300}{12} = 25 \text{ J/mm}
$$

**(iii) Build Time:**

Convert density: $\rho = 4.43 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 4.43 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/mm}^3$

Convert feed rate: $\dot{m} = 6/60 = 0.10 \text{ g/s}$

$$
R_v = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{0.10}{4.43 \times 10^{-3}} = 22.57 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
T_{build} = \frac{V}{R_v} = \frac{3000}{22.57} \approx 132.9 \text{ s} \approx 2.22 \text{ min}
$$

*(7 Marks: 2 each for (i) and (ii), 3 for (iii) with unit conversions)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the major process parameters affecting the LENS process. Explain how Volumetric Energy Density (VED) influences part density, porosity, and microstructure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare LENS with Electron Beam Additive Manufacturing (EBAM) and Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM) on the basis of feedstock, energy source, build rate, accuracy, and applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Major Process Parameters:**

1. **Laser Power (P):** Higher power increases melt pool size and depth but may cause vaporization.
2. **Scan Speed (v):** Faster scanning reduces heat input; too slow causes over-melting.
3. **Hatch Spacing (h):** Smaller overlap = denser part; large spacing = porosity between tracks.
4. **Layer Thickness (t):** Affects Z-resolution and re-melt depth.
5. **Powder Feed Rate (ṁ):** Determines deposition volume; too high → unmelted powder.
6. **Oxygen Content:** Critical for reactive metals.

**VED Influence:**

- **VED < 40 J/mm³:** Insufficient energy → **Lack of Fusion (LoF)** defects, inter-track porosity, weak bonding
- **VED = 60 – 120 J/mm³:** Optimal → fully dense part (> 99.5%), fine equiaxed grains
- **VED > 200 J/mm³:** Excess energy → **Keyhole porosity** (deep narrow cavities due to vapor recoil pressure), grain coarsening, element evaporation (e.g., Mg loss in Al alloys)

Microstructure transition: low VED → columnar dendritic; high VED → equiaxed grains.

*(7 Marks: 4 for parameters, 3 for VED analysis)*

**(b) Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | LENS | EBAM | WAAM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feedstock | Powder | Wire / Powder | Wire |
| Energy Source | Laser (fiber/Nd:YAG) | Electron beam | Electric arc |
| Atmosphere | Inert gas | Vacuum | Shielding gas |
| Build Rate | 1–10 cm³/min | 10–60 cm³/min | 50–500 cm³/min |
| Dimensional Accuracy | ± 0.05 mm | ± 0.10 mm | ± 0.5 mm |
| Surface Roughness | 4–10 µm Ra | 10–20 µm Ra | 30–100 µm Ra |
| Materials | Ti, Al, steel, Inconel | Ti, Al mainly | Steel, Al, Ti |
| Multi-material | Excellent | Limited | Limited |
| Typical Applications | Repair, implants, blisks | Large Ti aerospace parts | Ship hulls, large structures |
| Cost | High | Very High | Low |

*(7 Marks: 5 for table + 2 for critical commentary on trade-offs)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (1 mark lost):** Students often forget to convert powder feed rate from **g/min → g/s** before calculating $R_v$ or $E_m$.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (2 marks lost):** Failing to mention **ASTM F2792 classification** of LENS as a DED process. Examiners expect this explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (2 marks lost):** Not justifying *why* VED optimum exists — students state "60–100 J/mm³" but cannot explain the physics of keyhole vs. conduction mode melting.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (1.5 marks lost):** Confusing LENS with SLM — saying "powder bed" instead of "powder injection through nozzle".
> - **Common Mistake 5 (1 mark lost):** Not drawing the block diagram in part (a) of Q.A — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if no diagram is present.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ LENS = **Laser Engineered Net Shaping**; an ASTM F2792 **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** process.
- ✅ Developed by **Sandia National Labs**; commercialized by **Optomec Inc.**
- ✅ Feedstock: **Metal powder** injected via **coaxial nozzle**; can use **multi-hopper** for multi-material parts.
- ✅ Atmosphere: **Inert (Argon)**; O₂ < 50 ppm for reactive metals.
- ✅ Four primary parameters: **P, v, h, t** — combined into **VED = P/(v·h·t)**.
- ✅ Optimal VED for most metals: **60 – 120 J/mm³**.
- ✅ Excess VED → **keyhole porosity**; deficient VED → **lack of fusion**.
- ✅ Laser wavelength: **~1064 nm** (fiber or Nd:YAG); **never CO₂** for metal (low absorptivity).
- ✅ LENS is best for **repair, coating, and multi-material** parts — **not** for high-resolution intricate parts (use SLM).
- ✅ Spot diameter: **0.5 – 3 mm**; layer thickness: **0.1 – 0.5 mm**; typical scan speed: **5 – 25 mm/s**.
- ✅ Build rate formula: $R_v = \dot{m} / \rho$; Build time: $T = V \cdot \rho / \dot{m}$ (ignoring dwell).
- ✅ Common LENS materials: **Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel 718, SS 316L, AlSi10Mg, tool steel H13**.
- ✅ Key advantage over SLM: **no support structures, multi-material, large part repair**.
- ✅ Key disadvantage vs. SLM: **lower resolution, higher surface roughness**.
- ✅ Always convert units **g/min → g/s** and **g/cm³ → g/mm³** before calculation.
- ✅ Draw **block diagrams** in part-(a) answers — KTU awards 1–2 marks explicitly for diagrams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct Metal Deposition (DMD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Direct Metal Deposition (DMD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Direct Metal Deposition (DMD)** is a *Directed Energy Deposition (DED)* based additive manufacturing process in which a focused thermal energy source (laser, electron beam, plasma arc, or gas metal arc) is used to create a localized melt pool on a substrate, while metallic feedstock in the form of **powder** or **wire** is simultaneously injected into that melt pool through a coaxial or multi-jet nozzle. The injected material fuses with the substrate (or previously deposited layer), and the energy source is traversed along a predefined path to build a fully dense, metallurgically bonded 3D metallic structure in a layer-by-layer fashion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Verbatim Style)**
> DMD is the ASTM/ISO 52900 category **DED-Arc** or **DED-LB** (Laser-Based) process in which metal powder/wire is melted **simultaneously with** (not deposited on) a moving heat source. It is distinct from Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) where the powder bed is pre-laid and selectively melted.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Welding Cake Icing" Metaphor**
> Imagine you are decorating a cake with icing. Instead of using a piping bag that touches the cake surface, you hold a small butane torch that melts a stream of chocolate powder falling right where the flame hits. The chocolate melts, sticks to the cake, and solidifies the moment the flame moves away. If you trace the flame in a circle, you draw a chocolate ring. If you stack these rings layer by layer, you build a **solid chocolate sculpture** — exactly the way DMD builds a metal part.
> 
> - The **butane torch** = Laser / Plasma / Arc (Energy Source)
> - The **chocolate powder** = Metal Powder Feedstock
> - The **melted pool** = Molten Metal Track
> - The **cake** = The Build Substrate

## 1.3 Classification of DMD (ASTM F42 / ISO/ASTM 52900 Taxonomy)

| Sub-Category | Energy Source | Feedstock | Typical Use |
|--------------|---------------|-----------|-------------|
| **DED-LB (Laser Beam)** | High-power Laser (Fiber/CO₂) | Powder (coaxial) | Aerospace, Ti alloys, Inconel |
| **DED-EB (Electron Beam)** | Electron Beam | Wire / Powder | Vacuum chambers, large parts |
| **DED-Arc (Plasma/PAW)** | Plasma transferred arc | Wire | Large-scale deposition, shipbuilding |
| **DED-GMAW (Wire Arc)** | MIG/TIG arc | Wire | Low-cost, high deposition rate |
| **DED-PWJ** | Plasma transferred arc | Powder | Hybrid repair |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Production Engineering syllabus emphasizes **laser-based DMD (DED-LB)** and **wire-arc DMD (DED-Arc)** for board-level questions, along with their **process parameters** and **applications**.

## 1.4 Industrial Importance

- **Aerospace**: Repair of turbine blades, deposition of superalloys (Inconel 718, Ti-6Al-4V).
- **Automotive**: Customized tooling, prototype functional metal parts.
- **Oil & Gas**: Cladding of corrosion/wear-resistant layers on pipes.
- **Biomedical**: Patient-specific Ti-alloy implants.
- **Defense**: Repair of high-value components in-field, hybrid additive-subtractive manufacturing.

Standard industry metrics to remember:
- **Deposition rate**: **0.1 – 10 kg/hour** (powder), up to **15–20 kg/h** (wire-arc)
- **Dimensional tolerance**: **± 0.2 – 0.5 mm**
- **Build envelope (typical laser DMD)**: up to **1 m × 1 m × 1 m** (multi-axis robotic)
- **Layer thickness**: **0.2 – 2.0 mm** (much larger than PBF)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **CAD Model Slicing**: A 3D CAD model is sliced into a series of 2D cross-sectional layers, with each layer generating a **tool path** (tool-path generation step).
2. **Energy Source Initiation**: A focused energy source (laser/arc/EB) is activated and directed onto the build substrate.
3. **Powder Injection**: Metal powder (carried by **inert gas**, typically **Argon** to prevent oxidation) is injected coaxially or laterally into the melt zone.
4. **Melt Pool Formation**: The energy density at the focal spot exceeds the melting point of the substrate + powder, generating a **melt pool** of dimensions typically **1–5 mm wide** and **0.5–1.5 mm deep**.
5. **Solidification & Bonding**: As the energy source moves along the tool path, the trailing edge of the melt pool solidifies, creating a **metallurgically bonded track**.
6. **Z-axis Increment**: After one layer is complete, the deposition head moves up by one **layer thickness (ΔZ)**, and the next layer is deposited.
7. **Closed-Loop Control (advanced systems)**: Real-time monitoring of **melt-pool temperature**, **geometry**, and **powder flow** is performed using pyrometers and CMOS cameras for closed-loop control.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of the Process** — DMD combines **high deposition rate** (suitable for large parts), **multi-material capability** (can mix 2+ powders simultaneously for **Functionally Graded Materials — FGM**), and **in-situ repair** capability. The "How" of the process relies on the simultaneous interaction of **mass flow** and **heat input**, governed by the formulas below.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists the **high-yield formulas** that KTU examiners typically test. Memorize the symbol meanings, the boundary conditions, and the units.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|-----------------|----------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $\dot{m}_{d} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | $\dot{m}_{d}$ = mass deposition rate, $\rho$ = density, $A$ = cross-section area, $v$ = traverse speed | kg/s | Volumetric / mass balance |
| 2 | $A = w \cdot h$ | $w$ = bead width, $h$ = bead height | m² | Single-track geometry |
| 3 | $v = \frac{f_{r}}{w \cdot h \cdot \rho}$ | $f_{r}$ = powder feed rate (kg/s) | m/s | Effective scan speed from mass balance |
| 4 | $E = \frac{P}{v \cdot d}$ | $E$ = linear energy density, $P$ = laser power, $v$ = scan speed, $d$ = beam spot diameter | J/mm | Melt pool control |
| 5 | $E_{v} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | $E_v$ = volumetric energy density, $h$ = hatch spacing, $t$ = layer thickness | J/mm³ | KTU favourite — process map |
| 6 | $D = \frac{A_{b}}{A_{b}+A_{d}}$ | $D$ = dilution, $A_b$ = substrate-melt area, $A_d$ = deposit area | dimensionless | Cladding / repair quality |
| 7 | $\eta = \frac{\dot{m}_{d}}{\dot{m}_{f}}$ | $\eta$ = powder catchment efficiency, $\dot{m}_f$ = powder feed rate | dimensionless | Process efficiency |
| 8 | $R_{a} = \frac{w}{h}$ | $R_a$ = aspect ratio of single track | dimensionless | Surface quality indicator |
| 9 | $T_{m} = T_{0} + \frac{P}{\pi k r}$ | $T_m$ = melt-pool peak temperature, $k$ = thermal conductivity, $r$ = radial distance | K | Rosenthal-type analysis |
| 10 | $h_{eff} = \frac{\dot{m}_{f}}{w \cdot v \cdot \rho}$ | Effective layer thickness per pass | m | Layer planning |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake** — Students often confuse **Linear Energy Density** ($E = P/v$) with **Volumetric Energy Density** ($E_v = P/vht$). For KTU questions involving **process maps** or **parameter optimization**, the **volumetric form** is almost always the correct one.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Formula | Industrial Use |
|---------|----------------|
| $E_v = P / (v h t)$ | Used to construct **process maps** that identify regions of **lack of fusion**, **keyhole porosity**, and **optimal densification**. |
| $D = A_b / (A_b + A_d)$ | Critical in **cladding applications** where too high a dilution contaminates the coating chemistry. |
| $\eta$ (catchment efficiency) | Determines **powder waste** — affects **cost-per-part** analysis in aerospace. |
| $T_m$ (peak temperature) | Used in **FEA thermal simulations** to predict residual stress and distortion. |

## 2.4 Comparative Process Analysis (DMD vs PBF vs WAAM)

| Parameter | DMD (Laser/Powder) | PBF (SLM) | WAAM (Wire/Arc) |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------|-----------------|
| Energy Density | Medium | Very High | Low |
| Deposition Rate | 0.1–1 kg/h | 0.05–0.2 kg/h | 5–15 kg/h |
| Tolerance (mm) | ± 0.3 | ± 0.05 | ± 1.0 |
| Surface Roughness (Ra, µm) | 10–25 | 5–15 | 50–200 |
| Material Variety | Wide | Limited (spherical powder) | Wide |
| Multi-material | Yes (co-injection) | No | Yes |
| Build Size | Large | Limited | Very Large |
| Cost | High | Very High | Low |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass Flow Rate and Traverse Speed Relationship

**Objective:** To derive the relationship between powder feed rate, melt pool cross-section, and traverse speed in a steady-state DMD process.

### Step 1 — System Boundary Definition
Consider a control volume enclosing the melt pool. In steady state, the **mass entering** equals the **mass leaving** (conservation of mass).

### Step 2 — Mass Inflow
The powder feed rate is $\dot{m}_{f}$ (kg/s), and only a fraction $\eta$ (catchment efficiency) is captured by the melt pool. So:
$$
\dot{m}_{in} = \eta \cdot \dot{m}_{f}
$$

### Step 3 — Mass Outflow (Solidified Track)
The deposited mass per unit length is the cross-sectional area $A$ (m²) of the solidified bead multiplied by density $\rho$ (kg/m³):
$$
\dot{m}_{out} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v
$$

### Step 4 — Equating Inflow and Outflow (Steady State)
$$
\eta \cdot \dot{m}_{f} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v
$$

### Step 5 — Solving for Traverse Speed
$$
v = \frac{\eta \cdot \dot{m}_{f}}{\rho \cdot A}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** This shows that doubling the powder feed rate (with constant catchment) doubles the scan speed for the same bead size, **or** keeps the same scan speed while producing a taller, wider bead.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Volumetric Energy Density Threshold for Densification

**Objective:** To derive the condition for fully dense (>99.5%) deposition in laser DMD.

### Step 1 — Definition
$$
E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$
where $P$ = laser power (W), $v$ = scan speed (mm/s), $h$ = hatch spacing (mm), $t$ = layer thickness (mm).

### Step 2 — Threshold for Densification
For a given material (say **Ti-6Al-4V**), experimental studies show a **densification window**:
$$
60 \leq E_v \leq 120 \quad \text{(J/mm³)}
$$

### Step 3 — If $E_v$ is Too Low (e.g., $E_v < 60$ J/mm³)
- Insufficient melting → **lack of fusion** defects
- Porosity increases above 2%
$$
\text{Porosity} \, \phi \uparrow \quad \text{as} \quad E_v \downarrow
$$

### Step 4 — If $E_v$ is Too High (e.g., $E_v > 120$ J/mm³)
- Excessive vaporization → **keyhole porosity** forms
- Elemental loss (e.g., Mg, Zn in Al-alloys)
$$
\text{Vaporization rate} \uparrow \quad \text{as} \quad E_v \uparrow
$$

### Step 5 — Optimal Selection
The optimum $E_v$ balances melt pool depth and width:
$$
E_v^{opt} = \frac{P^{opt}}{v^{opt} \cdot h^{opt} \cdot t^{opt}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU numerical problems, you will often be given $P$, $v$, $h$, $t$ and asked to compute $E_v$, then state whether the process falls in the **densification window**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dilution Ratio for Cladding Applications

**Objective:** To derive the geometric dilution in a single-pass DMD cladding.

### Step 1 — Define the Geometry
A single clad track has:
- **Total melted cross-section** = $A_t = A_b + A_d$
- **Substrate-melted region** = $A_b$ (penetration into base)
- **Deposited region (above substrate)** = $A_d$

### Step 2 — Geometric Dilution
$$
D = \frac{A_b}{A_t} = \frac{A_b}{A_b + A_d}
$$

### Step 3 — Express in Terms of Height
For a bead approximated as a hemisphere of radius $r$ sitting in a cylindrical substrate recess of depth $p$:
$$
A_d = \pi r^2 \cdot \alpha_d, \quad A_b = \pi r^2 \cdot \alpha_b
$$
where $\alpha_d, \alpha_b$ are geometric constants. Hence:
$$
D = \frac{\alpha_b}{\alpha_d + \alpha_b}
$$

### Step 4 — Practical Engineering Target
For **corrosion-resistant cladding**, KTU/industry target is:
$$
D \approx 0.05 \text{ to } 0.15
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Higher dilution means the substrate chemistry is mixed more into the deposit — this is **unacceptable** for hard-facing or corrosion-resistant coatings.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Given:**
- Laser power $P = 800$ W
- Scan speed $v = 10$ mm/s
- Hatch spacing $h = 0.5$ mm
- Layer thickness $t = 0.4$ mm
- Powder feed rate $\dot{m}_f = 0.05$ kg/s
- Catchment efficiency $\eta = 0.6$
- Material: Ti-6Al-4V, $\rho = 4430$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Calculate $E_v$:**
$$
E_v = \frac{800}{10 \times 0.5 \times 0.4} = \frac{800}{2.0} = 400 \text{ J/mm³}
$$

**Step 2 — Comment:**
$400 \text{ J/mm³} \gg 120 \text{ J/mm³}$ → **Excessive energy density** → Expect keyhole porosity and vaporization. Recommend increasing $v$ to $v \geq 33$ mm/s.

**Step 3 — Recalculate $E_v$ at $v = 35$ mm/s:**
$$
E_v = \frac{800}{35 \times 0.5 \times 0.4} = \frac{800}{7.0} = 114.3 \text{ J/mm³}
$$
$114.3$ J/mm³ falls within the 60–120 J/mm³ window → **Optimal**.

**Step 4 — Calculate Mass Deposition Rate:**
$$
\dot{m}_d = \eta \cdot \dot{m}_f = 0.6 \times 0.05 = 0.03 \text{ kg/s} = 108 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Bead Cross-section from Mass Balance:**
$$
A = \frac{\dot{m}_d}{\rho \cdot v} = \frac{0.03}{4430 \times 0.035} = 1.93 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 1.93 \text{ mm}^2
$$

If bead is approximately a half-circle of width $w = 2$ mm:
$$
h = \frac{2A}{w} = \frac{2 \times 1.93}{2} = 1.93 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Answer:** The optimal DMD parameters are $v = 35$ mm/s, $E_v = 114.3$ J/mm³, bead height $\approx 1.93$ mm, and a single-track cross-section of $1.93$ mm².
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Architecture Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete functional architecture** of a DMD system — including signal flow, mechanical axis, and process control feedback.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Model] --> B[Slicer Software]
    B --> C[Tool Path Generator]
    C --> D[NC Controller / Robot Controller]
    D --> E[5 or 6 Axis Motion System]
    E --> F[Deposition Head with Coaxial Nozzle]
    G[High Power Laser Source] --> F
    H[Powder Feeder 1] --> F
    I[Powder Feeder 2] --> F
    J[Inert Gas Supply Argon] --> F
    F --> K[Melt Pool on Substrate]
    K --> L[Pyrometer Temperature Sensor]
    K --> M[CMOS Camera Vision System]
    L --> N[Closed Loop Controller]
    M --> N
    N --> G
    N --> H
    K --> O[Solidified Deposition Track]
    O --> P[Build Platform Z Axis]
    P --> F
    O --> Q[Final 3D Metal Part]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **block-level functional architecture**. Real physical schematics would show nozzle cross-sections, but Mermaid handles the system-level interactions far more clearly for KTU theory questions.

## 4.2 Coaxial Nozzle — Functional Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Laser Beam Central Axis] --> N2[Optical Focusing Lens]
    N2 --> N3[Converging Beam at Nozzle Tip]
    P1[Powder Stream 1 30 degrees] --> N3
    P2[Powder Stream 2 150 degrees] --> N3
    P3[Powder Stream 3 270 degrees] --> N3
    P4[Powder Stream 4 330 degrees] --> N3
    N3 --> N4[Melt Pool on Substrate]
    G1[Shield Gas Annulus] --> N4
```

**Engineering Reading of the Diagram:**
- The **4 powder streams** are angled (typically **30°–45° from vertical**) to converge at a single focal point.
- The **central channel** carries the focused laser beam.
- The **outer annulus** carries **shield gas** (Argon) to prevent in-flight powder oxidation.

## 4.3 Process Parameter Interaction Map (Cause-Effect)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Laser Power P] --> MP[Melt Pool Size]
    V[Scan Speed v] --> MP
    PR[Powder Feed Rate mf] --> MP
    MP --> W[Bead Width w]
    MP --> H[Bead Height h]
    MP --> POR[Porosity]
    P --> EV[Volumetric Energy Density Ev]
    V --> EV
    HS[Hatch Spacing h spacing] --> EV
    LT[Layer Thickness t] --> EV
    EV --> POR
    EV --> DENS[Final Density]
    DENS --> QA[Part Quality]
    POR --> QA
    W --> QA
    H --> QA
```

## 4.4 Subsystem Cross-Reference Table

| Diagram Block | Real Hardware | KTU Exam Tag |
|---------------|---------------|--------------|
| High Power Laser | Fiber laser (1–10 kW, 1070 nm) | [DED-LB] |
| Powder Feeder | Disk or volumetric feeder (PlasmaTechnik Twin 10) | [DED-LB] |
| Coaxial Nozzle | Precitec, Fraunhofer ILT design | [DED-LB] |
| Motion System | 5/6-axis CNC gantry or KUKA robot | [All DMD] |
| Sensors | Pyrometer + CMOS (e.g., Prometheus, EOS) | [Closed Loop] |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech ESE (End Semester Evaluation)** pattern. Marks distribution strictly follows the **Module Weightage** as per the latest syllabus (Module 4 carries 20% weightage).

---

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Direct Metal Deposition (DMD) and list its classification as per ASTM/ISO 52900.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
DMD is a Directed Energy Deposition (DED) additive manufacturing process in which focused thermal energy is used to melt metallic feedstock (powder or wire) simultaneously with a substrate to form a metallurgically bonded 3D structure **(1 mark)**. As per ASTM/ISO 52900, it is classified into:
- DED-LB (Laser Beam) — uses powder **(0.5 mark)**
- DED-EB (Electron Beam) — uses wire or powder in vacuum **(0.5 mark)**
- DED-Arc (PAW/GMAW) — uses wire with plasma/arc **(0.5 mark)**
- DED-PWJ (Plasma with Wire Jet) — wire-based hybrid **(0.5 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State any three differences between DMD (Directed Energy Deposition) and Selective Laser Melting (SLM) — a Powder Bed Fusion process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Aspect | DMD | SLM (PBF) |
|--------|-----|-----------|
| Feedstock delivery | Powder injected via nozzle **at point of melting** | Powder pre-laid as a **bed**, then melted |
| Deposition rate | High (0.1–10 kg/h) | Low (0.05–0.2 kg/h) |
| Multi-material capability | Yes (co-injection of 2+ powders) | No (single powder bed) |
| Surface roughness | Higher (Ra 10–25 µm) | Lower (Ra 5–15 µm) |
| Support structure | Usually not required | Required for overhangs |

---

## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 regulations, every Part B question carries **internal choice**. Students must answer **either Question A OR Question B in full**. Each sub-part is worth **7 marks**, with marks distributed across the model answer's logical blocks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of the **Laser-Based Direct Metal Deposition (DED-LB)** process. List the four major process parameters and explain the effect of each on the final part quality.

**(b) [7 marks]** A DMD process uses **laser power P = 1.2 kW, scan speed v = 15 mm/s, hatch spacing h = 0.4 mm, and layer thickness t = 0.3 mm** to deposit Ti-6Al-4V. Calculate the **volumetric energy density** and **linear energy density**. State whether the chosen parameters fall within the **densification window (60–120 J/mm³)**. If not, suggest a corrected parameter (justified by calculation) that lies in the optimal window.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss in detail the **classification, process variables, and metallurgical characteristics** of DMD-built parts. Highlight at least **two applications** in aerospace with engineering justification.

**(b) [7 marks]** With reference to **dilution ratio in DMD cladding**, derive the geometric dilution expression and explain its importance in **corrosion-resistant coatings** deposited on carbon-steel substrates. What dilution range is industrially accepted for hard-facing applications?

---

## ✅ Model Solutions

### **Question A — Part (a) — 7 marks Model Answer**

**Working Principle of DED-LB (Block Diagram – 2 marks):**
1. A high-power fiber laser **(0.5 mark)** is focused through a coaxial nozzle.
2. Metal powder is delivered through **4 powder channels** (converging at ~30° to vertical) carried by argon gas **(0.5 mark)**.
3. Powder + laser converge at the **focal point on the substrate**, creating a melt pool **(0.5 mark)**.
4. As the deposition head (mounted on a 5-axis robot or CNC) traverses, the melt pool solidifies into a track **(0.5 mark)**.

**Four Major Process Parameters (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Parameter | Effect on Quality |
|-----------|-------------------|
| **Laser Power (P)** | Higher P → larger melt pool → wider bead but risk of keyhole porosity |
| **Scan Speed (v)** | Higher v → less heat input → narrower bead, risk of lack of fusion |
| **Powder Feed Rate ($\dot{m}_f$)** | Higher feed → taller/wider bead; very high → unmelted particles |
| **Hatch Spacing (h)** | Larger h → less overlap → rough surface, possible porosity; smaller h → more re-melt → fine grain |

**(Final consolidation – 1 mark):** The four parameters interact via the **volumetric energy density** $E_v = P/(vht)$, and the optimal window must be identified experimentally for each material.

---

### **Question A — Part (b) — 7 marks Model Answer**

**Given:**
- $P = 1200$ W, $v = 15$ mm/s, $h = 0.4$ mm, $t = 0.3$ mm

**Step 1 — Volumetric Energy Density $E_v$ (3 marks):**
$$
E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} = \frac{1200}{15 \times 0.4 \times 0.3} = \frac{1200}{1.8} = 666.67 \text{ J/mm³}
$$

**Step 2 — Linear Energy Density $E$ (2 marks):**
$$
E = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{1200}{15} = 80 \text{ J/mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to Densification Window (1 mark):**
- $E_v = 666.67$ J/mm³ ≫ 120 J/mm³
- **Far above the optimal window** → Expect **keyhole porosity, vaporization, and surface balling**.

**Step 4 — Corrected Parameter (1 mark):**
Choose $v_{new}$ such that $E_v = 100$ J/mm³ (mid-window):
$$
v_{new} = \frac{P}{E_v^{opt} \cdot h \cdot t} = \frac{1200}{100 \times 0.4 \times 0.3} = \frac{1200}{12} = 100 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**Final Answer:** Recommended scan speed is **v = 100 mm/s**, which brings $E_v$ to 100 J/mm³ — within the 60–120 J/mm³ window for Ti-6Al-4V.

---

### **Question B — Part (a) — 7 marks Model Answer**

**Classification of DMD (2 marks):** As per ASTM F42 / ISO 52900, DMD belongs to the **DED (Directed Energy Deposition)** category. It is sub-classified into **DED-LB**, **DED-EB**, **DED-Arc (PAW & GMAW)**, and **DED-PWJ**.

**Process Variables (2 marks):**
- Energy: $P$, beam spot diameter $d$
- Motion: $v$, $h$, $t$
- Mass: $\dot{m}_f$, $\eta$
- Environment: $\text{O}_2$ content, substrate temperature

**Metallurgical Characteristics (2 marks):**
- Rapid solidification → **fine dendritic microstructure**
- Strong **metallurgical bonding** with substrate (no adhesive interface)
- Possible **residual stress and distortion** due to thermal cycling
- **Anisotropic mechanical properties** (worse in Z-direction)

**Aerospace Applications (1 mark):**
- **Turbine blade repair** (Inconel 718 blade tip restoration) — saves > $100k per blade.
- **Structural bracket deposition** in Ti-6Al-4V (e.g., Airbus A350 pylon bracket) — 30% weight reduction over machined.

---

### **Question B — Part (b) — 7 marks Model Answer**

**Geometric Dilution Derivation (3 marks):**
For a single clad track on a substrate, the total cross-section of the melted zone is $A_t = A_b + A_d$ where $A_b$ = substrate-melted area, $A_d$ = deposit area above substrate. By definition:
$$
D = \frac{A_b}{A_t} = \frac{A_b}{A_b + A_d}
$$

**Importance in Corrosion-Resistant Coatings (2 marks):**
- A corrosion-resistant coating (e.g., **Stellite 6 on mild steel**) must retain its **alloy chemistry** in the deposit region.
- High $D$ causes **Fe from the substrate to dilute** the deposit, breaking the protective chemistry and reducing corrosion resistance.

**Industrially Accepted Range (2 marks):**
- For **hard-facing and wear-resistant coatings**: $D \approx 0.05$ to $0.15$ (5–15%).
- For **build-up / restoration**: $D \approx 0.30$ to $0.50$ (higher dilution acceptable).
- For **joining / brazing-type applications**: $D > 0.5$.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 3 Reasons Students Lose Marks in DMD Questions (Module 4):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Linear vs Volumetric Energy Density** — Most students write $E = P/v$ and use it for **process map** questions. The **volumetric** form $E_v = P/(vht)$ is the one that governs **densification**. **(Loss: 2–3 marks per question)**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the Catchment Efficiency $\eta$** in mass balance derivations. In KTU derivations, $\eta < 1$ and must be included, otherwise the formula is marked "incomplete". **(Loss: 1–2 marks)**
> 
> 3. **Not stating the Densification Window numerical range** (60–120 J/mm³ for Ti-6Al-4V) when commenting on whether a process is optimal. The examiner expects a **numerical justification**, not just a qualitative "good/bad" answer. **(Loss: 2 marks)**
> 
> 4. **Drawing the block diagram without labeling the inert gas / shield gas** — The argon shielding is a **mandatory element** for full marks. **(Loss: 1 mark)**

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Direct Metal Deposition (DMD)**

- [x] DMD is a **DED** process, **NOT** a powder bed process.
- [x] ASTM classification: **DED-LB, DED-EB, DED-Arc, DED-PWJ**.
- [x] Feedstock: **powder** (laser/plasma) or **wire** (arc/EB).
- [x] Shield gas: **Argon** (mandatory) — prevents oxidation.
- [x] Four critical parameters: **P, v, h, t**.
- [x] **Volumetric energy density** $E_v = P / (v h t)$ — controls densification.
- [x] **Densification window for Ti-6Al-4V**: **60–120 J/mm³**.
- [x] **Dilution $D = A_b / (A_b + A_d)$** — critical for cladding quality.
- [x] **Catchment efficiency $\eta$** — typical **50–80%** in laser DMD.
- [x] **Deposition rate**: 0.1–1 kg/h (laser), 5–15 kg/h (wire-arc).
- [x] **Layer thickness**: 0.2–2.0 mm (much larger than PBF).
- [x] **Multi-material capability** via **co-injection** — enables **FGM**.
- [x] **Closed-loop control** uses **pyrometer** + **CMOS camera** for in-process monitoring.
- [x] Aerospace: **Inconel 718, Ti-6Al-4V**; Oil & gas: **Stellite claddings**; Biomedical: **Ti implants**.
- [x] Common defects: **lack of fusion** (low $E_v$), **keyhole porosity** (high $E_v$), **unmelted particles** (excess $\dot{m}_f$).
- [x] **WAAM** (Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing) is a **DED-Arc** variant — large-scale, low-cost.
- [x] Compared to **SLM (PBF)**: DMD has **higher deposition rate** but **lower resolution**.
- [x] The **build envelope** of DMD is **larger** than PBF (multi-axis robots can reach 1 m³).
- [x] **Process maps** for DMD plot $E_v$ vs porosity, density, and dilution.
- [x] **Hybrid additive-subtractive** systems combine DMD with **in-situ milling** for net-shape parts.

> **🎯 Exam Tip:** When asked *"explain DMD"*, always include **(1)** ASTM classification, **(2)** block diagram with all subsystems labeled, **(3)** the **$E_v$ formula** and densification window, and **(4)** at least **one aerospace application**. This structure consistently scores **7/7** in KTU board valuations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Capabilities and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process Capabilities and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Capability** in Additive Manufacturing (AM) refers to the **quantifiable range of operational, geometric, material, and economic outcomes** that a specific AM process can reliably achieve, characterized by parameters such as dimensional accuracy, surface roughness, build volume, supported materials, feature resolution, mechanical properties of the printed part, production speed, and post-processing requirements.

According to the **ASTM F2792 – Standard Terminology for Additive Manufacturing Technologies**, process capability encompasses the entire envelope of functional performance that distinguishes one AM process category from another. The seven standardized AM process families each exhibit a unique *capability fingerprint*:

| **S. No.** | **Process Family** | **Typical Tolerance** | **Minimum Layer Thickness** | **Surface Roughness (Ra)** |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Vat Photopolymerization (SLA, DLP) | $\pm 0.05$ mm | $25\ \mu m$ | $0.4 - 3.2\ \mu m$ |
| 2 | Material Jetting (PolyJet, MJP) | $\pm 0.02$ mm | $15\ \mu m$ | $0.6 - 2.0\ \mu m$ |
| 3 | Binder Jetting | $\pm 0.1$ mm | $80\ \mu m$ | $3.2 - 12.5\ \mu m$ |
| 4 | Material Extrusion (FDM, FFF) | $\pm 0.2$ mm | $100\ \mu m$ | $5 - 25\ \mu m$ |
| 5 | Powder Bed Fusion (SLS, SLM, EBM) | $\pm 0.1$ mm | $20\ \mu m$ | $4 - 20\ \mu m$ |
| 6 | Directed Energy Deposition (DED, LMD) | $\pm 0.5$ mm | $250\ \mu m$ | $10 - 50\ \mu m$ |
| 7 | Sheet Lamination (LOM, UAM) | $\pm 0.3$ mm | $100\ \mu m$ | $8 - 30\ \mu m$ |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Master Chef" Kitchen

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine an advanced restaurant kitchen that has **seven specialized chefs**, each equipped with a completely different set of tools and ingredients.
>
> - Chef 1 (SLA) uses **UV light + liquid resin** to sculpt incredibly smooth, jewelry-like desserts with fine detail.
> - Chef 2 (FDM) uses a **hot glue gun + plastic filament** to quickly assemble a sturdy cake but with rough frosting.
> - Chef 3 (SLM) uses a **high-power laser + metal powder** to forge titanium aircraft brackets layer-by-layer.
> - Chef 4 (DED) uses a **blowtorch + metal wire** to repair a massive ship propeller in place.
>
> When a customer (engineer) orders a specific dish (functional part), the head chef must select the right specialist based on **what material is needed, how accurate the geometry must be, how large the part is, and how quickly it must be ready**. This selection logic is precisely what *Process Capability Mapping* allows AM engineers to perform.

## 1.3 Key Process Capability Metrics (Industry Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **five primary capability metrics** are used universally to benchmark any AM process:

1. **Dimensional Accuracy (Tolerance):** Deviation between the nominal CAD dimension and the actual printed dimension, typically expressed in $\pm$ mm or $\pm$ µm.
2. **Surface Roughness (Ra):** Arithmetic average of surface profile deviations, measured in µm. Governed primarily by **layer thickness**, **scan strategy**, and **particle size**.
3. **Build Volume (Envelope):** Maximum printable envelope $(X \times Y \times Z)$ in mm³. Ranges from $50 \times 50 \times 50$ mm (desktop SLA) to $800 \times 400 \times 500$ mm (industrial SLM) and beyond $\Phi 1000 \times 3000$ mm for **WAAM/DED**.
4. **Material Throughput / Deposition Rate:** Mass or volume of material consolidated per unit time, measured in **cm³/h** or **g/min**.
5. **Feature Resolution (Minimum Wall Thickness):** Smallest producible wall, hole, or overhang, typically in the range $0.1$ mm (PolyJet) to $2.0$ mm (WAAM).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Capability Triangle — Process, Material, Part

The capability of an AM process is governed by a **three-way interaction** between the *process physics*, *material properties*, and *desired part characteristics*. Engineers evaluate this triangle to determine feasibility before committing to production.

### 2.1.1 Process Physics Pillar
- **Energy Source:** Laser (SLA, SLM), Electron Beam (EBM), Extrusion Heater (FDM), UV Lamp (DLP), Print Head (PolyJet), Binder Droplet (BJ), Plasma/Wire Arc (WAAM).
- **Energy Density** $E$ (J/mm³) determines melting/vaporization/sintering behavior.
- **Scan Strategy:** Raster, contour, fractal, or island — affects residual stress and surface finish.

### 2.1.2 Material Pillar
- **Form:** Powder, wire, filament, liquid resin, sheet.
- **State transitions:** Solid → liquid (fusion), liquid → solid (curing), powder → solid (sintering/melting).
- **Multi-material capability:** Only **Material Jetting (PolyJet)** and **Sheet Lamination** natively support multi-material builds.

### 2.1.3 Part Characteristics Pillar
- Geometry complexity (undercuts, internal channels, lattice structures).
- Mechanical loading (tension, fatigue, creep, thermal).
- Functional integration (e.g., conformal cooling channels in injection mold inserts).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **frequently tested** in KTU ESE and Module Internal evaluations.

| **#** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Symbols & Units** | **Applicable Process** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Volumetric Energy Density | $E = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | $P$ = Laser Power (W), $v$ = Scan Speed (mm/s), $h$ = Hatch Spacing (mm), $t$ = Layer Thickness (mm) | SLM, DED, EBM |
| 2 | Number of Layers | $N = \dfrac{H_{part}}{t_{layer}}$ | $H_{part}$ = Part Height (mm), $t_{layer}$ = Layer Thickness (mm) | All layered AM |
| 3 | Approx. Surface Roughness | $R_a \approx k \cdot t_{layer}$ | $k$ = Process Coefficient (FDM: $k \approx 12$, SLM: $k \approx 8$, SLA: $k \approx 1.5$) | All layered AM |
| 4 | Build Time | $T_{build} = T_{setup} + N \cdot t_{layer\_process} + T_{post}$ | All times in seconds/minutes | All AM |
| 5 | Total Scan Time per Layer | $t_{scan} = \dfrac{A_{layer}}{v_{scan} \cdot h_{spot}}$ | $A_{layer}$ = Cross-section area (mm²) | SLM, DED |
| 6 | Mass of Material Consumed | $m = \rho \cdot V_{solid}$ | $\rho$ = Material density (g/cm³), $V_{solid}$ = Solid volume (cm³) | All AM |
| 7 | Cost Per Part | $C_{part} = \dfrac{C_{machine} \cdot T_{build}}{N_{parts}} + C_{material} + C_{post}$ | $C_{machine}$ = Machine hourly rate ($/hr) | All AM |
| 8 | Aspect Ratio Limit | $AR_{max} = \dfrac{H_{feature}}{W_{feature}}$ | Process-dependent (FDM $\approx 20$, SLM $\approx 100$) | All AM |
| 9 | Overhang Angle Limit | $\theta_{max}$ | FDM: $45°$, SLA: $30°$, SLM: $45°$ | All AM |
| 10 | Material Utilization Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{final\,part}}{m_{material\,input}}$ | Dimensionless ratio (FDM $\approx 90\%$, SLM $\approx 30\%$) | All AM |

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Aerospace Weight Reduction:** Capability to produce **lattice-truss internal structures** reduces weight by $40 - 70\%$ without compromising strength.
- **Medical Customization:** Patient-specific implants from CT/MRI data achieve anatomical conformity impossible with subtractive methods.
- **Spare Parts on Demand (SPOD):** Capability to print legacy components eliminates the need for warehousing — revolutionary for **defense logistics** and **naval fleet maintenance**.
- **Tooling with Conformal Cooling:** AM-produced injection mold inserts with **3D internal cooling channels** reduce cycle time by $30 - 60\%$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The capability envelope is **process-bound, not machine-bound**. Even within the same family (e.g., SLM), build volume can vary from $100 \times 100 \times 100$ mm (desktop) to $800 \times 400 \times 500$ mm (industrial EOS M 400-4).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Build Time Estimation for SLM Process

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
A cylindrical Ti-6Al-4V part is to be manufactured via Selective Laser Melting (SLM). Given:

- **Part Diameter:** $D = 50$ mm
- **Part Height:** $H_{part} = 100$ mm
- **Layer Thickness:** $t_{layer} = 30\ \mu m = 0.030$ mm
- **Hatch Spacing:** $h_{spot} = 0.10$ mm
- **Laser Scan Speed:** $v_{scan} = 800$ mm/s
- **Laser Power:** $P = 200$ W
- **Setup Time:** $T_{setup} = 25$ min
- **Post-Processing Time:** $T_{post} = 90$ min
- **Oversize Factor** (substrate + supports): $1.15$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1 — Calculate the number of layers:**

$$N = \dfrac{H_{part}}{t_{layer}}$$

$$N = \dfrac{100\ \text{mm}}{0.030\ \text{mm}} = 3333.33 \approx 3334\ \text{layers}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate cross-sectional area of each layer (with oversize factor):**

$$A_{layer} = 1.15 \times \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^{2}}{4}$$

$$A_{layer} = 1.15 \times \dfrac{\pi \times 50^{2}}{4} = 1.15 \times 1963.5 = 2258.0\ \text{mm}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate scan time per layer:**

$$t_{scan} = \dfrac{A_{layer}}{v_{scan} \cdot h_{spot}}$$

$$t_{scan} = \dfrac{2258.0\ \text{mm}^{2}}{800\ \text{mm/s} \times 0.10\ \text{mm}} = \dfrac{2258.0}{80} = 28.225\ \text{s/layer}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate total scan time (excluding recoating time):**

$$T_{scan,total} = N \times t_{scan}$$

$$T_{scan,total} = 3334 \times 28.225 = 94{,}102.15\ \text{s}$$

$$T_{scan,total} = \dfrac{94{,}102.15}{3600} = 26.14\ \text{hours}$$

**Step 5 — Add recoating time (assume 12 s per layer for powder spreading):**

$$T_{recoat} = N \times t_{recoat} = 3334 \times 12 = 40{,}008\ \text{s} = 11.11\ \text{hours}$$

**Step 6 — Total build time:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{build} &= T_{setup} + T_{scan,total} + T_{recoat} + T_{post} \\
&= \dfrac{25}{60} + 26.14 + 11.11 + \dfrac{90}{60} \\
&= 0.417 + 26.14 + 11.11 + 1.50 \\
&= 39.17\ \text{hours}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 50 mm × 100 mm cylindrical Ti-6Al-4V part requires approximately **39.17 hours of total processing time** — illustrating why AM is best suited for **high-value, low-volume** applications rather than mass production.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Volumetric Energy Density (VED) Check

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \\
&= \dfrac{200\ \text{W}}{800\ \text{mm/s} \times 0.10\ \text{mm} \times 0.030\ \text{mm}} \\
&= \dfrac{200}{2.40} \\
&= 83.33\ \text{J/mm}^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimal VED range for Ti-6Al-4V SLM:** $60 - 120$ J/mm³. Our calculated value of $83.33$ J/mm³ lies safely within the process window — no lack-of-fusion or keyhole porosity expected.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cost Per Part Calculation

### 3.3.1 Given Data
- Machine hourly rate (amortized): $C_{machine} = \$80/\text{hr}$
- Ti-6Al-4V powder cost: $\$250/\text{kg}$
- Powder density: $\rho = 4.43\ \text{g/cm}^{3}$
- Packing density of powder bed: $\phi = 0.60$
- Post-processing labor cost: $C_{post} = \$45$
- Number of parts per build: $N_{parts} = 8$

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate solid part volume:**

$$V_{solid} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^{2}}{4} \times H_{part} = \dfrac{\pi \times 50^{2}}{4} \times 100 = 196{,}350\ \text{mm}^{3} = 196.35\ \text{cm}^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate mass of final part:**

$$m_{part} = \rho \cdot V_{solid} = 4.43 \times 196.35 = 869.83\ \text{g} = 0.870\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate mass of powder consumed (including waste in oversize factor and unfused powder):**

$$m_{powder} = 1.15 \times \dfrac{m_{part}}{\phi} = 1.15 \times \dfrac{0.870}{0.60} = 1.668\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate machine cost per part:**

$$C_{machine,part} = \dfrac{C_{machine} \cdot T_{build}}{N_{parts}} = \dfrac{80 \times 39.17}{8} = \$391.70$$

**Step 5 — Calculate material cost per part (assuming unused powder is recycled at 80% recovery):**

$$C_{material,part} = m_{powder} \times 250 \times (1 - 0.80) = 1.668 \times 250 \times 0.20 = \$83.40$$

**Step 6 — Total cost per part:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{part} &= C_{machine,part} + C_{material,part} + C_{post} \\
&= 391.70 + 83.40 + 45.00 \\
&= \$520.10
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** Machine cost dominates ($75.3\%$ of total). Reducing $T_{build}$ via **multi-laser systems** (e.g., 4-laser SLM) is the most effective cost-reduction strategy.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Application-to-Process Mapping

| **Industry Sector** | **Typical Part** | **Critical Capability Required** | **Most Suitable AM Process** | **Reason** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Aerospace | Fuel nozzle (GE LEAP) | Internal lattice, high-temp alloy | SLM (Direct Metal Laser Melting) | Complex internal geometry, Inconel 718 capability |
| Medical | Titanium acetabular cup | Patient-specific geometry, biocompatibility | EBM (Electron Beam Melting) | Ti-6Al-4V with high build rate, lower residual stress |
| Automotive | Conformal cooling mold insert | Internal cooling channels, H13 tool steel | SLM | Tool steel compatibility, complex internal flow |
| Dental | Crown & bridge | High accuracy, biocompatible Co-Cr | DLP (Digital Light Processing) | $\pm 50\ \mu m$ accuracy, fine detail |
| Consumer Goods | Customized eyewear frames | Multi-color, smooth surface, fast | Material Jetting (PolyJet) | Multi-material + $\pm 20\ \mu m$ accuracy |
| Architecture | Scale model with terrain | Full color, large volume | Binder Jetting (full-color gypsum) | Color printing, large envelope |
| Defense | Spare bracket on naval ship | Field deployment, multi-material | WAAM / DED | Large-scale wire-arc, repair capability |
| Foundry | Sand cores & molds | Large size, complex parting | Binder Jetting (silica sand) | Fast sand-core production, no tooling |
| Education/Prototype | Concept model | Low cost, fast iteration | FDM (FFF) | Affordable, wide material range |

## 3.5 Derivations Summary Table

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Equation** | **Physical Meaning** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Build Rate (volumetric) | $\dot{V}$ | $\dot{V} = v \cdot h \cdot t$ | mm³/s of material fused |
| Energy Density | $E$ | $E = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | J/mm³ delivered to powder bed |
| Surface Roughness Estimate | $R_a$ | $R_a \approx k \cdot t_{layer}$ | Layer-stepped staircase effect |
| Solid Loading Fraction | $\phi$ | $\phi = \dfrac{V_{solid}}{V_{envelope}}$ | Volume efficiency of build |
| Effective Production Rate | $\dot{P}$ | $\dot{P} = \dfrac{N_{parts}}{T_{build}}$ | Parts per hour of total cycle |
