# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define process capability in additive manufacturing. List any four key metrics used to characterize the capability of an AM process.**

**Model Answer:**
Process capability in additive manufacturing refers to the quantifiable envelope of operational performance and output characteristics that a specific AM process can achieve, including dimensional accuracy, surface finish, supported materials, build volume, mechanical properties of the printed part, and production rate.

The four key metrics are:
1. **Dimensional Accuracy (Tolerance)** — typically $\pm 0.02$ mm (PolyJet) to $\pm 0.5$ mm (DED).
2. **Surface Roughness (Ra)** — staircase-effect driven, ranging from $0.4\ \mu m$ (SLA) to $50\ \mu m$ (WAAM).
3. **Build Volume** — printable envelope $(X \times Y \times Z)$ ranging from desktop ($50 \times 50 \times 50$ mm) to industrial WAAM ($\Phi 3000 \times 1000$ mm).
4. **Material Throughput / Deposition Rate** — measured in cm³/h or g/min.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three major industrial applications of additive manufacturing in the medical field, with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Patient-Specific Orthopedic Implants:** Custom titanium acetabular cups and cranial plates manufactured using EBM (Electron Beam Melting) from CT-scan data — *example: Conformis knee implants*.
2. **Dental Prosthetics:** Co-Cr crowns and bridges produced via DLP or SLM with $\pm 50\ \mu m$ accuracy.
3. **Surgical Guides and Anatomical Models:** Patient-specific drill guides and 3D-printed organ models for pre-surgical planning, typically manufactured using SLA or Material Jetting.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare the process capabilities of **Powder Bed Fusion (SLM)** and **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** in terms of dimensional accuracy, material range, build volume, surface finish, and feature resolution. Mention one representative industrial application of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| **Capability Parameter** | **SLM (Powder Bed Fusion)** | **DED (Directed Energy Deposition)** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Dimensional Accuracy | $\pm 0.05$ to $\pm 0.1$ mm (high) | $\pm 0.3$ to $\pm 0.5$ mm (moderate) |
| Material Range | Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel 718, AlSi10Mg, SS 316L, maraging steel | Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel, SS, tool steels, gradient materials |
| Build Volume | $100 \times 100 \times 100$ mm (desktop) to $800 \times 400 \times 500$ mm (industrial) | No fixed envelope — multi-axis robotic deposition; parts >1 m possible |
| Surface Finish | Ra $4 - 20\ \mu m$ (better, enclosed powder bed) | Ra $10 - 50\ \mu m$ (rougher, larger melt pool) |
| Feature Resolution | $0.1 - 0.2$ mm thin walls | $1.0 - 2.0$ mm thin walls |
| Industrial Application | GE LEAP fuel nozzle (Inconel 718) | Large titanium structural aerospace parts (Boeing 787, Lockheed Martin) |

**Key Engineering Insight:** SLM delivers **superior accuracy and surface finish** for complex fine-feature parts; DED excels in **large structural components and repair applications** due to its multi-axis deposition flexibility.

---

**(b)** Explain in detail the applications of **additive manufacturing in the aerospace industry** with suitable examples. How does lattice topology optimization enhance aerospace part performance? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Major Aerospace Applications:**

1. **Fuel Injectors and Nozzles:** GE Aviation's LEAP engine fuel nozzle, traditionally assembled from **18 parts**, is now printed as **a single Inconel 718 component via SLM**, reducing weight by $25\%$ and improving fuel efficiency by $15\%$.

2. **Turbine Blade Repair:** DED is used to **rejuvenate worn high-pressure turbine blades** by depositing fresh superalloy material onto damaged tip regions, recovering the blade profile without scrapping the disk.

3. **Lightweight Structural Brackets:** Airbus A350 XWB uses **3D-printed titanium brackets** produced via SLM, achieving $30 - 40\%$ weight reduction over machined equivalents.

4. **Bionic Lattice Structures:** Internal honeycomb or octet-truss lattices replace solid infill, providing **high stiffness-to-mass ratios** ideal for satellite payload mounts.

5. **Engine Combustion Chambers:** SpaceX SuperDraco engine thrust chambers are 3D-printed in **Inconel via DED**, achieving rapid iteration cycles.

**Lattice Topology Optimization — Engineering Principle:**

Lattice structures consist of **repeating unit cells** (octet truss, Kelvin cell, gyroid) arranged to follow stress trajectories identified by **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)**. In regions experiencing high stress, denser lattices are placed; in low-stress zones, sparse lattices are used. The result is:

- **Weight Reduction:** $40 - 70\%$ versus solid parts.
- **Improved Specific Stiffness:** Mass-normalized stiffness exceeds $5\times$ compared to foam cores.
- **Multi-functional Integration:** Lattices provide passive thermal management (heat dissipation channels) and vibration damping.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (3 Marks Common Loss):** Students often list applications **without quoting specific numerical improvements** (e.g., "$25\%$ weight reduction", "$15\%$ fuel efficiency"). Always include **quantified data** wherever possible — examiners reward it heavily.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a **process selection methodology**, explain step-by-step how an engineer would select an appropriate AM process for manufacturing a **customized titanium hip implant** for a specific patient. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Define Part Requirements:**
- **Material:** Ti-6Al-4V (biocompatible, osseointegrative).
- **Geometry:** Patient-specific from CT-scan data; complex internal lattice for bone in-growth.
- **Mechanical Property:** Fatigue strength $> 500$ MPa (ISO 5832-3).
- **Surface Finish:** Ra $< 10\ \mu m$ on articulating surfaces; porous surface ($R_a \approx 50\ \mu m$) on bone-contact surfaces for in-growth.
- **Quantity:** Single unit per patient.

**Step 2 — Material Compatibility Filter:**
Eliminates FDM, SLA, SLS, DLP, Material Jetting (all polymer-based). Remaining options: **SLM, EBM, DED**.

**Step 3 — Accuracy & Surface Filter:**
- DED: $\pm 0.5$ mm tolerance, Ra $10 - 50\ \mu m$ — **borderline acceptable** but insufficient for articulating surface.
- SLM: $\pm 0.1$ mm, Ra $4 - 20\ \mu m$ — **acceptable** for articulating surface; requires post-machining/polishing for ultra-smooth bearing.
- EBM: $\pm 0.15$ mm, Ra $5 - 25\ \mu m$ — **acceptable**; faster build rate, lower residual stress.

**Step 4 — Feature Resolution & Lattice Capability:**
Both SLM and EBM can produce $0.3 - 0.5$ mm lattice struts required for bone in-growth. **SLM preferred** for finer lattice control.

**Step 5 — Cost & Lead Time:**
- EBM faster build time (1.2–1.5× speed of SLM), slightly lower cost.
- SLM provides higher accuracy → less post-machining.

**Step 6 — Final Selection: SLM (with EBM as alternative for faster turnaround).**

---

**(b)** Discuss the applications of **additive manufacturing in the automotive industry** and explain the concept of **Spare Parts on Demand (SPOD)** with a real-world example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Automotive Applications:**

1. **Rapid Prototyping:** FDM/SLA used in design studios of BMW, Tesla, and Tata Motors for **concept model iteration**, reducing concept-to-prototype cycle from weeks to days.
2. **Custom Tooling with Conformal Cooling:** SLM-printed H13 injection mold inserts with **3D internal cooling channels** reduce cycle time by $30 - 60\%$ and eliminate warpage in plastic parts.
3. **End-Use Lightweight Components:** Bugatti Chiron's **3D-printed titanium brake caliper** weighs only $2.9$ kg versus $4.9$ kg conventionally — world's largest functional titanium AM part.
4. **Customized Interior Trim:** Local Motors (Olli shuttle) and BMW MINI use **large-format AM** for bespoke dashboard and console trims.
5. **Functional Jigs and Fixtures:** Automotive OEMs use FDM-printed ergonomic assembly jigs, reducing ergonomic injuries on the shop floor.

**Spare Parts on Demand (SPOD) — Concept and Example:**

SPOD is the **on-demand digital manufacturing** of legacy or low-volume replacement parts directly from CAD files, **eliminating warehousing and minimum-order-quantities (MOQ)**. The supply chain shifts from **"make-store-ship"** to **"make-on-demand-ship-direct"**.

**Real-World Example:** The **U.S. Navy's USS Harry S. Truman** aircraft carrier deployed 3D printers to manufacture a replacement **linkage fitting** for the landing-gear system of an F/A-18 aircraft. The part had a 4-week lead time via conventional procurement; the on-board AM system produced the certified part in **48 hours**, saving critical flight-line downtime.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently **list applications generically** without referencing **specific companies or quantified benefits**. Always cite at least **one named OEM and one quantitative benefit** per answer to score full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Process Capability** is process-bound, not machine-bound — never confuse a specific machine's envelope with the entire process family capability.
- [x] **SLM** = best accuracy + fine features; **DED/WAAM** = best for large parts + repair; **SLA/DLP** = best surface finish; **FDM** = lowest cost; **Material Jetting** = only true multi-material AM.
- [x] **Volumetric Energy Density** $E = P/(v \cdot h \cdot t)$ is the single most important process parameter for PBF/DED; optimal Ti-6Al-4V window is $60 - 120$ J/mm³.
- [x] **Build time** is dominated by **scan time + recoating time**; setup and post-processing are secondary contributors.
- [x] **Surface roughness** scales linearly with layer thickness: $R_a \approx k \cdot t_{layer}$.
- [x] **Material utilization** is $90\%$ for FDM but only $30\%$ for SLM (excess powder is recovered but contaminated).
- [x] **Aerospace GE LEAP nozzle** = 18 parts consolidated to 1 (Inconel 718 SLM).
- [x] **Medical Ti-6Al-4V implants** = EBM or SLM, with lattice for bone in-growth.
- [x] **SPOD** = digital warehousing; revolutionizes naval/defense logistics.
- [x] **Conformal cooling** = 30-60% cycle-time reduction in injection molding.
- [x] **Cost per part** is dominated by **machine time ($75\%$ typical)**, not material.
- [x] **Multi-laser SLM** (4-laser systems) reduces build time by up to $4\times$, drastically reducing per-part cost.
- [x] Always cite **quantified data** in KTU answers — examiners reward numerics.
- [x] **ASTM F2792** is the standard reference for AM process classification — memorize the seven families.
- [x] Lattice topology optimization provides **40-70% weight reduction** for aerospace structures.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AM Defects: Prediction and Mitigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** AM Defects: Prediction and Mitigation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Additive Manufacturing (AM) Defects** are defined as any deviation from the intended geometry, microstructure, mechanical property, or surface quality of a part produced through a layer-by-layer additive process, arising from the complex interaction of process parameters, material properties, energy source characteristics, and environmental conditions. **Prediction** refers to the proactive identification of defect formation propensity *before or during* the build, using simulation, process monitoring, and data-driven analytics. **Mitigation** encompasses the engineering interventions—both *in-process* (parameter control) and *post-process* (heat treatment, surface finishing)—designed to eliminate or reduce defect severity below acceptable tolerance limits. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine baking a multi-layered cake (AM is essentially "baking metal" layer by layer): - **Porosity** ≈ Air bubbles trapped inside the cake because the batter was poured too quickly or the oven temperature was wrong. - **Warping/Residual Stress** ≈ The cake warps as it cools because one layer solidified faster than the layer beneath it. - **Lack of Fusion** ≈ Two cake layers didn't bond properly because the new batter was too cold when poured. - **Balling** ≈ The molten metal "balls up" instead of spreading smoothly—like water droplets on a greasy pan. Just as a baker controls oven temperature, pour rate, and cooling to get a perfect cake, an AM engineer must control **laser power, scan speed, layer thickness, hatch spacing, and cooling rate** to obtain a defect-free part. ### 1.3 Classification of AM Defects — High-Yield Overview > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Focus:** Questions on defect classification carry **2–3 marks** in Part A. Memorize the six broad categories. | # | Defect Category | Brief Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Geometric Defects** | Staircase effect, dimensional inaccuracy | | 2 | **Volumetric Defects** | Porosity (gas + lack of fusion), inclusions, voids | | 3 | **Surface Defects** | Balling, spatter, dross, high roughness | | 4 | **Microstructural Defects** | Cracks (hot/cold), anisotropy, undesired phases | | 5 | **Residual Stress Defects** | Distortion, warpage, delamination | | 6 | **Process-Induced Defects** | Keyholing, lack of penetration, over-melting | ### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Constants) - **Relative Density Threshold:** $\geq 99.5\%$ for aerospace-grade AM parts. - **Acceptable Surface Roughness ($R_a$):** $3.2$–$12.5\ \mu m$ (as-built), $\leq 0.8\ \mu m$ (post-machined). - **Volumetric Energy Density (VED) Sweet Spot:** typically $50$–$100\ \text{J/mm}^3$ for Ti-6Al-4V in LPBF. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Six Pillars of AM Defect Formation > [!NOTE] > Each defect has a *root cause fingerprint*—understanding the mechanism is the only way to predict and mitigate it. **Pillar 1 — Energy Density Mismatch** - Too little energy → lack of fusion porosity. - Too much energy → keyholing porosity, evaporation. - Governed by **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)**: $$ E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} $$ where $P$ = laser power (W), $v$ = scan speed (mm/s), $h$ = hatch spacing (mm), $t$ = layer thickness (mm). **Pillar 2 — Thermal Gradient & Residual Stress** - Steep thermal gradients → high residual stress → cracks and distortion. - Mitigation via preheating, scan strategy rotation (e.g., 67° rotation per layer), and island/hilbert scanning. **Pillar 3 — Powder Bed Quality** - Moisture, oxidation, irregular morphology → porosity and inclusions. - Moisture introduces hydrogen → porosity in Ti alloys. **Pillar 4 — Solidification Phenomena** - Columnar grain growth in directional cooling → mechanical anisotropy. - Hot tearing (liquation cracking) in alloys with wide freezing ranges. **Pillar 5 — Process Atmosphere** - Oxygen content $> 100\ \text{ppm}$ → oxidation, porosity in reactive metals (Ti, Al). - Inert gas (Ar, N₂) flow dynamics affect spatter redeposition. **Pillar 6 — Build Geometry** - Overhangs ($> 45°$) → sagging without supports. - Thin walls ($< 0.3\ \text{mm}$) → buckling and incomplete fusion. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **exam-critical**. Memorize the symbols, units, and physical meaning. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | Volumetric Energy Density | $\text{J/mm}^3$ | | 2 | $E_l = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot t}$ | Linear Energy Density | $\text{J/mm}^2$ | | 3 | $E_s = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}$ | Areal Energy Density | $\text{J/mm}^2$ | | 4 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{q \cdot t}{k \cdot \rho \cdot c_p}$ | Temperature rise (1-D heat flow) | K | | 5 | $R_a \approx h_t \cdot \tan\theta$ | Staircase roughness (slope angle $\theta$) | $\mu m$ | | 6 | $\sigma_r = \alpha \cdot E \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal residual stress | MPa | | 7 | $\text{Rel. Density} = 1 - \dfrac{V_{voids}}{V_{total}}$ | Porosity metric | dimensionless | | 8 | $\text{Porosity (\%)} = \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_{built}}{\rho_{solid}}\right) \times 100$ | Density-based porosity | \% | | 9 | $d_{melt} = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{2 P}{\pi \rho c_p v \Delta T_m \sqrt{\pi \kappa v}}}$ | Melt pool width (approx.) | mm | | 10 | $v_{crit} = \dfrac{P}{\pi \rho c_p d_m \Delta T_m}$ | Critical scan speed for keyholing | mm/s | **Note:** In the table above, $\alpha$ = CTE, $E$ = Young's modulus, $\Delta T$ = temperature differential, $k$ = thermal conductivity, $\rho$ = density, $c_p$ = specific heat, $\kappa$ = thermal diffusivity, $\theta$ = part slope angle from vertical, $h_t$ = layer thickness. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace (Ti-6Al-4V LPBF):** VED tuning to achieve $\geq 99.7\%$ density—required for fatigue-critical turbine brackets. - **Medical (Co-Cr-Mo hip implants):** Hot isostatic pressing (HIP) to close residual porosity before implantation. - **Automotive (AlSi10Mg):** In-situ melt pool monitoring for real-time defect detection in serial production. - **Space (Inconel 718 rocket injectors):** HIP + heat treatment cycle to eliminate cracks and achieve AMS 5662 specifications. ### 2.4 The Defect-Process-Property Linkage Every defect is the *output* of an upstream cause; the **Ishikawa (fishbone) logic** in AM follows: $$ \text{Defect} = f(\text{Process Params},\ \text{Material},\ \text{Machine},\ \text{Environment},\ \text{Design}) $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** When asked to "explain causes of X defect," always structure your answer along these five M's. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Energy Density and Its Role in Porosity **Step 1: Definition of VED** $$ E_v = \frac{E_{total}}{V_{melted}} $$ **Step 2: Energy delivered per unit time** is laser power $P$ (W = J/s). Energy delivered per unit scan length is $P/v$ (J/mm). Dividing by hatch spacing $h$ gives energy per unit area scanned (J/mm²): $$ E_{area} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h} $$ **Step 3:** The depth of melting is approximated by the layer thickness $t$ for single-layer fusion. The volume melted per unit length is $h \cdot t$. Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} E_v &= \frac{P/v}{h \cdot t} \\ &= \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Critical VED lower bound.** Below a critical $E_{v,crit}$, lack of fusion occurs because insufficient energy melts both the powder layer and a portion of the substrate: $$ E_{v,crit} \approx \frac{\rho \cdot (c_p \Delta T_m + L_f)}{1 - \eta_{abs}} $$ where $\eta_{abs}$ = absorptivity, $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion. **Step 5: Critical VED upper bound.** Above $E_{v,max}$, keyholing forms due to vaporization recoil pressure exceeding surface tension: $$ P_{recoil} \approx \dfrac{0.54 P_0}{\sqrt{T_s / T_v}} $$ **Step 6: Optimal VED window.** $$ E_{v,crit} \leq E_v \leq E_{v,max} $$ For Ti-6Al-4V: typically $E_v \approx 60$–$80\ \text{J/mm}^3$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Residual Stress & Distortion in Layer-wise Deposition **Step 1: Thermal gradient assumption.** During scanning, the melt pool has temperature $T_m$, while the solidified layer below is at $T_0$. The local gradient is: $$ G = \frac{T_m - T_0}{\delta} $$ where $\delta$ = thermal boundary layer thickness. **Step 2: After cooling, the constraint from the underlying solidified material prevents free thermal contraction, generating strain:** $$ \varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot (T_m - T_0) = \alpha \cdot \Delta T $$ **Step 3: Assuming elastic constraint, the residual stress is:** $$ \sigma_r = E \cdot \varepsilon_{th} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T $$ **Step 4: For a multi-layer build, the accumulated distortion (curvature $\kappa$) of a cantilevered plate follows Stoney's equation adapted to AM:** $$ \kappa = \dfrac{6 \cdot \sigma_r \cdot h_f}{M \cdot t_s^2} $$ where $h_f$ = layer height, $t_s$ = substrate thickness, $M$ = biaxial modulus. **Step 5: Mitigation through scan rotation.** The resultant vector of stress rotates by $\phi$ per layer, reducing net distortion. For a rotation of $\theta$ per layer over $n$ layers, the effective mean residual stress is: $$ \sigma_{eff} = \dfrac{\sigma_r}{n} \cdot \left\vert \sum_{k=1}^{n} e^{i k \theta} \right\vert $$ For $\theta = 90°$ and $n$ divisible by 4, $\sigma_{eff} \to \sigma_r / \sqrt{n}$ — explaining why **67° scan rotation is empirically optimal** (it never repeats exact alignment in finite builds). --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Porosity Classification by Morphology **Step 1:** Total porosity in a built part is the sum of two mechanisms: $$ P_{total} = P_{gas} + P_{LoF} + P_{keyhole} $$ **Step 2: Gas porosity** arises from entrapped gas in the powder: $$ P_{gas} = \dfrac{V_{gas,entrapped}}{V_{total}} \propto f(\text{powder gas content},\ \text{keyhole collapse}) $$ **Step 3: Lack of fusion porosity** (irregular, sharp-edged voids) occurs when: $$ E_v < E_{v,crit} \quad \text{AND} \quad d_{melt} < t_{layer} + d_{prev} $$ **Step 4: Keyhole porosity** (spherical, larger voids) occurs when: $$ v < v_{crit} \quad \text{OR} \quad E_v > E_{v,max} $$ **Step 5: Archimedes-based density measurement (experimental):** $$ \rho_{built} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{air} - m_{water}} \cdot \rho_{water} $$ **Step 6: Computed porosity:** $$ P_{\%} = \left( 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{built}}{\rho_{theoretical}} \right) \times 100 $$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Defect → Cause → Mitigation > [!IMPORTANT] > This matrix is **gold** for KTU 14-mark questions. Use it as a structured answer template. | Defect | Morphology | Root Cause | Primary Mitigation | Secondary Mitigation | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Lack-of-fusion porosity** | Irregular, sharp, layered voids | Low $E_v$, low $P$, high $v$ | Increase laser power | Reduce scan speed, increase overlap | | **Keyhole porosity** | Spherical, large voids | Excess $E_v$, low $v$ | Increase scan speed | Reduce power, use modulated waveform | | **Gas porosity** | Spherical, small, distributed | Powder moisture, gas entrapment | Dry powder ($<$ 50 ppm H₂O) | Hot isostatic pressing (HIP) | | **Hot cracking** | Intergranular, dendritic separation | Wide freezing range, high stress | Preheating ($>$ 200 °C for Ti) | Add nucleating inoculants (e.g., TiB₂) | | **Cold cracking** | Transgranular, post-solidification | High residual stress | Stress relief (650 °C / 2 h) | Optimize scan rotation | | **Delamination** | Layer separation at interfaces | Excessive thermal gradient | Preheating | Substrate preheating to $T_s$ | | **Balling** | Spherical metal beads on surface | Poor wetting, oxidation | Increase $P$, reduce $v$ | Use O₂ $<$ 50 ppm, optimize hatch | | **Warpage/Distortion** | Out-of-plane bending | Residual stress | Base plate preheating | Constrained supports, 67° rotation | | **Staircase effect** | Stepped surface | Discrete layering | Reduce layer thickness | Multi-axis printing, post-machining | | **Spatter ejection** | Satellite particles | Recoil pressure | Higher gas flow | Deflector plates, optimized scan path | ### 3.5 Process Parameter Sensitivity Ranking (Ti-6Al-4V LPBF) > [!NOTE] > From highest to lowest influence on density (descending order of impact): $$ P \ (\text{laser power}) \ > \ v \ (\text{scan speed}) \ > \ t \ (\text{layer thickness}) \ > \ h \ (\text{hatch spacing}) $$ Empirically validated process window for Ti-6Al-4V: $$ \begin{aligned} P &\in [180, 370]\ \text{W} \\ v &\in [600, 1400]\ \text{mm/s} \\ t &\in [20, 60]\ \mu m \\ h &\in [80, 140]\ \mu m \end{aligned} $$ ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"List any six common defects in Laser Powder Bed Fusion (LPBF) and state the primary process parameter responsible for each."** **Model Answer (6 points, ~3 marks):** | # | Defect | Primary Process Parameter | |---|---|---| | 1 | Lack of fusion porosity | Low Volumetric Energy Density (VED) | | 2 | Keyhole porosity | Excess VED (high $P$, low $v$) | | 3 | Balling | Poor wetting — low $P$ / high $v$ | | 4 | Warpage / distortion | High thermal gradient → high residual stress | | 5 | Hot cracking | Wide freezing range, high stress | | 6 | Staircase effect | Excessive layer thickness | | 7 | Spatter | Recoil pressure from vaporization | --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **"Define Volumetric Energy Density (VED). What is the typical VED window for Ti-6Al-4V in LPBF?"** **Model Answer:** $$ E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} $$ - Units: $\text{J/mm}^3$ - Optimal range for Ti-6Al-4V in LPBF: **$60$–$80\ \text{J/mm}^3$** - Below this range → lack of fusion; above → keyholing. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - Students often **list defects but fail to link them to a quantitative parameter (e.g., VED)** — this loses 2–3 marks. > - Always **state the VED range, scan strategy, and post-processing step** in mitigation answers. > - Sketches of melt pool cross-sections, process-window diagrams, or Ishikawa fishbones are highly valued — include at least one. > - For 14-mark questions, KTU expects **3–4 pages** of structured answer: definition → mechanism → causes → mitigation → diagram. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Choose A or B)* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **(a)** Classify the major defects encountered in Additive Manufacturing processes. Discuss the mechanism, causes, and effects of **porosity-related defects** in detail. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain the role of **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** in determining defect formation. Derive the VED expression and discuss how a process map (VED window) can be used to identify defect-free operating conditions for LPBF of Ti-6Al-4V. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Defect Classification and Porosity Defects (7 marks):** **1. Classification of AM Defects (3 marks):** - **Geometric** — staircase effect, dimensional inaccuracy, overhang sagging. - **Volumetric** — gas porosity, lack of fusion porosity, keyhole porosity, inclusions. - **Surface** — balling, spatter, dross, high roughness. - **Microstructural** — hot/cold cracks, anisotropy, undesired phases. - **Residual Stress** — warpage, distortion, delamination. **2. Porosity Defects — Mechanism, Causes, Effects (4 marks):** - **Gas porosity:** Entrapped gas from powder (e.g., argon in atomized powder) or vaporization of low-melting constituents. Forms spherical voids, typically $< 50\ \mu m$ in diameter. **Effect:** reduces fatigue life, lowers Young's modulus, acts as crack initiator. - **Lack-of-fusion (LoF) porosity:** Occurs when $E_v$ is below the critical value. Melt pool depth $<$ layer thickness + previous-layer depth. Results in **irregular, sharp-edged voids** oriented along layer interfaces. **Effect:** severe stress concentrators, delamination risk. - **Keyhole porosity:** Occurs when laser intensity exceeds vaporization threshold ($> 10^6\ \text{W/mm}^2$). Vapor pressure deforms melt pool into a deep, narrow cavity; upon collapse, gas bubbles are trapped. Produces **spherical voids of $50$–$200\ \mu m$**. **Mitigation summary:** - Optimize $P$ and $v$ to maintain $E_v$ in the **stable process window**. - Degas / dry powder to reduce gas porosity. - Use HIP (Hot Isostatic Pressing) at $920\ °C$, $100\ \text{MPa}$ for Ti-6Al-4V to close residual porosity. --- **Part (b) — VED Derivation and Process Map (7 marks):** **1. Derivation (3 marks):** Power delivered per unit length: $P/v$ (J/mm). Energy per unit scanned area: $P/(v \cdot h)$ (J/mm²). Volume melted per unit area = layer thickness $t$ (mm). $$ E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} $$ **2. Process Map for Ti-6Al-4V (4 marks):** The process map plots $P$ on the y-axis and $v$ on the x-axis, with iso-VED curves. The **defect-free window** is bounded by: - **Lower boundary ($E_{v,crit} \approx 50\ \text{J/mm}^3$):** Below this, LoF porosity dominates. - **Upper boundary ($E_{v,max} \approx 100\ \text{J/mm}^3$):** Above this, keyholing dominates. | Parameter | Lower Limit | Upper Limit | |---|---|---| | Laser Power $P$ | 180 W | 370 W | | Scan Speed $v$ | 600 mm/s | 1400 mm/s | | Hatch Spacing $h$ | 80 µm | 140 µm | | Layer Thickness $t$ | 20 µm | 60 µm | **Process window diagram (to be drawn):** ``` P (W) 400 | KEYHOLE | ************ 300 | ** ** | * DENSE REGION * 200 |* (99.5%+) * |* * 100 | **************** | LoF +--------------------------- v (mm/s) 400 800 1400 ``` The intersection of all four parameter constraints defines the **printable window**. Ex-situ verification is done via Archimedes density measurement and CT scanning. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Alternative Choice)* **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the mechanisms of **residual stress and distortion** in AM parts. Discuss the role of **scan strategy** and **preheating** in mitigating these defects. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe the various **in-situ monitoring techniques** used to predict defects during LPBF. How can machine learning enhance defect prediction accuracy? *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Residual Stress and Distortion (7 marks):** **1. Mechanism (3 marks):** During LPBF, the laser locally heats a small region of powder to $\sim 2500\ °C$ while the surrounding substrate remains at $\sim 100\ °C$. On cooling, the constraint from the cooler surrounding material prevents free contraction, generating **tensile residual stress** in the deposited layer and **compressive stress** in the substrate. $$ \sigma_r = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T $$ - For Ti-6Al-4V: $\alpha \approx 8.6 \times 10^{-6}\ /\text{K}$, $E \approx 110\ \text{GPa}$, $\Delta T \approx 2000\ \text{K}$ → $\sigma_r$ can reach $\sim 1.9\ \text{GPa}$, exceeding yield strength → **plastic deformation, warpage, and delamination**. **2. Scan Strategy Mitigation (2 marks):** - **67° inter-layer rotation:** distributes stress vectors so the resultant averages out, reducing peak distortion by $\sim 60\%$. - **Island/hilbert scanning:** divides the layer into small islands (e.g., $5 \times 5\ \text{mm}$), randomizing stress direction. - **Bidirectional vs. unidirectional scanning:** unidirectional has higher stress concentration. **3. Preheating (2 marks):** - Substrate/base-plate preheating to $200\ °C$–$350\ °C$ for Ti-6Al-4V reduces $\Delta T$ → lowers $\sigma_r$ proportionally. - Closed-loop chamber heating (e.g., $>\ 400\ °C$ in Aconity systems) almost eliminates cracking in Ni-superalloys. **Post-processing:** Stress-relief anneal at $650\ °C$ for 2 hours (in argon). --- **Part (b) — In-Situ Monitoring and ML-based Prediction (7 marks):** **1. In-Situ Monitoring Techniques (4 marks):** - **Pyrometry:** Single or multi-wavelength photodiodes measure melt-pool temperature. Anomalies (>15% deviation from setpoint) flag potential defects. - **High-speed CMOS cameras:** Capture melt-pool morphology at $>\ 10\ \text{kHz}$. Image features (width, length, brightness) correlate with porosity. - **Optical tomography / coaxial imaging:** Captures layer-wise images after each pass. - **Acoustic emission sensors:** Detect cracking events via high-frequency sound waves. - **Laser-induced breakdown spectroscopy (LIBS):** Detects compositional anomalies. - **X-ray / thermal imaging:** Some systems use in-situ X-ray for real-time pore tracking (research-grade). **2. Machine Learning Enhancement (3 marks):** - **Supervised learning** (CNN, Random Forest): trained on labelled images of defective/defect-free layers, achieves $> 95\%$ classification accuracy. - **Unsupervised learning** (Autoencoders, Isolation Forest): detects novel defects via reconstruction error. - **Real-time inference** at $>\ 1\ \text{kHz}$ enables **closed-loop control**: process parameters adapt within a layer. - **Digital twins** integrate ML with FEM simulation for predictive defect maps pre-build. **Industrial example:** EOSPRINT 2.0 with EOSTATE MeltPool monitoring achieves $>\ 99.7\%$ density in serial aerospace part production. --- ### 5.3 KTU Common Pitfall Recap > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Marks-Loss Traps (verified against past KTU evaluation patterns):** > 1. ❌ Confusing **keyhole porosity** with **lack-of-fusion porosity** — they are opposites in cause and morphology. > 2. ❌ Writing $E_v$ formula with **wrong units** (must be $\text{J/mm}^3$). > 3. ❌ Omitting the **numerical range** of VED for specific materials. > 4. ❌ Not mentioning **post-processing (HIP, stress relief)** in 14-mark mitigation answers. > 5. ❌ Failing to draw a **process-window diagram** when asked about defect prediction. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Volumetric Energy Density** $E_v = P/(v \cdot h \cdot t)$ is the **master variable** controlling porosity. - ✅ Three porosity types: **gas, lack-of-fusion, keyhole** — each with distinct morphology and VED relationship. - ✅ **Residual stress** scales as $E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ — reduce $\Delta T$ via preheating and scan rotation. - ✅ **Staircase effect** is intrinsic to layer-wise AM — minimized by reducing $t$ or using multi-axis deposition. - ✅ **Balling** indicates poor wetting — increase $P$, reduce $v$, improve atmosphere (O₂ $<$ 50 ppm). - ✅ **Cracks** in Ti alloys = mostly hot tearing; in Ni-alloys = mostly strain-age cracking (post-heat-treatment). - ✅ **Mitigation hierarchy:** (1) process parameters → (2) scan strategy → (3) environment control → (4) post-processing (HIP). - ✅ **In-situ monitoring** (pyrometer + CMOS camera) is the **predictive backbone** of modern LPBF. - ✅ **HIP at 920 °C / 100 MPa / 4 h** in argon is the industry standard for closing sub-surface porosity in Ti-6Al-4V. - ✅ **Acceptance density threshold:** $>\ 99.5\%$ for structural, $>\ 99.9\%$ for fatigue-critical parts. - ✅ **67° scan rotation** empirically distributes residual stress; 90° is also common for symmetric parts. - ✅ **CT scanning** is the gold-standard non-destructive method for defect quantification post-build.

topics.title: Post-Processing Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Post-Processing Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Post-Processing (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> In Additive Manufacturing (AM), **Post-Processing** refers to the entire sequence of auxiliary operations performed *after* the build cycle is complete and the part has been removed from the build platform/chamber, but *before* the part is deemed ready for its intended end-use application. These operations are essential to transform a "green part" or "raw build" into a fully functional, dimensionally accurate, aesthetically acceptable, and mechanically reliable component that satisfies design tolerances, surface finish (typically **Ra ≤ 1.6 μm** for functional parts), and material property specifications.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, post-processing is formally classified under the umbrella of **"secondary operations"** in the AM process chain, and includes:

- **Mechanical post-processing** (support removal, bead blasting, tumbling, grinding, CNC machining)
- **Thermal post-processing** (stress relief, annealing, solution treatment, Hot Isostatic Pressing)
- **Chemical post-processing** (vapor smoothing, chemical polishing, etching)
- **Surface treatment post-processing** (dyeing, coating, painting, electroplating, sealing)
- **Infiltration and impregnation** (for porous sintered parts)

## 1.2 The AM Process Chain (Position of Post-Processing)

$$
\text{AM Workflow} = \underbrace{\text{Design} \rightarrow \text{STL/3MF}}_{\text{Pre-Processing}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Build}}_{\text{Primary Process}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Post-Processing}}_{\text{Secondary Process}} \rightarrow \text{Quality Inspection} \rightarrow \text{Application}
$$

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Clay Potter's Workflow**
> Imagine a potter shaping a clay vase on a wheel. The wheel-throwing (primary shaping) gives the *form*, but the vase is still rough, wet, fragile, and ugly. Before the vase is sold, the potter must:
> 1. **Trim** the excess clay (analogous to **support removal**),
> 2. **Smooth** the surface with a wet sponge (analogous to **sanding/bead blasting**),
> 3. Let it **dry slowly** to avoid cracking (analogous to **stress-relief annealing**),
> 4. **Fire it in a kiln** to harden (analogous to **sintering/HIP**),
> 5. **Glaze and paint** it for aesthetics (analogous to **coating/dyeing**).
>
> Additive Manufacturing is identical — the printer only handles step 1 (build). Steps 2–5 are *post-processing*. In fact, **post-processing can account for 30 %–60 % of the total part cost and lead time** in metal AM.

## 1.4 Why is Post-Processing Indispensable in AM?

| As-Built Issue | Root Cause | Post-Processing Required |
|---|---|---|
| Stepped/staircase surface | Layer-by-layer deposition (typical layer thickness **0.02–0.2 mm**) | Sanding, polishing, CNC machining |
| Residual stresses | Steep thermal gradients during fusion | Stress relief annealing |
| Support structures | Required to overhang geometry | Manual/automated removal |
| Loose powder stuck to part | Powder bed processes (SLS, SLM, MJF, EBM) | Compressed air, bead blasting, vacuuming |
| Porosity (in metal AM) | Lack-of-fusion voids, gas entrapment | **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)** |
| Brittle as-built polymers | Partial polymerization (SLA/DLP) | **UV post-curing** |
| Brittle sintered ceramics/metal | Low green density from binder jetting | **Infiltration / re-sintering** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Highlight:** A frequently tested concept — *the as-built surface roughness Ra is directly proportional to the layer thickness* $t_l$. Empirically:
> $$R_a \approx k \cdot t_l$$
> where $k$ is a process-dependent constant (typically $k = 0.5$ to $0.8$). This single equation appears in numerous KTU past papers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Categorical Taxonomy of Post-Processing Operations

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups post-processing into **five distinct families**. Each family targets a specific defect class introduced during the build:

### Family 1 — Support Removal & Part Extraction

- **Manual break-off**: Operator pliers/hand tools (used for FDM ABS, PLA).
- **Mechanical machining**: Band saw, wire EDM, milling for fused metal supports (DMLS/SLM titanium).
- **Water-jet cutting**: For delicate lattice structures.
- **Automated robotic depowdering**: Closed-loop systems for powder-bed metal AM.

### Family 2 — Surface Cleaning & Powder Removal

- **Compressed air blasting** (5–8 bar) for loose powder.
- **Bead blasting / sand blasting**: Glass beads of **50–200 μm** diameter, propelled at 3–6 bar to remove partially sintered powder skin.
- **Ultrasonic cleaning**: 40 kHz bath in isopropyl alcohol for polymer AM.
- **Airbrush + vacuum extraction** for fine internal channels.

### Family 3 — Surface Finishing & Polishing

| Technique | Achievable Ra (μm) | Process Compatibility |
|---|---|---|
| Manual sanding (SiC paper, grit 80→2000) | 0.8 – 3.2 | All AM |
| Tumbling/Vibratory finishing | 0.4 – 1.6 | Polymer, soft metal |
| Bead blasting | 1.6 – 6.3 | DMLS, EBM, SLS |
| Chemical vapor smoothing | 0.05 – 0.4 | **SLS (PA12), MJF (PA12)** |
| CNC ball-end milling | 0.2 – 0.8 | Metal AM only |
| Electrochemical polishing | 0.1 – 0.4 | Conductive metals |
| Shot peening | 0.8 – 3.2 (and adds compressive stress) | Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel |

### Family 4 — Thermal Post-Processing (Heat Treatment)

This is the **most heavily tested family** in KTU ESE papers. It includes:

- **Stress-Relief Annealing**: Heat below the recrystallization temperature to relieve locked-in residual stresses without significantly altering microstructure.
  - For **Ti-6Al-4V (DMLS)**: 600 °C – 700 °C, 2 h, slow furnace cool.
  - For **AlSi10Mg (SLM)**: 300 °C – 350 °C, 2 h.
  - For **Inconel 718 (SLM)**: 870 °C – 980 °C, 1 h.
- **Solution Treatment + Aging (T6 for Al alloys)**: 530 °C × 6 h → water quench → 160 °C × 8 h aging.
- **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)**: 100–200 MPa Argon, 1100–1200 °C for Ti alloys. Eliminates **>99 %** of internal porosity.
- **Sintering (in binder jetting & SLS)**: Final fusion of binder-bounded powder.

### Family 5 — Infiltration & Curing

- **Infiltration**: Filling the porous matrix (40 %–60 % density green part) with a lower-melting metal (e.g., bronze infiltrating 316L stainless steel).
- **UV Post-Curing (SLA/DLP)**: 405 nm UV flood exposure × 30–120 min + 60 °C thermal cure to achieve full polymerization conversion.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following formulas are the high-yield equations for KTU Module 4 derivations and numerical problems. Memorize this table.**

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Formula} & \textbf{Physical Meaning} & \textbf{Units} \\
\hline
R_a \approx k \cdot t_l & \text{Surface roughness vs. layer thickness} & \mu\text{m} \\
\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T / (1-\nu) & \text{Biaxial thermal stress (clamped plate)} & \text{MPa} \\
\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T & \text{Thermal strain} & - \\
\dot{\varepsilon} = A \cdot \exp(-Q/RT) & \text{Creep / annealing rate (Arrhenius)} & \text{s}^{-1} \\
t_{1/2} \propto \exp(Q/RT) & \text{Time for half-stress-relief} & \text{s} \\
\rho_{rel} = \rho_{AM} / \rho_{th} & \text{Relative density (HIP quality metric)} & - \\
V_{inf} = V_{pore} \cdot \phi_{fill} & \text{Infiltrant volume required} & \text{mm}^3 \\
\rho_{th} = m / (V_{part} - V_{inf}) & \text{Density by Archimedes principle} & \text{g/cm}^3 \\
T_{cure} = T_{g} - 20\,^\circ\text{C} & \text{UV resin curing temperature (SLA/DLP)} & \,^\circ\text{C} \\
\sigma_{ult}^{HIP} / \sigma_{ult}^{AB} \approx (1 - \phi)^{-1/2} & \text{Balshin porosity-strength relation} & - \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

Where the symbols are:
- $E$ — Young's modulus (GPa)
- $\alpha$ — Coefficient of thermal expansion (10⁻⁶/K)
- $\nu$ — Poisson's ratio (dimensionless)
- $\Delta T$ — Temperature differential (K)
- $Q$ — Activation energy for creep (kJ/mol)
- $R$ — Universal gas constant (**8.314 J/mol·K**)
- $\phi$ — Porosity fraction
- $\phi_{fill}$ — Pore-filling efficiency (typically 0.85–0.95)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** Whenever an equation contains `|x|` or absolute-value-style notation in a markdown table, write it as `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid`. The vertical pipe `|` is **reserved** for column delimiters in markdown.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Post-Processing

- **Aerospace (Ti-6Al-4V brackets)**: HIP + CNC finish-machining yields fatigue life within **10 %** of wrought equivalents.
- **Medical implants (CoCr knee)**: Stress relief + electrochemical polishing pass **ISO 5832** biocompatibility.
- **Automotive (SLS PA12 manifolds)**: Vapor smoothing reduces Ra from ~12 μm to ~2 μm, enabling leak-tight pneumatic assemblies.
- **Jewelry (DLP/SLA castable resin)**: UV cure → burnout at 750 °C → investment casting — post-processing is *essential*, not optional.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Biaxial Residual Thermal Stress in an AM Plate

This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark derivation** under stress-relief annealing. We derive the locked-in stress when an AM plate cools through a temperature differential $\Delta T$ while rigidly clamped to the build plate.

### Step 1 — Free Thermal Strain
If the plate were *unconstrained*, it would freely contract upon cooling. The uniaxial thermal strain is defined as:

$$
\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = linear coefficient of thermal expansion, in K⁻¹
- $\Delta T = T_{solidus} - T_{ambient}$ in K

### Step 2 — Constrained Strain via Hooke's Law (3D Generalization)
When fully clamped, the actual mechanical strain $\varepsilon_{mech}$ must counteract the free thermal strain, so the total strain $\varepsilon_{tot} = 0$:

$$
\varepsilon_{tot} = \varepsilon_{mech} + \varepsilon_{th} = 0
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{mech} = -\varepsilon_{th} = -\alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

### Step 3 — Generalize to Plane Stress (Biaxial in-plane state)
For a thin plate, the out-of-plane stress $\sigma_z = 0$. The in-plane strains are:

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_x - \nu \sigma_y) + \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_y - \nu \sigma_x) + \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

For a square plate clamped equally on both edges, by symmetry $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma$. Setting both strains to zero:

$$
0 = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma - \nu \sigma) + \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $\sigma$
Factor the expression:

$$
0 = \frac{\sigma}{E}(1 - \nu) + \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

Rearrange to isolate $\sigma$:

$$
\frac{\sigma}{E}(1 - \nu) = -\alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
\sigma = -\frac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{1 - \nu}
$$

### Step 5 — Magnitude Form (Residual Stress in MPa)

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{th} = \frac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{1 - \nu}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the negative sign:** The plate is in *compression* if $\Delta T$ is imposed at heating, and in *tension* if $\Delta T$ represents cooling (typical AM scenario — as-built tensile residual stresses up to **60 %–80 %** of yield strength in DMLS Ti-6Al-4V).

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)
For **Ti-6Al-4V**:
- $E = 110$ GPa $= 110 \times 10^3$ MPa
- $\alpha = 8.6 \times 10^{-6}$ K⁻¹
- $\nu = 0.34$
- $\Delta T = T_{solidus} - T_{ambient} = 1660\,^\circ\text{C} - 25\,^\circ\text{C} = 1635$ K

$$
\sigma_{th} = \frac{110 \times 10^{3} \cdot 8.6 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1635}{1 - 0.34}
$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$
\text{Numerator} = 110 \times 10^{3} \cdot 8.6 \times 10^{-6} = 0.946 \text{ MPa/K}
$$

$$
0.946 \cdot 1635 = 1546.71 \text{ MPa (intermediate)}
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 1 - 0.34 = 0.66
$$

$$
\sigma_{th} = \frac{1546.71}{0.66} \approx 2343.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Observation:** This exceeds the yield strength of Ti-6Al-4V (~880 MPa), confirming that **as-built DMLS parts MUST undergo stress-relief annealing** to avoid distortion or cracking.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Arrhenius Stress-Relief Kinetics

The kinetics of stress relief follow an Arrhenius-type temperature dependence. The time $t$ required to relieve a fraction $f$ of the initial stress at temperature $T$ is:

$$
t = t_0 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{Q}{R T}\right)
$$

### Step 1 — Take natural logarithm of both sides

$$
\ln(t) = \ln(t_0) + \frac{Q}{R T}
$$

### Step 2 — Define two test conditions
At two different holding temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$, the corresponding stress-relief times are $t_1$ and $t_2$:

$$
\ln(t_1) = \ln(t_0) + \frac{Q}{R T_1}
$$

$$
\ln(t_2) = \ln(t_0) + \frac{Q}{R T_2}
$$

### Step 3 — Subtract the two equations

$$
\ln(t_1) - \ln(t_2) = \frac{Q}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for activation energy $Q$

$$
\boxed{Q = \frac{R \cdot \ln(t_1 / t_2)}{\left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)}}
$$

### Numerical Example — Ti-6Al-4V Stress Relief
- $T_1 = 873$ K, $t_1 = 4$ hours
- $T_2 = 973$ K, $t_2 = 1$ hour

$$
\ln\left(\frac{t_1}{t_2}\right) = \ln(4) = 1.386
$$

$$
\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{873} - \frac{1}{973} = 1.145 \times 10^{-3} - 1.028 \times 10^{-3} = 1.176 \times 10^{-4}\, \text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{8.314 \cdot 1.386}{1.176 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
Q \approx \frac{11.52}{1.176 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 97{,}959 \text{ J/mol} \approx 98 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

This matches the experimentally reported **Q ≈ 95–105 kJ/mol** for dislocation climb-controlled creep relief in Ti-6Al-4V — confirming the model.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Infiltrant Volume Calculation (Archimedes Density)

For binder-jetted bronze-infiltrated 316L stainless steel:

### Step 1 — Measure the green part density

$$
\rho_{green} = \frac{m_{green}}{V_{green}}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute pore volume

$$
V_{pore} = V_{green} \cdot (1 - \rho_{green}/\rho_{th,\,steel})
$$

### Step 3 — Infiltrant mass required

$$
m_{inf} = V_{pore} \cdot \phi_{fill} \cdot \rho_{th,\,bronze}
$$

### Step 4 — Final density after infiltration

$$
\boxed{\rho_{final} = \frac{m_{green} + m_{inf}}{V_{green}}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Surface Finish Selection by AM Process

$$
\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{AM Process} & \textbf{As-Built Ra (}\mu\text{m)} & \textbf{Best Finishing Route} & \textbf{Final Ra (}\mu\text{m)} \\
\hline
\text{FDM (PLA/ABS)} & 8 - 25 & \text{Sanding + acetone vapor (ABS)} & 0.4 - 1.6 \\
\text{SLA/DLP} & 0.4 - 5 & \text{UV cure + micro-polish} & 0.05 - 0.2 \\
\text{SLS (PA12)} & 8 - 15 & \text{Vapor smoothing} & 0.5 - 2 \\
\text{MJF (PA12)} & 6 - 12 & \text{Bead blasting} & 1.6 - 3.2 \\
\text{DMLS/SLM (steel)} & 5 - 20 & \text{HIP + CNC milling} & 0.2 - 0.8 \\
\text{EBM (Ti)} & 10 - 30 & \text{Bead blast + machining} & 0.4 - 1.6 \\
\text{Binder Jet (metal)} & 5 - 15 & \text{Sinter + infiltrate + polish} & 0.8 - 3.2 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Complete Post-Processing Decision Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[As-Built AM Part] --> B{Support Structures?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Support Removal]
    B -- No --> D[Powder Removal]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Material Class?}
    E -- Polymer --> F[UV Cure or Vapor Smoothing]
    E -- Metal --> G[Stress Relief Annealing]
    E -- Ceramic --> H[Sintering and Infiltration]
    F --> I[Surface Finishing]
    G --> J[HIP Optional]
    J --> I
    H --> I
    I --> K{Quality Inspection}
    K -- Pass --> L[Final Part]
    K -- Fail --> M[Rework Loop]
    M --> I
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Every as-built AM part enters at node `A`.
- The first branch `B` removes supports (mechanical depowdering follows).
- A second branch `E` routes to material-specific thermal/chemical post-processing.
- All paths converge at node `I` (surface finishing) and proceed to QA gate `K`.
- Failure at QA triggers a rework loop `M` back to surface finishing.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Stress-Relief Annealing Thermal Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Room Temp] --> B[Ramp Up 10 C per min]
    B --> C[Hold at Trelief 1 to 4 h]
    C --> D[Furnace Cool 5 C per min]
    D --> E[Room Temp Part Ready]
    styleC[fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical caution for KTU:** Rapid quench (water/air) after stress relief can *re-introduce* thermal gradients — always use slow furnace cooling.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Hot Isostatic Pressing Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Encapsulated Part] --> B[Load into HIP Vessel]
    B --> C[Vacuum Pump Down]
    C --> D[Heat to 1100 to 1200 C]
    D --> E[Pressurize Argon to 100 to 200 MPa]
    E --> F[Hold for 2 to 4 h]
    F --> G[Cool under Pressure]
    G --> H[Depressurize]
    H --> I[HIP Treated Part with 99 percent Densification]
```

**Working principle:** The combination of *isostatic* pressure (equal in all directions) and *elevated* temperature causes internal pores to plastically collapse and diffuse-bond shut, dramatically increasing fatigue life and ductility.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — UV Post-Curing Cycle for SLA/DLP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[As-Built Resin Part] --> B[Isopropyl Alcohol Wash 5 to 10 min]
    B --> C[UV Flood Cure 405 nm 30 min]
    C --> D[Thermal Cure 60 C 60 min]
    D --> E[Final Cured Part]
    styleC[fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333]
```

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Multi-Stage Post-Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Stage 1 Mechanical]
        A1[Depowdering]
        A2[Support Removal]
        A3[Bead Blasting]
    end
    subgraph S2[Stage 2 Thermal]
        B1[Stress Relief]
        B2[HIP or Sintering]
        B3[Solution Treatment]
    end
    subgraph S3[Stage 3 Surface]
        C1[Sanding and Polishing]
        C2[CNC Machining]
        C3[Vapor Smoothing]
    end
    subgraph S4[Stage 4 Quality]
        D1[CMM Dimensional Check]
        D2[Surface Profilometer]
        D3[CT Scan Internal Defects]
    end
    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the necessity of post-processing in additive manufacturing. List any four post-processing operations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Post-processing is essential because as-built AM parts possess **inherent defects** that render them unfit for direct use:

1. **Staircase surface roughness** — caused by layer-by-layer deposition; requires sanding/CNC machining.
2. **Residual stresses** — caused by steep thermal gradients; require **stress-relief annealing**.
3. **Internal porosity** — caused by lack-of-fusion voids; requires **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)**.
4. **Trapped/support structures** — required for overhangs; must be mechanically or thermally removed.

> The student should additionally state that **30 %–60 % of total part cost** in industrial AM is in post-processing, justifying its engineering importance.

**(Examiner allocation: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for two defects + 1 mark for listing operations.)**

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)? Mention its key process parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **HIP** is a thermal post-processing technique in which a metal AM part is subjected to **simultaneously high isostatic pressure (typically 100–200 MPa of Argon) and elevated temperature (typically 0.7–0.9 × T_melting, e.g., 1100–1200 °C for Ti alloys)** inside a sealed pressure vessel.

**Key process parameters:**
- **Pressure:** 100–200 MPa
- **Temperature:** 0.7–0.9 × T_melting (in Kelvin)
- **Hold time:** 2–4 hours
- **Atmosphere:** High-purity Argon
- **Cooling rate:** Slow, under pressure

HIP closes internal gas pores and lack-of-fusion voids, raising relative density to **>99.9 %** and substantially improving fatigue life.

**(Examiner allocation: 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for parameters.)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**(a)** Derive the expression for biaxial residual thermal stress in an AM plate cooled from solidus to ambient temperature. Discuss why stress-relief annealing is mandatory for **DMLS Ti-6Al-4V** parts and outline the recommended cycle.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

*(See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation. The student should reproduce:)*

Step 1 — Free thermal strain $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \Delta T$
Step 2 — Constrained condition: $\varepsilon_{tot} = 0 \Rightarrow \varepsilon_{mech} = -\alpha \Delta T$
Step 3 — Biaxial plane-stress with $\sigma_z = 0$ and $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma$:

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma - \nu\sigma) + \alpha \Delta T = 0
$$

Step 4 — Solve:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{th} = \frac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{1-\nu}}
$$

Step 5 — For **Ti-6Al-4V** with $E=110$ GPa, $\alpha = 8.6 \times 10^{-6}$ K⁻¹, $\nu = 0.34$, $\Delta T = 1635$ K:

$$
\sigma_{th} = \frac{110 \times 10^{3} \cdot 8.6 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1635}{0.66} \approx 2343 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since this **exceeds the yield strength (~880 MPa)**, as-built DMLS Ti-6Al-4V would distort/crack during support removal.

**Recommended Stress-Relief Cycle for Ti-6Al-4V:**
- Ramp up at **10 °C/min** to **700 °C**
- Hold for **2 hours** in vacuum or Argon
- Furnace cool at **5 °C/min** to 300 °C, then air cool

**(b)** Explain the **chemical vapor smoothing** process for SLS/MJF PA12 parts. State its advantages and limitations.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Vapor smoothing exposes the porous SLS/MJF surface to a **solvent vapor** (commonly **tetrahydrofuran (THF)** or proprietary solvents like **PostProcess Technologies' DECOAT/SMOOTH**). The vapor condenses on the part surface, partially dissolving the outer polymer skin and re-solidifying into a smooth, sealed finish.

**Process Steps:**
1. Place the as-built part in a sealed vapor chamber.
2. Heat solvent to its boiling point (THF: 66 °C).
3. Allow vapor to contact part for **20–60 s**.
4. Air dry — surface re-solidifies with $R_a \leq 2$ μm.

**Advantages:**
- Reduces Ra from ~12 μm to ~2 μm (≈80 % reduction).
- Seals surface pores — eliminates leak paths.
- Enhances fatigue strength.
- Uniform finish on complex internal channels.

**Limitations:**
- Dimensional change: **0.5 %–2 %** shrinkage may occur.
- Not suitable for all polymers (avoid ABS, ASA — they warp).
- Toxic solvents require fume extraction.
- Surface hardness decreases marginally.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternative]`
**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the complete post-processing sequence for a **DMLS 316L stainless steel** aerospace bracket. Discuss the role of HIP in this sequence.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

*(Student should draw a flowchart similar to Section 4.5 and explain each stage.)*

**Complete Sequence for DMLS 316L Bracket:**

1. **Build-plate removal** — Wire EDM or band saw cuts the part from the substrate.
2. **Support removal** — Manual cutting using carbide tools; supports are typically the same 316L material.
3. **Stress-relief annealing** — 900 °C × 2 h, slow furnace cool (relieves ~80 % of residual stress).
4. **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)** — 1150 °C, 100 MPa Argon, 4 h → density rises to **>99.95 %**.
5. **Solution annealing** — 1050 °C × 30 min, water quench.
6. **CNC finish machining** — Critical tolerances (typically ±0.05 mm), surface finish Ra ≤ 0.8 μm.
7. **Bead blasting + passivation** (HNO₃/HF dip) for corrosion resistance.
8. **Dimensional CMM + CT inspection**.

**Role of HIP:**
- Closes internal lack-of-fusion pores.
- Improves fatigue life by **10×** in rotating-bending tests.
- Enhances ductility and impact toughness.
- Critical for aerospace qualification per **AMS 7000-series** specifications.

**(b)** Explain **UV post-curing** of SLA/DLP parts. Derive an expression for the glass-transition-based curing temperature and justify why thermal post-cure at $T_g - 20\,^\circ\text{C}$ is optimal.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

UV post-curing is a chemical post-processing step where the as-built photopolymer (e.g., acrylate or epoxy resin) is subjected to **405 nm UV flood exposure** to drive the residual monomer conversion from ~70 %–85 % (post-build) to >95 % (post-cure).

**Mechanism:**
- Photoinitiator absorbs UV → generates free radicals → crosslinks residual C=C double bonds.
- Higher conversion = higher $T_g$, hardness, and dimensional stability.

**Optimal Cure Temperature — Derivation:**

The cure reaction rate follows Arrhenius kinetics:

$$
\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = k_0 \exp(-E_a/RT) \cdot (1 - \alpha)^n
$$

where $\alpha$ is the degree of conversion and $n$ is the reaction order.

Empirically, the glass transition temperature $T_g$ rises monotonically with $\alpha$. At full conversion ($\alpha \rightarrow 1$), $T_g \rightarrow T_{g,\infty}$. The DiBenedetto relation:

$$
T_g(\alpha) = T_{g,0} + \frac{(T_{g,\infty} - T_{g,0}) \cdot \lambda \alpha}{1 - (1-\lambda)\alpha}
$$

For most SLA resins, $T_{g,\infty} - T_{g,0} \approx 100\,^\circ\text{C}$ and $\lambda \approx 0.5$.

**Optimal cure temperature:**

$$
\boxed{T_{cure} = T_{g,\infty} - 20\,^\circ\text{C}}
$$

**Justification:**
- At $T_{cure} = T_g - 20\,^\circ\text{C}$, polymer chain mobility is high (rubbery state) → residual monomers diffuse and crosslink efficiently.
- Above $T_g$: dimensional inaccuracy and part deformation.
- Below $T_g$: vitrification halts conversion prematurely.

**For Formlabs Clear Resin:** $T_{g,\infty} \approx 70\,^\circ\text{C}$, so $T_{cure} = 50\,^\circ\text{C}$ for 60 minutes following UV exposure.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 Post-Processing Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "1−ν" denominator** in the biaxial thermal stress derivation → loses **3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Confusing SLA post-curing with FDM annealing** — they are entirely different physical processes.
> 3. **Stating HIP pressure in bar instead of MPa** — KTU uses SI units; **1 bar = 0.1 MPa**.
> 4. **Skipping the role of Argon gas** in HIP — examiners specifically ask for the *atmosphere* used.
> 5. **Not mentioning furnace cooling** for stress relief — quenching will introduce new residual stresses.
> 6. **Omitting powder removal steps** in flowcharts — the examiner expects it as the *first* step after support removal.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Post-Processing Techniques — KTU 2024 Revision Snapshot**

- **Definition:** Post-processing = all secondary operations between part removal and end-use.
- **Five families:** Mechanical, Thermal, Chemical, Surface, Infiltration/Curing.
- **As-built Ra ≈ 0.5–0.8 × layer thickness** — the master equation.
- **Biaxial thermal stress formula:** $\sigma_{th} = E\alpha\Delta T / (1-\nu)$ — must be memorized.
- **Stress-relief cycles (must memorize):**
  - Ti-6Al-4V: **700 °C / 2 h / furnace cool**
  - AlSi10Mg: **300 °C / 2 h**
  - Inconel 718: **870 °C / 1 h**
- **HIP parameters:** **100–200 MPa Argon**, **0.7–0.9 × T_melt**, **2–4 h** hold.
- **HIP eliminates >99 % porosity** and boosts fatigue life **~10×**.
- **Vapor smoothing** of PA12 reduces Ra from ~12 μm → ~2 μm using THF vapor.
- **UV post-cure** for SLA/DLP: **405 nm × 30 min** + **60 °C × 60 min**.
- **Optimal cure temp = $T_g - 20\,^\circ\text{C}$** (vitrification rationale).
- **Infiltration volume:** $V_{inf} = V_{pore} \cdot \phi_{fill}$; density by Archimedes.
- **Bead blasting:** glass beads **50–200 μm**, pressure **3–6 bar**.
- **Cost impact:** post-processing accounts for **30 %–60 %** of total AM part cost.
- **Process chain order:** Depowder → Support removal → Stress relief → HIP → Machining → Surface finish → Inspection.
- **Constant to remember:** Universal gas constant $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K.
- **Standard finish target:** $R_a \leq 1.6 \mu\text{m}$ for functional mechanical parts.

---

**End of Module 4 — Post-Processing Techniques Notes**
*Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | 2024 NEP-Aligned Scheme*
