# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Design for Additive Manufacturing (DfAM). Mention any two major advantages.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Design for Additive Manufacturing (DfAM) is the engineering discipline of designing components specifically optimized for layer-by-layer additive manufacturing processes, exploiting geometric freedom, part consolidation, and topology optimization while respecting process constraints such as minimum wall thickness, support requirements, and anisotropy.

> **Two Major Advantages (1 mark):**
> 1. **Part Consolidation** — multiple components of an assembly are merged into a single printed part, reducing BOM, fasteners, and assembly time.
> 2. **Complexity is Free** — internal lattice structures, organic topologies, and conformal features can be produced with negligible cost penalty compared to solid parts.

---

### **Q2. What is the self-supporting overhang angle in AM, and why is it important?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **self-supporting overhang angle** is the critical angle (measured from the vertical) below which an overhanging surface cannot be printed without auxiliary support structures. The **empirically accepted threshold is θₛ = 45°** from the vertical for powder-bed fusion (DMLS/SLS) and FDM processes.

**Importance (2 marks):**
- Beyond 45°, the **unsupported layer collapses or sags** due to insufficient material below to bond with.
- Maintaining overhangs within the safe angle **eliminates support structures**, reducing material waste, post-processing time, and surface scarring from support removal.
- It is a **primary design constraint** in DfAM — features that violate the 45° rule must be redesigned (chamfered, teardropped, or re-oriented).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 4 carries internal choice. **Answer ANY ONE** of the two full-14-mark questions below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — DfAM Process Deep Dive** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **seven DfAM guidelines** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The seven guidelines of DfAM are:

1. **Part Consolidation** — Merging multiple components into a single printed part. *Example:* GE LEAP fuel nozzle — 18 parts consolidated into one DMLS-printed nickel-superalloy nozzle with 25% weight reduction.

2. **Topology Optimization** — Algorithmic removal of material from low-stress regions to produce organic, organic-shaped load paths. *Example:* Aircraft bracket redesigned with TO showing giraffe-necked curved members.

3. **Lattice & Cellular Structures** — Use of repeating unit cells (gyroid, Schwarz primitive) to fill internal volumes. Provides tailored stiffness, energy absorption, and thermal exchange. *Example:* Conformal bone-scaffold implants.

4. **Minimize / Eliminate Supports** — Redesign overhangs to fall within the 45° self-support zone. Use teardrop-shaped holes in horizontal planes instead of circular holes. Reduces material and post-processing cost by up to **70%**.

5. **Hollow Parts with Escape Holes** — Convert solid volumes into thin-walled shells with **escape holes of diameter ≥ 4 mm** to drain unfused powder (DMLS) or uncured resin (SLA). Escape holes must be on the lowest surface of the build orientation.

6. **Functional Integration** — Combine multiple functions (structural, thermal, fluidic, electrical) into a single feature. *Example:* Conformal cooling channels in injection-mold inserts reducing cycle time by 40%.

7. **Build Orientation Planning** — Strategic choice of part orientation to minimize stair-stepping on critical surfaces, reduce support volume, and align layer lines perpendicular to principal loads for maximum strength.

---

**(b)** A DMLS part is built with layer thickness **t = 0.05 mm**. A critical cosmetic surface is inclined at **θ = 30°** from the horizontal. Calculate: **(i)** the maximum stair-stepping error, and **(ii)** the peak-to-valley height. Comment on whether post-machining is required if the cosmetic tolerance is **± 0.05 mm**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Layer thickness, $t = 0.05$ mm
- Surface angle from horizontal, $\theta = 30°$
- Cosmetic tolerance = $\pm 0.05$ mm

**Step 1 — Stair-Stepping Error (δₛ) — (3 marks):**

$$\delta_s = t \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

$$\delta_s = 0.05 \times \cos(30°)$$

$$\delta_s = 0.05 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 0.05 \times 0.8660$$

$$\delta_s = 0.0433 \text{ mm} \approx 43.3 \text{ μm}$$

**Step 2 — Peak-to-Valley Height (P-V) — (2 marks):**

$$P\text{-}V = t \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

$$P\text{-}V = 0.05 \times \sin(30°) = 0.05 \times 0.5 = 0.025 \text{ mm} = 25 \text{ μm}$$

**Step 3 — Tolerance Comparison — (2 marks):**

- Stair-stepping error δₛ = **0.0433 mm** is **less than** the cosmetic tolerance of **0.05 mm**.
- Peak-to-valley = **0.025 mm** is also within tolerance.
- **Conclusion:** Post-machining is **NOT required** for this cosmetic surface; the as-built DMLS surface meets the tolerance band. ✅

> **However**, since 30° is well below the 45° self-supporting angle, this surface would require **support structures** during the build — a trade-off the designer must accept or eliminate by **re-orienting** the part or **re-angling** the cosmetic face.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss **Topology Optimization** and **Lattice Structures** as two major DfAM strategies. Derive the Gibson-Ashby scaling law and explain the difference between **stretch-dominated** and **bending-dominated** cellular structures. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Topology Optimization (TO) — (2 marks):**

TO is a computational structural optimization technique that removes material from low-stress regions of a design domain, subject to a load case and boundary conditions. The mathematical objective is to minimize **compliance** $c(x)$ (inverse of stiffness) subject to a **volume fraction constraint** $f$:

$$\min c(x) = U^T K U = \sum_{e=1}^{N} E_e(x_e) u_e^T k_0 u_e$$

Subject to:
$$\frac{V(x)}{V_0} \leq f \quad ; \quad K U = F$$

**Lattice Structures — (2 marks):**

Lattices are repeating arrays of unit cells (e.g., body-centered cubic, octet truss, gyroid) that fill a 3D volume with a network of struts and nodes. They are characterized by **relative density** $\rho^* = \rho_{\text{lattice}} / \rho_{\text{bulk}}$, typically in the range 0.05–0.40.

**Derivation of Gibson-Ashby Law — (2 marks):**

By dimensional analysis and experimental fitting of cellular solids, the effective modulus $E^*$ scales as a power law of relative density:

$$\frac{E^*}{E_s} = C \cdot (\rho^*)^n$$

The exponent $n$ depends on the **deformation mode** of the unit cell:
- **Stretch-dominated** (octet truss, cubic): $n \approx 1.0$ — struts carry axial load efficiently.
- **Bending-dominated** (BCC, foam-like Kelvin): $n \approx 1.8$ to $2.0$ — struts bend before stretching.

**Practical implication:** Stretch-dominated lattices deliver **higher stiffness per unit mass**, making them preferred for structural applications; bending-dominated lattices excel in **energy absorption** (crashworthiness, cushioning).

---

**(b)** A bracket weighing **2.4 kg** in its original solid Ti-6Al-4V design is to be redesigned as a **gyroid lattice-filled** AM part. If the solid core occupies **30%** of the bounding volume and the gyroid lattice has a **relative density of 0.20**, calculate: **(i)** the bulk mass of Ti-6Al-4V required (ρ_Ti = 4.43 g/cm³), **(ii)** the final mass of the AM bracket, and **(iii)** the percentage mass savings. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Original mass, $M_0 = 2.4$ kg = 2400 g
- Solid core volume fraction = 30% of bounding volume
- Gyroid relative density, $\rho^* = 0.20$ (i.e., 20% of the lattice envelope is solid)
- Density of Ti-6Al-4V, $\rho_{\text{Ti}} = 4.43$ g/cm³

**Step 1 — Bounding Volume of Original Bracket — (2 marks):**

$$V_{\text{bound}} = \frac{M_0}{\rho_{\text{Ti}}} = \frac{2400}{4.43} = 541.76 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Mass of Solid Core in AM Bracket — (2 marks):**

$$V_{\text{core}} = 0.30 \times 541.76 = 162.53 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$M_{\text{core}} = V_{\text{core}} \times \rho_{\text{Ti}} = 162.53 \times 4.43 = 720.0 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of Gyroid Lattice Section — (1.5 marks):**

$$V_{\text{lattice region}} = 0.70 \times 541.76 = 379.23 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$V_{\text{lattice solid}} = 0.20 \times 379.23 = 75.85 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$M_{\text{lattice}} = 75.85 \times 4.43 = 336.0 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4 — Total Mass and Savings — (1.5 marks):**

$$M_{\text{AM total}} = 720.0 + 336.0 = 1056.0 \text{ g} = 1.056 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Mass Savings \%} = \frac{2400 - 1056}{2400} \times 100\% = 56\%$$

**Conclusion:** The redesigned AM bracket weighs only **1.056 kg**, achieving a **56% mass reduction** versus the original 2.4 kg solid bracket. ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Confusing relative density with volume fraction:** Relative density $\rho^* = 0.20$ applies to the *lattice region only*, not the entire bounding volume. Students often wrongly multiply $\rho^*$ by the entire bounding volume.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Density is in **g/cm³** while mass is in **grams**. Students mixing kg/cm³ lose a full mark.
> 3. **Skipping Step 1:** Many students directly use the 2.4 kg figure for the new design — **incorrect**, because the redesign reshapes the bounding volume.
> 4. **Gibson-Ashby derivation:** Examiners expect the **dimensional-analysis argument** (Buckingham's Pi) before the empirical power-law form. Skipping this loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DFAM — Last-Minute KTU Board Revision Sheet**

- ✅ **Definition:** DfAM = systematic design methodology fully exploiting AM's geometric and material freedoms while respecting process limits.
- ✅ **Core Philosophy:** *"Complexity is Free; Volume is the only Cost."*
- ✅ **Seven Pillars (in order):** Part Consolidation → Topology Optimization → Lattice Structures → Support Minimization → Hollowing + Escape Holes → Functional Integration → Build Orientation.
- ✅ **Self-Supporting Overhang Angle:** **θₛ = 45° from vertical** (memorize).
- ✅ **Stair-Stepping Error:** $\delta_s = t \cdot \cos(\theta)$.
- ✅ **Peak-to-Valley Height:** $P\text{-}V = t \cdot \sin(\theta)$.
- ✅ **Minimum Wall Thickness:** FDM ≥ **0.8 mm**; SLA ≥ **0.3 mm**; DMLS ≥ **0.3–0.4 mm**.
- ✅ **Escape Hole Diameter:** **≥ 4 mm**, placed on **lowest surface** of build orientation.
- ✅ **Lattice Relative Density Range:** **0.05 to 0.40**.
- ✅ **Gibson-Ashby Law:** $E^*/E_s = C \cdot (\rho^*)^n$; $n \approx 1$ (stretch), $n \approx 1.8$–$2.0$ (bending).
- ✅ **Topology Optimization Constraint:** Volume fraction $f$ typically **0.3 to 0.5**.
- ✅ **Anisotropy Index:** Layer lines should be oriented **perpendicular to principal tensile load** for max strength.
- ✅ **Hole-on-Horizontal-Plane Rule:** Use **teardrop shapes** for self-supporting horizontal holes.
- ✅ **Build Orientation Trade-offs:** Surface finish vs. support volume vs. mechanical strength vs. build time.
- ✅ **Industrial DfAM Heroes:** GE LEAP nozzle (consolidation), Bugatti brake caliper (TO), SpaceX SuperDraco (functional integration).
- ✅ **Exam Tip:** Always cite **process-specific** numbers (45° for DMLS, 0.8 mm wall for FDM) — vague answers lose marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Lightweight and Porous Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET752 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Lightweight and Porous Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Lightweight Structures** in Additive Manufacturing refer to engineered geometries where material is strategically placed only along load paths, reducing the overall mass while retaining (or even enhancing) the required mechanical, thermal, or functional performance. They are realized through **Design for Additive Manufacturing (DfAM)** principles using three primary strategies: **topology optimization**, **lattice/strut-based cellular structures**, and **shell/multi-wall thin geometries**.

**Porous Structures** are a subset of lightweight structures characterized by controlled internal voids, open or closed cells, with relative densities typically **ρ*/ρs < 0.3** (where ρ* is the apparent density and ρs is the solid parent material density). They include **Triply Periodic Minimal Surfaces (TPMS)** and stochastic foams, and are extensively used in **biomedical implants, heat exchangers, and acoustic dampers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course expects students to differentiate between *lattice structures* (discrete periodic, strut/node based) and *TPMS* (continuous, mathematically implicit surfaces), and to articulate the relationship between **relative density, stiffness, and energy absorption** through the **Gibson–Ashby scaling laws**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of a **bird's bone** (e.g., a hollow femur of an eagle) versus a **marble block** of the same outer dimensions:
- The marble is solid — heavy, stiff, but uses material where it is **not needed**.
- The bird bone is **hollow on the outside** with a **trabecular (sponge-like) internal lattice** — material is placed exactly where the bird's flight load paths demand it.

This is precisely the philosophy of **lightweight AM design**: do not subtract material from a solid (like machining); instead, **add material only where physics says it should be**. The same analogy applies to the *Eiffel Tower* (open truss) versus a solid iron column — both carry the same load, but the tower uses 60–70% less mass.

> [!NOTE]
> Porous structures are like a **kitchen sponge** — bulk volume appears solid, but contains interconnected voids that dramatically reduce mass and can host functional properties (fluid flow, cell ingrowth, energy absorption).

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

In KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic is a **high-weightage, frequently tested concept** because it:
1. Tests **analytical understanding** of cellular solid mechanics (Gibson–Ashby equations).
2. Requires **classification skills** (strut-based vs TPMS vs topology-optimized).
3. Connects directly to **biomedical, aerospace, and automotive applications** — frequent application-based questions appear.

> [!TIP]
> Examiner's Tip: Always mention **relative density (ρ*/ρs)** as the governing parameter — most KTU questions hinge on this dimensionless number.

---

## 1.4 Classification Overview

| Category | Examples | Typical ρ*/ρs | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solid (baseline) | Machined block | **1.0** | Reference |
| Shell / Thin-Wall | Hollow box, honeycomb | 0.2–0.5 | 2D dominant |
| Strut Lattice | BCC, FCC, Octet truss | 0.05–0.4 | Discrete beams |
| TPMS | Gyroid, Schwarz P/D | 0.1–0.5 | Implicit surface |
| Stochastic Foam | Voronoi, open-cell foam | 0.1–0.3 | Random, biomimetic |
| Topology Optimized | Organic free-form | 0.2–0.5 | Load-path driven |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Design Philosophy

The design of lightweight and porous structures in AM is governed by **three pillars**:

1. **Mechanical Efficiency** — Maximize stiffness/strength-to-weight ratio.
2. **Functional Integration** — Embed properties (heat transfer, cell-ingrowth, energy absorption).
3. **Manufacturability** — Self-supporting geometry (no support structures needed) → lower build cost.

## 2.2 The Relative Density Master Parameter

The single most important dimensionless parameter is **Relative Density**:

$$\rho^* / \rho_s$$

where:
- **ρ*** = Apparent (bulk) density of the porous/lattice structure.
- **ρs** = Density of the solid parent material (e.g., Ti-6Al-4V ≈ **4.43 g/cm³**).

> When **ρ*/ρs → 0**, the structure approaches an idealized open-cell foam; when **→ 1**, it approaches the solid.

## 2.3 Gibson–Ashby Scaling Laws (The Heart of the Topic)

**Sir William Gibson and Michael Ashby (1982, 1997)** developed the foundational scaling relationships for cellular solids. For an open-cell foam of relative density **ρ*/ρs**, the effective mechanical properties scale as:

**Relative Young's Modulus:**
$$E^* / E_s = C_1 \, (\rho^* / \rho_s)^2$$

**Relative Compressive (Plateau) Strength:**
$${\sigma^* / \sigma_{ys}} = C_2 \, (\rho^* / \rho_s)^{3/2}$$

**Relative Shear Modulus:**
$$G^* / G_s = C_3 \, (\rho^* / \rho_s)^{3/2}$$

**Relative Poisson's Ratio (for open-cell foams):**
$$\nu^* \approx 0.33 \;\; (\text{approximately constant, independent of } \rho^*/\rho_s)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants:** For **open-cell polymeric foams**, C₁ ≈ **1.0**, C₂ ≈ **0.3**. For **metallic lattices (BCC, octet-truss)**, C₁ ≈ **0.1–0.4**, C₂ ≈ **0.1–0.3** (lower because of joint and buckling effects). KTU examiners often quote **C₁ = 1, C₂ = 0.3** for the textbook case.

**Physical Interpretation:**
- Modulus scales as **(ρ*/ρs)²** → a structure at 30% relative density retains only **~9%** of the solid modulus.
- Strength scales as **(ρ*/ρs)^(3/2)** → a structure at 30% relative density retains **~16%** of the solid strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **non-linear, super-efficient** scaling that makes porous structures attractive: **massive mass savings with disproportionately smaller property loss.**

## 2.4 Triply Periodic Minimal Surfaces (TPMS) — Mathematical Foundation

TPMS are implicit surfaces defined by a level-set equation **f(x, y, z) = t**, where **t** controls the volume fraction. They are *minimal surfaces* (zero mean curvature everywhere) and naturally smooth — ideal for AM.

| TPMS Type | Implicit Equation f(x, y, z) | Symmetry |
|---|---|---|
| **Schwarz Primitive (P)** | $\cos(x) + \cos(y) + \cos(z) = t$ | Cubic |
| **Schwarz Diamond (D)** | $\sin(x)\sin(y)\sin(z) + \sin(x)\cos(y)\cos(z) + \cos(x)\sin(y)\cos(z) + \cos(x)\cos(y)\sin(z) = t$ | Diamond cubic |
| **Gyroid** | $\sin(x)\cos(y) + \sin(y)\cos(z) + \sin(z)\cos(x) = t$ | Cubic, chiral |
| **Neovius** | $3[\cos(x)+\cos(y)+\cos(z)] + 4\cos(x)\cos(y)\cos(z) = t$ | Cubic |
| **Schoen I-WP** | $2[\cos(x)\cos(y) + \cos(y)\cos(z) + \cos(z)\cos(x)] - [\cos(2x)+\cos(2y)+\cos(2z)] = t$ | Body-centered cubic |

> All coordinates are **scaled by 2π/L**, where **L** is the unit cell size.

## 2.5 Strut Lattice Unit Cells

| Lattice | Z (Coordination No.) | Relative Density Formula (approx.) | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simple Cubic (SC) | 6 | $\rho^*/\rho_s \approx 2\pi (d/L)^2$ | Brittle, weak |
| BCC | 8 | $\rho^*/\rho_s \approx (3\sqrt{3}\,\pi/4)(d/L)^2$ | Anisotropic, bending-dominated |
| FCC | 12 | Higher than BCC | More isotropic |
| BCCZ (reinforced) | 14 | With vertical strut added | Improved strength |
| **Octet-Truss** | 12 | Highest of cubic | **Stretch-dominated**, stiffest |

> [!TIP]
> **Stretch-dominated** (octet, FCC) lattices are far stiffer than **bending-dominated** (BCC, SC) lattices at the same relative density — a high-yield KTU point.

## 2.6 Mass Reduction Formula

$$\text{Mass Reduction (\%)} = \frac{M_{solid} - M_{AM}}{M_{solid}} \times 100$$

For a uniform density material, this reduces to:
$$\text{Mass Reduction (\%)} = \left(1 - \frac{\rho^*}{\rho_s}\right) \times 100$$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho^*/\rho_s$ | Relative density | dimensionless |
| 2 | $E^*/E_s = C_1(\rho^*/\rho_s)^2$ | Gibson–Ashby modulus | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\sigma^*/\sigma_{ys} = C_2(\rho^*/\rho_s)^{3/2}$ | Gibson–Ashby strength | dimensionless |
| 4 | $G^*/G_s = C_3(\rho^*/\rho_s)^{3/2}$ | Shear scaling | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\nu^* \approx 0.33$ | Poisson's ratio (open-cell) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{MR\%} = (1 - \rho^*/\rho_s)\times 100$ | Mass reduction | % |
| 7 | $f(x,y,z) = t$ | TPMS level-set | varies |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do **NOT** use the vertical pipe `|` for absolute value inside table cells. Use `\vert` or `\mid` if needed, or rewrite as a square / squared expression to avoid breaking the markdown.

## 2.8 Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Bracket, hinge, and rib designs with topology optimization → 40–60% mass savings in Boeing 787 and Airbus A350 components.
- **Biomedical:** TPMS-Gyroid hip implants with **ρ*/ρs ≈ 0.3–0.5** match bone modulus (avoiding *stress shielding*).
- **Heat Exchangers:** TPMS Gyroid with 80% porosity → high surface-area/volume for convective cooling.
- **Energy Absorption:** Lattice crash-boxes absorb impact energy at near-constant plateau stress.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Numerical Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are **fully worked-out derivations and numerical evaluations**. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No step-skipping placeholders are used.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Gibson–Ashby Modulus Scaling from Beam Mechanics

We derive why $E^*/E_s \propto (\rho^*/\rho_s)^2$ for an open-cell foam.

**Step 1 — Start with a single strut (edge) of the unit cell.**
Consider one slender beam of length **L**, square cross-section of side **t**, made of the solid material of Young's modulus **E_s**.

**Step 2 — Write the bending stiffness of one beam.**
The second moment of area for a square cross-section is:
$$I = \frac{t^4}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the axial stiffness of one beam under axial load.**
$$k_{axial} = \frac{E_s \, A}{L} = \frac{E_s \, t^2}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the bending stiffness of one beam under transverse load.**
$$k_{bend} = \frac{E_s \, I}{L^3} = \frac{E_s \, t^4}{12 L^3}$$

**Step 5 — Relate the beam thickness t to the relative density of the unit cell.**
For an open-cell foam, the relative density is proportional to the cross-sectional area divided by the unit-cell face area:
$$\rho^*/\rho_s \propto (t^2 / L^2) = (t/L)^2$$

So we can write:
$$t/L = (\rho^*/\rho_s)^{1/2}$$

**Step 6 — Express the effective modulus ratio.**
The macroscopic modulus is proportional to the *beam stiffness per unit volume*. For bending-dominated deformation:
$$E^* \propto \frac{k_{bend}}{L} \propto \frac{E_s \, t^4}{L^4}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute t/L = (ρ*/ρs)^(1/2).**
$$E^* \propto E_s \left(\frac{t}{L}\right)^4 \propto E_s \left[(\rho^*/\rho_s)^{1/2}\right]^4 = E_s \, (\rho^*/\rho_s)^2$$

**Step 8 — Introduce the empirical constant C₁.**
$$E^*/E_s = C_1 (\rho^*/\rho_s)^2 \quad \text{...(Gibson–Ashby)}$$

This completes the derivation. The exponent **n = 2** arises naturally because the modulus depends on **t⁴** (bending) while relative density depends on **t²** — hence **4/2 = 2**.

> [!NOTE]
> For **stretch-dominated** lattices (octet-truss), the deformation mode is *axial* rather than bending, so $E^*/E_s \propto (\rho^*/\rho_s)^1$ (linear scaling) — these lattices are far more efficient.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relative Density of a Simple Cubic (SC) Lattice

**Step 1 — Define the unit cell.**
A cubic unit cell of side **L** with one strut (rod) of circular cross-section, diameter **d**, along each of the 3 axes.

**Step 2 — Compute strut volume.**
Each strut has volume $\frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot L$. There are 3 full struts in the cell (edges shared with 4 cells, but a primitive cell has 3 edges of length L each):
$$V_{struts} = 3 \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4} L$$

**Step 3 — Compute cell volume.**
$$V_{cell} = L^3$$

**Step 4 — Compute relative density.**
$$\rho^*/\rho_s = \frac{V_{struts}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{3 \pi d^2}{4 L^2}$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of the slenderness ratio d/L.**
$$\rho^*/\rho_s = \frac{3\pi}{4} \left(\frac{d}{L}\right)^2 \approx 2.36 \, (d/L)^2$$

**Step 6 — Example: d = 0.5 mm, L = 5 mm.**
$$\rho^*/\rho_s = 2.36 \times (0.5/5)^2 = 2.36 \times 0.01 = 0.0236 \approx 2.36\%$$

**Step 7 — Interpret.**
A lattice with struts of 0.5 mm diameter in a 5 mm cell has only **2.36%** of the solid density — a **97.6% mass reduction**, but with severely compromised stiffness (since $E^*/E_s = 1 \times (0.0236)^2 = 0.000557$, i.e., **0.056% of the solid modulus**).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Strength Scaling (σ*/σys)

The same beam approach applies, but failure occurs when the **maximum stress in the beam** reaches the yield strength σys.

**Step 1 — Maximum bending stress in a beam under load F, length L, section modulus Z = t³/6.**
$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{(FL/2)}{t^3/6} = \frac{3FL}{t^3}$$

**Step 2 — At failure, σmax = σys.**
$$F_{fail} = \frac{\sigma_{ys} \, t^3}{3L}$$

**Step 3 — The macroscopic failure stress σ*** is F_fail divided by the cell cross-sectional area L².
$$\sigma^* = F_{fail}/L^2 = \frac{\sigma_{ys} \, t^3}{3L^3} = \frac{\sigma_{ys}}{3} (t/L)^3$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (t/L) = (ρ*/ρs)^(1/2).**
$$\sigma^* = \frac{\sigma_{ys}}{3} \left[(\rho^*/\rho_s)^{1/2}\right]^3 = \frac{\sigma_{ys}}{3} (\rho^*/\rho_s)^{3/2}$$

**Step 5 — General form with empirical constant C₂.**
$$\sigma^*/\sigma_{ys} = C_2 (\rho^*/\rho_s)^{3/2}$$

> For ideal open-cell foam, **C₂ = 1/3 ≈ 0.3** — this is the textbook value used in KTU problems.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 1: Modulus and Mass Reduction

**Problem:** A Ti-6Al-4V lattice structure (E_s = **114 GPa**, ρ_s = **4.43 g/cm³**) is designed with a relative density of **ρ*/ρs = 0.25**. Calculate (a) the effective Young's modulus, (b) the effective compressive strength, and (c) the mass reduction percentage. Use C₁ = 1, C₂ = 0.3, σys = **880 MPa**.

**Step (a) — Effective Young's Modulus.**
$$E^* = C_1 \cdot E_s \cdot (\rho^*/\rho_s)^2$$
$$E^* = 1 \times 114 \times (0.25)^2 = 114 \times 0.0625 = 7.125 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step (b) — Effective Compressive Strength.**
$$\sigma^* = C_2 \cdot \sigma_{ys} \cdot (\rho^*/\rho_s)^{3/2}$$
$$\sigma^* = 0.3 \times 880 \times (0.25)^{3/2}$$
$$(0.25)^{3/2} = (\sqrt{0.25})^3 = (0.5)^3 = 0.125$$
$$\sigma^* = 0.3 \times 880 \times 0.125 = 33.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step (c) — Mass Reduction.**
$$\text{MR\%} = (1 - 0.25) \times 100 = 75\%$$

**Result Summary:**
- Effective modulus dropped from 114 GPa to **7.125 GPa** (factor of 16).
- Effective strength dropped from 880 MPa to **33 MPa** (factor of ~27).
- But the **mass is reduced by 75%** — an excellent trade-off for non-load-critical applications.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 2: Required Cell Size for a Stiffness Target

**Problem:** A polymer lattice (E_s = **3 GPa**, ρ_s = **1.2 g/cm³**, C₁ = 1) must achieve an effective modulus of **E* = 150 MPa**. Find the required relative density and the corresponding mass reduction.

**Step 1 — Apply Gibson–Ashby equation.**
$$E^*/E_s = C_1 (\rho^*/\rho_s)^2$$
$$\frac{150}{3000} = 1 \times (\rho^*/\rho_s)^2$$
$$0.05 = (\rho^*/\rho_s)^2$$

**Step 2 — Solve for ρ*/ρs.**
$$\rho^*/\rho_s = \sqrt{0.05} = 0.2236 \approx 22.4\%$$

**Step 3 — Mass Reduction.**
$$\text{MR\%} = (1 - 0.2236) \times 100 = 77.6\%$$

**Conclusion:** A **22.4% relative density** polymer lattice provides the target modulus with a **77.6% mass saving**.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example 3: TPMS Gyroid Volume Fraction

**Problem:** A Gyroid TPMS structure is generated in a unit cube [0, 2π]³ using the equation:
$$\sin(x)\cos(y) + \sin(y)\cos(z) + \sin(z)\cos(x) = t$$
Determine the value of **t** that yields approximately **50% volume fraction (ρ*/ρs = 0.5)**.

**Step 1 — Recognize that the Gyroid is symmetric about t = 0.**
The function **f(x,y,z) = sin(x)cos(y) + sin(y)cos(z) + sin(z)cos(x)** is odd and has mean value zero over the unit cell.

**Step 2 — At t = 0, the surface divides space into two equal-volume regions.**
By symmetry, the volume fraction at t = 0 is exactly **0.5**.

**Step 3 — Effect of varying t.**
- As **t** increases positively, the solid region shrinks → volume fraction decreases.
- As **t** decreases negatively (toward negative values), the solid region grows → volume fraction increases.
- The mapping **V_f ≈ 0.5 − k·t** (where k is a cell-specific constant) is approximately linear for small t.

**Step 4 — Result.**
**t = 0 gives 50% volume fraction.** For other volume fractions, the user controls t in the CAD software (e.g., nTopology, Materialise Magics, MSLattice).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — DfAM Process Flow for Lightweight Structures

The following Mermaid block depicts the end-to-end **Design for Additive Manufacturing** workflow, from loading conditions to final print-ready file.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Define Functional Requirements] --> A1[Identify Load Case and Boundary Conditions]
    A1 --> A2[Choose Design Strategy]
    A2 --> A21[Option 1: Topology Optimization]
    A2 --> A22[Option 2: Lattice Infill]
    A2 --> A23[Option 3: TPMS Internal Structure]
    A2 --> A24[Option 4: Shell or Thin Wall]
    A21 --> B0[CAD Generation in nTopology / Altair Inspire]
    A22 --> B0
    A23 --> B0
    A24 --> B0
    B0 --> C0[Validate Relative Density Target]
    C0 --> C1[Apply Gibson-Ashby Check]
    C1 --> D0[Finite Element Analysis FEA]
    D0 --> D1[Check Stress and Displacement]
    D1 --> E0{Design Passes?}
    E0 -- No --> A1
    E0 -- Yes --> F0[Slice and Generate G-code]
    F0 --> G0[AM Process: SLM / SLS / FDM / SLA]
    G0 --> H0[Post-Processing and Testing]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Classification of Lattice and Porous Structures

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CLS[Lightweight AM Structures] --> CS1[Strut-Based Lattices]
    CLS --> CS2[TPMS Structures]
    CLS --> CS3[Stochastic Foams]
    CLS --> CS4[Topology Optimized]
    CS1 --> SB1[Simple Cubic SC]
    CS1 --> SB2[Body-Centered Cubic BCC]
    CS1 --> SB3[Face-Centered Cubic FCC]
    CS1 --> SB4[Octet Truss]
    CS1 --> SB5[BCC with Z Reinforcement]
    CS2 --> TP1[Gyroid]
    CS2 --> TP2[Schwarz Primitive P]
    CS2 --> TP3[Schwarz Diamond D]
    CS2 --> TP4[Neovius]
    CS2 --> TP5[Schoen IWP]
    CS3 --> ST1[Voronoi Tessellation]
    ST1 --> ST2[Open-Cell Random Foam]
    ST1 --> ST3[Closed-Cell Random Foam]
    CS4 --> TO1[Organic Free-Form Load-Path]
    TO1 --> TO2[Generative Design Output]
    SB2 --> BD1[Bending-Dominated: Weaker]
    SB4 --> SD1[Stretch-Dominated: Stiffer]
    TP1 --> TM1[Chiral: Excellent Fluid Flow]
    TP1 --> TM2[Biocompatible: Bone Ingrowth]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Property vs Relative Density Trade-off Curve (Schematic Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PA[Relative Density rho by rho_s] --> PB{Region of Operation}
    PB -- 0.0 to 0.1 --> PC[Ultra-Light Foams: Energy Absorption, Crash]
    PB -- 0.1 to 0.3 --> PD[Structural Lattice: Aerospace Panels, Sandwich Cores]
    PB -- 0.3 to 0.5 --> PE[Biomedical Implants: Bone-Mimicking Modulus]
    PB -- 0.5 to 0.7 --> PF[Load-Bearing Components: Aerospace Brackets]
    PB -- 0.7 to 1.0 --> PG[Near-Solid: Localized Reinforcement Only]
    PA --> SA[Effective Modulus E by E_s]
    PA --> SB[Effective Strength sigma by sigma_ys]
    SA --> SC[Scales as rho by rho_s squared]
    SB --> SD[Scales as rho by rho_s to power 1.5]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Lattice Infill in a Bracket

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EX[External Solid Shell] --> SH1[0.5 mm Top Face Sheet]
    EX --> SH2[0.5 mm Bottom Face Sheet]
    EX --> SH3[0.5 mm Side Wall]
    SH1 --> IN1[Internal Lattice Infill Region]
    SH3 --> IN1
    SH2 --> IN1
    IN1 --> LZ[Lattice Type: Octet Truss]
    LZ --> P1[Unit Cell Size: 5 mm]
    LZ --> P2[Strut Diameter: 0.6 mm]
    LZ --> P3[Relative Density Target: 0.20]
    P3 --> RT[Result: 80 percent Mass Reduction with 16 percent Effective Modulus]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** A typical AM lightweight bracket is a **sandwich construction** — solid outer skin for surface features and load introduction, with internal lattice infill carrying bulk loads. This is exactly how **Airbus A350 cabin bracket** and **GE Concept Laser fuel nozzle** parts are designed.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** for density, modulus, strength (always write GPa, MPa, g/cm³).
> 2. **Confusing the exponent 2 vs 1.5** between modulus and strength scaling.
> 3. **Conflating *relative density* with *porosity***. Porosity = 1 − (ρ*/ρs), and these two are **complementary**, not synonyms.
> 4. **Not stating the Gibson–Ashby constants C₁, C₂** in numerical answers — marks deducted.
> 5. **Mixing up bending-dominated (BCC) with stretch-dominated (Octet)** lattices — a classic 1–2 mark trap.
> 6. **Skipping the equation form** for TPMS — at least *one* level-set equation must be written for full marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Triply Periodic Minimal Surfaces (TPMS) and list any two examples with their implicit equations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
TPMS are smooth, periodic, zero-mean-curvature surfaces that divide space into two interpenetrating but non-intersecting domains. They are mathematically defined by an implicit level-set equation **f(x, y, z) = t**, where t controls the volume fraction. Being smooth and self-supporting, they are ideal for AM.

**Examples:**
1. **Gyroid:** $\sin(x)\cos(y) + \sin(y)\cos(z) + \sin(z)\cos(x) = t$
2. **Schwarz Primitive (P):** $\cos(x) + \cos(y) + \cos(z) = t$

(1 mark definition + 1 mark each for the two equations.)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the Gibson–Ashby scaling laws for the effective Young's modulus and compressive strength of an open-cell foam. Mention the typical values of the empirical constants.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For an open-cell cellular solid of relative density $\rho^*/\rho_s$:

$$E^*/E_s = C_1 \left(\rho^*/\rho_s\right)^2$$

$$\sigma^*/\sigma_{ys} = C_2 \left(\rho^*/\rho_s\right)^{3/2}$$

- For ideal open-cell polymer foams: **C₁ ≈ 1.0** and **C₂ ≈ 0.3**.
- For metallic strut lattices, these constants are lower (C₁ ≈ 0.1–0.4, C₂ ≈ 0.1–0.3) due to joint effects and buckling.

(1 mark for modulus equation, 1 mark for strength equation, 1 mark for constants.)

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, explain the **Topology Optimization** approach for designing lightweight AM components. Discuss the SIMP (Solid Isotropic Material with Penalization) method and its key parameters.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A sandwich panel of dimensions 200 mm × 200 mm × 10 mm is manufactured in AlSi10Mg (E_s = **70 GPa**, ρ_s = **2.67 g/cm³**, σys = **200 MPa**) with a **Gyroid TPMS core** of relative density **ρ*/ρs = 0.30**. Calculate:
1. The effective Young's modulus of the core using the Gibson–Ashby relation (assume C₁ = 0.5).
2. The effective compressive strength of the core (C₂ = 0.3).
3. The total mass of the panel if face sheets are 0.5 mm thick solid AlSi10Mg.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Topology Optimization (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Topology Optimization (TO) is a mathematical optimization method that distributes material within a given design space to maximize performance (e.g., minimize compliance) subject to loads and constraints.

**SIMP Method — Solid Isotropic Material with Penalization (3 marks):**
In SIMP, the design domain is discretized into finite elements, each assigned a **pseudo-density** $\rho_i \in [0, 1]$. The element stiffness is interpolated as:
$$E_i = \rho_i^p \cdot E_s$$
where **p ≥ 3** is the **penalization exponent**. A high p pushes pseudo-densities toward discrete 0 or 1 (void or solid), avoiding intermediate gray regions.

**Key Parameters (2 marks):**
- **Volume fraction constraint** V* (typical 30–50%).
- **Penalization factor p** (typically 3).
- **Filter radius** (to ensure mesh-independent, manufacturable solutions).
- **Boundary conditions** and **load cases**.

**Workflow:** Define design space → apply loads/BCs → run SIMP iteration → check manufacturability → export to STL.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** ρ*/ρs = 0.30, E_s = 70 GPa, σys = 200 MPa, C₁ = 0.5, C₂ = 0.3.

**1) Effective Young's Modulus (2 marks):**
$$E^* = C_1 \cdot E_s \cdot (\rho^*/\rho_s)^2$$
$$E^* = 0.5 \times 70 \times (0.30)^2 = 0.5 \times 70 \times 0.09 = 3.15 \text{ GPa}$$

**2) Effective Compressive Strength (2 marks):**
$$\sigma^* = C_2 \cdot \sigma_{ys} \cdot (\rho^*/\rho_s)^{3/2}$$
$$(\rho^*/\rho_s)^{3/2} = (0.30)^{1.5} = (0.30) \times \sqrt{0.30} = 0.30 \times 0.5477 = 0.1643$$
$$\sigma^* = 0.3 \times 200 \times 0.1643 = 9.86 \text{ MPa}$$

**3) Total Mass of Panel (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Volume of solid panel = 200 × 200 × 10 = 400,000 mm³ = 400 cm³.

**Step 2:** Volume of core region = 200 × 200 × (10 − 0.5 − 0.5) = 200 × 200 × 9 = 360,000 mm³ = 360 cm³.

**Step 3:** Core apparent volume of material = 360 × 0.30 = 108 cm³.

**Step 4:** Volume of two face sheets = 2 × (200 × 200 × 0.5) = 40,000 mm³ = 40 cm³ (fully solid).

**Step 5:** Total solid-equivalent volume = 108 + 40 = 148 cm³.

**Step 6:** Total mass = 148 × 2.67 = **395.16 g ≈ 395 g**.

(Compare with the fully solid panel mass = 400 × 2.67 = 1068 g → mass reduction ≈ **63%**.)

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **strut-based lattices** and **TPMS structures** for AM. Sketch and explain at least three unit cells in each category. Discuss their relative merits.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A stainless steel (E_s = **200 GPa**, ρ_s = **7.85 g/cm³**, σys = **300 MPa**) octet-truss lattice is designed with relative density **ρ*/ρs = 0.15**. Using Gibson–Ashby with C₁ = 0.3, C₂ = 0.2, calculate:
1. Effective Young's modulus and strength.
2. The maximum axial load the lattice can carry if its cross-section is 30 mm × 30 mm.
3. Comment on whether the lattice is bending- or stretch-dominated.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Strut Lattices vs TPMS (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | Strut-Based Lattices | TPMS Structures |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Discrete 1D beams/nodes | Continuous 2D implicit surface |
| Mathematical form | Explicit, vertex-edge graph | Implicit level-set f(x,y,z)=t |
| Surface area | Lower | Higher (very high SA/V ratio) |
| Manufacturability | Depends on strut angle | Always self-supporting |
| Best for | Structural stiffness | Heat exchangers, biomedical, flow |
| Failure mode | Joint / strut buckling | Surface rupture |
| Examples | SC, BCC, FCC, Octet | Gyroid, Schwarz P/D, Neovius |

**Sketches (2 marks):** Three unit cells each (SC, BCC, Octet for strut-based; Gyroid, Schwarz P, Schwarz D for TPMS).

**Merits (1 mark):**
- Strut lattices: High stiffness per unit mass, predictable failure, easier to analyze.
- TPMS: No stress concentrations, smooth fluid flow, bone-mimicking porosity, no support structures required.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** ρ*/ρs = 0.15, E_s = 200 GPa, σys = 300 MPa, C₁ = 0.3, C₂ = 0.2, A = 30 × 30 = 900 mm².

**1) Effective Properties (2 marks):**
$$E^* = 0.3 \times 200 \times (0.15)^2 = 60 \times 0.0225 = 1.35 \text{ GPa}$$
$$\sigma^* = 0.2 \times 300 \times (0.15)^{1.5}$$
$$(0.15)^{1.5} = 0.15 \times \sqrt{0.15} = 0.15 \times 0.3873 = 0.0581$$
$$\sigma^* = 0.2 \times 300 \times 0.0581 = 3.486 \text{ MPa}$$

**2) Maximum Axial Load (2 marks):**
$$P_{max} = \sigma^* \times A = 3.486 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 900 \text{ mm}^2 = 3137.4 \text{ N} \approx 3.14 \text{ kN}$$

**3) Bending or Stretch Dominated? (3 marks):**
The **Octet-truss** lattice has coordination number Z = 12, the maximum possible for a cubic truss. With high Z and tetrahedral symmetry, it is **stretch-dominated** (members carry primarily axial loads, not bending). This is confirmed by the **low exponent** of effective modulus scaling — for an ideal stretch-dominated lattice, $E^*/E_s \propto (\rho^*/\rho_s)^1$, while for bending-dominated it is $\propto (\rho^*/\rho_s)^2$. The given C₁ = 0.3 < 1 is consistent with a stretch-dominated but real (with joint effects) lattice. Hence, **this octet-truss lattice is stretch-dominated**, providing superior stiffness-to-weight performance.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Bullet Point Density Sheet**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] **Relative density** $\rho^*/\rho_s$ is the **master parameter** for all porous/lattice structures.
- [ ] **Porosity** = $1 - \rho^*/\rho_s$ (complementary to relative density).
- [ ] **Mass Reduction (%)** = $(1 - \rho^*/\rho_s) \times 100$.

### Gibson–Ashby (MUST memorize)
- [ ] $E^*/E_s = C_1 (\rho^*/\rho_s)^2$ — **modulus scales with square**.
- [ ] $\sigma^*/\sigma_{ys} = C_2 (\rho^*/\rho_s)^{3/2}$ — **strength scales with 1.5 power**.
- [ ] $C_1 \approx 1$, $C_2 \approx 0.3$ for ideal open-cell foam.
- [ ] Modulus exponent of **2** is for **bending-dominated**; exponent of **1** is for **stretch-dominated**.

### Lattice Families
- [ ] **Bending-dominated (weak):** SC, BCC — Z ≤ 8.
- [ ] **Stretch-dominated (stiff):** Octet-truss, FCC — Z ≥ 12.
- [ ] Higher Z → higher stiffness → higher specific strength.

### TPMS (MUST know at least 2 equations)
- [ ] **Gyroid:** $\sin(x)\cos(y) + \sin(y)\cos(z) + \sin(z)\cos(x) = t$
- [ ] **Schwarz P:** $\cos(x) + \cos(y) + \cos(z) = t$
- [ ] **Schwarz D:** $\sin(x)\sin(y)\sin(z) + \sin(x)\cos(y)\cos(z) + \cos(x)\sin(y)\cos(z) + \cos(x)\cos(y)\sin(z) = t$
- [ ] **Neovius:** $3[\cos(x)+\cos(y)+\cos(z)] + 4\cos(x)\cos(y)\cos(z) = t$

### DfAM Philosophy
- [ ] Topology optimization gives **load-path-driven** organic geometry.
- [ ] SIMP uses **penalization exponent p ≥ 3** to enforce 0/1 design.
- [ ] Lattice infill + solid skin = **sandwich construction** for AM brackets.

### Engineering Applications
- [ ] **Aerospace brackets:** 40–60% mass savings (Boeing 787, Airbus A350).
- [ ] **Biomedical implants:** Gyroid with $\rho^*/\rho_s \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ matches bone modulus (avoid stress shielding).
- [ ] **Heat exchangers:** TPMS Gyroid with 80% porosity maximizes SA/V.
- [ ] **Energy absorption:** Lattice crash-boxes with plateau-stress profile.

### Key Material Data (for Numerical Problems)
- [ ] Ti-6Al-4V: E_s ≈ 114 GPa, ρ_s ≈ 4.43 g/cm³.
- [ ] AlSi10Mg: E_s ≈ 70 GPa, ρ_s ≈ 2.67 g/cm³.
- [ ] SS 316L: E_s ≈ 200 GPa, ρ_s ≈ 7.85 g/cm³.
- [ ] Polymer (PA12): E_s ≈ 1.6 GPa, ρ_s ≈ 0.95 g/cm³.

### Common Numerical Tricks
- [ ] $(0.25)^{3/2} = 0.125$.
- [ ] $(0.5)^{3/2} = 0.354$.
- [ ] $(0.30)^{3/2} \approx 0.164$.
- [ ] $(0.15)^{3/2} \approx 0.058$.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For any numerical problem on this topic, **always state the Gibson–Ashby equation, the constants C₁, C₂ you are using, and the units of the final answer** — these are the three things examiners scan for when awarding partial marks.
